representative.title: Protected-sender gate suppresses label-moves but still applies star and category to protected mail
representative.evidence: For protected senders, move_via_label=True (base.py:334) skips add_labels (341), but base.py:356-363 still call self.star() and self.apply_category() unconditionally. On Outlook (folder, LABEL_IS_MOVE=True), star() is a PATCH adding flagStatus (outlook.py:557-587, no /move) and apply_category() is a PATCH adding a categories array (outlook.py:323-362, no /move). Neither ejects from inbox, so the never-archive safety invariant holds — but a protected sender the user may treat as do-not-touch still gets flagged/categorized, contradicting the 'neutralize every out-of-inbox operation' framing only at the policy level, not the safety level.
representative.rationale: Code confirms star/category run for protected senders; correctly scoped as a policy/UX decision, not a safety-invariant breach — low severity stands.

representative.title: 1Password subprocess uses check_output with broad except; password failures swallowed, and op CLI arg injection via env-controlled item name
representative.evidence: Lines 114-121: `try: result = subprocess.check_output(["op","item","get",op_item,"--account",op_account,f"--field={op_field}"], text=True).strip(); return result except Exception as e: logger.warning(...)` then falls through to `raise ValueError(...)` at 123. Part (a) is real: broad `except Exception` swallows the true cause (e.g. op binary missing) and emits a generic ValueError. Part (b) `--field={op_field}` f-string interpolation into argv could allow op-flag injection IF op_field were attacker-controlled.
representative.rationale: Broad-except masking is real (low); the 'arg injection' half is overstated because op_account/op_item/op_field are entirely user-controlled env vars, not an attacker channel — no privilege boundary is crossed.

representative.title: Docstring says '>30 days' but query uses 'older_than:7d' (also comment says 7 days) — stale/inconsistent threshold
representative.evidence: Line 3 docstring: 'Mark old (>30 days) emails ... as READ'. Line 41: query = f"label:{category} is:unread older_than:7d". Lines 39-40 inline comment also says '7 days'. The docstring's 30-day claim contradicts the actual 7-day threshold.
representative.rationale: Real doc/behavior mismatch on a mutating op, but blast radius is read-state only (removes UNREAD, never archives/deletes) so medium is inflated; downgraded to low.

representative.title: Feed image_url points to an HTML app page (/app/), not an image asset
representative.evidence: acp/feed.py:47 `"image_url": f"{base}/app/"` -> product_feed.json:12,32 image_url = https://mail.example.com/app/. api/app.py:162 `app.mount("/app", StaticFiles(directory=str(_WEB_DIR), html=True), name="web")` serves /app/ as an HTML dashboard (index.html via html=True), not an image asset. A feed consumer fetching image_url for a thumbnail gets text/html.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: image_url resolves to an HTML page, not an image; minor feed-quality/spec smell, low severity is accurate.

representative.title: VIP attribution re-runs regex over all VIP patterns and attributes a message to the FIRST matching pattern only; overlapping VIPs undercounted, import re inside loop
representative.evidence: Lines 761-772: after cat_result.is_vip, iterates vip_senders, `import re` (764) inside the loop body, `re.search(vip.pattern, msg.sender, re.IGNORECASE)` then `break` on first match (765-772). First-match-only attribution; uncompiled unvalidated pattern; no try/except. If two patterns match a sender, only the first dict-iteration match is credited.
representative.rationale: Confirmed and slice marks corroborated=true. Real but low-impact: first-match-only undercounts overlapping VIPs in a display-only report; in-loop import is harmless. The missing-error-guard portion overlaps U548 (same call site).

representative.title: Archive move and folder move share no idempotency/verification — re-running archive on an already-archived message issues a redundant move and a failed move is only logged
representative.evidence: archive() (544-555) POSTs /move with destinationId='archive' with no prior check of current folder and no read-back; the sole success signal is _api_post not raising (line 551-552). apply_label (527-537) likewise moves without verification. There is no idempotency guard analogous to apply_category's membership check.
representative.rationale: Factually accurate. A redundant re-move on an already-archived message is harmless (Graph treats it as a no-op move), so impact is low; the 'silent stay in inbox on failure' relies on the separate round-1 #652 alias concern.

representative.title: merge() silently merges actions for DIFFERENT message_ids; only the docstring says 'assumed'
representative.evidence: core/models.py:97-98 hardcodes message_id=self.message_id with no equality check; docstring line 96 only says 'same message_id assumed'. Confirmed merge() has NO production caller — grep '\.merge(' finds it only in tests/test_models.py:100-126. The defect (silent adoption of self.message_id) is real as written.
representative.rationale: Accurate read of the code, but severity medium is overstated for an unguarded public method with zero callers; it is a latent API-hardening gap, not an active money/data-path bug.

representative.title: combined_text space-joins sender and subject, letting subject tokens satisfy sender/domain-anchored patterns (the .gov-anchor root cause)
representative.evidence: core/models.py:58 returns f"{self.sender} {self.subject}".lower(). The cross-file corroboration holds: core/rules.py:1007-1008 builds the identical inline string and _find_best_label (rules.py:1032) does re.search(pattern, combined_text). BUT grep shows combined_text the PROPERTY has zero production callers (only core/models.py:56 defines it; rules.py inlines its own copy) — so hardening the property is moot. Critically, the protected-archive gate does NOT use this string: is_protected_sender (rules.py:797) parses the sender header via _iter_sender_domains and the .gov terminal-label rule operates on the PARSED sender domain, never on combined_text. So a subject-injected 'irs.gov' affects categorization/priority labeling only, not the never-archive gate.
representative.rationale: Real field-conflation in the categorization path, but severity high is inflated: the protected/archive gate is sender-parsed and immune, and the model property is dead code; blast radius is mis-labeling, not mis-archiving.

representative.title: Legitimate .gov senders are NOT protected when From contains a display name or angle brackets — endsWith('.gov') runs against the whole raw, unparsed sender string
representative.evidence: Line 106: if (value.includes("courts.ca.gov") || value.endsWith(".gov")). value = whole lowercased From string. 'IRS Refunds <noreply@irs.gov>' lowercases to '...<noreply@irs.gov>' which ends with '>', so endsWith('.gov') is false AND includes('courts.ca.gov') is false -> falls through to NOT protected. core/rules.py is_protected_sender parses the address via getaddresses/_resolve_addr and _gov_protected anchors on the terminal label.
representative.rationale: Real defect (display-name/angle-bracket gov From fails open) but a bare 'noreply@irs.gov' WITHOUT brackets still ends with '.gov' and IS protected, so it is conditional on header shape; the demo also passes a bare-address senders/check (the UI sends bare addresses), so 'the common case' is overstated for this demo's own input path. Downgraded from high to medium.

representative.location: lines 679-704 (SAMPLE_PLANS) and 932-933 (catch -> localPlans() fallback render); cross-ref api/plans.py PLANS pro/business (name="Pro"/"Business", price_display $19/$49) and startCheckout(p.id)
representative.title: Static-demo fallback renders different plan NAMES and prices than checkout actually charges (Inbox safety audit/$29-$49 vs Pro/$19) — buyer deception on the live Cloudflare demo
representative.evidence: SAMPLE_PLANS does use id 'pro' name 'Inbox safety audit' price '$29-$49' and id 'business' name 'Assisted cleanup' '$149-$299' (lines 689-702); plans.py prices Pro $19/mo, Business $49/mo (plans.py:87-125). But the 'STATIC Cloudflare demo with NO API backend' premise is FALSE: wrangler.toml routes uma.4444j99.dev to worker.mjs which serves GET /v1/billing/plans returning $19/$49 'Pro'/'Business' (worker.mjs:24-46,265). The page fetches /v1/billing/plans first (line 900); on the live demo that fetch succeeds, so the worker plans render and SAMPLE_PLANS (catch branch line 933) never executes. Checkout on the worker returns 503 (worker.mjs:269-270), so the page shows 'Billing is not live here' and never reaches Stripe.
representative.rationale: The source divergence is real, but the 'buyer deception on the live demo' claim rests on a wrong deployment model — the live worker serves correct prices and SAMPLE_PLANS is a dead-fallback for a static host that this repo does not deploy.

representative.location: line 7 (import stripe) and line 14 (client = TestClient(app)); same module-level TestClient in test_api.py:10, test_acp.py:11, test_receipts.py:8, test_web.py:7
representative.title: Test modules construct TestClient(app) and import stripe at import time with no importorskip guard — pytest collection hard-crashes when optional API deps are absent
representative.evidence: Verified absent in this env: `import stripe` and `import fastapi` both raise ModuleNotFoundError. test_billing.py:7 `import stripe`, :8 `from fastapi.testclient import TestClient`, :14 `client = TestClient(app)` all run at collection time with no guard. Confirmed the 5 API modules construct `client = TestClient(app)` at module scope (test_api.py:10, test_acp.py:11, test_receipts.py:8, test_web.py:7) while test_mcp.py:11 uses `pytest.importorskip("mcp")`. So pytest collection of these modules ERRORs (not skips) on a partial install. NOTE: stripe/fastapi/pydantic/uvicorn ARE declared in requirements-api.txt, so this is NOT an undeclared-dependency defect — purely a partial-install test-robustness gap.
representative.rationale: Real and reproducible: collection ERROR vs SKIP on a deps-absent run; CI's full install masks it. Severity lowered to low — CI installs requirements-api.txt, and the failure mode is only the ergonomic 'core-only test run' scenario, no production blast radius.

representative.title: Audit disabled silently when --dry-run, so dry-run apply previews bypass receipt unless audit object passed; _record_dry_run_intent only runs if audit is not None
representative.evidence: _make_audit returns None when getattr(args,'dry_run',False) (365-366). cmd_label (437) and cmd_escalate (845) call _make_audit, so audit is None during dry-run. In run_labeler the dry-run recorder is gated `if actions and dry_run and audit is not None:` (289), which is never True from the CLI since dry_run implies audit is None. Thus _record_dry_run_intent (55-89) and the gate at 289-290 are unreachable via the only CLI caller.
representative.rationale: Confirmed dead code under current CLI wiring: _record_dry_run_intent can only fire if a caller passes a non-None audit together with dry_run=True, which cmd_label never does (audit is forced None in dry-run). The documented 'preview records intended disposition' feature is unreachable from the CLI. Low — dead feature, not a runtime fault.

representative.title: Unguarded dict indexing on labels list and header lookups can KeyError/crash
representative.evidence: Line 8: `label_id = next((l['id'] for l in results['labels'] if l['name'] == 'Misc/Other'), None)` directly subscripts results['labels'] (no .get). Inside cb (line 26): `headers = response['payload']['headers']` and lines 27-28 iterate `h['value']`/`h['name']`. A message returned by messages().get(format='metadata') without a 'payload' key (e.g. some drafts/chat shapes) raises KeyError inside the batch callback. Contrast with line 16 (`.get('messages', [])`) which IS guarded.
representative.rationale: Direct unguarded subscripting on lines 8/26/27/28 is present as described; read-only diagnostic so low blast radius is correct.

representative.location: line 2 (Python-3.10+ badge) and line 241 (Prerequisites: Python 3.10 or later) vs CLAUDE.md:278 and api/app.py:34 / requirements-mcp.txt:2-3 / Dockerfile:7
representative.title: Three-way contradiction on the minimum Python version (3.9 vs 3.10)
representative.evidence: README.md:2 badge 'Python-3.10+' and :241 'Python 3.10 or later'. CLAUDE.md:278 'Python 3.9+'. api/app.py:33-34 comment 'the core API stays importable on 3.9'; requirements-mcp.txt:2 'the official `mcp` SDK requires Python >=3.10, while the core engine stays importable on 3.9+'; Dockerfile:7 'The core stays 3.9-importable'. pyproject.toml contains ONLY [tool.pytest.ini_options] and [tool.* pythonpath] — no [project] requires-python.
representative.rationale: Genuine three-way doc inconsistency on the Python floor with no authoritative requires-python pin; but it is documentation/version drift, not a runtime defect, so medium is inflated to low.

representative.title: Static product_feed.json bakes the placeholder host mail.example.com into committed buyer-facing product/image/checkout/seller URLs
representative.evidence: product_feed.json hardcodes https://mail.example.com in checkout_url (line 4), url (10/30), image_url (12/32), seller_url (20/40). acp/feed.py:72-73 write_static_feed defaults base_url="https://mail.example.com"; the live route feed() (feed.py:67-69) resolves host from request.base_url, but the COMMITTED artifact carries the placeholder. A static host (Cloudflare demo) serving this file points an agent at a non-existent host for the checkout entrypoint.
representative.rationale: Placeholder host is genuinely baked into the committed buyer-facing artifact including checkout_url; low severity is correct since it is a regenerable artifact and the live FastAPI route self-resolves.

representative.title: Module-singleton Store connection is never closed in production and not coupled to FastAPI lifespan — connection/handle leak across reloads
representative.evidence: get_store (445-452) builds a process-wide Store with an open connection (line 127); api/app.py lifespan (48-53) only enters the MCP session manager, never calls store.close(). set_store(None) in tests/conftest.py:26,33 replaces the singleton without closing the prior connection. grep finds Store.close() called nowhere in production.
representative.rationale: Verified: no close() in app lifecycle and conftest abandons connections. FD/WAL-sidecar leak under reloads and in the test suite. Low (no data risk).

representative.title: Org name in seed.yaml (labores-profani-crux) disagrees with actual git remote (a-organvm)
representative.evidence: seed.yaml:1 comment and seed.yaml:8 `org: labores-profani-crux`. git remote -v => https://github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation.git. server.json:3 => "io.github.a-organvm/universal-mail-automation". CLAUDE.md:320 => Org: `organvm-iii-ergon`. Three distinct org identifiers confirmed on disk.
representative.rationale: The factual drift across the four surfaces is real and corroborated; severity stays low because it is metadata/governance drift, not a code defect, and no in-repo consumer of seed.yaml's org field was found to actually misroute (that impact claim is speculative).

representative.title: All advertised endpoints use placeholder host mail.example.com, not the live uma.4444j99.dev
representative.evidence: llms.txt lines 6-14 do hardcode https://mail.example.com. But this static file is a GENERATED artifact: scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:28 sets BASE = os.environ.get('PUBLIC_BASE_URL', 'https://mail.example.com') and writes it via build_llms_txt(BASE) (lines 99-100). DEPLOY.md:79 documents PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://<host> as the override. Critically, the live server serves /llms.txt DYNAMICALLY with the real request host: api/well_known.py:84-85 returns build_llms_txt(str(request.base_url)). So an agent doing GET /llms.txt against the deployed host never sees mail.example.com. mail.example.com is also an RFC-2606 reserved placeholder, not a typo'd real host.
representative.rationale: Real (committed file shows the placeholder) but overstated: it is an intentional generator default with a documented override and the runtime endpoint resolves the live host, so 'every integration fails' does not hold.

representative.title: Hardcoded absolute /Users/4jp paths make plist non-portable to any other machine/user
representative.evidence: Read of com.user.gmail_labeler.plist confirms hardcoded /Users/4jp paths: ProgramArguments[1]=line10 '/Users/4jp/Code/organvm/universal-mail--automation/run_automation.sh', WorkingDirectory=line13, StandardOutPath=line15, StandardErrorPath=line17 '/Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/...'. deploy.sh line 48 'cp "$PLIST_SRC" "$PLIST_DEST"' copies verbatim with no sed/envsubst path substitution (confirmed full Read of deploy.sh, no rewriting anywhere). HOWEVER deploy.sh line 9 PLIST_SRC points to com.user.mail_automation.plist, NOT this gmail_labeler file. deploy.sh only boots OUT a stale com.user.gmail_labeler (line 57); it never bootstraps THIS file.
representative.rationale: Hardcoded-paths/no-substitution defect is real and verified, but the specific causal chain ('deploy.sh copies THIS *.plist -> broken LaunchAgent on fresh clone') misreads deploy.sh, which deploys the sibling mail_automation plist, not this one; severity downgraded to medium since this file is not on the active deploy path.

representative.title: Government .gov anchors are wrong because regex runs against combined 'sender subject' text, not the sender alone
representative.evidence: Confirmed r'\.gov$' is near-useless against combined_text='noreply@ssa.gov your benefits update' (matches=False; only matches when SUBJECT ends in .gov). BUT the second anchor r'@.*\.gov' DOES match the real gov sender ('noreply@ssa.gov your benefits update' -> True), so gov senders are NOT generally missed. The false-positive claim is real: 'me@evil.com talked about x.gov stuff' -> r'@.*\.gov' matches (True).
representative.rationale: Overstated: the finding claims both anchors are 'effectively broken for their stated purpose' and that 'a real gov sender can miss'. The greedy @.*\.gov actually catches the common real case; the genuine defect is the false-positive over-match (any '@' before any '.gov' in subject) plus the dead r'\.gov$' anchor. Severity downgraded since the real gov-detection mostly works.

representative.title: pyproject.toml has no [project] table / build backend / console_scripts — package is not installable
representative.evidence: pyproject.toml is exactly 3 lines: only [tool.pytest.ini_options] with testpaths and pythonpath. No [project], no [build-system], no requires-python, no [project.scripts]. setup.py and setup.cfg are absent (ls: No such file). mcp_server/server.py:154-155 defines main() with argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="universal-mail-mcp") but no console entry point declares it. NOTE: contrary to the finding, mcp_server/__main__.py DOES exist (148 bytes), so `python -m mcp_server` works from a checked-out tree.
representative.rationale: Factually accurate that the package is not pip-installable/distributable and the version floor is prose-only; but the finding's claim about `python -m mcp_server` requiring cwd-on-path is weak since __main__.py exists, and the deploy path (Docker uvicorn from /app) is acknowledged-benign, so real blast radius is low not medium.

representative.location: line 30 (single combined `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt`)
representative.title: The 3.9-isolation contract is undermined by always installing requirements-mcp.txt in CI
representative.evidence: ci.yml:30 always installs all three requirements files on every matrix interpreter. requirements-mcp.txt:1-6 documents the ISOLATED contract ('Install only where you run the MCP server') and tests/test_mcp.py:11 uses pytest.importorskip('mcp') precisely as the 3.9-safety net. CI never exercises a core-only (requirements.txt only) install path, so the advertised 'core importable on 3.9 without mcp' is unvalidated.
representative.rationale: Real but lower-impact: the isolation contract is genuinely never exercised, but since 3.9 isn't in the matrix the combined install does not currently break anything. Severity low (matches the architecture-honesty concern, not a live failure).

representative.title: SWEEP_RULES target label names diverge from core taxonomy ('Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/RealEstate' not in LABEL_RULES)
representative.evidence: Verified against core/rules.py: it defines `Dev/Infrastructure` (line 89, NO Work/ prefix) and has NO `Work/RealEstate` (grep found only Dev/GitHub, Dev/Infrastructure, Dev/GameDev). SWEEP_RULES use `Work/Dev/Infrastructure` (lines 30,37,66) and `Work/RealEstate` (line 60) — divergent. `Education/Research` (line 54) DOES exist (core/rules.py line 353), so that part of the finding is wrong. get_label_id returns None for nonexistent labels -> line 91 skips with a warning, making Notion/Backblaze/Termius/Majestic sweeps no-ops unless those exact labels were hand-created.
representative.rationale: Taxonomy divergence for Work/Dev/Infrastructure and Work/RealEstate is real (Education/Research portion is a false sub-claim); effect is silent no-op rules, not corruption, so low.

representative.title: frozen=True gives a false immutability guarantee: mutable Set fields can be mutated in place
representative.evidence: core/models.py:25 @dataclass(frozen=True) with docstring line 30 'Immutable'; labels (line 49) and categories (line 53) are Set[str] via default_factory=set. frozen blocks attribute reassignment only — msg.labels.add(...) is not prevented. test_immutability (tests/test_models.py:59-63) only asserts that reassigning msg.id raises, never tests set mutation, so the shallow guarantee is unverified by the suite.
representative.rationale: Accurate: frozen is shallow and the docstring overstates immutability; severity low is appropriate (no confirmed in-place mutation of a cached instance in the current providers, but the hazard is real).

representative.title: test_immutability asserts the wrong (overstated) guarantee and never exercises set-content mutation
representative.evidence: core/models.py declares labels: Set[str] = field(default_factory=set) (line 49) and categories (line 53) on a @dataclass(frozen=True). frozen=True blocks attribute rebinding (msg.id = ...) but not in-place mutation, so msg.labels.add('x') succeeds silently. test_immutability (test_models.py:57-63) only reassigns msg.id and asserts AttributeError; it never tries an in-place set mutation, leaving the real mutability hole uncovered.
representative.rationale: Accurate test-coverage gap: the test validates only attribute-rebind immutability while the mutable Set fields remain mutable in place and untested.

representative.title: STATUS_EXPIRED is defined but never assigned; sessions never expire and remain completable indefinitely
representative.evidence: models.py:22 STATUS_EXPIRED='expired' is referenced only in update_session's terminal-state guard (router.py:211-212). grep across api/ and acp/ shows no assignment of STATUS_EXPIRED to any session status, no TTL/created_at expiry sweep, and no created_at age check in complete_session. acp_sessions has created_at (store.py:88) but nothing reads it for expiry. A READY session is completable indefinitely.
representative.rationale: Confirmed dead/unenforced lifecycle state; sessions never expire. Informational/correctness, low.

representative.title: Broad 'except Exception' around categorize_with_tier silently degrades categorization to None with no logging
representative.evidence: service.py:63-66: `try: categorization = _jsonable(categorize_with_tier(...)) except Exception: categorization = None` with comment 'never block the protect-check' — no logger call in the handler.
representative.rationale: Accurate: every categorize_with_tier exception is swallowed to None with zero logging, so a recurring rules-engine bug would be invisible while the endpoint returns 200/categorization:null. Low severity stands.

representative.title: get_label_id uses results['labels'] without .get(), KeyError if no labels key
representative.evidence: Confirmed: line 76 `for l in results['labels']:` direct subscript, raising KeyError if 'labels' absent. Verified other code is defensive: gmail_labeler.py line 102 and recount.py line 6 both use `results.get('labels', [])`. Also confirmed get_label_id is invoked twice per rule (lines 88-89) re-fetching the full label list each time — N round trips inefficiency.
representative.rationale: Real but minor robustness gap; Gmail labels().list practically always returns a 'labels' key for a normal account, so low.

representative.title: report command issues a separate API list call per label with no pagination/cost guard and swallows query into broad except
representative.evidence: Lines 478-487: loops every LABEL_RULES key, for gmail runs `provider.list_messages(f"label:{label}", limit=1)` (481), count=result.total_estimate (482). Verified gmail.list_messages passes q=query verbatim to the API (providers/gmail.py:148) with no quoting; total_estimate=resultSizeEstimate (gmail.py:166). Catch is generic `except Exception as e` printing 'Error ({e})' (486-487).
representative.rationale: Confirmed: one list call per label (no batching/cost guard), hierarchical labels like 'Work/Dev/GitHub' passed unquoted to Gmail search (may mis-match), and resultSizeEstimate presented as a count. Low — report is an informational command; counts being approximate/occasionally wrong is a quality issue not a data-integrity one.

representative.title: Nav-dot active-state indexing relies on data-section values being a 0-based dense sequence matching DOM order
representative.evidence: Lines 260-265 build one <a> per section[data-section] in DOM order; lines 270-272: const idx = e.target.dataset.section; ... if (dots[idx]) dots[idx].classList.add('active'). idx is the raw attribute string ('0'..'7'); JS array indexing coerces it. Works only because sections are authored data-section="0".."7" in DOM order (lines 136,148,161,172,183,195,210,237). Reordering/skipping/non-numeric values would highlight the wrong/no dot; the if-guard prevents a crash but the mapping silently misbehaves.
representative.rationale: Latent attribute-to-array-index coupling is real as described; cosmetic, generator-controlled, no current misbehavior — low is accurate.

representative.location: lines 5-11 (delivery web_app status: not_started; revenue subscription status: planned) vs implemented api/billing.py + web/index.html + acp/router.py
representative.title: ecosystem.yaml status fields are stale — claims web_app not_started and subscription revenue merely planned while both are implemented
representative.evidence: ecosystem.yaml lines 5-11: `delivery: - platform: web_app / status: not_started` and `revenue: - platform: subscription / status: planned / priority: critical`. All three claimed implementations exist and are non-trivial: api/billing.py (12KB) implements GET /v1/billing/plans (line 94-95), POST /v1/billing/checkout (105-106, calls stripe checkout.sessions.create at 139), POST /v1/billing/portal (146-147), POST /v1/billing/webhook (170-171, signature-verified fail-closed); web/index.html is 979 lines / 40KB and is mounted at /app via api/app.py:162 (`app.mount('/app', StaticFiles(...))`); acp/router.py (14KB) exists; stripe>=15.2,<16 is declared in requirements-api.txt; docs/plans/2026-06-02-handoff-cloudflare-ci-domain.md confirms the worker deploys to Cloudflare (CI job 'Deploy Cloudflare share demo' succeeded).
representative.rationale: The machine-readable manifest reports the commerce surface as not_started/planned while checkout, portal, webhook, plans catalog, a 979-line dashboard, and Cloudflare deployment are all built — confirmed stale; severity stays low (data/reporting drift, no runtime or security impact).

representative.location: lines 1-15 (whole blueprint; envVars only sets PYTHONUNBUFFERED)
representative.title: render.yaml declares no Python/runtime version and no dependency install step, fully delegating reproducibility to the unpinned Dockerfile base `python:3.11-slim`
representative.evidence: render.yaml L6 'runtime: docker', L9 'autoDeploy: true', L13-14 envVars only sets PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 (no PYTHON_VERSION, no install step). Dockerfile L1 'FROM python:3.11-slim' is a moving tag, not digest-pinned; requirements installed at build via 'pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt' (Dockerfile L8-10) with no hash-lock. So every push rebuilds against whatever 3.11.x patch + dep versions resolve at that moment, and autoDeploy ships it with no human gate.
representative.rationale: Factual claims all check out; this is a genuine reproducibility/hardening weakness (no digest pin, no hash-lock, autoDeploy), but it is a config hygiene concern not a functional defect — low severity is correct.

representative.title: IMAP command/argument injection via unescaped label and mailbox names
representative.evidence: Confirmed: f'"{label}"' quoting (279/288/303/319) without escaping, create(label) raw (352), select(mailbox) raw (158), message_id passed raw to STORE/COPY/FETCH. CRLF/quote injection is theoretically possible. BUT label names come from the static LABEL_RULES taxonomy and message_id is server-issued UIDs; no attacker-controlled string reaches these paths in current code. The 'folder name synced from server' vector is not implemented here.
representative.rationale: Injection surface exists but is not reachable with attacker-controlled input today; severity overstated — partial/low.

representative.title: Single shared connection serialized by one RLock negates WAL read concurrency (docstring claim is false)
representative.evidence: Single self._conn (line 127); _fetch_one (426) and _fetch_all (435) both acquire the same self._lock RLock (129) that every write holds. WAL concurrency requires separate connections, so all access is fully serialized; the docstring at 21-24 ('WAL keeps reads concurrent with the writer') is inaccurate for this design.
representative.rationale: Confirmed throughput/latency claim + false docstring rationale; no correctness/data impact and writes are tiny per the design, so low.

representative.location: check_protected_sender line 94 (sender[:4096], subject[:4096]) vs api/schemas.py SenderCheckRequest max_length=4096
representative.title: MCP check_protected_sender truncates to 4096 chars silently rather than rejecting, diverging from the API's validation contract
representative.evidence: server.py:94 passes sender[:4096], subject[:4096] to service.check_sender — silent truncation. schemas.py:29-30 bounds the HTTP SenderCheckRequest with max_length=4096 which REJECTS (422) longer input. So an over-length sender yields 422 on REST but a (truncated) protected verdict on MCP.
representative.rationale: Real divergence; truncation could in theory drop a trailing protected-domain anchor. But From-header local-part/domain that matters is at the start, real headers are far under 4096, and is_protected_sender matches anywhere — so the wrong-verdict risk is marginal. Low.

representative.title: X-GM-LABELS value not IMAP-quoted/escaped; labels with spaces, quotes, or backslashes corrupt the STORE command
representative.evidence: Lines 279/303 interpolate `f'"{label}"'` with no escaping of embedded quotes/backslashes. archive() (315) passes literal "\\Inbox" → STORE value `"\Inbox"`. Labels are from a static taxonomy today (LABEL_RULES keys, no embedded quotes), so practical breakage is limited; the \Inbox quoting works for Gmail's X-GM-LABELS in practice.
representative.rationale: Real lack of escaping but inputs are currently controlled; correctness risk is latent — low.

representative.title: CI never exercises the documented 3.9 core floor nor the stripe/mcp lower version bounds
representative.evidence: Same root cause as U007/U241: matrix ["3.11","3.12"] (ci.yml:15) + combined install (ci.yml:30). Lower bounds (stripe>=15.2 in requirements-api.txt, mcp>=1.2 in requirements-mcp.txt, fastapi>=0.110, pydantic>=2.0) are never validated because pip resolves to latest allowed. 3.9 core-only path untested.
representative.rationale: Accurate but is the same defect as U007 (missing 3.9 floor) plus the U241 install-coupling point, just severity-rated 'low'. Marking duplicate of U007 (same-file root cause: CI never exercises the 3.9 floor).

representative.location: line 1163 (def calculate_email_age_hours(email_date: Optional["datetime"]) -> float) with no `from __future__ import annotations` at top (line 8) and `datetime` imported only locally at line 1176
representative.title: Forward-ref annotation Optional["datetime"] is unresolvable — NameError if type hints are ever introspected
representative.evidence: Confirmed: no `from __future__ import annotations` in core/rules.py (grep returned nothing); datetime imported only locally at line 1176; parameter annotated Optional["datetime"] (string forward-ref). grep for get_type_hints across the repo returned zero hits, so it is latent.
representative.rationale: Real but latent: string annotation does not crash at def time (import test passes), but get_type_hints/pydantic/schema introspection would NameError. Nothing in-repo introspects it. Low is correct.

representative.title: subscription.created/updated with status=None writes nothing for status but still forces current_period_end and may demote plan, producing a row whose plan changed without a corresponding status change
representative.evidence: billing.py:248 status=obj.get('status') can be None; 251 derives plan_id from price (can be None via plan_id_for_price). store.py:213 skips None columns, so plan is rewritten while status is left untouched -> a decoupled (plan,status) pair from one event.
representative.rationale: Real internal-consistency gap; benign under the current status-gated entitlement resolver. Overlaps U717 (same None-status root cause) but adds the plan-rewritten-while-status-skipped angle. Low.

representative.location: Shopping patterns: r'uber' (243), r'target' (247), r'square' (252)... Personal r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' (488)
representative.title: Many bare-substring keyword rules over-match common English words and brand prefixes
representative.evidence: Reproduced: 'tuberculosis update' -> Shopping (r'uber' line 243); 'retargeting strategies' -> Shopping (r'target' line 247). Note: r'square' at line 250 (squarespace) not 252, r'discover' at 185 (Finance/Payments tier 2), r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' at 488 (Personal tier 1). The bare unanchored substrings do over-match common English/brand prefixes as claimed.
representative.rationale: Real, broad pattern of bare-substring over-matching; some push into elevated tiers (Personal tier1, Finance/Payments tier2). Low individually but collectively erodes accuracy. Minor line-number drift in the finding does not change the verdict.

representative.title: list_messages caps maxResults at `limit` but caller paginates expecting full pages, conflating per-page limit with total limit
representative.evidence: Line 144 `page_size = min(limit, LIST_PAGE_SIZE)` passes maxResults (a per-page cap) as the value of `limit`, while base.py documents limit as 'Maximum messages to return per page' (base.py:126). The CLI (cli.py) loops on next_page_token so the two interpretations align in practice, but a single-call caller passing limit>500 gets only one page.
representative.rationale: Confirmed API-contract ambiguity; benign for the actual CLI caller, low severity.

representative.location: tests/test_mailapp.py lines 9-25 (only star+due_date test); providers/outlook.py:557-578; providers/mailapp.py:293-294
representative.title: No test exercises the one provider that actually consumes due_date (Outlook); the sole due_date star test targets Mail.app which ignores it
representative.evidence: Grep across tests/ shows the ONLY due_date-driving test is test_mailapp.py:19 (datetime(2026,6,1)). It targets MailAppProvider, whose star() at providers/mailapp.py:293-294 is documented 'due_date is ignored by Mail.app'. The sole provider that consumes due_date is Outlook (providers/outlook.py:572-578: due_str = due_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z") with timeZone:UTC), and grep confirms no test calls OutlookProvider.star() or instantiates it for star — so the strftime/dueDateTime formatting path has zero test coverage. The companion UTC defect is real: strftime zeroes the time to T00:00:00 and ignores the original time/tzinfo, then hardcodes timeZone:UTC, so a naive datetime is silently stamped midnight-UTC.
representative.rationale: Coverage gap is real and exactly as described: the one due_date test exercises a provider that ignores due_date, leaving the only consuming provider (Outlook) untested. Test-quality, low blast radius.

representative.title: Stale-claim window uses wall-clock time.time(); a backward clock step (NTP correction) can make a fresh 'processing' claim appear stale or never expire
representative.evidence: Line 337 compares _now() (time.time(), 106-107) minus stored created_at (time.time() at insert, 331) against the 60s timeout. Both are wall-clock; no monotonic source. A backward/forward NTP step makes the delta unreliable.
representative.rationale: Code-true use of non-monotonic wall-clock for the staleness window. Requires a clock anomaly to manifest and the money invariants still hold (per U739), so low. monotonic-across-restart caveat the finding raises is itself valid, making it a genuine design fragility.

representative.title: close() leaves the singleton pointing at a closed connection; any later call raises 'Cannot operate on a closed database' on every method
representative.evidence: close() (140-142) calls self._conn.close() but does not clear the module singleton _STORE (441) nor null self._conn; get_store (445-452) returns the same instance, and no method guards against a closed connection. A later call would raise sqlite3.ProgrammingError('Cannot operate on a closed database').
representative.rationale: Code-true: close() leaves the singleton pointing at a dead connection with no reconnect/guard. Distinct from U520 (never-closed). Production path never calls close(), so latent — low. Tests use set_store(None) (which drops the ref without close), so the trigger requires someone to call store.close() while keeping the singleton.

representative.title: Folder names containing a literal '/' collide with the hierarchical path separator, corrupting the folder cache and routing moves to the wrong folder
representative.evidence: Line 270 builds full_name = f"{parent_name}/{folder['displayName']}" and line 607 does parts = label.split('/'). A literal '/' inside an Outlook displayName is indistinguishable from the hierarchy separator: a top-level folder named 'Work/Dev' and a real Work>Dev hierarchy both key to 'Work/Dev', and split('/') will resolve/create against the wrong node.
representative.rationale: Genuine separator-collision; distinct from the round-1 recursion/duplicate note. Probability low (folder names rarely contain '/'), so low severity.

representative.title: mail_export.tsv is git-tracked; currently header-only but the schema is sender/subject PII and the file is the AppleScript export target
representative.evidence: git ls-files --error-unmatch mail_export.tsv succeeds (tracked). git check-ignore exits 1 (NOT gitignored), while .gitignore DOES list config/protected_senders.local.txt and audit/*.jsonl for the same PII reason. Read shows exactly one content line: the header `mailbox_name\tmessage_id\tsender\tsubject\tdate_received\tread\tflagged\tsize_bytes` with zero data rows (file is 77 bytes). HOWEVER export_mail_snapshot.applescript line 1 writes to `(POSIX path of (path to home folder)) & "mail_automation/mail_export.tsv"` = /Users/4jp/mail_automation/mail_export.tsv, which is NOT the repo-root tracked file /Users/4jp/Code/organvm/universal-mail--automation/mail_export.tsv.
representative.rationale: Real hygiene issue (tracked PII-schema file, no .gitignore entry, against the repo's own precedent), but overstated: no PII leaks today (header-only) AND the export script does not write to this repo path, so it is not the 'exact output target' claimed — leak would require a manual copy into the repo, not a single run; severity downgraded medium->low.

representative.title: RESET_STATE default 'true' wipes GmailLabeler resume state, and save(None,0,{}) may not match how run() reads token
representative.evidence: final_sweep.py:9 RESET_STATE defaults 'true'; line 12 calls app.state_manager.save(None, 0, {}) unconditionally, which writes the shared GmailLabeler state file. BUT gmail_labeler.py:40 defines STATE_FILE = "labeler_state.json" (NOT 'gmail_state.json' as the finding claims; gmail_state.json is a separate file used only by core/config.py and is absent on disk — labeler_state.json is the one that exists). Critically, run() only reads/writes the page token when query == "has:nouserlabels" (gmail_labeler.py:285-286, and the saves at 307-308/321-322 are all guarded by that same condition). For query="label:Uncategorized" the sweep starts fresh (page_token=None) and never touches the saved token, so there is NO cross-query contamination via the token; the sweep run itself cannot corrupt or be corrupted by the main pass's token.
representative.rationale: Real mechanism (RESET_STATE=true clobbers the main has:nouserlabels resume token in labeler_state.json) but the finding mislabels the file ('gmail_state.json'), invents a non-existent race/cross-contamination (no concurrency; the sweep query never reads/writes the token), and the run() guard means the only true effect is the line-12 reset overwriting a prior pass's token — a benign re-scan, not data loss. Severity overstated.

representative.title: Same draining-query-with-page-token hazard as gmail_labeler.py; plus no state persistence so a crash loses all progress
representative.evidence: Confirmed structurally: pages via nextPageToken (lines 405,449) while modify() at 432 adds a user label, removing each message from the 'has:nouserlabels' result set, so the result set shifts under pagination (potential skip). No StateManager here (grep: only gmail_labeler.py imports core.state.StateManager). verify_labeling_complete line 485 uses resultSizeEstimate. The shift-skip is a known Gmail-pagination hazard but is self-healing on rerun because the query drains; severity is bounded by that.
representative.rationale: The pagination-shift and no-state observations are real, but the practical risk is low: the query is self-draining so a rerun catches residue, and the finding itself concedes this. Low.

representative.title: data[0].split() on a failed/None search and most-recent-N slice assume sequential UID ordering
representative.evidence: Line 107 `uids = data[0].split()` runs only after res=='OK' (105-106); if data[0] were None it would AttributeError (minor edge). Line 112 tail-slice assumes ascending UID order for 'most recent', which IMAP SEARCH does not guarantee. Messages outside the slice are simply never evaluated -> left in inbox (safe), but dry-run counts can mislead.
representative.rationale: Real but minor; outcome is safe (unscanned mail stays in inbox), only count accuracy affected. Low.

representative.title: Gmail batch label-get with no error surfacing and no batch-size chunking — silently drops failed/over-limit entries
representative.evidence: Lines 21-23 callback appends only on `if not exception:` with no else/log, so per-label errors vanish (confirmed, overlaps U899). The 'one batch over ALL labels, >100 silently dropped' claim is conditional/uncertain: googleapiclient's new_batch_http_request raises at add() when exceeding the 1000-request limit (not 100, and not a silent tail-drop) and the current taxonomy size is unverified; this part is speculative.
representative.rationale: The error-swallowing half is real, but the batch-size/silent-tail-drop half is overstated and condition-dependent.

representative.title: mcp SDK IS properly declared (false-positive killer)
representative.evidence: requirements-mcp.txt:6 declares `mcp>=1.2,<2`. mcp_server/server.py imports the mcp SDK; api/app.py:39 imports `from mcp_server.server import http_app, mcp` inside try/except and degrades gracefully (app.py:41-45). Dockerfile and ci.yml:30 both install requirements-mcp.txt. The dependency is genuinely declared and installed on the deploy target.
representative.rationale: Correct reconciliation — mcp is properly declared and installed; any 'undeclared mcp import' claim is indeed a false positive.

representative.title: uvicorn started via shell-form CMD as root with no worker/uvicorn pinning
representative.evidence: No USER directive anywhere in the Dockerfile (FROM python:3.11-slim, never drops privileges) and line 19 binds --host 0.0.0.0, so uvicorn runs as root. Shell-form CMD is acknowledged as intentional for ${PORT:-8000} expansion. This is a standard container-hardening gap, not a functional bug.
representative.rationale: Accurately described and self-scoped as info-level hardening; container does run as root with no USER, confirmed.

representative.title: Doc/install mismatch: CLAUDE.md tells users to check 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler' but deploy installs com.user.mail_automation
representative.evidence: CLAUDE.md:104 = 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler'. deploy.sh installs/bootstraps com.user.mail_automation (line 51 LABEL="com.user.mail_automation", line 60 launchctl bootstrap "$AGENT_ID" "$PLIST_DEST" where PLIST is com.user.mail_automation.plist), and line 57 explicitly boots out the OLD com.user.gmail_labeler (confirming rename). deploy.sh:74 prints 'Status: launchctl list | grep mail_automation'. README.md:553 = 'launchctl list | grep mail_automation'.
representative.rationale: Stale label name in CLAUDE.md: scheduler was renamed gmail_labeler -> mail_automation everywhere (deploy.sh, README) but CLAUDE.md line 104 was not updated, so its status grep matches nothing; severity lowered to low since impact is a misleading status check in agent-guidance doc, not a functional break.

representative.title: health connect/disconnect not in try/finally; failure after connect leaks the connection
representative.evidence: Lines 504-517: `try: provider.connect(); healthy, message = provider.health_check(); provider.disconnect()`. If health_check() raises, control jumps to `except Exception` (515) and disconnect() never runs. Unlike cmd_label/cmd_report/cmd_summary/etc. which use `with provider:` (439, 475, 542) guaranteeing __exit__/disconnect.
representative.rationale: Real: health uses manual connect/disconnect without try/finally, so an exception after connect leaks the IMAP socket / OAuth session. Downgraded medium->low: cmd_health is a one-shot CLI invocation that exits immediately after (process teardown reclaims the socket); the leak window is a single short-lived process, not a long-running service.

representative.title: cmd_pending omits VIP config loading, so pending output's is_vip/tier reflects only built-in VIPs, inconsistent with other commands
representative.evidence: cmd_label (422-423), cmd_summary (523-524), cmd_vip (718-719), cmd_escalate (828-829) each call load_config()+apply_vip_senders_from_config. cmd_pending (622-631) jumps straight to get_provider with no load_config/apply_vip_senders_from_config call. Line 666/674 then compute cat_result.is_vip via categorize_with_tier, which reflects only built-in VIPs without the config-applied ones.
representative.rationale: Confirmed inconsistency: pending's is_vip flag ignores user-configured VIP senders that summary/vip/escalate honor. Real but cosmetic — affects only the displayed VIP star marker on pending items, not any apply action. Low.

representative.title: Type confusion: corrupt state with non-int total_processed crashes run on first batch
representative.evidence: total_processed = state.get_total() (173). get_total() (core/state.py:101-103) returns self.state.get('total_processed', 0) with no type coercion; the default only applies when the key is absent. Line 295 `total_processed += len(actions)` then does str + int -> TypeError if the persisted value was a string. _load (state.py:46-56) does no schema validation, only JSON parse.
representative.rationale: Confirmed mechanically: a hand-corrupted state file with total_processed as a string crashes on the first processed batch. Downgraded medium->low: requires an externally-corrupted state file (the code itself always writes an int via save()), so it is a robustness gap against manual tampering, not a defect in normal operation. The exception is caught at 320-324, state re-saved (re-corrupting), then re-raised as an unhandled traceback.

representative.title: log_level loaded but never applied, and not validated against valid logging levels
representative.evidence: config.log_level populated at config.py:191 (YAML) and 268-269 (env). cli.py:48-51 hardcodes `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)`; only override is cli.py:1194-1195 `if args.verbose: logging.getLogger().setLevel(logging.DEBUG)`. grep confirms config.log_level is read only in tests. So `log_level: ERROR` in config.yaml has zero effect; value is also unvalidated.
representative.rationale: Confirmed dead config knob plus missing validation, matching the slice claim; low severity (cosmetic/UX surprise, no crash since the value is never applied).

representative.title: Pitch page shows placeholder marketing copy that contradicts the shipped, detailed pricing and feature set
representative.evidence: Line 177: feature card 'In Development / Feature list coming soon.'; lines 200-204: 'Business model details coming soon.' with badges 'subscription'/'pre-launch'; hero/solution/cta repeat the meta description 'Email workflow automation and scheduling' (lines 142,166,240). Contradicts shipped pricing: api/plans.py has Free / Pro $19/mo (price_cents=1900) / Business $49/mo (price_cents=4900), $0.01/triage metered, ACP credit packs; pricing.md (1379 bytes) exists. README ships full product description.
representative.rationale: Placeholder copy genuinely contradicts the shipped pricing/feature surfaces; defect confirmed, but severity is low because the file is an undeployed orphan not seen by any prospect.

representative.title: Folder cache pagination not followed ($top 100 only) — mailboxes with >100 folders miss entries, causing duplicate folder creation
representative.evidence: Line 253 _api_get(GRAPH_API_FOLDERS, params={"$top": 100}) and line 268 childFolders also $top:100, neither follows @odata.nextLink (unlike list_messages which returns next_link at 469). Confirmed omission.
representative.rationale: Real omission, but >100 folders at a single level is uncommon for personal Outlook mailboxes; missing cache entries trigger create-then-fallback (which mostly fails safe). Low. corroborated=false echoed.

representative.location: run_sweep line 91 (`if not add_id`) vs lines 88-89, 114
representative.title: Missing 'remove' label is silently tolerated, leaving source label attached forever
representative.evidence: Confirmed: line 91 `if not add_id:` guards only add_id; remove_id (line 89) is never guarded. Line 114 `"removeLabelIds": [remove_id] if remove_id else []` silently sends [] when remove_id is None, so the source label is never stripped and no warning is logged (contrast line 92 for add_id).
representative.rationale: Real asymmetry / missing warning, but blast radius is small: a missing Misc/Other label also means the label-scoped query matches nothing, so the practical effect is a no-op rule, not data loss; downgraded to low.

representative.title: Broad except Exception swallows YAML parse errors as empty config
representative.evidence: Lines 124-126: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...); return {}`. Any parse/IO failure is swallowed and the system continues on pure defaults. yaml.YAMLError and OSError are not distinguished; a malformed config silently degrades to default Gmail behavior.
representative.rationale: Confirmed broad-except silent fallback. Whether a malformed config 'should' be fatal is a design judgment; low severity — logged at warning, no crash, but custom rules/VIPs silently vanish.

representative.title: VIP tier and tier_config can disagree when VIP tier is outside 1-4, yielding wrong folder/star/inbox behavior
representative.evidence: Reproduced: add_vip_sender('boss', pattern='boss@x', tier=5) -> categorize_with_tier('boss@x.com','hi') yields result.tier=5 but result.tier_config.number=1 (line 983 PRIORITY_TIERS.get(5, PRIORITY_TIERS[1]) falls back to tier-1 config). add_vip_sender (900-925) does not validate tier.
representative.rationale: Real divergence between result.tier and tier_config for out-of-range VIP tier. Low: requires a user misconfiguring a VIP tier outside 1-4 (no default VIPs ship); blast radius is downstream code trusting tier vs tier_config inconsistently.

representative.title: Test never exercises tamper-detection on GET, and verification only passes because sign and verify share the same in-process ephemeral key
representative.evidence: api/receipts.py: verify(body,sig) -> sign(body) -> _signing_key(), which returns env RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY or a module-global _EPHEMERAL_KEY (secrets.token_bytes(32) cached per process). So in-process sign and verify ALWAYS share the same key by construction — the cross-restart/rotation failure mode named in the docstring ('Receipts signed now will not verify after a restart') cannot be exercised by these tests. The GET endpoint get_receipt (lines 114-141) returns rec['signature'] verbatim with NO server-side recompute/verify, so a tampered stored row is not rejected by the endpoint; the test asserts tamper-detection only via in-process receipts.verify at test_receipts.py:52, never against the server. grep confirms RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is never set anywhere in tests/, so the durable-key path is uncovered. test_sign_then_verify_roundtrip (11-17) likewise only proves in-process sign/verify symmetry plus a body-mutation flip.
representative.rationale: Every factual claim is accurate against current code, but this is a test-coverage/over-confidence gap (production code is unchanged and correct for the in-process case), not a code defect — blast radius is limited to false test confidence, so medium is overstated; downgraded to low.

representative.title: CHANGELOG omits all of the commerce/API/MCP/ACP/Cloudflare work — stale vs current repo state
representative.evidence: CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] (lines 10-13) lists only 'Platinum Sprint: CI/CD workflow, standardized badge row, ADR documentation' and 'Initial CHANGELOG'. The tree contains substantial unrecorded features: api/ (app.py, billing.py, store.py, plans.py, schemas.py, well_known.py), mcp_server/, acp/ package (router.py, feed.py, models.py, payment.py = Agentic Commerce Protocol), wrangler.toml (Cloudflare share Worker), Stripe billing, signed-receipt ledger (tests/test_receipts.py), pricing tiers (api/plans.py). requirements-api.txt and requirements-mcp.txt exist. None of this work appears in the changelog.
representative.rationale: Real documentation-staleness gap: changelog materially understates the project; verified by directory listing and grep showing the commerce/API/MCP/ACP/Cloudflare surfaces all exist on disk but are absent from CHANGELOG. Low severity is correct (docs only, no runtime/correctness impact).

representative.title: GEMINI.md contains only auto-generated ORGANVM system boilerplate — no project-specific guidance, contradicting AGENTS.md which calls it the 'overview'
representative.evidence: GEMINI.md is entirely the auto-generated block: ORGANVM:AUTO:START (line 1) through ORGANVM:AUTO:END (line 114) covering System Context, Active Directives, Trivium, Logos, plus a Google prompting note (line 71: '**Prompting (Google)**: context 1M tokens') and a generic Conductor OS Integration section (lines 119-124). Grep confirms zero mention of the project's own surfaces (no 'CLI', 'provider', 'gmail_labeler', 'rules', 'categoriz', or 'email' in the file). AGENTS.md:9 verbatim: '- Reference docs: `GEMINI.md` (overview) and `PROCESS.md` (rule-mapping plan).'
representative.rationale: AGENTS.md cites GEMINI.md as the project 'overview', but the file holds only system-wide ORGANVM boilerplate with no project-specific guidance, so the cross-reference is misleading; documentation-only, no code blast radius.

representative.title: Value-score penalty keys on bare 'off' and '%' substrings, over-penalizing legitimate subjects
representative.evidence: Line 48: any(x in subject_lower for x in ['digest','summary','log','alert','notification','receipt','order','off','%']). Substring 'off' matches 'office','offer','kickoff','cutoff','offsite'; '%' matches any percent-bearing subject. Penalty is -20 (line 49).
representative.rationale: Bare-substring matching of 'off' and '%' over-penalizes legitimate subjects; confirmed logic flaw in an advisory heuristic, low severity is correct.

representative.location: line 28 (from cli import get_provider, run_labeler) -> cli.py:48 logging.basicConfig
representative.title: Importing the FastAPI app triggers logging.basicConfig() as an import-time side effect
representative.evidence: service.py:28 does module-scope `from cli import get_provider, run_labeler`. cli.py:48-51 calls logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s') at module top level. api/app.py:28 imports api.service, so importing the app triggers cli's import-time basicConfig as a side effect.
representative.rationale: Verified import-time root-logger reconfiguration; classic library side-effect anti-pattern. Low severity is correct (best fixed by moving basicConfig into cli's __main__).

representative.title: Docstring claims 'CLI > env > config file' precedence but load_config never applies CLI args
representative.evidence: Module docstring line 5 and load_config docstring 153-156 promise 'CLI > env > config file > defaults', but the body (166-179) only does _apply_yaml_config then _apply_env_config. There is no config_path-vs-CLI args parameter for overrides; load_config() takes only config_path and env_prefix. CLI flags are handled separately via argparse in cli.py.
representative.rationale: Confirmed doc/implementation mismatch — load_config never applies a CLI tier despite the documented contract; low severity (documentation accuracy, no runtime defect).

representative.title: agent-commerce.md says 'Five endpoints' but then lists six ACP routes
representative.evidence: Line 61 reads 'Five endpoints implement the verified Agentic Checkout spec:' but the single contiguous fenced block (lines 64-69) lists SIX routes: POST /acp/checkout_sessions (create), GET /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} (retrieve), POST /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} (update), POST .../complete, POST .../cancel, and GET /acp/feed.json (product feed discovery). There is no separator line distinguishing the five checkout_sessions endpoints from the feed.json discovery endpoint, so the prose count 'Five' does not match the six routes presented beneath it.
representative.rationale: Genuine numeric/prose inconsistency: 'Five' matches only the checkout_sessions endpoints while the block visually presents six routes with no separation; pure documentation accuracy issue with no code blast radius, so low severity stands.

representative.title: Second frontend (docs/pitch) is an orphan: not deployed and not linked from anywhere, so its stale copy will drift unnoticed
representative.evidence: wrangler.toml lines 9-10: [assets] directory = "./web". ci.yml's only deploy job (cloudflare-share-deploy, lines 78+) runs wrangler-action deploy on that worker. web/ contains only index.html (40KB, the served surface). Repo-wide grep for 'docs/pitch' returns ONLY the bug-sweep findings.json self-references — zero references from any workflow, doc, or served HTML. So docs/pitch/index.html is a git-tracked auto-generated artifact with no consumer.
representative.rationale: Orphan/dead-surface claim verified end to end; it is the load-bearing mitigant that lowers U198/U199 severity. Correctly rated low.

representative.title: total_archived double-counts overlapping labels and miscounts because Gmail messages carry multiple labels
representative.evidence: Line 12 includes UNREAD in relevant_labels: `... or l['id'] in ['INBOX', 'TRASH', 'UNREAD']`. Line 41 only excludes by NAME 'INBOX'/'TRASH', so the UNREAD system label (name 'UNREAD') passes and its messagesTotal is added at line 42. Gmail messages carry multiple labels, so summing messagesTotal across user labels + INBOX + UNREAD genuinely over-counts; 'TOTAL ORGANIZED ARCHIVE' (line 45) is not a real message count.
representative.rationale: Both claims (multi-label double-counting and UNREAD inclusion in the total) are exactly what the current code does; low severity is correct for a reporting-only script.

representative.title: Test coverage gap: 'corrupt' test only exercises invalid JSON, never valid-JSON-wrong-shape
representative.evidence: test_state.py:23-29 writes 'NOT VALID JSON {{{' which only triggers the json.JSONDecodeError branch at core/state.py:52. core/state.py _load() (lines 46-56) performs NO schema/type validation: valid JSON that is a list/str/null is returned verbatim and stored as self.state, after which self.state.get(...) (e.g. line 99/103) or defaultdict(int, self.state.get('history', {})) in get_history() (line 112) would crash on a wrong-typed value. No test in the file exercises valid-JSON-wrong-shape; the only other negative test is test_missing_file_returns_defaults (lines 31-34).
representative.rationale: Accurately describes both the test and the unvalidated load path in core/state.py; a genuine test-quality coverage gap, severity 'low' is correct.

representative.title: Green design tokens were referenced ~13 times but never defined until this dirty diff (pre-existing broken branding)
representative.evidence: git show HEAD:web/index.html: --green/--green-2/--green-soft are NOT defined in :root (only --accent* at lines 18-21) yet referenced at HEAD lines 79,245,266,271,277,278,285,287,297, etc.; `grep -- '--green:'` on HEAD returns nothing (exit 1). The dirty diff adds the three definitions (diff lines 20-22). So the committed HEAD shipped with ~13 undefined var(--green*) references resolving to invalid/inherited values.
representative.rationale: Verified against HEAD: the green tokens were undefined in the committed page and are fixed only by the uncommitted diff — a real (now-being-fixed) branding regression.

representative.location: lines 9-10 (pip install -r ...) — no constraint/hash file, no pip version pin
representative.title: Docker build installs unhashed, unpinned dependencies with `pip install` and no `--require-hashes`, allowing supply-chain drift between identical source builds
representative.evidence: Lines 9-10: `pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt` with no constraints file and no --require-hashes. All three requirements files use `>=` floors (google-api-python-client>=2.0.0, fastapi>=0.110, uvicorn[standard]>=0.29, pydantic>=2.0, mcp>=1.2,<2; stripe>=15.2,<16). render.yaml has autoDeploy:true + runtime:docker, so a rebuild of the same commit resolves newer transitive versions. Only stripe and mcp have ceilings; fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/google-*/urllib3 float freely.
representative.rationale: Factually accurate supply-chain non-reproducibility; low severity is appropriate (best-practice gap, not an active exploit).

representative.title: README 'First Run' Outlook/IMAP examples inherit a Gmail-only default query
representative.evidence: cli.py:1014 default="has:nouserlabels" with help (:1015) 'Query to filter messages (default: has:nouserlabels for Gmail)'. README First Run :286 'python3 cli.py label --provider outlook' and :289 '--provider imap --host imap.mail.me.com' show no --query override, so they inherit the Gmail-specific search syntax against Outlook Graph / IMAP.
representative.rationale: Accurate: README presents non-Gmail first-run commands that silently inherit a Gmail-only default query the help text itself scopes to Gmail; low-severity doc accuracy issue.

representative.title: Division by zero / statistics.mean on empty list crashes report if all batch fetches fail
representative.evidence: Callback returns early on exception (lines 82-83), so msg_data/value_scores stay empty if all GETs fail. Line 146 statistics.mean(value_scores) raises StatisticsError on empty; lines 151-152 divide by len(msg_data) -> ZeroDivisionError. Pre-fetch guard at line 178 only covers the list() result, not fetch failures.
representative.rationale: Real crash path on total batch-fetch failure with no post-fetch empty guard; severity low because it requires every GET in every chunk to error.

representative.title: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES references labels that do not exist in the core taxonomy (Work/Dev/*, Work/RealEstate)
representative.evidence: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES lines 34-37 list 'Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/Dev/GitHub', 'Work/Dev/Code-Review', 'Work/RealEstate'. grep of core/rules.py shows LABEL_RULES defines these as 'Dev/Infrastructure' (line 89), 'Dev/GitHub' (line 71), 'Dev/Code-Review' (line 83) with NO 'Work/' prefix, and `grep -n 'RealEstate|Work/'` returns nothing — no 'Work/RealEstate' rule exists. If the account taxonomy matches the core rules engine, the query `label:Work/Dev/...` returns 0 messages and those classes are never archived (silent no-op).
representative.rationale: Label-name mismatch between this script and the core taxonomy is real; impact is a silent functional gap, not corruption, so low severity holds.

representative.title: Broad except re-raises after saving but the surrounding cmd_label has no handler — provider __exit__ runs but process exits with traceback, no exit code mapping
representative.evidence: run_labeler `except Exception as e:` logs, saves state, `raise` (320-324). cmd_label calls run_labeler inside `with provider:` (439-450) with no try/except, so the exception propagates out of `return args.func(args)` (1201) and crashes with a traceback + default exit 1, bypassing the 0/1/2 mapping at 455-457. KeyboardInterrupt branch (316-319) does NOT re-raise — falls through to `return result` (332), so Ctrl-C is reported as a normal (partial-success) run.
representative.rationale: Confirmed both halves: a mid-run Exception escapes as an unstructured traceback (no clean exit-code mapping), and KeyboardInterrupt is silently treated as a successful partial run rather than an interruption. Inconsistent error semantics. Low — these are exit-code/UX inconsistencies; the state is still saved before the crash, so no data loss.

representative.location: run_labeler line 311 time.sleep(1.0) inside while loop
representative.title: Fixed 1s throttle per batch is unconditional even in dry-run and even on the last (break) iteration
representative.evidence: Line 311 `time.sleep(1.0)` is unconditional, executes before the `if not page_token: break` check (313-314), and is not gated on `not dry_run`. A 10k-message dry-run paginating 100 at a time sleeps ~100s for no API reason; the final page also sleeps before breaking.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: fixed throttle fires even in dry-run and on the terminal iteration. Minor perf/UX waste, no correctness impact. Low.

representative.title: Provider-specific flags (--host/--user/--password/--gmail-extensions/--account) accepted for every provider but only used by imap/mailapp; silently ignored otherwise
representative.evidence: provider_group defines --host/--user/--password/--account/--gmail-extensions for all subcommands (982-1002). cmd_label passes them all into get_provider (425-432). get_provider's gmail branch returns GmailProvider() (116) and outlook returns OutlookProvider() (133), ignoring host/user/password/account/gmail-extensions entirely. No validation warns the user.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: provider-specific flags are accepted globally and silently ignored for gmail/outlook; e.g. `--provider gmail --host imap.example.com` silently ignores host while using Gmail OAuth. Low — confusing UX, but no security/data harm (the ignored flags can't redirect the connection).

representative.title: Negative or zero --limit is passed unvalidated straight to provider.list_messages
representative.evidence: argparse --limit is type=int with no bound (e.g. 1093-1097, 1133-1138). summary/pending/vip/escalate pass args.limit straight into provider.list_messages(limit=args.limit) (545, 648, 746, 857). gmail.list_messages does page_size = min(limit, LIST_PAGE_SIZE) (gmail.py:144), so a negative limit yields a negative maxResults sent to the Gmail API; limit=0 fetches nothing while reporting a clean run.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: no lower-bound validation on --limit for the read-only triage commands; negative -> provider-dependent error/UB, 0 -> silent empty run. Low — requires deliberately passing a nonsensical value; worst case is an API error or an empty report, not data corruption.

representative.title: RunAtLoad=true on gmail_labeler plist would trigger a full multi-provider run immediately on load/login, in addition to the 9 AM schedule
representative.evidence: Lines 25-26 of com.user.gmail_labeler.plist: '<key>RunAtLoad</key>\n<true/>'. diff confirms sibling com.user.mail_automation.plist has '<false/>' at the same line. So if THIS plist were manually loaded, run_automation.sh (full multi-provider mutating run) would fire immediately at load/login in addition to the 09:00 StartCalendarInterval. The true-vs-false inconsistency across two otherwise byte-identical plists is real.
representative.rationale: RunAtLoad=true is factually present and the immediate-on-load side-effect is real; severity stays low because deploy.sh never bootstraps this file (it boots it out, line 57), so the trigger requires a manual mis-load — the finding's own 'if gmail_labeler were ever loaded' framing is the accurate scope.

representative.location: SELF_NAME default line 23 + PERSONAL def lines 38-40
representative.title: Default SELF_NAME 'your-name' creates a PERSONAL smart mailbox matching the literal substring 'your-name'
representative.evidence: Line 23 `SELF_NAME = os.environ.get('SELF_NAME', 'your-name')`; line 39 uses it as the PERSONAL mailbox's From ContainsString expression. No guard checks whether the placeholder is still in effect (grep found no warning/abort on the default). Running without setting the env var installs a PERSONAL smart mailbox that matches the literal substring 'your-name'.
representative.rationale: Verified footgun: unset env var silently writes a non-functional placeholder rule into live Mail config; minor blast radius (cosmetic/garbage rule), hence low.

representative.title: Sample config file written without atomicity or overwrite guard
representative.evidence: Lines 419-423: `if path: path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True); with open(path, "w") as f: f.write(sample)` — unconditional 'w' open, no exists() check, no backup. An existing config at that path is overwritten silently.
representative.rationale: Confirmed no overwrite guard. Severity low — create_sample_config is an explicit user-invoked helper to scaffold a sample, not part of automated runs; grep shows it's not called from cli.py automation paths.

representative.title: verizon (a telecom) is in Finance/Banking, mislabeling carrier mail as a Critical financial-alert
representative.evidence: Line 146 lists r'verizon' under Finance/Banking (tier 1 Critical, in PRIORITY_LABELS line 511). Reproduced: 'bill from verizon' -> Finance/Banking tier 1. Verizon is a telecom, not a bank.
representative.rationale: Real misplacement that inflates the Critical/starred bucket with carrier mail. Low (taxonomy quality, no data loss; arguably intentional to treat carrier bills as financial alerts).

representative.location: AI/Services patterns 113-124 (r'claude', r'openai', r'anthropic', r'ollama', r'perplexity'); AI/Grok r'grok' (129)
representative.title: Bare-substring AI/brand patterns over-match unrelated mail
representative.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('art@museum.com','Claude Monet art print') -> AI/Services tier 3. Lines 114-123 are bare case-insensitive substrings (r'openai', r'anthropic', r'claude', r'perplexity', r'ollama') with no host/word boundary, unlike the (^|@|\.)...\b anchoring used for meta.com (line 121) and the finance/travel domains.
representative.rationale: Real inconsistent anchoring -> AI/Services false positives. Low (tier 3 Delegate, no archival/critical impact).

representative.title: Tech/Google bare substrings 'workspace'/'gcp' over-match unrelated mail
representative.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('rent@coworkspace.com','rent coworkspace downtown') -> Tech/Google tier 2. Lines 232-233 list bare r'gcp' and r'workspace'; both over-match ('coworkspace', 'workspaces').
representative.rationale: Real bare-substring over-match elevating unrelated mail to Tech/Google tier 2 Important. Low (taxonomy noise, no data loss). Same class as U577/U578/U582 but distinct patterns.

representative.location: Finance/Payments r'plaid' (172); Finance/Banking r'chime' (149); Professional/Jobs r'monster' (380); Travel r'kayak' (321); Shopping r'nike'/'nordstrom'/'zara'/'macys' (250-264)
representative.title: Additional bare-brand substrings collide with common English / unrelated products
representative.evidence: Reproduced: 'plaid shirts on sale' -> Finance/Payments tier 2 (r'plaid' line 175); 'Your monster energy rewards' -> Professional/Jobs tier 2 (r'monster' line 380); 'wind chime sale' -> Finance/Banking tier 1 Critical (r'chime' line 149). Note: r'plaid' is line 175 not 172, but the pattern and effect are as described.
representative.rationale: Real bare-brand substring over-match; r'chime' into tier-1 Critical is the worst as the finding notes. Low (false-positive inflation, no data loss). Minor line-number drift only.

representative.title: VIP label_override is not validated against LABEL_RULES; a bogus override yields default time_sensitive=True silently
representative.evidence: Reproduced: add_vip_sender(label_override='NotARealLabel') -> categorize_with_tier returns label='NotARealLabel', time_sensitive=True. Line 987-988: label=vip.label_override verbatim; LABEL_RULES.get(label,{'time_sensitive':True}) silently defaults. No validation against LABEL_RULES keys.
representative.rationale: Real: an unvalidated VIP label_override propagates as the result label (and, per the finding, into action.add_labels), potentially creating a junk label/folder. Low: requires user-supplied VIP config (no default VIPs); the runtime claim about provider apply is plausible but not verified in this slice.

representative.title: No idempotency — re-running duplicates smart mailboxes
representative.evidence: Three unconditional `make new smart mailbox` calls at lines 10, 17, 23 with no name-existence check; only the group at lines 3-5 is wrapped in try. Mail.app does not dedupe smart mailboxes by name, so re-running produces duplicate '1. Needs Action' / '2. Finance (Recent)' / '3. Dev Alerts' entries.
representative.rationale: No idempotency guard is genuinely present; low severity correct for a one-time convenience script the user runs by hand.

representative.title: File handle leaks on error: close access is outside any try and the open path may not exist
representative.evidence: Line 4 `set outFile to open for access (outPath as POSIX file) with write permission`; the matching `close access outFile` is at line 41, outside any try. The `tell application "Mail"` block (lines 11-39), `set boxName to name of mbox` (line 16), and the outer `repeat with mbox` (line 15) are NOT inside a try — only the inner per-message body (25-36) and the `messages of mbox` access (18-22) are guarded. An error in `name of mbox` or the Mail tell terminates the script before line 41, leaking the open file-access handle (AppleScript requires explicit `close access` to release it). Separately, outPath is `~/mail_automation/mail_export.tsv` (lines 1) and there is no mkdir/folder-existence step, so `open for access` at line 4 fails if `~/mail_automation` does not exist.
representative.rationale: Both sub-claims hold against current code: close access sits outside all error handling and the outer Mail/mbox-name path is unguarded, plus no directory is created before opening; low severity is correct for a single-user local export utility.

representative.title: TSV delimiter/newline injection corrupts output rows
representative.evidence: Line 33 builds `rowText` by concatenating `boxName`, `message id`, `senderAddress`, `subjectText`, `dateText`, etc. joined with literal `tab`, and line 35 writes `rowText & linefeed`. Sender display names and subjects are written raw with no escaping/sanitization of embedded tabs or CR/LF, so any subject/sender containing a tab or newline shifts columns or injects phantom rows into the TSV.
representative.rationale: Raw field values are written into a tab/linefeed-delimited file with zero escaping; subjects/senders routinely contain those characters, so downstream TSV parsing can break — genuine correctness defect, correctly rated low.

representative.title: Hardcoded placeholder sender list ships as-is (no-op or wrong matches if run unedited)
representative.evidence: Line 1 literally: set importantSenders to {"boss@company.com", "admissions@university.edu", "familymember@example.com"} — template placeholders. No guard/warning that the list is unconfigured. Run unedited it flags nothing real (or over-matches via the line-5 `contains`).
representative.rationale: Placeholder list ships as-is with no unconfigured-warning; low severity appropriate for a manual-edit AppleScript helper.

representative.title: Iterating live inbox messages with bare try swallows errors; no progress/logging
representative.evidence: Lines 13-18: bare `try` wrapping `set s to sender of msg` and `set flagged status of msg to true` with no `on error` clause; no logging or count output anywhere. Same swallow pattern as archive_old_inbox.applescript (line 16) and route_bulk_senders.applescript (line 25).
representative.rationale: Bare try silently discards per-message read/flag failures with no signal; non-destructive op so low severity is correct.

representative.title: No timeout on op subprocess invocation
representative.evidence: gmail_auth.py:21-27 calls subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, text=True, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE) with no timeout=. auth/onepassword.py:33-38 calls subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, text=True, capture_output=True) also with no timeout=. Neither _run_op passes a timeout, so a hung `op` invocation (e.g. awaiting biometric/desktop approval) blocks the caller indefinitely.
representative.rationale: No-timeout claim is literally true in both current files; blast radius is low because these are interactive local-auth utilities (flow.run_local_server is inherently interactive), and the 'ties up a hosted request worker' angle is speculative/overstated but does not negate the underlying missing-timeout defect.

representative.file: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md
