representative.location: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md:23-24,70-71,216-217
representative.title: Committed guide has line-broken email addresses corrupting the documented rules
representative.evidence: Lines 23-24: '• service@american' then 'express.com' on next line (should be service@americanexpress.com). Lines 70-71: bare bullet '•' then 'email@zara.com'. Lines 216-217: bare bullet '•' then 'scholar-alerts-noreply@google.com'. All three split points match the finding exactly.
representative.rationale: Tracked markdown guide has three soft-wrapped/line-broken sender addresses that copy-paste into malformed iCloud rule conditions; doc-hygiene defect, not executable code, so low severity is accurate.

representative.title: connect() with injected service sets _connected but never initializes label cache, leaving _label_cache empty
representative.evidence: connect() lines 82-84: when `self._service is not None`, it sets _connected=True and returns early WITHOUT calling _init_label_cache() (only the OAuth path at line 89 calls it). So an injected service leaves _label_cache empty: apply_label treats every label as missing and creates it, remove_label falls back to raw names, reverse-lookup returns nothing.
representative.rationale: Confirmed divergence between injected-service and OAuth paths; primarily a test-path latent issue, low severity.

representative.title: COPY-to-folder does not verify ensure_label_exists succeeded; create('NO') swallowed so COPY to nonexistent folder fails silently-ish
representative.evidence: ensure_label_exists (352-355) treats both 'OK' and 'NO' from create() as success and caches into _created_folders. A genuine CREATE failure (invalid name/permission) returning NO is cached as created, so a retry short-circuits at line 348-349 and never re-attempts; the subsequent COPY (288) returns NO → apply_label returns False. The mis-classification of NO is real, though 'NO' most commonly does mean 'already exists'.
representative.rationale: Real NO-as-success caching defect; low impact (apply_label still returns False on the failed COPY).

representative.title: IMAP connection leaked if login() fails
representative.evidence: Lines 139-140: self._connection is assigned the IMAP4_SSL object, THEN login() is called. If login raises, _connection stays set with an open socket and the exception propagates. disconnect() (143-153) does guard `if self._connection` and will logout, so the `with` path (base.py:82-87) recovers; direct callers that don't use the context manager and don't call disconnect leak the socket.
representative.rationale: Real but narrow leak (only non-context-manager direct use); context-manager path self-heals — low.

representative.title: Due-date formatting truncates time to midnight and forces UTC, mis-stating the due date across timezones
representative.evidence: Line 574 `due_str = due_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z')` hard-codes midnight and a 'Z' literal regardless of due_date.tzinfo or time, then sets timeZone='UTC' (577). core/models.py declares due_date: Optional[datetime] with no tz normalization, so a tz-aware local datetime is rendered as its naive Y-M-D at 00:00 UTC, shifting the To Do due date by up to a day.
representative.rationale: Genuine date/tz bug; affects only the optional To Do due-date display, cosmetic-ish — low severity confirmed.

representative.title: Resolving Newsletters mailbox is unguarded and can abort the whole script
representative.evidence: Lines 16-21 resolve `set bulkMailbox to mailbox bulkMailboxName` (and the account variant) with no enclosing `try`/`on error` (the only `try` in the file is lines 25-30 inside the loop). Line 3 comment: 'create this mailbox in Mail first'. If the 'Newsletters' mailbox does not exist, the `mailbox bulkMailboxName` reference raises an unhandled error and the script aborts with no graceful message.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: mailbox resolution is outside any error handler and the file warns the mailbox must pre-exist, so a missing mailbox aborts the run.

representative.location: lines 14-17 (produces: 'Email workflow automation and scheduling' artifact; consumes: [])
representative.title: seed.yaml produces/consumes does not describe the implemented commerce product (subscriptions, ACP credit packs, MCP)
representative.evidence: seed.yaml:14-17 declares a single generic artifact and `consumes: []`. On disk the commerce surfaces exist: api/billing.py (imports stripe), acp/router.py + acp/product_feed.json (ACP credit packs), mcp_server/server.py (MCP tool server). None are represented in the seed produces list.
representative.rationale: The under-described produces contract is a real, verifiable drift between seed.yaml and the implemented revenue-bearing surface; severity low because it is contract-completeness drift with no proven downstream breakage (no in-repo seed.yaml graph consumer found).

representative.title: Tests only exercise valid numeric/string inputs; no coverage for the int() crash or invalid provider/log_level
representative.evidence: core/config.py:273 `config.batch_size = int(os.getenv(f"{prefix}BATCH_SIZE"))` is unguarded — non-numeric env value raises ValueError; the only test (test_config.py:170) feeds clean "500". No validation of default_provider/log_level exists (config.py:188-191, 266-269 assign raw values; no checks), and tests only feed valid values. `custom_rules` is stored at config.py:249-250 but a repo-wide grep shows it is never read/consumed anywhere (only the dataclass field def, the assignment, and a YAML comment), confirming the '(it never does)' sub-claim; extra_priority_labels/extra_keep_in_inbox are likewise dead. No test asserts custom_rules is applied.
representative.rationale: Every sub-claim checks out against current source: unguarded int(), no provider/log_level validation, custom_rules populated-but-never-consumed, and happy-path-only coverage. Severity stays low — these are test-coverage gaps, not a live production crash.

representative.title: merge tests normalize with set()/sorted(), so they cannot detect order loss or the List-vs-Set contract violation
representative.evidence: merge() at core/models.py:100 does add_labels=list(set(self.add_labels + other.add_labels)) — set() loses insertion order. test_merge_combines_labels asserts set(merged.add_labels) == {'A','B'} (test_models.py:101) and test_merge_deduplicates asserts sorted(merged.add_labels) == ['A','B','C'] (line 108). Both normalize with set()/sorted() before comparing, so they cannot detect the order-loss in the List[str] contract.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: the assertions are invariant under reordering, so they assert nothing about the declared ordered-list semantics and cannot fail on the order-loss defect.

representative.location: missing coverage for merge() message_id / sender
representative.title: No test covers merge() mismatched message_id or the sender coalescing rule
representative.evidence: TestLabelAction (test_models.py:74-127) contains no test passing two actions with different message_id (merge docstring 'same message_id assumed', core/models.py:96 — unguarded invariant) and no test for sender=self.sender or other.sender coalescing (core/models.py:99). The LabelAction docstring (lines 71-74) ties sender to a fail-closed protected-sender gate, making the coalescing security-relevant yet untested.
representative.rationale: Verified absence: both the mismatched-message_id path and the security-relevant sender coalescing rule are public-method behaviors with zero test coverage.

representative.title: No test for state-file write failure or concurrent/partial-write corruption
representative.evidence: core/state.py:91-95 — `try: with open(self.filename, 'w') as f: json.dump(self.state, f, indent=2) except Exception as e: logger.error(...)` swallows every exception, returns None (signature `-> None`, line 74), surfaces nothing to the caller. The open mode 'w' truncates the file before json.dump runs, so a crash mid-dump leaves a partial/unparseable file (non-atomic). The save tests (test_state.py:38-71) only assert happy-path writes; no test covers an unwritable path or a truncated/partial write.
representative.rationale: Claims about save()'s exception-swallowing and non-atomic truncating write match core/state.py exactly, and no save-failure/atomicity test exists; legitimate coverage gap. Independent root cause from U478 (save-side vs load-side), so not a duplicate; severity 'low' is honest for a test-quality finding.

representative.title: postJSON collapses all transport and JSON-parse errors to a single indistinguishable {ok:false,status:0} / {data:null}
representative.evidence: postJSON returns {ok:false,status:0,data:null} on fetch throw (line 791) and sets data=null on JSON parse failure (line 788), collapsing network-down, CORS-blocked, non-JSON-200, and real-503 into indistinguishable states. The preview handler treats `status===503 || !ok || !data` identically (line 841) and shows 'Static share mode' for all of them, so a transient network blip is misreported as static mode.
representative.rationale: Verified: error states are conflated and a real backend failure is masked behind the benign 'static share mode' notice; low UX/observability defect, no security impact.

representative.title: wranglerVersion pinned only to major "4" — non-reproducible builds, supply-chain drift
representative.evidence: ci.yml:102 `uses: cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4` (floating major tag) and ci.yml:105 `wranglerVersion: "4"` (floating major). Both can change between identical commits; the step holds CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN (env line 84) and deploys to production.
representative.rationale: Accurate hardening observation. Low/info-grade: floating major tags are extremely common practice; the security upside of SHA-pinning is real but the realistic risk is low. Overlaps U682 (which covers all actions including this one).

representative.title: Third-party actions pinned to mutable major tags instead of commit SHAs
representative.evidence: ci.yml:19 actions/checkout@v4, :22 actions/setup-python@v5, :71 codecov/codecov-action@v4, :88 actions/checkout@v4, plus :102 cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4 — all mutable major-tag pins. The cloudflare-share-deploy job has CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN in env (line 84).
representative.rationale: Correct: no SHA pinning anywhere. Standard supply-chain hardening gap; severity low (theoretical re-tag attack). Subsumes U681's wrangler-action point but is a broader distinct finding.

representative.title: pip cache keyed on default lockfiles may miss requirements-api/mcp changes
representative.evidence: ci.yml:23-25 setup-python uses `cache: 'pip'` with no `cache-dependency-path`. The install step (ci.yml:30) installs requirements.txt + requirements-api.txt + requirements-mcp.txt. setup-python's default pip cache hashing resolves requirements*.txt globs in some versions, but the key is not explicitly scoped to all three files.
representative.rationale: Plausible config nit. Note: actions/setup-python's default pip cache-dependency hashing actually globs `requirements*.txt`/`*.lock` style patterns, so all three MAY be hashed — the precise behavior is version-dependent. Real-but-low; the worst case is a stale-cache install, not incorrect dep resolution (pip still resolves against the files). Severity low.

representative.title: Orphan account created by webhook may be left without its customer link if set_subscription then fails, leaking unreferenced rows
representative.evidence: billing.py:218 create_account commits (store.py:158-164) and returns; the customer-link write happens later in set_subscription (231/255), a separate committed transaction. If set_subscription raises (UNIQUE collision / dict param) the orphan account row persists unlinked with a live api_key (store.py creates api_key by default at 156).
representative.rationale: Real non-atomic create+link leaving an unreachable account with a usable api_key. Low frequency (requires a failing second step) and the orphan has no plan/customer so no entitlement granted; low.

representative.title: escalate: when no actions built (e.g., all dry-run) result.success_count is set to escalated_count, but with dry_run escalated_count>0 and actions empty conflates 'previewed' with 'succeeded'
representative.evidence: Lines 902-930: actions are appended only `if not args.dry_run` (902). In dry-run actions is empty, so the else branch `result.success_count = escalated_count` (930) reports escalations as successes even though apply_actions never ran. Same dry-run-reports-success pattern as cmd_label's else branch (291).
representative.rationale: Confirmed: in dry-run escalate sets success_count to escalated_count with nothing applied. Note: due to U166 (age always 0 for 3/4 providers) escalated_count is itself usually 0, limiting visible impact. Low — accounting cosmetic, and partly masked by the upstream no-op.

representative.title: remove_label only removed when computed label differs from remove_label; equality check is exact-string and case-sensitive
representative.evidence: cli.py:274 `if remove_label and label != remove_label:` then :275 action.remove_labels.append(remove_label). The comparison is a plain Python `!=` (case-sensitive, no .lower()/normalization). The documented re-label use-case in main() epilog (cli.py:958) `--remove-label "Misc/Other"` relies on exact-case equality with the rule-produced label to be skipped.
representative.rationale: The exact-case comparison is real as described; impact is minor churn only if an operator deliberately mismatches case on a self-supplied flag, so low severity.

representative.title: Relay senders resolve to email='' so the Gmail self-match can never fire for relayed self-mail
representative.evidence: For an UNprotected relay whose local-part lacks `_at_`, line 732-734 returns ('', real_domain) with email_=''. _self_match (783) computes `addr.rpartition('@')[0]` on addr='' yielding '' which is never in SELF_LOCALPARTS. Also _self_match requires `domain in GMAIL_DOMAINS` (781), and the recovered real domain is rarely gmail.com — so the self-match cannot fire for relayed self-mail regardless.
representative.rationale: Accurate: relayed self-mail won't match _self_match. Impact is low — is_protected_sender still fails closed for undecodable relays, and self via gmail (non-relay) is unaffected; the weakening is real but narrow.

representative.location: categorize_with_tier VIP-no-override branch lines 991-995 vs normal branch 1006-1008
representative.title: VIP-without-override path re-categorizes on combined_text but defaults time_sensitive=True, diverging from non-VIP default of the rule's own value
representative.evidence: Line 993 `label = _find_best_label(combined_text)` then 994 `rule = LABEL_RULES.get(label, {})`, 995 `time_sensitive = rule.get('time_sensitive', True)`. _find_best_label (1038) returns `best_match or 'Misc/Other'` — always a LABEL_RULES key — so `.get(label,{})` always hits a real rule and reads its own time_sensitive (Marketing=False, line 451). The `True` default at 995 only applies if label absent from LABEL_RULES, which never happens.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: the 'VIP is time-sensitive' default is effectively unreachable; a VIP matching Marketing gets time_sensitive=False. Low severity — tier still comes from vip.tier, so VIP priority is preserved; only the time_sensitive flag diverges from the comment's intent.

representative.title: Personal rule's generic keywords (family/mom/dad) at tier 1 risk elevating marketing mail to Critical when it outranks competing rules
representative.evidence: Line 488 `"patterns": [r"family", r"mom", r"dad"]` at priority 18, tier 1 (490), and Personal is in KEEP_IN_INBOX (519). These are unanchored substrings matched via re.search against combined sender+subject. A subject like 'family plan' or 'Mom-approved deal' from a sender matching no higher-priority rule would be labeled Personal/Critical/kept-in-inbox.
representative.rationale: Real precision defect: bare 'family'/'mom'/'dad' substrings at tier 1. But priority 18 is near-lowest (only Awaiting Reply 19 and Misc 999 are lower) so almost any commercial keyword (Marketing 17, etc.) outranks it; over-classification fails toward keeping mail in inbox, not archiving. Low severity.

representative.title: 'message is in mailbox does contain Finance/Dev' depends on Gmail labels existing as Mail.app mailboxes
representative.evidence: Lines 18 and 24 match `message is in mailbox, qualifier:does contain value:"Finance"` and `"Dev"`. These depend on Mail.app mailboxes named with those substrings existing (e.g. synced Gmail labels Finance/Banking, Work/Dev/*). The assumption is unstated and the views match nothing if those mailboxes are absent.
representative.rationale: The cross-provider mailbox-existence dependency is a real, statically-visible assumption; low severity is right and the condition is config-dependent.

representative.location: main() line 130; normalize_sender vs is_protected_sender domain basis
representative.title: Categorization uses normalize_sender's FIRST address only while protection uses the union; a multi-address From categorizes off one address
representative.evidence: Verified core/rules.py: normalize_sender returns the FIRST address only (docstring 740-743, returns single tuple) while is_protected_sender unions every address via _iter_sender_domains (800-805). Line 130-131 categorizes off normalize_sender's real_domain, falling back to `categorize_from_strings(real_domain or frm, subj)` when empty. Protection is sound; only the archive-bucket label for non-protected multi-address senders can be off.
representative.rationale: Premise accurate and protection-safe as the finding itself concedes; label-only mislabel, low.

representative.title: health_check swallows ALL exceptions into a generic unhealthy status, masking auth vs network vs quota distinctions
representative.evidence: base.py:409-413: `try: self.list_messages(limit=1) ... except Exception as e: return False, str(e)`. Bare Exception is caught and collapsed to (False, str(e)), so callers cannot programmatically distinguish auth failure vs network vs throttle — only a free-text string is returned. This is an accurate read of the code.
representative.rationale: Real observability/coarse-error-handling weakness in a diagnostic helper; low blast radius (health_check is informational), so info-to-low severity is appropriate.

representative.title: AppleScript repeat upper bound uses string-interpolated arithmetic that breaks for non-int page_token
representative.evidence: Line 128 `start_offset = int(page_token) if page_token else 0` is OUTSIDE the try (try begins at line 171), so a non-numeric page_token raises ValueError uncaught, crashing rather than returning empty.
representative.rationale: Confirmed in code: bad page_token crashes before the try block. Low severity since this provider's own tokens are always numeric; only a cross-provider token misuse triggers it.

representative.title: Direct dict indexing msg['id'] in list_messages will KeyError if Graph omits id; not defensively handled like other fields
representative.evidence: Line 461 id=msg['id'] (hard index) inside the parse loop at 443-467 while every sibling field uses msg.get(...). The try at 436-440 wraps only the _api_get, not the loop, so a missing 'id' raises KeyError that propagates out of list_messages uncaught. Same pattern at 255 (folder['displayName'], folder['id']) and 270-271 in _fetch_child_folders (though those are inside try/except).
representative.rationale: Real inconsistent defensive handling; $select includes id so Graph normally returns it, making the KeyError an edge case (malformed/partial item) — low.

representative.location: process_batch() lines 257-262; batchModify lambda closure over `body`
representative.title: Lambda in batch-modify retry closes over loop variable `body`; safe today but a latent closure-capture bug
representative.evidence: Lambda at 258-260 references `body` rebuilt each id_chunk iteration (252) and is invoked synchronously by _execute_with_backoff at 258, so it reads the correct body today. Finding explicitly says 'safe today', a latent closure-capture footgun only under refactor. (Note: U788 is the duplicate of this same observation.)
representative.rationale: Accurate description of a latent (non-active) footgun; not a present-tense defect, so info.

representative.title: TLD heuristic for relay domain recovery uses 2<=len<=18 all-alpha terminal label, can pick a wrong real domain for categorization
representative.evidence: Lines 702-704: `real = [c for c in cands if c.split('.')[-1].isalpha() and 2 <= len(c.split('.')[-1]) <= 18]; return min(real, key=lambda c: c.count('.')) if real else ''`. This is a TLD heuristic (alpha terminal label, fewest dots) that can pick a wrong real domain for categorization. It is only invoked at line 732 AFTER the protection loop (723-728), so it never affects the fail-closed protection decision.
representative.rationale: Heuristic is brittle as described but provably limited to categorization (protection iterates all candidates first). Info severity correct.

representative.title: Rename conflict handling updates local index incorrectly, and rename can collide with existing Gmail label causing API error mid-pass
representative.evidence: Line 63 `name_to_meta[new] = meta` does leave the old key present and stores the old meta object (true). But the finding's central claim — a later rename hitting an unexpected 'label name exists' HttpError that gets 'silently skipped' — cannot occur with the static RENAMES (lines 15-25): all 9 targets are distinct, freshly-prefixed paths (Work/, Tech/, Marketing/, Finance/) and none collides with another pending rename. The HttpError IS caught and logged (lines 64-65), which the finding itself concedes. No argparse/no other caller (grep confirms standalone script).
representative.rationale: Bookkeeping nugget is real but the HttpError-silent-skip narrative is speculative and the failure path is already caught; overlaps the latent defect described in U817.

representative.title: $top passed unbounded to Graph; values >1000 are rejected/clamped, and large $top with $orderby+$filter can 400
representative.evidence: Line 429 `"$top": limit` with no clamp. Main label loop caps batch_limit at 100 (cli.py 'min(limit - processed_this_run, 100)'), but cmd_escalate (cli.py 855-858) calls list_messages(limit=args.limit) directly with default 500 and user-overridable --limit (1093-1097). 500 < Graph's 1000 cap; >1000 only via explicit --limit 5000.
representative.rationale: The unbounded-$top defect is real (no clamp), but the >1000-rejection only fires on a user passing --limit>1000; default paths stay <=500. The $orderby+$filter sub-claim overlaps U134/U093 (needs_runtime). Partial, low.

representative.title: Mail.app star test mocks _run_applescript and so never exercises AppleScript message_id interpolation/injection surface
representative.evidence: Test at line 12 monkeypatches provider._run_applescript = lambda script: scripts.append(script) or "ok", and line 25 only asserts 'flagged status' in scripts[0] — confirming the f-string template, not execution or escaping. providers/mailapp.py:297 does interpolate message_id raw into AppleScript via f-string: 'set targetMsg to first message whose id is {message_id}'. Test uses numeric '123' (line 18), never a metacharacter-bearing id, and _run_applescript is fully mocked so no real interpolation/execution is probed.
representative.rationale: Coverage observation is accurate, but framing it as an 'injection-shaped' surface overstates risk: Mail.app message ids are internally-generated integers (not attacker-controlled), so this is a narrow test-quality gap, not a security finding — hence partial.

representative.title: Only fetches up to SAMPLE_SIZE but messages.list maxResults=2000 exceeds Gmail's 500 cap, silently returning fewer
representative.evidence: Line 175: service.users().messages().list(userId='me', labelIds=[label_id], maxResults=SAMPLE_SIZE).execute() with SAMPLE_SIZE=2000 (line 18). No nextPageToken pagination loop. Gmail API clamps maxResults to 500. Line 98 chunks min(len(messages), SAMPLE_SIZE)=min(~500,2000)=~500. Line 68 prints the actual len(messages), and line 136/151/152 divide by len(msg_data), so percentages use the real (~500) count.
representative.rationale: The under-fetch (no pagination, ~500 cap vs intended 2000) is real, but the 'report claims a 2000-message analysis it never performed' part is false — the report prints the actual count; downgraded to low.

representative.title: Two plists invoke the same run_automation.sh at the same 9:00 time — duplicate/overlapping schedule risk if both loaded
representative.evidence: diff com.user.gmail_labeler.plist com.user.mail_automation.plist shows ONLY two deltas: line6 Label (com.user.gmail_labeler vs com.user.mail_automation) and line26 RunAtLoad (<true/> vs <false/>). Both share ProgramArguments=/bin/bash run_automation.sh, StartCalendarInterval Hour=9 Minute=0, and identical log paths /Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/launchd.std{out,err}.log. The 'double-processing if both loaded' claim is factually accurate for the byte-identical schedule/logs.
representative.rationale: The structural duplication and identical 9:00 schedule + shared log paths are confirmed, but deploy.sh actively boots OUT com.user.gmail_labeler (line 57) and only bootstraps mail_automation (line 60), so the standard install path never loads both; the concurrent-double-run is only reachable via manual mis-load, so severity is low not medium.

representative.title: CTA "View on GitHub" link points to stale/renamed org (organvm-iii-ergon) that only resolves via GitHub's rename redirect
representative.evidence: Line 242 verbatim: View on GitHub. Real git remote is https://github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation.git; server.json line 3 declares io.github.a-organvm/universal-mail-automation. README badges still use organvm-iii-ergon too (lines 1,7,252,...). So the canonical org is a-organvm and the CTA points at the renamed/stale org.
representative.rationale: The wrong-canonical-URL defect is real and confirmed, but the page is an undeployed orphan (see U466), so the dependence-on-redirect / takeover blast radius is much smaller than medium; downgraded to low.

representative.title: server.json advertises a PyPI package universal-mail-automation 0.1.0 that the repo cannot build (pyproject.toml has no [project] metadata)
representative.evidence: server.json:6-14 declares packages[0]={registryType:pypi, identifier:'universal-mail-automation', version:'0.1.0', transport stdio}. pyproject.toml has no [project] name/version/build-system, so this repo cannot build that pypi artifact. Cross-file claim is real. The name in server.json is `io.github.a-organvm/universal-mail-automation` and llms.txt:6 advertises `stdio: python -m mcp_server`.
representative.rationale: Real cross-file inconsistency (advertised pypi install surface is unbuildable from source), but severity is overstated: no such package is published on PyPI today, so the failure is an aspirational/manifest-drift issue, not an active broken install path. Distinct consequence from U226 so not a duplicate, but same missing-[project] root cause.

representative.location: lines 38-45 (try/except around `from mcp_server.server import http_app, mcp`)
representative.title: MCP import failure (including a missing/renamed `mcp` internal symbol) silently downgrades a paid `/mcp` endpoint to 404 at INFO log level
representative.evidence: app.py:38-45 wraps the import in `except Exception` and logs at logger.info (line 45); a failure sets _MCP_AVAILABLE=False and the /mcp mount at line 143 is skipped. mcp_server/server.py:32 does import the internal path `from mcp.server.streamable_http import TransportSecuritySettings`, and requirements-mcp.txt pins `mcp>=1.2,<2` (wide 1.x range). So the fragile-internal-path + INFO-only-degrade mechanism is real. However the finding's parenthetical 'missing/renamed `mcp` internal symbol' conflates this with app.py:39's `mcp as _mcp`, which is mcp_server.server's OWN FastMCP instance (server.py:73), not an SDK symbol.
representative.rationale: Mechanism is real but overstated: it is a graceful-degradation design choice (intentional 3.9 fallback) logged at INFO, not a silent bug on the core paid surface; downgrade to low.

representative.location: run_labeler, lines 172, 186-190 (page_token = state.get_token(); passed to provider.list_messages)
representative.title: Malformed page token from state is replayed unvalidated to the provider
representative.evidence: page_token = state.get_token() (172) -> state.py:97-99 returns self.state.get('next_page_token') with no type check; passed verbatim to provider.list_messages(page_token=...) (186-190). is_resumable() (state.py:131-133) checks only `is not None`. No string validation. But save() (state.py:84) only ever writes the provider-returned next_page_token (a str or None), so the code never produces a non-string token itself.
representative.rationale: Real but overstated/narrow: the unvalidated-replay only triggers on an externally hand-edited state file storing a non-string next_page_token. In normal operation the token is always a provider-issued string or None. Robustness gap against tampering, not a live defect — low, partial.

representative.title: IMAP SEARCH query not encoded/quoted; multi-word criteria and non-ASCII break or inject extra search tokens
representative.evidence: uid('search', None, query) passes a single positional query string; imaplib splits the arg by spaces internally (it does tokenize space-separated args), so 'FROM example.com SUBJECT "x"' is not sent as one literal token in the way claimed. The genuine residual is CHARSET=None for non-ASCII search terms. Note: this path is largely moot because U084 shows the query is Gmail-dialect and fails before quoting matters.
representative.rationale: CHARSET-for-non-ASCII concern is real but the tokenization claim misreads imaplib; low and overlapping with U084.

representative.title: fetch HEADER parsing assumes data[0] is a 2-tuple; raw/atom responses raise TypeError unhandled
representative.evidence: Line 221 guards `data[0] is None` but line 224 indexes data[0][1] without isinstance(data[0], tuple) — unlike the X-GM-LABELS (238) and FLAGS (254) parsing which DO guard the tuple shape. A non-tuple FETCH response would raise TypeError. base.batch_get_details (base.py:151) would need to be the catcher; it is not per-item-guarded for this. However real IMAP servers return a tuple for BODY[...] fetches, so this is an edge case.
representative.rationale: Real inconsistency vs the guarded parses below, but unlikely on conforming servers; low.

representative.title: Per-page O(n) message indexing causes quadratic cost and re-fetch of full message list each page
representative.evidence: Line 140 `set allMsgs to messages of targetMailbox` then line 146 `item i of allMsgs` per iteration, re-run each page (line 144 repeat). The materialization/indexing cost is AppleScript-runtime-dependent; the timeout->empty->silent-truncation chain is real (line 172-175).
representative.rationale: Performance characterization (O(N) per access / O(N^2) sweep) is plausible but a runtime claim about AppleScript collection access; the silent-truncation consequence is the verifiable part. Real but overstated/needs runtime to quantify.

representative.title: MCP server's documented standalone HTTP run command needs uvicorn/starlette that requirements-mcp.txt does not declare
representative.evidence: requirements-mcp.txt has only `mcp>=1.2,<2` (line 6); uvicorn[standard]>=0.29 and fastapi (Starlette) live in requirements-api.txt. server.py:20 docstring documents `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app`. BUT the file header (lines 3-5) instructs `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt`, and EVERY real install path installs all three: Dockerfile RUN line installs all three, ci.yml:30 installs all three, render.yaml uses the Docker runtime. The CMD itself runs `uvicorn api.app:app` (the API entrypoint, which has uvicorn from requirements-api.txt and mounts /mcp), not the standalone `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app`.
representative.rationale: Real gap only under a hypothetical MCP-only install (requirements.txt + requirements-mcp.txt alone) that no instruction or deploy path actually performs; overstated as a deployability break.

representative.title: Live audit counts interpolated into innerHTML without escaping (DOM-XSS sink)
representative.evidence: a.protected_held/archived/moved/labeled/kept are interpolated into innerHTML with only `?? 0` (lines 866-870), no escapeHtml. Confirmed unescaped. However both real backends type-coerce these to int: FastAPI AuditSummary (schemas.py:54-61, all `int`) and worker.mjs triagePreview emits numeric literals. Exploit requires API_BASE pointed at a hostile origin; API_BASE = window.__MAIL_AUTOMATION_API_BASE__ || '' (line 677) with no injection site found anywhere in the repo, so it is same-origin on the only deployment.
representative.rationale: Real missing-escape on numeric sinks but blocked by int-typed schemas on both shipped backends; only exploitable under an attacker-controlled API_BASE that the deployment never sets — defense-in-depth, not a live vuln.

representative.title: categorization.tier interpolated into innerHTML unescaped; server schema does not coerce it
representative.evidence: Confirmed asymmetry: line 812 `const tier = cat.tier != null ? \`tier ${cat.tier}\` : ""` is raw, while line 813 escapes cat.label via escapeHtml; both flow into out.innerHTML (816/818). SenderCheckResponse.categorization is Optional[dict] (schemas.py:36) — NOT coerced, so a non-numeric tier would pass through. But the categorization engine emits integer tiers (PRIORITY_TIERS 1-4; worker.mjs emits numeric tier 1/3/4), and the value is server-derived from rules, not user input. Exploit needs the engine/backend to emit HTML in tier, or a hostile API_BASE (never set).
representative.rationale: The label-escaped/tier-unescaped inconsistency is a real latent sink worth a one-line fix, but tier is server-controlled and numeric in practice; not exploitable on the shipped surfaces.

representative.title: Checkout redirect assigns server-supplied URL to window.location with no host validation
representative.evidence: Line 888: `if (ok && data && data.url) { window.location.href = data.url; }` with no scheme/host allowlist — confirmed unvalidated redirect sink. Reachable only on a 2xx /v1/billing/checkout response carrying `url`. On the only deployment the worker returns 503 for /v1/billing/checkout (worker.mjs:269-270), so this branch never fires on the demo; on the FastAPI backend data.url is a server-built Stripe Checkout Session URL, not attacker-supplied. Requires a compromised backend or hostile API_BASE (never set) to weaponize.
representative.rationale: Real missing host-allowlist on a money-path redirect (good hardening target), but not reachable on the demo and the legit backend supplies a trusted Stripe URL; severity downgraded from medium.

representative.title: Worker /llms.txt diverges from the canonical builder and advertises non-existent worker routes
representative.evidence: Worker lines 307-318 hardcode an llms.txt that does NOT match build_llms_txt (well_known.py:54-75): the canonical version lists MCP, ACP checkout, product feed; the worker version lists only Health, Sender check, Triage preview, Pricing, Audit receipt. The worker version does NOT advertise mcp/acp (the description's claim it 'advertises the MCP/ACP surfaces' is the inverse of reality — the canonical one does). gen_commerce_artifacts.py (lines 99-100) writes root llms.txt but never touches worker.mjs, so drift is real.
representative.rationale: Drift is real and confirmed, but the finding's specific claim that the worker advertises non-existent mcp/acp routes is backwards — the worker omits them; the canonical builder includes them. Overstated/misread, hence partial.

representative.title: Hardcoded Mail version path 'V10' will silently fail on other macOS Mail versions
representative.evidence: Line 17 hardcodes `~/Library/Mail/V10/MailData/SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist` with no V* discovery (grep shows no globbing of the Mail dir). But lines 99-100 `if not PLIST_PATH.exists(): raise SystemExit(...)` exit LOUDLY with a clear message — it does NOT 'silently fail' as the title claims.
representative.rationale: Hardcoded version-specific path is real (portability limitation), but the 'silently fail' characterization is wrong — the script SystemExits with a clear error.

representative.title: codecov-action@v4 used without CODECOV_TOKEN — coverage upload can fail/skip silently on this private-org repo
representative.evidence: ci.yml:71 uses codecov/codecov-action@v4 with no `token:` input and no CODECOV_TOKEN referenced anywhere; ci.yml:74 `fail_ci_if_error: false` so an upload failure is swallowed. README.md:8 shows a static 'coverage-pending' badge. allow-secret
representative.rationale: Config facts confirmed, but the 'will fail/skip' core claim is needs_runtime — tokenless v4 upload can succeed for public repos and the actual pass/fail depends on Codecov's plan/ratelimit at runtime. Real observability gap, severity low (no functional impact). Marking partial (overstated certainty).

representative.title: run_credits coerced with int(account.get('run_credits',0)) can raise ValueError/TypeError on a non-numeric stored value, 500-ing the entitlement check
representative.evidence: int(account.get('run_credits', 0)) at plans.py:192 would raise on a non-numeric stored value. But schema enforces run_credits INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 (store.py:51) and add_credits/consume_credit write int() values (store.py:229,242), so a non-int is not reachable via any code path. Additionally entitlements_for is never called (U029), so the exception cannot occur at runtime today.
representative.rationale: Plausible fail-mode but doubly gated: schema prevents non-int storage AND the function is dead. Genuine hardening note, very narrow — low.

representative.location: run_labeler lines 197-201, cmd_summary 552-556, cmd_pending 655-659, cmd_vip 750-754, cmd_escalate 866-870
representative.title: batch_get_details detection via hasattr but fallback dict-comprehension calls get_message_details per id which may return None and partially populate; no error isolation
representative.evidence: All commands do `if hasattr(provider,'batch_get_details'): details = provider.batch_get_details(msg_ids) else: details = {m.id: provider.get_message_details(m.id) for ...}` (e.g. 198-201). In the fallback, a get_message_details raising aborts the whole comprehension. But gmail.batch_get_details (providers/gmail.py:213-258) already isolates per-message errors via a callback collecting failed_ids and retries them individually, and consuming loops iterate details.items() (not msg_ids), so a dropped id is skipped, not a KeyError.
representative.rationale: Partial: the fallback-path 'one error kills the page' claim is real (dict comprehension has no per-item isolation). But the 'missing keys cause silent drops vs the if-not-msg check' framing is overstated — loops iterate details.items(), so omitted ids are simply absent, never KeyError, and only the non-Gmail fallback lacks isolation (Gmail's batch path isolates). Low.

representative.title: Demo doc lists /v1/triage/preview as verified but omits that POST /v1/triage is a silent no-op that always returns dry_run:true
representative.evidence: worker.mjs lines 255-258 and 260-263 both route to triagePreview(); triagePreview() (lines 172-198) always returns `dry_run: true` (line 177) and `run_id: "demo_preview"` (line 196). Doc 'Verified Demo Endpoints' (lines 18-22) lists `POST /v1/triage/preview` but NOT `/v1/triage`, and never states /v1/triage is a no-op preview. However the audit values are NOT fixed 'regardless of input': total=Math.max(capped,3), protected_held and archived derive from the `limit` body field (lines 173-183), so the '3/2/1 regardless of input' claim is overstated — those are the values only when limit<=1 or unset.
representative.rationale: Real documentation accuracy gap (/v1/triage is wired but undocumented and silently a dry-run preview), but the 'fabricated fixed audit regardless of input' characterization misreads code where audit fields are input-derived — hence partial.

representative.title: Label auto-creation in _init_labels has no error handling; a create failure aborts the entire run before any processing
representative.evidence: Lines 101-113 call labels().list and labels().create() with NO try/except and NOT via _execute_with_backoff (confirmed: only try/except blocks in the file are at 82, 171, 257, 290 — none wrap _init_labels). Any create failure (429/limit/collision) propagates out of __init__ (line 76) and aborts before processing. HOWEVER the finding's supporting claim that a 'legacy get_or_create_label' exists and wraps creation is false — grep shows no get_or_create_label/execute_with_retry anywhere in gmail_labeler.py.
representative.rationale: Core defect (unguarded, non-backoff label creation aborts the whole run) is real; the comparison-to-legacy-helper justification is fabricated, so partial. Low severity (startup-only, fail-loud).

representative.location: main() lines 161-166; per-message archive with no rate-limit/error backoff
representative.title: Apply loop has no per-message error backoff and EXPUNGEs after every message; large applies are slow and a mid-loop server error aborts the rest
representative.evidence: Lines 161-165 have no try/except, so an imaplib exception in archive_uid propagates and aborts remaining moves (finally only logs out, 167-171). True that COPY fallback expunges per message. But the claim 'Moved/failed can never report a non-fatal failure for the MOVE path' is overstated: archive_uid returns False whenever COPY returns non-OK (73-74), which IS counted as failed (164-165); only raised exceptions escape the count.
representative.rationale: Real (no in-loop error isolation) but partly overstated about the failure counter.

representative.title: Decoded header parts joined with space can corrupt RFC2047 multi-encoded-word values and ignores decode errors silently
representative.evidence: Tested: truly adjacent encoded-words (no separator) are returned by decode_header as a SINGLE part, so ' '.join inserts no spurious space in that case — the headline claim is not reproduced. The residual real issues: ' '.join can mis-join an encoded-word part adjacent to a plain-text part, and errors='ignore' (line 34) silently drops undecodable bytes.
representative.rationale: Main 'spurious space between adjacent encoded words' claim is wrong per decode_header behavior; only minor residual — low.

representative.title: Fixture's broad except binds _payment_mod=None and can skip payment-client reset, leaking state between tests
representative.evidence: tests/conftest.py L27-31: `try: from acp import payment as _payment_mod; _payment_mod.set_payment_client(None) except Exception: _payment_mod = None` — both import AND reset are in one try; L34-35 teardown is guarded by `if _payment_mod is not None`. But acp/payment.py L113-117 set_payment_client is just `global _CLIENT; _CLIENT = client` — it cannot raise. The setup call at L29 also clears any prior client before EVERY test (autouse), so a leak would be re-cleared next test.
representative.rationale: Code structure is exactly as described (real broad-except smell coupling import+reset and masking import errors), but the claimed cross-test state leak/order-dependence is unreachable with the current non-raising set_payment_client — it is purely refactor-contingent, and the autouse setup reset further nullifies any leak.

representative.title: Test asserts _independently_protected(12345) is False, locking in 'non-str silently swallowed' as correct
representative.evidence: core/rules.py:797 `if not sender or not sender.strip()` — 12345 is truthy so it reaches 12345.strip(), which raises AttributeError; core/audit.py:113-114 `except Exception: return False`. So _independently_protected(12345) returns False (fail-OPEN), while the gate fails CLOSED for blank/None (rules.py:798 returns True; audit.py:837? confirmed via _independently_protected('') is True at test line 437). The asymmetry the finding describes is real and the test (line 438) does cement it.
representative.rationale: Real, narrow fail-open asymmetry confirmed, but the contract types sender as str (gate is the real enforcement; audit.py docstring explicitly 'declines to assert a protection it cannot compute'); only triggers if a non-str sender reaches the helper, so low not actionable-high.

representative.title: Reverse price->plan mapping is O(n) over plans reading env each iteration; if two plans share/misconfigure the same price env value, first match wins silently
representative.evidence: plan_id_for_price (plans.py:169-172) iterates PLANS in insertion order and returns the first stripe_price_id match, reading os.environ each iteration via the stripe_price_id property (line 48). If STRIPE_PRICE_PRO and STRIPE_PRICE_BUSINESS were set to the same id, 'pro' (inserted first) would win. The function IS used live (billing.py:251,291). No uniqueness guard exists.
representative.rationale: Behavior is correctly described and the function is on the live webhook path, but the trigger is a deploy misconfiguration (duplicate price env), not a code defect. O(n)/env-read-per-iter is a non-issue at 3 plans. Low.

representative.title: Provider query string is length-bounded but not otherwise validated; passed straight to provider.list_messages enabling provider-side query abuse
representative.evidence: api/schemas.py:46 `query: str = Field(default="has:nouserlabels", max_length=2048)` — bounded length only, no content allowlist. api/app.py:97-107 `_run` passes `query=req.query` straight into `service.run_triage(...)`, which forwards it to the provider's native query (list_messages). So the factual claim (query content is unvalidated and flows to the provider) is accurate.
representative.rationale: The code does pass an unvalidated, length-bounded provider-native query through to list_messages exactly as described, but this is intended passthrough design (each provider must accept its own search syntax), not a code defect; the real risk hinges entirely on the unauthenticated-endpoint concern owned by the app.py finding, so this is real-but-overstated as a standalone schema bug.

representative.location: record() lines 152/184/200 (message_id typed str but accepted/stored unvalidated)
representative.title: message_id accepted and stored without type/empty validation; empty or non-str ids silently pollute the violations trail
representative.evidence: record() declares message_id: str (line 150) and never validates it; appended raw to violations (184) and stored as entry['message_id'] (200). LabelAction.message_id is typed str (core/models.py:84) but has no non-empty constraint, so '' is constructible. An empty id in violations yields ['']  which weakens AuditInvariantError (256-259) / receipt_line (272) forensics. Unlike sender (guarded by isinstance in _domain_of:91), message_id has no defensive normalization.
representative.rationale: Real but speculative/overstated: no caller in the codebase passes an empty message_id (cli.py:79, base.py:384, gmail.py:489 all pass action.message_id from real provider results), and the type contract is str. A defensive validation would be nice-to-have; it is not a demonstrated defect with current callers.

representative.title: Duplicate/overlapping flash-sale regexes — one alternation is dead, and broad keywords mislabel transactional mail
representative.evidence: Line 435 `(% ?off|limited[- ]time|exclusive offer|flash sale|use code|coupon)` and line 437 `(flash sale|\d+% ?off|clearance sale)` both contain `flash sale` (redundant). However line 437 also adds `\d+% ?off` and `clearance sale` not in 435, so the alternation is NOT wholly dead. r'discount' (436), and Finance/Payments r'statement' (188) and r'invoice' (189) are unanchored substrings.
representative.rationale: Partial: 'flash sale' is genuinely duplicated but the claim 'one alternation is dead' overstates — line 437 carries unique tokens. Marketing is priority 17; 'statement'/'invoice' sit in Finance/Payments (priority 8, tier 2) — a higher-priority bucket, contradicting the 'dragging to lower-priority archive' framing for those. Low severity precision nit.

representative.title: appleid.com endswith check treats any '*.appleid.com' as a relay, mis-routing legitimate appleid subdomains
representative.evidence: Line 720 `if domain in RELAY_DOMAINS or domain.endswith('.appleid.com')` enters relay decoding for any *.appleid.com subdomain. appleid.com is in PROTECTED_SENDERS (563). For a real (non-encoded) subdomain like e.appleid.com, _relay_domain_candidates(local) yields junk and _best_relay_domain may return '' (704), so line 736-737 falls through to `return (addr, domain)` keeping the carrier domain — which _is_protected_domain still protects via appleid.com. Protection is safe; only categorization routing is affected.
representative.rationale: The finding itself concedes protection is safe and only routing is muddied. The endswith('.appleid.com') boundary is correct (would not match 'evil.appleid.com.attacker'). A minor categorization-quality nit, info/low.

representative.location: run() exception handler lines 333-335 saving page_token
representative.title: On fatal mid-batch error, state is saved with the NEXT page token though the current batch was only partially modified
representative.evidence: page_token is advanced only at line 319 AFTER process_batch returns (line 311), so a mid-batch raise saves the token that produced the CURRENT page (still pointing at the page being processed). On resume that page is reprocessed; for the default draining query already-labeled messages drop out and are skipped (safe), and total_processed (312) was not incremented so counts under-report. The non-transactional state claim is true, but the practical blast radius is mostly a count discrepancy, not data loss.
representative.rationale: Real non-atomic state-on-crash, but consequences are largely benign (safe reprocess + minor count under-report); severity-appropriate at low, hence partial vs the inflated framing.

representative.title: logout() may leave socket open on certain server errors; no close()/shutdown fallback
representative.evidence: Lines 145-153: `if self._connection: try: self._connection.logout() except Exception: logger.debug(...) finally: self._connection=None; self._current_mailbox=None`. No explicit shutdown()/close() fallback in the except path.
representative.rationale: Real but minor: imaplib.logout() itself calls self.shutdown() which closes the socket; a leak only on the narrow case where logout raises before shutdown. FD-leak-across-cycles framing is overstated for typical single-process daily runs.

representative.title: Pagination next_token off-by-one boundary when total is exactly a multiple of limit
representative.evidence: Line 201 `next_token = str(next_offset) if next_offset < total else None`. Finding's own worked example shows the exact-multiple boundary is CORRECT. The residual claim is offset drift under concurrent mailbox mutation between pages.
representative.rationale: The titled off-by-one is a false alarm (finding admits 'Correct here'); only the concurrent-mutation drift edge-case remains, which is a low-severity inherent limitation of offset pagination.

representative.title: Invalid-YAML test asserts silent {} fallback as correct, masking a swallow-all error path
representative.evidence: load_yaml_config (core/config.py:119-126) does catch `Exception` broadly and return `{}` on malformed YAML, and the test (test_config.py:78-82) asserts the `{}` fallback as correct without asserting any log/warning — so the test-coverage gap (no assertion that the failure is observable) is real. However the finding's framing that the failure is fully unobservable / silent is inaccurate: line 125 emits `logger.warning(f"Failed to load config from {path}: {e}")`, so the failure IS logged at WARNING; it is simply not asserted by the test. The 'partially-parsed dangerous config' scenario is not demonstrable — yaml.safe_load returns parsed data or raises, it does not partially parse into the except branch.
representative.rationale: Real test-quality gap (no assertion that the swallow is logged) but overstated: the source already logs a warning, so the error path is not as silent/unobservable as claimed.

representative.title: Escalation boundary values (exactly 24h and exactly 72h) are untested
representative.evidence: core/rules.py:1122 `if email_age_hours < 24` and core/rules.py:1131 `if 24 <= email_age_hours < 72` confirm the two branch boundaries (24, 72). tests/test_rules.py TestEscalation cases pass ages 12, 48, 50, 80, 100 (lines 269,274,280,285,289,293,298,303) — none equal 24.0 or 72.0, so an off-by-one flip of < to <= at either boundary would not be caught. The claim accurately reads both the code and the test ages.
representative.rationale: Real coverage gap (exact-boundary cases 24.0/72.0 untested) but it is a test-suite enhancement, not a production defect; correctly low/low — overstated only insofar as it is filed as a bug finding.

representative.title: Expanded Stripe 'customer'/'subscription' object (dict) would be used as a customer id, causing wrong lookups or a 500
representative.evidence: Same code as U286: billing.py:227/245/266/273 assume obj.get('customer') (and obj.get('subscription') at 228) are string ids with no isinstance guard. A dict would mis-lookup (orphan create) or raise sqlite3.InterfaceError in store.py -> uncaught -> 500 -> unmarked -> redelivery loop.
representative.rationale: Plausible only under an expand/API-version config that returns expanded objects, which cannot be confirmed statically; default webhooks send id strings. Root-cause duplicate of U286.

representative.location: 417, 441 (archive via INBOX in remove_ids + batch key grouping)
representative.title: Star id resolution uses cache 'STARRED' but ensure_label_exists never adds STARRED to cache, so star always appends literal 'STARRED' (benign) while add label dedup may be defeated
representative.evidence: STARRED is resolved at line 420 via _label_cache.get('STARRED','STARRED') without routing through ensure_label_exists, so STARRED is never cached; ensure_label_exists caches system labels name->name only when called (lines 350-353). batchModify accepts mixed opaque ids and literal system names in the sorted-tuple key (line 441), so functionally fine. Pure benign-duplication note.
representative.rationale: Accurate dead-code/harmless-duplication observation with no behavioral impact — info.

representative.location: 248 (batch.execute not wrapped to reset between backoff retries)
representative.title: On batch.execute rate-limit retry, results dict may receive duplicate parses but request_id keys dedupe — no corruption, but failed_ids not cleared
representative.evidence: failed_ids is per-chunk (line 225) and never reset across _execute_with_backoff retries of batch.execute (line 248); results[request_id]=msg is idempotent (line 234). A message failing attempt 1 then succeeding on a re-exec stays in failed_ids and is re-fetched individually (lines 252-255), overwriting an identical good result — wasted quota only. Confirmed in source; only matters if execute() raises a global HttpError (rare, since per-request errors go to callback).
representative.rationale: Real but strictly wasted-quota under a rare retry path; no data corruption — info as self-rated.

representative.title: 'first message whose id is' searches only current/default mailbox context, may target wrong or missing message
representative.evidence: Line 213/265 `first message whose id is {message_id}` is unscoped (no mailbox/account container), while list_messages scopes to a specific mailbox/account (line 139). Whether Mail.app resolves an unscoped `message` against an ambiguous default and whether ids collide across accounts is AppleScript runtime behavior.
representative.rationale: Plausible wrong-target/missing-message concern, but only confirmable by executing against Mail.app; cannot verify statically. Severity lowered (apply_label re-derives via ensure_label_exists; failure returns False, message stays put).

representative.title: Date condition 'is less than value:30' is likely wrong units for a date-received filter
representative.evidence: Line 19 verbatim: `{condition type:date received, qualifier:is less than value:30}`. Whether Mail.app interprets the bare integer 30 as a relative day-count, an absolute date coercion, or an error can only be confirmed by executing the script against Mail.app; the finding itself is confidence:low and states 'this is untested'.
representative.rationale: AppleScript Mail smart-mailbox date semantics are not statically determinable from this source; plausible but requires Mail.app at runtime to confirm wrong/empty behavior.

representative.title: Batch HTTP retry re-executes an already-consumed BatchHttpRequest on rate limit, likely raising instead of retrying
representative.evidence: Line 164 passes the bound batch_get.execute into _execute_with_backoff. googleapiclient BatchHttpRequest is documented single-use; re-executing it after a rate-limit HttpError would generally raise rather than retry the constituent gets. But whether the batch-level execute() raises a 403/429 classified as rate-limited (vs surfacing per-request errors only through the callback->failed_ids path at 168-182) depends on googleapiclient version behavior at runtime; cannot be confirmed purely statically.
representative.rationale: Plausible API-misuse making the top-level batch retry ineffective, but its actual trigger/behavior is library-version- and runtime-dependent; per-request retry path still covers the common case.

representative.title: is_read/is_starred parsed from raw AppleScript boolean string is fragile to localization/format
representative.evidence: Line 195 `is_read.lower() == "true"` / line 196 `is_flagged.lower()`. Whether `(msgRead as string)` (line 160) yields exactly 'true'/'false' across locales is AppleScript runtime behavior.
representative.rationale: Locale-sensitivity of AppleScript boolean coercion cannot be confirmed statically; finding itself rates low confidence. Plausible but needs runtime.

representative.title: Email validation regex is trivially permissive
representative.evidence: Line 882: `if (!email || !/.+@.+\..+/.test(email))`. This regex is trivially permissive (accepts 'x@y.z', and HTML-ish junk). The email then posts to /v1/billing/checkout (line 887). The finding itself notes the address is escaped where rendered, so it is a data-quality issue, not XSS.
representative.rationale: Accurate but minor: it is a known-weak client-side email check; the real validation/normalization belongs server-side at Stripe customer creation. Info severity.

representative.title: Path.expanduser() evaluated at import time freezes HOME
representative.evidence: Lines 17-21: DEFAULT_CONFIG_PATHS built at import with Path('~/...').expanduser(); ~ resolved once at import. find_config_file() (139-141) iterates these frozen paths. The MAIL_AUTOMATION_CONFIG env path (132-134) IS expanded reactively, but the defaults are not.
representative.rationale: Confirmed: ~ expansion is frozen at import for the default paths, affecting test harnesses/HOME changes. Info severity — almost never matters in practice (HOME rarely changes mid-process).

representative.title: resultSizeEstimate used as exact count; it is an ESTIMATE and unreliable for completion checks
representative.evidence: Line 485 reads results.get('resultSizeEstimate', 0) and line 486 declares SUCCESS when ==0. Gmail API documents resultSizeEstimate as an approximation. Used only in a print/return-bool for an end-of-run banner (verify_labeling_complete), no mutation or control flow depends on it beyond a console message.
representative.rationale: Accurate API-semantics observation but purely cosmetic (drives only a printed SUCCESS/WARNING line). The finding itself calls it cosmetic. Info.

representative.title: Hardcoded label-rename map with no dry-run mutates live Gmail label taxonomy immediately
representative.evidence: main() (line 32) calls svc.users().labels().update(...).execute() (line 61) directly against userId='me' with no --dry-run, no argparse, and no confirmation prompt (grep for argparse/dry-run/sys.argv returns nothing). The factual claim is accurate.
representative.rationale: Accurate observation, but renames are non-destructive (relabel, not delete) and this is a missing-preview/blast-radius note rather than a code defect — info severity.

representative.title: Unanchored .replace("Z", ...) on the ISO timestamp corrupts any string containing a stray 'Z'
representative.evidence: Lines 455 and 506 use unanchored .replace('Z','+00:00'), a global substring replace. Graph returns Z only as the trailing UTC designator, so a stray internal 'Z' would only appear in a malformed value, producing a ValueError that is swallowed to date=None.
representative.rationale: Technically true that the replace is unanchored, but Graph timestamps never contain a non-trailing 'Z', so the only effect is on already-malformed input which is already swallowed — cosmetic smell, info severity.

representative.title: Plan run-cap renders 'NaN' if the API returns a non-numeric monthly_run_cap
representative.evidence: Lines 906 and 936 both compute `${Number(p.monthly_run_cap).toLocaleString()} runs / mo` after only `== null` guard. A non-numeric string would yield 'NaN runs / mo'. (The finding's own self-correction is right: `== null` does catch undefined, so the undefined sub-claim is moot.) In practice both backends emit integer or null caps (plans.py monthly_run_cap:int|None; worker.mjs numeric|null), so NaN requires a malformed backend.
representative.rationale: Real fragility — no NaN guard — but the value comes from typed backends, so it is a graceful-degradation nit; info severity.

representative.title: Committed discovery artifacts are byte-in-sync with their generators (verified, no drift)
representative.evidence: build_agent_manifest (well_known.py:17-51) emits field-for-field the committed .well-known/agent.json (spec_version:34, oauth_scopes:45, all URLs from base) and build_feed (feed.py:33-64) matches acp/product_feed.json. scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py exists. Endpoint mappings confirmed: /v1/billing/plans→billing.py:94, /v1/audit/{run_id}→receipts.py:114, senders/check→app.py:79, triage/preview→app.py:85, /acp/checkout_sessions→router.py:166, /acp/feed.json→feed.py:67, /mcp mount→app.py:143, /openapi.json auto. (Could not run byte-diff: fastapi not installed in verify env; confirmed by structural field-level read instead.)
representative.rationale: Positive verification that the discovery surface is in sync with its generators and advertises only real routes; all cited route line numbers check out, so the clean-surface record stands as info.

representative.title: Bare `except:` in extract_domain swallows all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt
representative.evidence: Lines 27-33: try: ... except: return 'unknown' (bare except). Grep confirms the same anti-pattern in auto_drain.py line 63, with its own def extract_domain at line 60. The try body (split/strip on a string) cannot raise, so the handler is effectively dead.
representative.rationale: Bare except confirmed and repeated across scripts; pure code smell over unreachable code, info severity is correct.

representative.title: Local protected-senders file with real PII exists on disk and is correctly gitignored / never committed (verified clean)
representative.evidence: git check-ignore -v returns '.gitignore:8:config/protected_senders.local.txt' (matched). git ls-files returns empty (untracked). git log --all -- config/protected_senders.local.txt returns empty (never in history). File contains real protected-sender PII across legal, financial, government, and self-mailbox entries; specific values redacted for public review artifact safety. Loader core/rules.py:583 def _load_local_protected() reads ./config/protected_senders.local.txt; invoked at line 616 (_local_domains, _local_selfs = _load_local_protected()). 35 domains + 1 self parse cleanly.
representative.rationale: Positive privacy verification: PII-bearing local file is correctly gitignored, untracked, and absent from all history; loader reads it as designed. No leak.

representative.title: Scope assumption wrong: create_smart_mailboxes.scpt is plain UTF-8 AppleScript source, NOT a compiled binary — it IS reviewable (and unreviewed)
representative.evidence: `file create_smart_mailboxes.scpt` -> 'Unicode text, UTF-8 text'; first bytes are literal source 'tell application "Mail"...'. grep for 'create_smart_mailboxes' across *.sh/*.py/*.plist returns no hits (only this review's own findings.json/REPORT.md). 27 lines of readable AppleScript, unreferenced by deploy.sh, run_automation.sh, or any launchd plist.
representative.rationale: Both claims verified: it is plaintext source (not a compiled binary, so no binary coverage gap) and it is unwired from the scheduling pipeline; info severity correct.

representative.location: lines 6-7 (docstring: 'The heavy mcp SDK is imported lazily by mcp_server.server')
representative.title: mcp_server package docstring misstates lazy-import behavior — server.py imports the mcp SDK eagerly at module top level
representative.evidence: mcp_server/__init__.py lines 6-7 state the mcp SDK 'is imported lazily by :mod:`mcp_server.server`'. But mcp_server/server.py imports it eagerly at module top level: line 31 `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` and line 32 `from mcp.server.streamable_http import TransportSecuritySettings` (also line 34 `from api import service`). No lazy/deferred import of mcp exists inside server.py functions.
representative.rationale: Documentation defect only: server.py imports the mcp SDK eagerly, so the docstring's 'imported lazily by server' wording is factually wrong; no runtime/behavioral impact, so info severity stands.

representative.title: Dead/confusing config: module-level DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID reads env but __init__ re-reads the same env, so DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID is never used
representative.evidence: Line 63 defines DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID', ''); __init__ line 120 does self.client_id = client_id or os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID') — re-reading the env directly. Repo-wide grep shows DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID is referenced only at its definition (line 63) and in the review docs; never consumed by any code.
representative.rationale: Dead, mildly misleading constant. Pure cosmetic/maintainability — info severity confirmed (the candidate's info matches; REPORT.md mislabels it 'high').

representative.title: Unused module constant GRAPH_API_MESSAGES (inbox-only) — never referenced; list_messages builds its own URL
representative.evidence: Line 25 defines GRAPH_API_MESSAGES = f'{GRAPH_API_BASE}/me/mailFolders/inbox/messages'. Repo-wide grep shows it referenced only at its definition and in review docs; list_messages (422-426) builds its own URL from _folder_cache / mailFolders/{folder}/messages and never uses the constant.
representative.rationale: Genuinely unused module constant. Cosmetic — info severity confirmed.

representative.location: lines 52-53 (a { color: var(--green); } a { color: var(--accent); })
representative.title: Duplicate conflicting anchor color rule (dead CSS)
representative.evidence: Lines 52-53 are two consecutive `a { color: var(--green); }` then `a { color: var(--accent); }`; the second wins by source order, making line 52 dead. Both --green and --accent resolve to #0f766e (lines 18,22), so no visible effect today.
representative.rationale: Confirmed dead/duplicate CSS rule and a real maintenance hazard if the tokens diverge; cosmetic, info severity as filed.

representative.title: Group-creation failure silently swallowed by bare try
representative.evidence: Lines 3-5: `try` / `make new mailbox with properties {name:"Ω Automation"}` / `end try` with no `on error` clause and no logging. Any failure other than already-exists is silently swallowed with no operator signal.
representative.rationale: Bare try with no handler is exactly as described; info severity correct since swallowing the already-exists case is partly intentional and this is a benign manual script.

representative.location: lines 19-26 packages_distributions shim; line 12 unused 'import re' is used; line 14 socket
representative.title: Dead/likely-unnecessary importlib_metadata shim with bare-except swallow
representative.evidence: Lines 19-26 monkeypatch importlib_metadata.packages_distributions inside a try/except that swallows all exceptions (line 25 `except Exception: pass`). grep confirms packages_distributions is only assigned, never read elsewhere in the module - dead shim. The finding's own parenthetical concedes 're' IS used (verified: re.search at line 358) and socket IS used (socket.error at line 304), so those are non-issues.
representative.rationale: Dead-code shim with bare-except swallow is real but cosmetic copy-paste cruft; the import-re/socket asides are explicitly non-defects. Info severity as filed.

representative.title: providers package __all__ omits the public DTO ListMessagesResult
representative.evidence: providers/__init__.py:14 imports only `EmailProvider, ProviderCapabilities` and __all__ (lines 16-19) lists only those two. providers/base.py defines three public classes: ProviderCapabilities (line 20), ListMessagesResult (line 39), EmailProvider (line 46). ListMessagesResult is omitted from the package facade. tests/test_api.py:8 and tests/test_audit.py:29 import `ListMessagesResult` directly from providers.base. Repo-wide grep for `from providers import` returns zero hits — every consumer already reaches into providers.base.
representative.rationale: Accurate facade-incompleteness observation; ListMessagesResult is a public DTO not re-exported, but since no code uses `from providers import` anywhere, there is no functional breakage — purely cosmetic, info severity stands.

representative.location: line 12 (from googleapiclient.errors import HttpError, module top level)
representative.title: providers.gmail imports googleapiclient at module top level, so direct import crashes without the optional Google dependency
representative.evidence: Line 12 imports googleapiclient.errors at module top level; providers/__init__.py uses lazy getters and __all__ excludes gmail, so importing the package does not pull gmail. google-api-python-client>=2.0.0 IS declared in requirements.txt (core dep), so this is NOT an undeclared dependency — confirmed only as a descriptive note that a deep import raises raw ModuleNotFoundError when the optional google stack is absent. Finding self-rates info.
representative.rationale: Dependency is declared and the lazy boundary works as designed; finding is an accurate info-level boundary note, not a defect.

representative.title: Direct dict indexing on profile fields can KeyError; assumes keys always present
representative.evidence: Line 13: `total_msgs = profile['messagesTotal']` and line 22: `print(f"📨 Email Address: {profile['emailAddress']}")` both use direct dict indexing on the getProfile() response. No .get() guard, no try/except. A missing key (or an error-shaped response) raises KeyError and crashes check_health().
representative.rationale: Claim accurately describes the code; both fields are normally present in a Gmail getProfile response, so the failure mode is narrow, and this is a read-only diagnostic tool — info severity is appropriate.

representative.title: Health-check batch callback uses parameter name 'id' shadowing builtin and drops errors silently
representative.evidence: Lines 32-34: `def cb(id, resp, exc):` / `if not exc:` / `label_stats[resp['name']] = resp`. The param `id` shadows the builtin (cosmetic). When `exc` is truthy the label is silently skipped — no log, no print — so the unread report can be incomplete with no indication. `resp['name']` is also unguarded direct indexing.
representative.rationale: All three sub-claims are literally present in the code; impact is low because this is a read-only health report where a dropped label only yields an incomplete listing, not data corruption.

representative.location: cmd_label exit code lines 454-457 vs cmd_escalate 945-947
representative.title: apply error_count>1 returns generic exit 1 but partial-success runs with some errors are indistinguishable from total failure
representative.evidence: cli.py:455-457 cmd_label returns 2 on violation else `0 if result.error_count==0 else 1`; cli.py:945-947 cmd_escalate identical pattern. A run with 1 error out of 1000 and a run where all failed both return exit 1.
representative.rationale: Accurate observability gap: exit status conflates partial and total failure; purely a refinement for automation gating, info severity.

representative.title: Subclass dataclasses redeclare 'name' with a default while base has it without — fragile field ordering
representative.evidence: ProviderConfig (25-31) declares `name: str` with no default as its first field; subclasses (37,46,58,68) redeclare `name: str = '...'`. Verified the file imports cleanly today (no TypeError), so it works currently. The hazard is prospective: adding a no-default field to ProviderConfig after a defaulted one would trigger 'non-default argument follows default argument'.
representative.rationale: Confirmed as a latent maintainability footgun, not a present defect — current code is valid. Info severity, matching the slice's own rating.

representative.title: label_counts annotated as bare 'dict' not Dict[str,int]
representative.evidence: core/models.py:121 'label_counts: dict = field(default_factory=dict)' is a bare dict annotation; add_label_stat (line 126) does self.label_counts.get(label, 0) + 1 assuming int values. Accurate type-hygiene observation; the TypeError scenario requires a non-int value to be injected (no such path observed in-repo).
representative.rationale: True style/type-hygiene point exactly as stated; info severity appropriate — no demonstrated runtime failure path.

representative.title: add_label_stat is not thread-safe (read-modify-write on shared dict) — lost counts if apply_actions is ever parallelized
representative.evidence: core/models.py:124-126 is a non-atomic read-modify-write on a shared dict. Confirmed shared usage: base.py:343-345 and gmail.py:444-446 call result.add_label_stat per label on a single ProcessingResult, and base.py docstring (315-316) explicitly invites 'Providers with batch APIs should override for better performance'. All current paths are sequential, so today it is safe.
representative.rationale: Latent concurrency hazard correctly described and currently dormant (all loops sequential); info severity is right.

representative.title: No Content-Security-Policy meta tag; inline script/style only (defense-in-depth gap, currently low risk)
representative.evidence: File has inline <style> (line 9) and inline <script> (line 247) with no Content-Security-Policy meta tag and no security headers. No fetch(), no innerHTML, no user input — all DOM writes (textContent at line 263) use a hardcoded section id. The file is static and not served by the ./web worker.
representative.rationale: Factually accurate defense-in-depth-only observation on a static, no-input, undeployed page; correctly self-rated info.

representative.title: Broad `except Exception` swallows logout errors (acceptable) but no close of select state / no NOOP error surfacing
representative.evidence: Lines 168-171 wrap imap.logout() in bare `except Exception: pass`. The finding itself calls this acceptable for cleanup; the only substantive note is reduced observability if the connection died mid-apply.
representative.rationale: Accurate but acknowledged-acceptable cleanup pattern; informational only.

representative.title: apply_category default returns False even when CATEGORIES capability is set, with comment 'Subclasses override' — silent no-op if a subclass forgets
representative.evidence: base.py:268-270: after the CATEGORIES capability check, `return False  # Subclasses override`. A subclass advertising CATEGORIES but not overriding apply_category silently drops categories with no error/log. The one real CATEGORIES provider (Outlook) DOES override it (outlook.py:323), so no current provider hits the trap.
representative.rationale: Accurate description of a silent-no-op design trap; no live provider triggers it, so info severity is correct.

representative.title: Base batch_get_details default has no throttling and could hammer the API; not an abstractmethod so a provider can silently inherit a slow/unsafe sequential path
representative.evidence: base.py:167-172 loops get_message_details with no rate limiting; not an abstractmethod. A provider declaring BATCH_OPERATIONS but not overriding inherits the sequential path with no consistency check. Gmail (the batch-capable provider) does override (gmail.py:213). So capability/impl divergence is possible but not present in shipped code.
representative.rationale: Correct maintainability observation about capability-vs-implementation divergence; no current provider is affected, so info severity holds.

representative.title: IMAP star() ignores capability gating and always attempts \\Flagged even when STAR capability absent (non-gmail mode)
representative.evidence: __init__ adds STAR capability only `if use_gmail_extensions` (95-98). star() (328-335) and unstar() (337-344) override the base and unconditionally issue `uid('STORE', id, '+FLAGS', r'(\Flagged)')` regardless of capability, bypassing base.star's `if not (self.capabilities & STAR): return False` guard (base.py 230-232).
representative.rationale: Genuine declared-capability-vs-behavior inconsistency: a caller told STAR is unsupported (non-gmail mode) can still successfully star; harmless in practice (generic IMAP supports \Flagged), hence info.

representative.title: Redirect test accepts any 3xx and only checks Location header, not that the dashboard is actually reachable via the redirect
representative.evidence: tests/test_web.py:18-20 asserts `r.status_code in (301, 302, 307, 308)` and `r.headers['location'] == '/app/'` with `follow_redirects=False`, never following through to confirm /app/ returns 200 in the same flow. api/app.py:159-160 returns `RedirectResponse(url='/app/')` (FastAPI default 307), so a regression changing the status code to e.g. 301 would still pass the broad tuple assertion.
representative.rationale: Accurate test-quality observation: the assertion accepts any of four 3xx codes and only checks the Location header, so it cannot detect a semantically-different-but-still-3xx redirect; correctly self-scoped as info severity, not a runtime defect.

representative.title: Reads run inside whatever transaction the shared connection currently holds, so a read can observe an uncommitted in-flight writer's data or a stale snapshot
