representative.evidence: All reads use self._conn (the same connection writes use), serialized by self._lock (426,435). No write method calls a _fetch_* helper between its INSERT and commit, so the 'read partially-applied state' window is not exercised by any current code path.
representative.rationale: Technically true that reads share the writer's connection/transaction, but the RLock fully serializes calls and no method interleaves a read between write statements, so there is no observable defect today. The docstring's 'WAL keeps reads concurrent' is aspirational, not load-bearing. Downgrade to info — architectural note, not a bug.

representative.title: Outlook due-date strftime hard-codes 'Z' (UTC) and zeroes the clock, mislabeling naive/local or non-UTC due_date at the wrong UTC date
representative.evidence: Line 574 does emit `due_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z")` with timeZone UTC, ignoring tzinfo. BUT a repo-wide grep shows action.due_date is NEVER assigned anywhere in cli.py or core/ (only declared default None in models.py:93 and merged in :107). base.apply_actions:357 always calls star(due_date=action.due_date) = None. The `if due_date:` branch (572) is dead code.
representative.rationale: The strftime/tz bug is technically real but unreachable in the current codebase since no caller ever populates due_date — downgraded to info. corroborated=false echoed.

representative.title: Legacy labeler start/finish banners stamp naive local time while audit.py stamps UTC — same local-vs-UTC inconsistency as state.py
representative.evidence: Confirmed: lines 390 and 472 use naive datetime.now().strftime(...); core/audit.py:198 uses datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(). BUT gmail_labeler_legacy.py does NOT import or call audit.py at all - these are console print banners, not entries written to the same receipt log. The 'mixed in one run log, cross-correlation wrong by UTC offset' premise does not hold because the two timestamps never land in the same artifact.
representative.rationale: The naive-vs-UTC observation is factually true but the stated impact (mixing in a run log with audit receipts) is incorrect for this file - these are ephemeral console banners. Downgraded low->info; cosmetic.

representative.title: README documents the non-standard ~/System/Logs path, propagating the convention error rather than ~/Library/Logs
representative.evidence: README.md:261 'Create the log directory at ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/', :544 'logs written to ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/', :556 'tail -f ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/launchd.stdout.log'. plist:15,17 use /Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/. deploy.sh:11 LOG_DIR="$HOME/System/Logs/mail_automation". So README accurately documents the code's actual (non-standard) path.
representative.rationale: Real convention nit — ~/System/Logs is non-standard vs ~/Library/Logs — but README is faithfully documenting the code, not introducing the error; the 'grows unbounded' harm is minor/speculative, so info not low.

representative.title: Batch callback registered twice (on the batch AND per-request) causing each callback to fire twice — double-counting
representative.evidence: Line 101: batch = service.new_batch_http_request(callback=callback); line 103: batch.add(..., callback=callback). In google-api-python-client the per-request callback supersedes the batch-level one, so each callback fires exactly once. No double-counting occurs at runtime.
representative.rationale: Finding itself concedes it fires once; this is redundant/misleading code, not a defect that double-counts. Downgraded to info.

representative.title: Public package API omits names that consumers actually use (apply_vip_senders_from_config, AuditLog, AuditInvariantError)
representative.evidence: Facts confirmed: apply_vip_senders_from_config is defined at core/config.py:300 and imported via deep path `from core.config import load_config, apply_vip_senders_from_config` at cli.py:44 (used at lines 423/524/719/829). core/audit.py exists with class AuditInvariantError (line 69) and class AuditLog (line 117); api/service.py:21 does `from core.audit import AuditInvariantError, AuditLog`, and cli.py:24,367 likewise. None of these three names appear in core/__init__.py's import block or __all__ (lines 42-78). So the re-export gap is real.
representative.rationale: Real omission but overstated as a 'defect': deep imports work correctly, nothing crashes, and core.audit is plausibly an intentionally separate surface; this is a curation/style observation, not a bug — adjusted to info.

representative.title: Gmail-override safety tests stub _execute_with_backoff/_drop_if_protected so the override path is partly mocked away
representative.evidence: test_audit.py:289-290 stub gp.ensure_label_exists = lambda label: label and gp._execute_with_backoff = lambda fn, _desc: fn(); line 333-336 do the same plus gp._drop_if_protected = lambda action: False. Production _execute_with_backoff (providers/gmail.py:97-126, real retry/backoff loop) and real ensure_label_exists (gmail.py:344-369) are indeed not exercised by THESE two tests. The stub signature (fn, _desc) matches the real 2-positional-arg signature, so the stub is faithful.
representative.rationale: Accurate coverage observation, but no production defect — the stubbing is necessary to avoid live API calls and the finding itself self-classifies as 'coverage note rather than a definite defect'; framing it as actionable overstates it, hence partial/info.

representative.title: Limit input max=1000 is not enforced before sending to the unauthenticated preview endpoint
representative.evidence: Line 838 sends `parseInt($("limit").value,10) || 50` with no clamp to the max=1000 declared at line 594 — confirmed no client-side cap. But the server enforces the real bound: schemas.py TriageRequest.limit = Field(..., ge=1, le=MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT=1000) (schemas.py:16,47), so an over-large value is rejected (422) by FastAPI, and worker.mjs caps internally (triagePreview Math.* on Number(limit)). The client offers no mitigation but the server is the authoritative gate.
representative.rationale: Real that the HTML max is advisory and unclamped, but the unmetered-cost concern is mitigated server-side by the le=1000 schema bound; client-only nit, info severity.

representative.location: lines 16-21 (agentic_commerce.spec_version "2026-04-17") and product_feed.json line 1 ("version": "2026-04-17") vs acp/payment.py STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION line 28 ("2026-04-22.preview")
representative.title: Advertised ACP spec_version (2026-04-17) trails the Stripe SPT preview version the charge path actually requires (2026-04-22.preview)
representative.evidence: agent.json:17 `"spec_version": "2026-04-17"` and product_feed.json:2 `"version": "2026-04-17"` match acp/__init__.py:19 API_VERSION (enforced exactly by acp/router.py:74 `_gate`). acp/payment.py:28 `STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION = "2026-04-22.preview"` is used only at payment.py:86 as the server-side `stripe_version` option on the internal PaymentIntent create. The two version numbers are real and distinct.
representative.rationale: Both facts verified, but the harm theory is overstated: the Stripe SPT preview version is an internal server-side header on a delegated-token PaymentIntent the agent never constructs; it is not part of the ACP 2026-04-17 checkout spec the manifest advertises (acp/__init__.py docstring explicitly separates the two axes), so no agent-buildable token shape depends on it. No public-surface conflation harm.

representative.location: lines 22-28 (api block + oauth_scopes: []) vs api/app.py triage/preview/audit endpoints which require no auth (and acp surfaces accepting any bearer)
representative.title: agent.json declares oauth_scopes [] and no auth requirement, faithfully mirroring an API that enforces no auth — the manifest normalizes the unauthenticated surface
representative.evidence: agent.json:28 `"oauth_scopes": []`. grep for `Depends|Bearer|Authorization|require_` in api/app.py returns nothing — /v1/triage (app.py:91), /v1/triage/preview (:85), /v1/senders/check (:79), GET /v1/audit/{run_id} require no auth. acp/router.py:80-83 `_gate` accepts ANY non-empty bearer without validating it against any store. The manifest accurately mirrors this unauthenticated contract.
representative.rationale: The finding concedes the manifest is 'technically consistent with the code'; agent.json correctly describes the real (unauthenticated) surface, so this is not a defect IN the JSON file but a restatement of a code-side auth concern that lives in api/app.py and acp/router.py — out of scope for this artifact.

representative.title: Credential write-back has no audit/success logging, so a wrong-field or wrong-vault write is undetectable
representative.evidence: Confirmed op_item_edit returns None with no success log (lines 94-117) and store_json_secret returns silently (lines 263/272). No INFO confirmation of which item/field/vault was written. However store_json_secret is dead code (U446); the live write path is gmail_auth.store_token_info, which also lacks success logging.
representative.rationale: Observability gap is real but minor and sits partly on dead code; pure enhancement suggestion, info-level.

representative.location: is_resumable, lines 131-133 vs get_token usage in cli.py:172
representative.title: is_resumable only null-checks the token; an empty-string or non-string token is reported resumable
representative.evidence: core/state.py:133 `return self.state.get("next_page_token") is not None`. An empty-string token would report resumable. But save() stores whatever page_token the provider returns; providers return None at end-of-pages (gmail_labeler.py:308 passes None explicitly), not ''. No evidence any provider emits '' as a cursor.
representative.rationale: The '' edge case is speculative — no code path is shown to store an empty-string token. A minor correctness smell at most; info.

representative.title: Unwritable-audit tests rely on directory-as-path raising, an OS/behavior-specific trigger
representative.evidence: test lines 449-451 / 460-462 mkdir() a path then pass it as AuditLog(path=...); core/audit.py:224-226 `with open(self.path, 'a' ...) ... except OSError`. On POSIX open(dir,'a') raises IsADirectoryError (OSError subclass) so the catch fires; the tests assert degradation indirectly via `log.write_error is not None` (lines 454/472), not the specific exception class. Brittleness claim is factually correct.
representative.rationale: Accurate description of an OS-behavior-dependent fault-injection trigger; works on this POSIX environment and is a deliberate test-design choice, not a defect — a test-quality nit, info severity.

representative.location: imports line 23 (re) and line 22 (email) unused; stats dict not defaultdict
representative.title: Minor: unused imports and re-decode of UID per fetch
representative.evidence: import re (line 23) is unused — grep confirms 're.' appears nowhere in the file. But 'email' (line 21, not 22) IS used: line 84 email.message_from_bytes(...). The claimed double uid.decode() is real: line 150 and line 156 each call uid.decode() for the same uid. stats uses stats.get(target,0)+1 (line 152), not a defaultdict (cosmetic).
representative.rationale: Mixed: 're' unused and double-decode are correct, but the title's claim that 'email' is unused is wrong (used at line 84). Cosmetic/info only.

representative.title: DB path default read from environment at module import time
representative.evidence: store.py:39 does read os.environ.get('MAIL_DB_PATH', 'data/app.db') at module import. However get_store() at store.py:451 re-reads os.environ.get('MAIL_DB_PATH', DEFAULT_DB_PATH) at first-use, so the live override IS honored; only the literal 'data/app.db' fallback is captured at import, which never changes. The finding itself states 'the practical effect is benign here'.
representative.rationale: Factually true that there is an import-time env read, but it is redundant/harmless because the runtime read in get_store() honors the live env — a cosmetic nit, correctly rated info.

representative.title: set -e plus `source` of op env file aborts whole run if any `op read` fails
representative.evidence: The real sourced file ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh wraps every `op read` substitution with `... 2>/dev/null) || true` (lines 26-28) and guards the whole block with `if command -v op &>/dev/null; then ... fi`. A failing `op read` therefore returns 0 via `|| true` and does NOT trip `set -e`. The finding's premise (an unguarded op lookup aborting the run at the source line) does not hold against the actual env file.
representative.rationale: The env file already defends against set -e abort with `|| true` on each op read; no unguarded failure path exists as described.

representative.title: Headline trust claim ('signed audit receipt', 'never archives government/financial/legal/platform mail') contradicts the live worker.mjs
representative.evidence: The finding compares this llms.txt against cloudflare/worker.mjs, but this static llms.txt is generated from api/well_known.py (build_llms_txt) and served by the FastAPI app — NOT the worker. On the FastAPI surface the headline claims are accurate: api/receipts.py implements a real HMAC-SHA256 signed, re-derivable receipt (sign/verify at lines 66-74, SIGNATURE_ALGORITHM='HMAC-SHA256'), and the gate is genuinely fail-closed — api/app.py:112-122 raises HTTP 500 'SAFETY GATE VIOLATION' on service.AuditInvariantError, never 200. worker.mjs is a separate 'share demo' that serves its OWN shorter /llms.txt (worker.mjs:307-318), so its hardcoded receipt does not describe this file's advertised surface.
representative.rationale: Mis-attributes the demo worker's behavior to the FastAPI surface that actually emits/serves this llms.txt, where the signed-receipt and fail-closed-gate claims are true.

representative.title: MCP destructive/readonly hint assertions silently skip on the Python 3.9 CI floor
representative.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 sets `python-version: ["3.11", "3.12"]` (no 3.9 lane), and line 30 runs `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt` (requirements-mcp.txt contains `mcp>=1.2,<2`). So `mcp` is installed in every CI lane and `pytest.importorskip("mcp")` at test_mcp.py:11 does NOT skip; the safety assertions at lines 39-41 (readOnlyHint/destructiveHint) and 34 (outputSchema) execute on every CI run. mcp_server/server.py:87/97/109 confirm the annotations being asserted exist.
representative.rationale: The claimed '3.9 floor that silently skips the safety assertions' does not exist in CI — the matrix is 3.11/3.12 and CI explicitly installs the mcp SDK, so these assertions run every build; the 3.9 mention is only a docstring rationale for dep isolation, not a CI lane.

representative.title: DEPLOY.md Fly.io instructions are generic and unverifiable; no fly.toml ships and secrets list is Gmail-only
representative.evidence: DEPLOY.md:29-33 Fly.io block sets only GMAIL_OAUTH_OP_REF/GMAIL_TOKEN_OP_REF, but lines 52-54 explicitly document all commerce/agent surfaces as fail-soft ('absent the relevant secret, the catalog/pure endpoints still work and the money/charge endpoints return a clean 503 billing is not configured'). The full secrets table is present in the SAME file at lines 56-65, and the Credentials section (35) and Commerce section (50-65) both follow the Fly.io block.
representative.rationale: Documented intended fail-soft behavior with the secrets table in the same doc; the 'no pointer back' claim misreads the file. Not a defect.

representative.title: Provider capability matrix claims Gmail batch 'Yes (1000/batch)' but text elsewhere references batchModify 1000 — verify against gmail.py batch size
representative.evidence: providers/gmail.py:31 'BATCH_MODIFY_SIZE = 1000   # Max IDs per batchModify (API limit)', applied at :452 'for i in range(0, len(msg_ids), BATCH_MODIFY_SIZE)' / :453 chunk. README:488 'Batch operations: Yes (1000/batch)' and :493 'up to 1,000 messages in a single API call' match the code exactly.
representative.rationale: The numeric claim flagged as unverified is verified CORRECT against the provider constant; the documentation is accurate, so there is no defect.

representative.title: Sender/subject sent to API with no client-side length bound
representative.evidence: Line 807 posts `{ sender, subject: $("subject").value }` with no client length cap — true. But the claim that 'SenderCheckRequest's subject/sender bounds are not enforced' is wrong: schemas.py:29-30 sets sender = Field(max_length=4096) and subject = Field(default="", max_length=4096), so the FastAPI backend rejects oversized input (422). The worker reads body.sender without a length issue (pure string ops). The authoritative server bound exists.
representative.rationale: The premise that the server lacks sender/subject bounds is false — schemas.py enforces max_length=4096 on both; the client simply lacks a redundant pre-check, which is immaterial.

findings.title: Triage endpoints have NO authentication and never enforce plan entitlements or consume credits
findings.evidence: grep for Depends/Authorization/middleware/HTTPBearer/api_key in api/app.py returns exit 1 (zero matches). TriageRequest (api/schemas.py:39-52) has no api_key/account field. _run (app.py:97-130) calls service.run_triage directly with no account lookup. store.consume_credit (store.py:235) and plans.entitlements_for (plans.py:175) have ZERO runtime callers — only tests and the definition site. triage() honors req.dry_run, so dry_run=False mutates the configured mailbox unauthenticated.
findings.rationale: Triage path has no auth and never debits credits or checks the monthly cap; both halves verified against current code.

findings.title: Triage endpoints enforce NO entitlement / quota / credit debit — paid credits are never consumed
findings.evidence: consume_credit only at store.py:235 (+tests); entitlements_for only at plans.py:175; monthly_run_cap is only a dataclass field (plans.py:37/74/92/111) never read at runtime; scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py is the only non-test consumer (artifact generation, not the run path). acp/router.py:287 credits run_credits via fulfill_once, but nothing decrements it on a triage. MCP _triage (mcp_server/server.py:129) also calls service.run_triage with no metering.
findings.rationale: Credits are minted (ACP) but never spent and the cap is never enforced; the metered product is dispensed free and unbounded.

findings.title: POST /v1/triage has no authentication or authorization
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U001: no Depends/HTTPBearer/Authorization on /v1/triage (grep exit 1), req.dry_run controllable so dry_run=False mutates the mailbox unauthenticated, no per-account attribution. This is the authz half of the same defect.
findings.rationale: Identical defect (no auth on the live triage endpoint) already captured by U001.

findings.title: Triage execution path NEVER enforces monthly_run_cap, run_credits, or plan entitlements — paid limits and purchased credits are sold but unenforced
findings.evidence: service.run_triage(service.py:70-135) takes provider/query/limit/dry_run/etc and NO account_id/api_key; it builds a provider, runs the labeler, asserts no gate violations, and returns — never touching credits/caps. app._run (app.py:97-130) and mcp_server/server.py:_triage (129-146) both call service.run_triage with the same signature; neither passes nor looks up an account. grep confirms enforcement primitives have no live callers: `consume_credit` appears only at api/store.py:235 (def) and in tests/test_store.py; `entitlements_for` appears only at api/plans.py:175 (def) — zero callers in api/, mcp_server/, acp/; `monthly_run_cap` is referenced only for display (scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:46, api/plans.py catalog rows 37/58/74/92/111/189) and never as a runtime check. Conversely credits ARE sold/minted: acp/router.py:290 calls store.fulfill_once -> api/store.py:365/384 UPDATE accounts SET run_credits = run_credits + ?. So real-money ACP credit packs add run_credits but the consuming triage loop never debits them and never enforces the Free 50 / Pro 5000 caps.
findings.rationale: Verified: all three triage entry points lack any account lookup, credit debit, or run-cap check; consume_credit/entitlements_for have no production callers while fulfill_once does mint paid credits — the metered/credit/cap monetization model is genuinely unenforced (unlimited free triage; purchased credits never consumed).

findings.title: Protected-sender gate completely bypassed: every inbox message >90 days is archived with zero sender check
findings.evidence: Lines 15-21 of archive_old_inbox.applescript move EVERY inbox message with `date received of msg < cutoffDate` to targetMailbox, keyed solely on age (line 17-18). There is no is_protected_sender call, no PROTECTED_SENDERS check, no audit, no dry-run. core/rules.py defines is_protected_sender (L787), _gov_protected (L652), _is_protected_domain (L666); core/audit.py docstring (verified lines 1-9) states the headline guarantee 'a protected sender ... is NEVER archived or moved out of the inbox', enforced by AuditInvariantError. CLAUDE.md L181 and AGENTS.md L17 (`osascript archive_old_inbox.applescript`) ship this script as a supported tool.
findings.rationale: A shipped, documented tool unconditionally archives 91-day-old mail from lawyers/banks/.gov/self, directly violating the product's stated protected-sender guarantee with no gate or audit backstop; data-loss class, critical confirmed.

findings.title: COPY+DELETE fallback expunges the ENTIRE mailbox per message, deleting any other \Deleted-flagged messages
findings.evidence: Line 76 calls `imap.expunge()`; Python's imaplib signature is `IMAP4.expunge(self)` (verified, no UID arg) -> mailbox-wide EXPUNGE that purges ALL \Deleted-flagged messages in the selected INBOX, not just this uid. Called once per archivable uid inside the apply loop (lines 161-165), so O(n) full expunges. The COPY+STORE+expunge fallback (72-77) fires only when UID MOVE returns non-OK or raises imaplib.IMAP4.error (67-71).
findings.rationale: Genuine unscoped-EXPUNGE data-loss path; downgraded critical->high because it only triggers when the server lacks RFC 6851 MOVE (iCloud, the primary target, supports MOVE) AND unrelated \Deleted mail already exists in INBOX.

findings.title: CI never exercises the Python 3.9 (or 3.10) floor the codebase is architected around
findings.evidence: ci.yml:15 matrix is ["3.11","3.12"]; 3.9 floor is documented in CLAUDE.md:278 ('Python 3.9+'), Dockerfile:6-7 ('core stays 3.9-importable without it'), requirements-mcp.txt:2 ('mcp SDK requires >=3.10... core stays importable on 3.9+'), api/app.py:33-46 lazy MCP import 'core API stays importable on 3.9', tests/test_mcp.py:11 pytest.importorskip('mcp'). core/rules.py:1163 does use Optional["datetime"] (string-quoted forward ref, no `from __future__ import annotations`).
findings.rationale: Real gap: the advertised 3.9 (and 3.10) import path is never executed in CI; the matrix only runs 3.11/3.12. Severity trimmed to medium since the 3.9 'support' is a soft/secondary claim (the top README badge actually says 3.10+), not a hard product contract.

findings.title: .gitignore does not cover *_state.json or mail_export.tsv, so runtime mailbox state will be committed on the next run
findings.evidence: git check-ignore returns NOT IGNORED for labeler_state.json, gmail_state.json, outlook_state.json, imap_state.json, mailapp_state.json, mail_export.tsv. .gitignore (37 lines) has no *_state.json or mail_export.tsv pattern. core/config.py:39/48/60/70 default each provider state_file to '<provider>_state.json'; gmail_labeler.py:40 STATE_FILE='labeler_state.json' -> StateManager(STATE_FILE) at line 71; core/state.py:92 opens self.filename in CWD (repo root) and json.dump writes it. labeler_state.json IS ALREADY TRACKED (git ls-files) and contains real per-category counts (total_processed=36470, real label histogram). mail_export.tsv is also tracked.
findings.rationale: Real coverage gap: provider state files write to repo root and are not git-ignored; labeler_state.json is already committed with real mailbox category counts. Severity adjusted high->medium: leaked data is aggregate label counts (counts, not addresses/subjects); the tracked mail_export.tsv currently holds only the header row (no sender PII rows committed yet), so present blast radius is metadata, not raw PII contents.

findings.title: Docker build copies entire repo (no .dockerignore) — bakes data/app.db (API keys, Stripe customer ids) and local secrets into the image
findings.evidence: Dockerfile line 12 is `COPY . .` with no .dockerignore (`ls .dockerignore` -> No such file or directory). The build context working tree contains gitignored sensitive files that COPY . . would bake into image layers: data/ (state_store.db dir present; store.py:38 documents data/app.db as holding 'customer ids and api keys'), config/protected_senders.local.txt (PII, gitignored per .gitignore comment 'real lawyer/banks/gov/self'), and multiple *.log files (gmail_labeler.log 3.8MB, auto_drain.log/auto_drain_run.log ~1MB each). git check-ignore confirms data/state_store.db, gmail_labeler.log, config/protected_senders.local.txt are all gitignored — so git-tracking protects the repo but Docker COPY ignores .gitignore entirely.
findings.rationale: Accurate: no .dockerignore, COPY . ., and the named secret/PII files genuinely exist in the build context; high severity holds for an autoDeploy:true docker service shipping API keys/PII into registry layers.

findings.title: Fulfillment credits the account resolved from the request's bearer api_key, not the buyer who paid / the session's original account — credits can be granted to the wrong account
findings.evidence: router.py:188 _persist(session_id, resp, total_runs) is called with no account_id (defaults None), so create_session never binds the session to a key. router.py:287-289 complete_session resolves account = store.get_account_by_api_key(ctx.api_key) and create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key) on miss; _gate (79-83) accepts any non-empty Bearer. So whoever calls /complete is credited, not the creator/payer. The body's payment_data.token (264) is charged independently. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Code exactly matches: unbound session + caller-derived credit target + unvalidated bearer. Real authorization defect.

findings.title: Bearer token is accepted without ANY validation against the account store — auth is effectively unauthenticated
findings.evidence: _gate only checks auth.startswith('Bearer ') and non-empty remainder (router.py:80-83); never calls get_account_by_api_key. complete_session auto-creates an account for any unknown key (router.py:289).
findings.rationale: Accurate, but identical root cause to U016 which states it more completely (incl. blast-radius cap).

findings.title: Idempotency key is not namespaced by api_key (Authorization), enabling cross-account replay / confused-deputy disclosure
findings.evidence: _begin_idempotency calls store.idempotency_begin(ctx.idempotency_key, scope, _request_hash(raw)) (router.py:104-106). store.py:313-353 keys the idempotency_keys table on `key` (PRIMARY KEY) with scope+request_hash only — api_key is never part of the key or stored. On a same-key/same-body collision from a different caller, store.py:350-353 returns the first caller's response_json verbatim, which (after completion) contains buyer PII and the order permalink (router.py:318-322).
findings.rationale: Confirmed cross-account replay/disclosure: idempotency scope omits the credential. Distinct mechanism (replay store) from the bearer-validation cluster.

findings.title: Credits are fulfilled to whoever calls /complete, not to the account that owns/created the session; bearer token is never validated
findings.evidence: create_session/_persist pass account_id=None (router.py:188). complete_session: account = store.get_account_by_api_key(ctx.api_key); if None -> create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key, plan='free') (287-289); fulfill_once(session_id, account['id'], total_runs) (290). _gate (79-83) only checks 'Bearer ' prefix + non-empty. So session created by token A can be completed/credited by token B; any attacker string becomes a credited account. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Most complete statement of the unbound-session + caller-credit defect; code confirms every step.

findings.title: No ownership check on reading ACP sessions or audit receipts — any valid bearer token reads any session/receipt
findings.evidence: GET /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} (router.py:193-197) calls _gate(require_idempotency=False) then returns row['data']['response'] for any id — no ownership comparison. Response includes buyer name/email/phone (models.Buyer, models.py:30-33) and order.payment_id after completion (router.py:304-307,318-322). receipts.py:114-115 GET /v1/audit/{run_id} has NO gate at all (no auth dependency); returns any receipt by id incl. summary with payment_id/amount/checkout_session_id (router.py:300-308).
findings.rationale: IDOR/BOLA confirmed on both surfaces; /v1/audit is fully unauthenticated. Ids are unguessable (token_hex) so it is obscurity-mitigated, but no authz binding exists.

findings.title: ACP fulfillment credits a free auto-created account but those credits gate nothing downstream
findings.evidence: Charge at router.py:262, fulfill_once credits run_credits at 290. grep confirms consume_credit() has zero non-test callers and entitlements_for() has zero callers outside its own def (api/plans.py:175). api/app.py _run (97-130) and mcp_server/server.py _triage (129-146) call service.run_triage with no identity and never debit credits. So the purchased balance gates nothing.
findings.rationale: Charge-for-unenforced-entitlement confirmed; the money-in side exists, the money-honored side is absent.

findings.title: ACP bearer token is never validated against the account store; any attacker-chosen bearer auto-provisions an account and is credited on a successful charge
findings.evidence: _gate returns GateContext(api_key=auth[len('Bearer '):].strip()) for any non-empty string, no accounts lookup (79-93). complete_session get_account_by_api_key then create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key, plan='free') on miss (287-289). new_api_key issues uma_+token_urlsafe(32) (store.py:110-112) — the only legitimate issuance path — but it is never required. Blast-radius note verified: consume_credit zero non-test callers; /v1/triage (app.py:91-130) no auth/no debit. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Canonical, most complete statement of the unvalidated-bearer/auto-provision defect including the accurate blast-radius cap. Confirmed.

findings.title: No ownership check on retrieve/update/cancel — any valid-format bearer can read, mutate, or cancel ANY checkout session by id (IDOR)
findings.evidence: _load(session_id) (158-162) fetches by id only, never compares acp_sessions.account_id or ctx.api_key. get_session returns full response; update_session (200-232) mutates buyer/items; cancel_session (342-359) flips status to canceled — all for any caller passing _gate. No per-account scoping anywhere.
findings.rationale: Read/mutate/cancel IDOR confirmed across all three endpoints. Distinct from U014 (which also covers the no-auth /v1/audit receipt path), but the session-IDOR portion overlaps; kept separate as it adds update/cancel mutation.

findings.title: Credit applied before receipt minted: receipt failure loses the order permanently while returning 200 COMPLETED
findings.evidence: Ordering is charge (262) -> fulfill_once credits atomically (290) -> sign + save_receipt (312-317) -> _persist (337) -> _complete_idempotency (338). No try/except. If sign()/save_receipt() raises after fulfill_once returned True, the credit is applied but the request 500s and idempotency stays 'processing'. A later retry: Stripe dedups the charge, fulfill_once returns False, control reaches the `elif order is None` branch (323-330) producing HTTP 200 status=completed with NO order/permalink and an 'already_fulfilled' message. The signed order receipt is lost.
findings.rationale: The crash-after-credit-before-receipt window and its 2xx-no-order replay outcome are real in this exact control flow. NOTE: save_receipt itself does not wrap in try/except here (unlike receipts.persist), so it can propagate.

findings.location: complete_session lines 286-290; api/store.py new_api_key line 110; store.py get_account_by_api_key 170-175
findings.title: Two distinct ACP callers presenting the same Bearer string share one credit balance and can drain each other
findings.evidence: accounts.api_key is UNIQUE (store.py:50). get_account_by_api_key matches on the raw bearer (170-175). Two callers both sending 'Bearer x' resolve to the same single account row, pooling credits into one balance. No per-tenant namespacing.
findings.rationale: Mechanically real and distinct from misattribution (it is balance-pooling). Downgraded to medium: requires two parties to deliberately share an identical bearer literal AND, per U015/U020, the pooled credits currently buy nothing, capping real-world impact.

findings.location: complete_session lines 286-290; api/app.py _run 97-130; mcp_server/server.py _triage 129-146; absence of any consume_credit caller
findings.title: ACP-purchased run credits are unredeemable: no path reads, consumes, or authenticates against run_credits
findings.evidence: fulfill_once/add_credits increment accounts.run_credits (store.py:365-389,223-233) but grep shows consume_credit has zero non-test callers and entitlements_for zero callers outside its def. _run (app.py:97-130) and _triage (server.py:129-146) take no identity and never debit. No balance-read endpoint exists.
findings.rationale: Confirmed; same underlying 'credits redeem nowhere' fact as U015/U021 but framed as the closed ACP loop. Shares root with U015.

findings.location: complete_session fulfillment lines 282-290 + 337 (no cap/plan consequence of credits)
findings.title: ACP fulfillment credits run_credits and binds the account, but the credited balance gates nothing — purchased reach is non-spendable on the only execution surfaces
findings.evidence: complete_session credits via fulfill_once (290) and persists account_id on the session (337). No execution surface debits run_credits (verified: run_triage in service.py/app.py/server.py take no account and never call consume_credit/entitlements_for).
findings.rationale: Same defect as U015/U020 — purchased credits are inert because no consumption wiring exists. Duplicate.

findings.title: No invariant reconciling charged amount (grand_total of stored line_items) against credited total_runs at /complete
findings.evidence: amount = models.grand_total(current['line_items']) (257) and total_runs = row['data']['total_runs'] (246) are read from the stored response; _persist writes them together (154). create_session/update_session always derive both from the same build_line_items(...) call against plans.CREDIT_PACKS (router.py:175,219-220 / 174), so they stay in lock-step on every write path that exists. There is no /complete-time re-derivation, true.
findings.rationale: The 'no reconciliation invariant at /complete' observation is accurate, but every CURRENT write keeps amount and runs coupled; divergence requires a hypothetical future endpoint, manual DB edit, or partial migration. The cited update else-branch (223-224) reads line_items and total_runs from the same row['data'] dict, so it does NOT actually diverge. Real defense-in-depth gap, but no exploitable inconsistency in current code -> overstated severity.

findings.title: /v1/triage and /v1/triage/preview have NO authentication and NO metering — entire paid model unenforced
findings.evidence: Combines U001 (no auth) + U002 (no metering). Confirms 'store never imported' claim: grep 'store' in api/app.py, api/service.py, mcp_server/server.py returns exit 1. TriageRequest has no api_key/account field (schemas.py:39-52). All claims verified, but this is the same auth+metering gap as U001/U002.
findings.rationale: Same whole-subsystem money-path gap as U002; severity actually critical (finder under-rated it 'high'), but it duplicates U002. Upgrading adjusted_severity to match real blast radius.

findings.title: Webhook marks event processed in a separate, non-atomic step after the handler — crash or concurrent redelivery can double-grant or drop the event
findings.evidence: billing.py:191 is_event_processed read, :196 _handle_event, :203 mark_event_processed with return discarded. Same check-then-act defect as U025/U026/U271. store.py:249-260 mark_event_processed IS the atomic INSERT-OR-IGNORE primitive whose bool is discarded.
findings.rationale: Real defect but identical root-cause to U025; the second half (crash-before-mark leaving event unrecorded) is the intended retry behavior tested by test_webhook_handler_failure_not_marked_and_redelivery_grants (test_billing.py:88).

findings.title: Webhook idempotency is check-then-act (TOCTOU) — concurrent redeliveries can double-process / double-grant
findings.evidence: billing.py:191 `if store.is_event_processed(event_id): return ...` (read), :196 handler, :203 `store.mark_event_processed(...)` return value discarded. store.py:249-260 returns True only for first INSERT-OR-IGNORE writer — designed as an atomic claim but used as a post-hoc write, not the gate. The three calls are not wrapped in one transaction (store uses one lock per call, store.py:217).
findings.rationale: Genuine TOCTOU: two concurrent redeliveries can both pass the read and both run _handle_event. Severity downgraded high->medium: set_subscription is idempotent and entitlements ignore plan/status entirely (entitlements_for has zero callers), so double-processing produces near-identical state; the orphan-create path (U028) is the only non-idempotent side effect and requires both racers to miss resolution.

findings.title: Webhook idempotency is a non-atomic check-then-act; concurrent redeliveries can double-grant
findings.evidence: Same lines 191/196/203 as U025. Correctly cites test_webhook_handler_failure_not_marked (test_billing.py:88, confirmed present).
findings.rationale: Identical root-cause and remediation to U025.

findings.title: Stripe subscription state is recorded but never enforced — plan/status writes have no runtime effect on triage
findings.evidence: grep across the entire repo: entitlements_for is defined at plans.py:175 and referenced ONLY in docs/session transcripts — zero application callers. /v1/triage (app.py:91-130) calls service.run_triage with provider/query/limit only; grep of service.py+schemas.py for get_account/api_key/entitlement returns nothing. Webhook persists plan/status via set_subscription (billing.py:231,255,269,276) but no runtime reader consumes it.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: billing is a recording subsystem with no authorization consumer; the docstring claim (billing.py:21-23) that subscription status is the single source of truth is unrealized for the product's core action. Severity high stands — paid vs canceled accounts get identical triage behavior.

findings.title: Unknown-customer subscription event silently mints a brand-new orphan account with a live API key
findings.evidence: billing.py:218 `created = store.create_account(account_id=account_id, plan='free')` is the unconditional fall-through when neither metadata account_id resolves (212) nor customer maps (214-216). create_account (store.py:155-156) auto-generates a real api_key (uma_ prefix). Reached from subscription.created/updated at billing.py:247.
findings.rationale: Mechanically confirmed: an unresolvable subscription event mints a fresh account with a live api_key. Severity high->medium: the path is only reachable for a validly signed Stripe event (signature verified at billing.py:182-185), so 'unauthenticated-content' framing is overstated; real triggers are out-of-band/cross-environment customers and event reordering, not arbitrary attackers.

findings.title: entitlements_for() is dead code — defined but never called by any request path
findings.evidence: grep -rn 'entitlements_for' --include='*.py' . returns exactly one hit: the definition at api/plans.py:175. No endpoint, service, MCP tool, ACP handler, or test references it. billing.py:248-256 (_handle_event) re-implements the active/canceled downgrade independently and never calls entitlements_for; the comment at billing.py:250 ('entitlements also enforce this') is aspirational.
findings.rationale: The function is genuinely uncalled by any request path; the entitlement-resolution layer has zero runtime effect. Severity downgraded from high to medium: it is unreachable governance code, not an active mischarge — no caps are advertised-but-bypassed because no caller ever invoked enforcement.

findings.title: GET /v1/audit/{run_id} returns stored signature WITHOUT recomputing/verifying it against the stored body
findings.evidence: get_receipt (lines 122-142) reads rec via get_store().get_receipt(run_id), rebuilds body from stored columns (125-131), and returns signature: rec['signature'] verbatim (135). It never calls verify(body, rec['signature']). The verify() function exists (71-74) but is invoked nowhere server-side (grep shows only test callers). So a tampered summary_json paired with a stale signature is served as authoritative with no mismatch flag.
findings.rationale: Behavior is exactly as claimed; server never re-verifies its own ledger. Downgraded high->medium: the design intent is third-party verification (verify() is exported and clients verify), so the body+signature pair is self-checking by the consumer; the gap is the server doesn't fail-fast on its own tampered row, which is a defense-in-depth weakness, not a money-loss bug.

findings.title: Ephemeral signing key regenerated per process; previously-signed receipts become permanently unverifiable after restart/across workers
findings.evidence: _signing_key() (44-57): when RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY unset, _EPHEMERAL_KEY = secrets.token_bytes(32) per process, only logger.warning once. store.py docstring (21-24) explicitly states 'the API is a single process' — but a restart still rotates the key, so receipts persisted before restart (save_receipt wrote signature durably, store.py 287) can never re-verify. The MCP server advertises stateless_http (horizontal scaling), making cross-worker mismatch plausible.
findings.rationale: Real silent trust failure on the misconfigured (env-unset) production path; correctly described. Downgraded high->medium because it only manifests when the deploy secret is forgotten (the code warns loudly and the durable-key path is the documented production config).

findings.title: ACP order-receipt save is NOT best-effort (router calls store.save_receipt directly), so a receipts-table write failure 500s AFTER credit is applied
findings.evidence: router.py complete_session: store.fulfill_once(...) commits the credit at line 290; THEN order_id minted (296), receipts.sign() (312) and store.save_receipt() (313-317) are called with NO try/except. receipts.persist() (88-108) deliberately wraps save_receipt in 'except Exception' (106) — the ACP path bypasses persist() entirely. So a receipts-table write failure after fulfill_once raises an unhandled 500 with credit already applied, receipt missing, and idempotency not yet completed (_complete_idempotency is at 338, after save_receipt).
findings.rationale: Confirmed and corroborated: the two receipt-write call sites have opposite error semantics, and the unprotected one is on the money path post-credit-commit. High severity stands — inconsistent committed state on a paid transaction.

findings.location: consume_credit lines 235-246 (and absence of any caller)
findings.title: consume_credit is defined and atomic but never invoked, so credit balances are decorative — they are only ever added (add_credits/fulfill_once), never spent
findings.evidence: grep -rn 'consume_credit' across repo returns only api/store.py:235 (definition) + tests/test_store.py:39,42. No caller in api/ or acp/. app.py /v1/triage (lines 91-94) → _run() never reads run_credits or debits. The atomic UPDATE...WHERE run_credits>=? is correct but dead.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: consume_credit is defined, atomic, and never invoked by production; the metered-credit economy adds but never spends.

findings.title: consume_credit() is never called by any production code path — credits are sold/added but never debited
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U033: consume_credit has no production caller (only api/store.py:235 + tests/test_store.py:39,42). add_credits (223) and fulfill_once (384) only increment; router.py:290 calls fulfill_once on purchase.
findings.rationale: Identical defect to U033 (credits added/sold but never debited) at the same location; merged.

findings.location: create_account 158-164; set_subscription 217-221; fulfill_once 374-389; idempotency_begin 322-344 (no rollback anywhere in file)
findings.title: Exception mid-method leaves a dangling open write transaction on the single shared connection, poisoning all later requests
findings.evidence: Confirmed: no try/except/rollback anywhere in store.py; every write does execute()+commit() on the single shared self._conn (line 127). sqlite3 default isolation_level opens an implicit BEGIN on first DML. However sqlite3's Python wrapper auto-rolls-back a pending transaction when the NEXT statement that triggers an implicit BEGIN runs after a failed one; more importantly each method's commit() is reached on its own path and a raised exception propagates without leaving DML uncommitted (no second statement executes). The 'poisons all later requests / connection permanently corrupted' escalation overstates SQLite's behavior — a failed implicit transaction is discarded, not held across unrelated future calls.
findings.rationale: Real gap (missing explicit rollback / no `with self._conn:`) but the 'permanent connection poisoning of all later requests' claim overstates sqlite3 semantics; downgraded to medium.

findings.title: Cross-process duplicate idempotency claim raises IntegrityError (unhandled 500) because the in-process RLock cannot serialize a second process
findings.evidence: idempotency_begin (322-332) does _fetch_one_nolock(SELECT) then conditional INSERT under self._lock (RLock, per-process). _begin_idempotency in acp/router.py:104 surfaces states. The INSERT is not wrapped in try/except IntegrityError. The defect only manifests under multi-process deployment; the module docstring (lines 21-24) explicitly states 'The API is a single process' and get_store() builds one in-process singleton. No evidence the deploy runs >1 worker.
findings.rationale: Real only under an unconfirmed multi-process deployment; single-process is the documented/assumed mode, so severity downgraded to medium and corroboration is conditional.

findings.title: set_subscription silently no-ops on an unknown account_id (rowcount 0) and returns None, so a webhook success path can drop a subscription state change with no error
findings.evidence: set_subscription (191-221) issues UPDATE accounts SET ... WHERE id = ? and returns None with no rowcount check. billing.py:225-237 resolves account via _resolve_account (which creates if missing, line 218) then calls set_subscription, then billing.py:203 marks event processed. The webhook returns 200 regardless of rows affected.
findings.rationale: Confirmed silent no-op on unknown id with no caller signal; mitigated in practice because _resolve_account creates the account first, so it lowers to medium.

findings.location: new_api_key line 110 (only caller create_account line 156); no request handler
findings.title: new_api_key() mints uma_ keys but no endpoint ever issues one to a buyer — there is no authentication front door
findings.evidence: grep shows new_api_key referenced only at api/store.py:110 (def) and :156 (create_account fallback). No HTTP/MCP/ACP route returns an issued uma_ key. acp/router.py:289 stores the caller's raw bearer (ctx.api_key) as api_key. _gate (router.py:80) accepts any non-empty Bearer with no validation against the accounts table.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: issuance plumbing exists but is unwired and any non-empty bearer is a fundable identity; this is an architecture/auth design gap rather than a discrete runtime crash, so medium.

findings.location: list_receipts (lines 300-310); no caller in api/receipts.py or any router
findings.title: Retained receipt-history monetization is unwired — list_receipts() has no endpoint, so the 90-day/1-year 'retained receipt history' sold on Pro/Business cannot be delivered
findings.evidence: grep 'list_receipts' returns only api/store.py:300 (def) + tests/test_store/test_acp. api/receipts.py exposes ONLY @router.get('/v1/audit/{run_id}') → get_receipt (single by id, lines 114-122). No GET /v1/receipts or export route. plans.py advertises retained_receipt_days=90 (line 94) / 365 (line 113) and 'compliance export' (line 120), and there is no TTL/pruning of receipts.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: list_receipts has no endpoint and the advertised retained/export feature is unbuilt; a feature-gap/correctness defect rather than a crash, so medium.

findings.title: AppleScript movers duplicate destructive inbox-mutation logic outside the audited engine, with no shared gate/audit — divergent-substrate safety regression
findings.evidence: For the file under review, the asserted in-file defect ('archive_old_inbox performs inbox mutation with no core.rules.is_protected_sender, no PROTECTED_SENDERS, no core.audit.AuditLog receipt, no assert_no_violations, no dry-run') is exactly the defect already captured by U005 at lines 15-21. The cross-file generalization (route_bulk_senders.applescript, flag_important_senders.applescript sharing the same gap) is real and the architecture claim checks out — providers/mailapp.py L52-55 confirms the audited move path lives only in the Python provider, and the standalone .applescript files bypass it — but those other files are out of scope for this slice.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause as U005 for this file (unguarded inbox mutation outside the audited engine); the multi-file framing is accurate but its in-file instance is U005.

findings.title: Pagination is broken: loops re-issue the same query and break after one full page instead of paging through nextPageToken
findings.evidence: Lines 92-96: `while True: results = service.users().messages().list(userId='me', q=query, maxResults=1000).execute()` with NO pageToken anywhere (grep for nextPageToken/pageToken returns nothing). Lines 112-115: `if not archivable: if len(ids) < 1000: break; continue`. When a full page of 1000 IDs is returned and every sender is_protected (archivable empty), no batchModify runs (line 122 is skipped), the INBOX label is not removed, so the next list() returns the identical 1000 messages and the loop `continue`s forever.
findings.rationale: The all-protected full-page path is a genuine non-terminating loop because progress depends solely on batchModify shrinking the result set, which never happens when archivable is empty.

findings.title: Pagination never uses nextPageToken; relies on items leaving the query, and can infinite-loop on an all-protected full page
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U041: no pageToken (lines 94-96), and the `if not archivable / len(ids)==1000 / continue` infinite-loop path at lines 112-115. Same defect described with the same two sub-claims (re-querying page 1 and the all-protected hang).
findings.rationale: Same root-cause defect as U041 within this file; the technical claim is correct but it restates U041.

findings.title: Unbounded outer while-loop can run forever / loop indefinitely when domains keep mapping to the same Misc/Other
findings.evidence: Line 100 `while True:` breaks only at lines 111-113 when source label empty. Line 133 fetches headers only for `messages[:100]`. Lines 190-196: `if moves_performed == 0:` block ends in bare `pass` with the literal comment 'we might loop forever.' (line 192). Only `time.sleep(2)` (line 199) between iterations. If bulk-move query yields nothing (broken `from:` match) or target label missing (continue at 148-150), moves_performed stays 0 and the still-non-empty bucket is re-sampled.
findings.rationale: Real no-progress / runaway-loop hazard, self-admitted in code; severity downgraded to medium since it is a LEGACY/STANDALONE script not chained into run_automation (grep shows no automation reference) and 500-msg sampling usually does produce moves.

findings.title: Marketing keyword list contains 'off' and '%' as bare substrings, mis-classifying many domains/subjects into Marketing
findings.evidence: Line 46 KEYWORDS['Marketing'] literally contains 'off' and '%'. classify_domain lines 73-76 does `if term in combined_text: return category` (plain substring). 'off' is a substring of 'office', 'offer', 'official', 'kickoff'; '%' matches any percent sign in a subject. First-match-wins over dict order.
findings.rationale: Substring matching with 2-char/1-char tokens is a genuine over-categorization bug; downgraded from high because this only mislabels between category labels (not INBOX removal/archive) per the file header, so blast radius is misroute not data loss.

findings.title: Destructive bulk label move has NO dry-run guard and runs immediately on execution
findings.evidence: grep for argparse/--apply/--dry-run/dry_run in auto_drain.py returns nothing. `__main__` (line 201-202) calls drain_loop() directly. batchModify executes immediately (line 178). icloud_triage.py by contrast defaults to dry-run and requires --apply (icloud_triage.py:12,86,154-156). Aggressive fallback 'Notification' confirmed at line 88.
findings.rationale: Accurate: no guard exists, in contrast to a sibling script. Downgraded to medium because the operation is label-to-label moves out of Misc/Other (not destructive deletion/INBOX removal), so harm is reversible mislabeling, not data loss.

findings.location: drain_loop() line 157; bulk move query construction
findings.title: auto_drain has NO protected-sender gate on its category moves and builds Gmail queries from untrusted sender domains
findings.evidence: Lines 171-178 batchModify with addLabelIds=[target], removeLabelIds=[source] for ALL `from:{domain} label:Misc/Other` results; no is_protected_sender() call anywhere in file (grep confirms). File header lines 11-14 explicitly admits gate absence. Verified the chaining premise: archive_sorted.py ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES (lines 30-50) DOES include 'Notification' (44) and 'Marketing' (45), so a protected sender misrouted to those by auto_drain would later be archived by archive_sorted.
findings.rationale: The cross-script misroute-then-archive path is real and corroborated by archive_sorted.py; severity downgraded to medium because it is a latent chained risk (auto_drain not wired into automation; archive_sorted is a separate manual invocation) and the file header documents the known gate gap.

findings.title: Bulk move inner loop never advances pageToken; infinite loop if a batchModify silently fails to clear the query
findings.evidence: grep confirms `nextPageToken` is read (line 185) but `pageToken` is NEVER passed back to the list call (lines 161-163 have only userId/q/maxResults). Inner `while True` (159) relies solely on batchModify removing the source label. On HttpError the except (181-183) logs + sleeps 5s and does NOT break, then falls through to `if 'nextPageToken' not in search_res: break`; a permanently-failing batchModify (e.g. 400 bad label id) leaves the same page matching the query forever → infinite spin. moves_performed not incremented on failure.
findings.rationale: Confirmed broken pagination + no-progress guard on the failure path; downgraded from high to medium since it requires a persistently failing batchModify and the script is standalone/manual.

findings.title: Substring keyword matching massively over-categorizes (e.g. 'off', 'pay', 'work', 'code' match inside unrelated words)
findings.evidence: Same root mechanism as U044: lines 73-76 `if term in combined_text: return category` plus short tokens — 'pay'/'tax'/'account' (Finance/Banking, line 40), 'work'/'offer' (Professional/Jobs, line 44), 'off'/'%' (Marketing, line 46). 'pay' is substring of 'paypal'/'repayment', 'work' of 'network'/'framework'. First dict-order match wins (Finance/Banking iterated first).
findings.rationale: Real over-categorization from substring matching; this is the same defect surface as U044 (Marketing 'off'/'%') extended to other categories. Same root cause as U044.

findings.title: Pagination never advances pageToken — relies on mutation to terminate, can loop/stall or miss messages
findings.evidence: Confirmed: lines 98-102 `list(userId='me', q=rule['query'], maxResults=BATCH_SIZE)` never pass pageToken=; line 122 `if "nextPageToken" not in results: break`. The loop relies on batchModify (line 117) stripping Misc/Other to drain the query. The pagination-redundancy claim is real. BUT the infinite-loop sub-claim is overstated: every rule's `remove` is 'Misc/Other' (lines 31,37,43,...), and the query is `from:X label:Misc/Other`. If Misc/Other does not exist (remove_id None), the label-scoped query matches zero messages -> line 105-106 `if not messages: break`. The only path to non-termination requires Misc/Other to exist (so query matches) yet get_label_id to fail resolving it (case-insensitive compare at line 77 makes this implausible).
findings.rationale: Non-advancing pageToken is real and re-fetches page 1, but it converges by label-drain in the normal case and the infinite-loop premise is a narrow corner; severity inflated from high.

findings.title: Pagination never advances pageToken; loop relies on label removal to drain the query and can busy-loop if remove_id is None
findings.evidence: Same code as U049. The factual core (no pageToken carried; line 114 sends removeLabelIds=[] when remove_id is None) is confirmed. The escalation to 'infinite loop' depends on `from:X label:Misc/Other` matching messages while remove_id is None — but if Misc/Other label is absent the query returns no messages (break at 105-106), and if present, case-insensitive get_label_id (line 77) resolves it. Re-listing page 1 each pass is quota-wasteful (true).
findings.rationale: Duplicate-root with U049; real quota waste but the busy-loop claim is a narrow theoretical corner, so downgraded from high.

findings.title: Dry-run inflates success_count and processed_count by the full action list, masking nothing applied
findings.evidence: Lines 283-300: in dry-run, `actions and not dry_run` is False so the else branch runs `result.success_count += len(actions)` (291); then unconditionally `processed_this_run += len(actions)` (293), `result.processed_count += len(actions)` (294), `total_processed += len(actions)` (295), and `if state: state.save(page_token, total_processed, ...)` (299-300). So dry-run reports success_count==processed_count and DOES mutate the state file.
findings.rationale: Two distinct real defects bundled: (a) dry-run counts previewed as 'successful' in print_stats, and (b) dry-run advances/persists state. (a) is cosmetic; (b) is the real harm. Severity medium not high — only manifests when --state-file is combined with --dry-run, a narrow combination, and the persisted page-token advance is the genuinely damaging part covered by U052.

findings.title: Dry-run persists/advances state file page token, corrupting later real resume
findings.evidence: Line 299-300 `if state: state.save(page_token, total_processed, stats, provider=provider.name)` runs inside the loop body with no `not dry_run` guard; also fires in the KeyboardInterrupt (318-319) and Exception (322-323) handlers. state.save (core/state.py:84-93) overwrites next_page_token and total_processed on disk. A later real run reads get_token() (line 172) and resumes from the advanced token, skipping previewed messages.
findings.rationale: Real: dry-run is not side-effect-free when --state-file is supplied; the persisted resume cursor advances so a subsequent real run skips the pages the dry-run only previewed. Same root cause as U051(b) but is a defensible standalone finding (state-file corruption path vs counter inflation). Downgraded high->medium: requires the specific --state-file + --dry-run combination, and --state-file defaults to none.

findings.title: Live demo's protected-sender set is 3 domains vs the engine's 14+ — IRS/SSA/DocuSign/Apple/Google/Anthropic/1Password-equivalents all UNprotected on the shipped site
findings.evidence: worker.mjs line 1: PROTECTED = new Set(["courts.ca.gov","chase.com","1password.com"]) and senderCheck (82-169) only protects courts.ca.gov/.gov, chase.com, 1password.com. core/rules.py EXAMPLE_PROTECTED_SENDERS (lines 560-580) lists docusign.net, irs.gov, ssa.gov, studentaid.gov, login.gov, apple.com, appleid.com, google.com, accounts.google.com, anthropic.com, 1password.com, meta.com, facebookmail.com, chase.com + synthetic placeholders + a merged local file. web/index.html:354 promises 'court, bank, gov, account, and client mail' protection.
findings.rationale: The live worker's protection coverage (3 domains + bare .gov) is materially narrower than the engine's 14+ generic defaults; apple.com / accounts.google.com / docusign.net / irs.gov etc. all return 'Can move' on the shipped demo while marketing promises they are held.

findings.title: Legitimate .gov senders are NOT protected when From contains a display name or angle brackets — endsWith('.gov') runs against the whole raw, unparsed sender string
findings.evidence: Line 106: if (value.includes("courts.ca.gov") || value.endsWith(".gov")). value = whole lowercased From string. 'IRS Refunds <noreply@irs.gov>' lowercases to '...<noreply@irs.gov>' which ends with '>', so endsWith('.gov') is false AND includes('courts.ca.gov') is false -> falls through to NOT protected. core/rules.py is_protected_sender parses the address via getaddresses/_resolve_addr and _gov_protected anchors on the terminal label.
findings.rationale: Real defect (display-name/angle-bracket gov From fails open) but a bare 'noreply@irs.gov' WITHOUT brackets still ends with '.gov' and IS protected, so it is conditional on header shape; the demo also passes a bare-address senders/check (the UI sends bare addresses), so 'the common case' is overstated for this demo's own input path. Downgraded from high to medium.

findings.location: GET /v1/audit/{run_id} lines 281-305; cross-ref api/receipts.py sign()/get_receipt() lines 66-142
findings.title: Live /v1/audit/{run_id} fabricates an UNSIGNED, hardcoded receipt — the demo's 'tamper-evident signed receipt' headline trust artifact is fake
findings.evidence: worker.mjs 281-305 returns a fixed body {run_id, receipt:`Signed receipt for ${runId}`, audit:{total:3,protected_held:2,archived:1,...}} with NO signature/signed_body/algorithm. api/receipts.py get_receipt (lines 114-142) returns {signed_body, signature, algorithm:'HMAC-SHA256', verify:'...', created_at} and 404s on missing receipts (123-124); sign() (66-68) is real HMAC-SHA256. agent.json advertises 'audit_receipt: independent, HMAC-signed, re-derivable' and worker /llms.txt line 315 advertises /v1/audit/{run_id}.
findings.rationale: The worker's headline 'signed receipt' is a literal label string with zero cryptographic content; an auditing agent has nothing to verify. Directly contradicts the advertised trust artifact.

findings.title: Demo /v1/audit returns a fabricated 'Signed receipt' string with no signature — contradicts the product's headline 'signed, independently verifiable' claim
findings.evidence: Worker body confirmed (281-305): receipt is `Signed receipt for ${runId}`, no signed_body/signature/algorithm, counts hardcoded. api/receipts.py 114-142 confirms the real shape. HOWEVER docs/cloudflare-share-demo.md 'Verified Demo Endpoints' (lines 18-22) lists ONLY /health, /app/, /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage/preview, /v1/billing/plans — it does NOT list /v1/audit. The worker's own /llms.txt (line 315) DOES advertise /v1/audit/{run_id}, and agent.json advertises receipt_verification + 'HMAC-signed'.
findings.rationale: Core contradiction is real and confirmed (duplicate of U055's defect), but the specific corroborating claim 'docs/cloudflare-share-demo.md lists /v1/audit as a Verified Demo Endpoint' is false — that doc omits /v1/audit. Other advertisements (llms.txt, agent.json) do hold.

findings.title: 1password.com protection is substring-based and the chase.com fallback check is dead code
findings.evidence: Line 153: protected: value.includes("chase.com") || value.includes("1password.com"). chase.com already returned at line 128, so the chase.com disjunct here is dead (unreachable as true). 1password.com is matched by raw substring on the full lowercased From (no boundary), so 'x@1password.com.attacker.com' -> protected:true. PROTECTED Set (line 1) is never referenced in the file (grep confirms no other use).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: dead chase disjunct, substring-spoofable 1password, unused PROTECTED Set. Severity medium not high — this is a demo share surface, and the substring-spoof / dead-code is real but lower blast radius than the coverage gap.

findings.location: whole file (no handlers for these paths) vs web/index.html:630-642,667 and api/well_known.py
findings.title: Worker serves a frontend that links to /.well-known/agent.json, /server.json, /mcp, /acp/checkout_sessions, /openapi.json, /docs — none of which the worker implements
findings.evidence: Worker dispatch (238-322) handles only /health, /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage/preview, /v1/triage, /v1/billing/{plans,checkout,portal,webhook}, /v1/audit/*, /llms.txt; everything else -> serveApp -> env.ASSETS.fetch. web/index.html links: /.well-known/agent.json (638), /server.json (638), /mcp (630,667), /acp/checkout_sessions (634), /docs (343,666), /llms.txt (668). web/ contains ONLY index.html (ls confirms), so those asset paths 404. api/well_known.py mounts /.well-known/agent.json, /openapi.json, /acp/feed.json (FastAPI); the worker exposes none.
findings.rationale: Confirmed route divergence: the For-builders/Discovery links and advertised agent.json/server.json resolve to 404/SPA on the worker host. Downgraded to medium — it is a non-canonical demo surface and these are informational links, not the safety core. Echoing corroborated:false.

findings.title: Worker exposes POST /v1/triage as an alias of the dry-run preview, contradicting the real API where /v1/triage performs a live, fail-closed run
findings.evidence: worker.mjs 255-263: both /v1/triage/preview and /v1/triage route to triagePreview(...) which always returns dry_run:true, run_id:'demo_preview' and ignores body fields beyond provider/limit. api/app.py 85-95: /v1/triage/preview -> _run(dry_run=True); /v1/triage -> _run(dry_run=req.dry_run) via service.run_triage (live provider run, can raise AuditInvariantError -> non-200).
findings.rationale: Confirmed contract divergence on the apply path. Severity medium — the worker is explicitly a non-canonical demo (docs/cloudflare-share-demo.md states the Python app is authoritative and the worker exposes no live mailbox), so 'silently moves mail' is not the actual risk; the risk is contract confusion.

findings.title: Hardcoded absolute /Users/4jp paths make plist non-portable to any other machine/user
findings.evidence: Read of com.user.gmail_labeler.plist confirms hardcoded /Users/4jp paths: ProgramArguments[1]=line10 '/Users/4jp/Code/organvm/universal-mail--automation/run_automation.sh', WorkingDirectory=line13, StandardOutPath=line15, StandardErrorPath=line17 '/Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/...'. deploy.sh line 48 'cp "$PLIST_SRC" "$PLIST_DEST"' copies verbatim with no sed/envsubst path substitution (confirmed full Read of deploy.sh, no rewriting anywhere). HOWEVER deploy.sh line 9 PLIST_SRC points to com.user.mail_automation.plist, NOT this gmail_labeler file. deploy.sh only boots OUT a stale com.user.gmail_labeler (line 57); it never bootstraps THIS file.
findings.rationale: Hardcoded-paths/no-substitution defect is real and verified, but the specific causal chain ('deploy.sh copies THIS *.plist -> broken LaunchAgent on fresh clone') misreads deploy.sh, which deploys the sibling mail_automation plist, not this one; severity downgraded to medium since this file is not on the active deploy path.

findings.title: Same hardcoded /Users/4jp absolute paths (ProgramArguments, WorkingDirectory, logs) — the plist deploy.sh actually installs
findings.evidence: Plist hardcodes /Users/4jp absolute paths: ProgramArguments (lines 9-10 /bin/bash + /Users/4jp/Code/organvm/universal-mail--automation/run_automation.sh), WorkingDirectory (line 13), StandardOutPath/StandardErrorPath (lines 15,17). deploy.sh:9-10,46-48 confirms it is the installed plist via literal `cp "$PLIST_SRC" "$PLIST_DEST"` with NO envsubst/sed templating; deploy.sh:60 bootstraps it. So on any machine whose home != /Users/4jp the LaunchAgent points at non-existent paths. CAVEAT: the cited provenance line is 98-99 (not 99 alone) and says 'dead Workspace/ paths' — but the current plist points at /Users/4jp/Code/organvm/... which DOES exist (ls -ld confirmed); so the provenance 'nonexistent paths' framing is stale w.r.t. the current corrected path. The portability defect itself is real.
findings.rationale: Real hardcoded-home portability defect confirmed against current source; severity downgraded high→medium because it is single-tenant tooling (engine documented as single-user, no onboarding) and the only consumer deploy.sh runs on the same user 4jp, so blast radius is a future-multi-user/different-home scenario, not a live break.

findings.title: Unguarded int() cast on MAIL_AUTO_BATCH_SIZE crashes entire CLI/config load at startup
findings.evidence: Lines 272-273: `if os.getenv(f"{prefix}BATCH_SIZE"): config.batch_size = int(os.getenv(f"{prefix}BATCH_SIZE"))` — bare int() with no try/except. DRY_RUN (271) and IMAP_GMAIL_EXTENSIONS (287) use tolerant string-membership parsing; this is the lone unguarded numeric cast. load_config() is called at cli.py:422,523,718,828, so a non-int env value (e.g. '100x') raises ValueError before any command runs.
findings.rationale: Real unguarded cast that aborts every CLI command, but severity 'high' is inflated — config.batch_size is never actually consumed (only read in tests), so blast radius is a startup crash on a deliberate typo, not data loss; downgrade to medium.

findings.title: vip_senders regex patterns from untrusted YAML are never validated; invalid regex crashes categorization
findings.evidence: config.vip_senders assigned verbatim (259-260); apply_vip_senders_from_config forwards vip_data['pattern'] into add_vip_sender (config.py:315-322) which stores it in VIP_SENDERS (rules.py:919) with no re.compile. check_vip_sender at rules.py:885 runs `re.search(vip.pattern, sender_lower, re.IGNORECASE)` lazily — a malformed pattern (e.g. '[') raises re.error mid-categorization. tier/star taken via .get with no type check (config.py:319-320).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: patterns are validated nowhere and fail lazily at runtime; severity downgraded to medium since it requires the user to write a broken regex/type in their own config, not external input.

findings.title: Priority ties resolved by dict-insertion order silently misroute critical mail (gov mail with 'unsubscribe'/'newsletter' -> Marketing/Reference)
findings.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('Benefits <noreply@ssa.gov>','newsletter: ... unsubscribe here') -> label=Marketing, tier=4. Priority histogram confirms 17 is shared by Marketing, Tech/Storage, Personal/Government in that insertion order; _find_best_label line 1033 uses strict '<' so the first-inserted same-priority rule wins. Marketing is defined at line 429, Personal/Government at 468 (Marketing wins the tie).
findings.rationale: Real defect: a gov email containing any bulk-mail keyword is demoted from Critical(tier1) to Reference(tier4) by dict-insertion order alone. Sub-claim 'priority 9 Tech/Security beats Personal/Health' and 'priority 8 Finance/Payments beats Finance/Tax' confirmed by histogram (ties exist).

findings.title: irs.gov / ssa.gov / studentaid.gov never categorize as Personal/Government (Critical) because lower-priority earlier rules match first
findings.evidence: Reproduced exactly: categorize_with_tier('IRS <noreply@irs.gov>','Your tax refund status') -> label=Finance/Tax, tier=2 (NOT Personal/Government tier 1). irs.gov is listed both in Finance/Tax (priority 8, line 203) and Personal/Government (priority 17, line 476); the lower priority number (8) wins via line 1033.
findings.rationale: Distinct from U064: this is lower-priority-rule-wins (8<17), not a same-priority tie. Government mail from irs.gov silently downgraded from Critical to Important; the tier table promises Critical.

findings.title: Government .gov anchors are wrong because regex runs against combined 'sender subject' text, not the sender alone
findings.evidence: Confirmed r'\.gov$' is near-useless against combined_text='noreply@ssa.gov your benefits update' (matches=False; only matches when SUBJECT ends in .gov). BUT the second anchor r'@.*\.gov' DOES match the real gov sender ('noreply@ssa.gov your benefits update' -> True), so gov senders are NOT generally missed. The false-positive claim is real: 'me@evil.com talked about x.gov stuff' -> r'@.*\.gov' matches (True).
findings.rationale: Overstated: the finding claims both anchors are 'effectively broken for their stated purpose' and that 'a real gov sender can miss'. The greedy @.*\.gov actually catches the common real case; the genuine defect is the false-positive over-match (any '@' before any '.gov' in subject) plus the dead r'\.gov$' anchor. Severity downgraded since the real gov-detection mostly works.

findings.title: Non-UTF-8 local protected_senders config crashes the module at import time, disabling the entire safety gate
findings.evidence: Reproduced: with PROTECTED_SENDERS_FILE pointing at a file containing byte 0xff, `import core.rules` raises UnicodeDecodeError ('utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xff). Line 600 opens with encoding='utf-8'; line 611 only catches (FileNotFoundError, OSError). UnicodeDecodeError is a ValueError subclass, not caught. _load_local_protected runs at import (line 616), so the whole module fails to import.
findings.rationale: Docstring promises 'Never raises.' A user-supplied non-UTF-8 protected_senders config takes down the entire safety gate and every consumer at import. Fix: catch UnicodeDecodeError / errors='replace' / broaden except.

findings.title: Invalid user-supplied VIP regex pattern raises re.error and crashes categorization with no guard
findings.evidence: Reproduced: add_vip_sender('bad', pattern=r'[unclosed') then check_vip_sender('anyone@x.com') raises re.error (PatternError: 'unterminated character set'). Line 885 calls re.search(vip.pattern, ...) with no compile-time validation in add_vip_sender (lines 900-925) and no try/except.
findings.rationale: Real: an invalid user/config-supplied VIP regex crashes categorize_with_tier -> categorize_message for the whole run. Severity adjusted to medium: VIP_SENDERS ships EMPTY (lines 841-855 are all commented out), so this only triggers when a user adds a malformed pattern via config/add_vip_sender; not a default-path crash.

findings.title: Government mail demoted to archived Marketing on a same-priority (17) tie
findings.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('noreply@cityplanning.lacounty.gov','Public meeting notice — unsubscribe here') -> Marketing tier 4; ('clerk@records.ny.gov','Newsletter: civic updates') -> Marketing tier 4. Same priority-17 tie + strict '<' + insertion-order root cause as U064.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U064 (gov demoted to Marketing on the priority-17 tie). The R2 nuance (EQUAL priority vs lower priority) is exactly what U064's body already covers ('priority 17 is shared by Marketing... Personal/Government IN THAT INSERTION ORDER').

findings.title: Non-atomic state-file write can corrupt resume state on crash
findings.evidence: core/state.py:92-93 `with open(self.filename, "w") as f: json.dump(self.state, f, indent=2)` — truncate-in-place, no temp+os.replace. save() is called repeatedly mid-run (cli.py:300/319/323, gmail_labeler.py:308/322/332/335). _load (52-56) silently resets to default on JSONDecodeError, so a torn write loses the resume token.
findings.rationale: Non-atomic write of a crash-recovery state file is a real defect; downgraded from high to medium because blast radius is at-most a re-run from scratch (idempotent labeling), not data loss, and the crash window is narrow.

findings.title: No schema/version validation: corrupt-but-valid JSON is loaded and used unguarded
findings.evidence: core/state.py:50-51 returns `json.load(f)` verbatim with no isinstance(dict) check; subsequent getters do self.state.get(...). A top-level list/str/int/null state file would raise AttributeError on the next getter or assignment. Test only covers decode-error path (tests/test_state.py:test_corrupted_file_returns_defaults uses 'NOT VALID JSON').
findings.rationale: Real missing-validation gap, but only triggerable by an externally-malformed-yet-valid-JSON state file (the tool itself never writes a non-dict), so realistic severity is low.

findings.location: get_history, lines 105-112; consumed in cli.py:174 / cli.py:238 / save:86
findings.title: get_history crashes when stored 'history' is a non-mapping (TypeError/ValueError)
findings.evidence: core/state.py:112 `return defaultdict(int, self.state.get("history", {}))`. If state['history'] is a str/list/int, defaultdict's constructor raises ValueError/TypeError, propagating out of cli.py:174 `stats = state.get_history()` and gmail_labeler.py:73. No coercion to {}.
findings.rationale: Genuine crash path, but only reachable if the state file's history key is corrupted to a non-mapping — the writer always stores a dict (save:86 dict(history)). Low realistic severity; subset of the same corrupt-state theme as U071.

findings.title: save() swallows ALL write exceptions and returns normally — failed persist looks like success
findings.evidence: core/state.py:91-95 wraps the write in `except Exception` -> `logger.error(...)` and returns None; callers ignore the return (cli.py:300/319/323). Contrast core/audit.py:145/233 which sets self.write_error so the CLI can surface it — state.save has no such mechanism. Broad Exception also masks json.dump TypeError on non-serializable state.
findings.rationale: Confirmed silent-failure: a failed persist is indistinguishable from success and the run continues. Downgraded to medium — re-processing labeled mail is idempotent, not destructive.

findings.title: No concurrency guard / file lock — two concurrent runs race and corrupt the state file
findings.evidence: No file locking exists (confirmed by reading whole file). However the concurrency claim depends on two processes actually running against the same state_file simultaneously, which is config/deployment-dependent (CLAUDE.md mentions a launchd daily job plus manual runs). The torn-write half of this finding duplicates U070/U194/U196/U591.
findings.rationale: Real (no lock), but overstated: concurrent execution is a deployment assumption not demonstrated in code, and the torn-write portion is a duplicate of the non-atomic-write findings. Partial.

findings.title: Double-escaped regex patterns can never match real email text
