findings.evidence: Empirically verified: r"render\\.com" (file source, line 62) is the char sequence ['r','e','n','d','e','r','\\','\\','.','c','o','m'] and re.search returns None on 'render.com' but matches a literal backslash. AST-extracted categorize() routes render.com/pieces.app/meta.com/self.inc/builtin.com -> 'Uncategorized'. NUANCE: e.godaddy.com still -> 'Services/Domain' (bare 'godaddy' pattern at line 239 catches it; the dead e\.godaddy\.com is redundant, not impactful), and ibo.org has no fallback bare pattern so only a sender whose text has no other Marketing keyword lands in Uncategorized. Line cites are off by a few (meta is 78 not 79) but patterns correctly identified.
findings.rationale: Double-backslash patterns are genuinely dead for 5 of 7 senders; severity downgraded from high to medium because this is an unwired standalone legacy file (no .sh/.yaml/cli reference; only main() invokes it with max_emails=None) and is relabel-only/non-destructive, and 2 of the 7 cited patterns (godaddy, ibo) are either redundant or rarely impactful.

findings.title: COPY+EXPUNGE fallback expunges the WHOLE mailbox, not just the one message, risking data loss of other \Deleted mail
findings.evidence: Identical claim: line 76 `imap.expunge()` is unscoped (no arg) and runs per-message in loop 161-165. Same code, same root cause as U006.
findings.rationale: Same unscoped-EXPUNGE defect as U006.

findings.title: COPY-fallback archive runs unscoped EXPUNGE, risking deletion of other \Deleted messages in the mailbox
findings.evidence: Same defect; note its 'same class as providers/imap.py' cross-ref is slightly off — providers/imap.py:312-326 does COPY+STORE \Deleted but does NOT call expunge at all, so the unscoped-expunge specifically lives in icloud_triage.py line 76.
findings.rationale: Duplicate of U006; the providers/imap.py analogy is imperfect (that file never expunges).

findings.title: labeler_state.json (real mailbox stats) is git-tracked in a public repo despite CLAUDE.md flagging *_state.json as runtime state to avoid
findings.evidence: `git ls-files labeler_state.json` returns the path (tracked). `git cat-file -p HEAD:labeler_state.json` shows total_processed=36470 and the per-category history (Misc/Other=35521, Tech/Security=372, Finance/Payments=302, Finance/Banking=151, Professional/Jobs=79, etc.) exactly as claimed. `git log --follow` traces it to the initial commit 00ff9d7, so the value is recoverable from history. `git check-ignore labeler_state.json` exits 1 (NOT ignored); .gitignore has no *_state.json entry. CLAUDE.md 'Files to Avoid Modifying' does list '*_state.json (runtime state for resumption)'. `gh repo view` confirms isPrivate=false (PUBLIC).
findings.rationale: Real runtime-state file with private mailbox volume/composition is tracked and publicly exposed since the first commit; severity downgraded high->medium because the leak is coarse metadata (counts, no addresses/subjects/bodies/credentials).

findings.title: MCP triage tool has no auth/account/metering — unlimited free live triage for any connected agent
findings.evidence: mcp_server/server.py imports only `api.service` (line 34) and `api.schemas` (line 35) — never `api.store`. _triage (129-146) calls service.run_triage with provider/query/limit/dry_run/remove_label/tier_routing/vip_only and NO account/api_key. grep confirms entitlements_for and consume_credit have ZERO runtime callers (only tests + the credit-pack fulfillment in acp/router.py). plans.py:130-139 advertises the metered '$0.01/triage run' add-on and Business-tier MCP access; meter_event_name='triage_run' is defined but emitted nowhere. The only guard on the surface is _transport_security() DNS-rebinding.
findings.rationale: Real: no auth/credit/cap/meter on the MCP triage path. But the SAME gap exists on the HTTP path (api/app.py _run never checks credits/caps either), so this is system-wide, not an MCP-unique unlimited-free-triage hole; severity downgraded from high to medium accordingly.

findings.title: MCP triage tool is the agent-facing paid surface yet performs no credit debit, no cap check, and emits no meter event — agents triage unlimited for free
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U079: service.run_triage called at server.py:132 with no consume_credit / entitlements_for / monthly_run_cap / Stripe meter event. Confirmed entitlements_for & consume_credit have no runtime callers outside tests/ACP fulfillment.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U079 restated from R2 with the meter-event angle; merged.

findings.title: Hosted /mcp endpoint is dead-on-arrival in production: default allowed_hosts excludes the real deploy domain so every request 421s
findings.evidence: render.yaml sets only PYTHONUNBUFFERED — no MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS, autoDeploy=true. With it unset, _transport_security() (48-58) returns enable_dns_rebinding_protection=True with allowed_hosts=['localhost','127.0.0.1','localhost:8000','127.0.0.1:8000']. The installed MCP SDK transport_security.py _validate_host does exact match (or ':*' wildcard suffix only), returning 421 'Invalid Host header' for a real onrender.com Host. DEPLOY.md:64,72-73 advertises '/mcp is live' and instructs setting MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS=<your-domain>.
findings.rationale: Real: a blueprint deploy that skips the DEPLOY.md step 421s every /mcp call. But MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS IS a documented required step (DEPLOY.md:73), so 'dead-on-arrival' is a documented-config foot-gun, not an unavoidable break — downgraded high→medium.

findings.location: triage() lines 109-126; module docstring lines 10-13; INSTRUCTIONS lines 60-68
findings.title: Prompt-injection 'preview by default' guarantee is a single model-controlled boolean (dry_run=False), not a capability gate or confirmation step
findings.evidence: dry_run is a plain bool tool arg (line 117, default True) the model fully controls; flipping to False (line 125 passes it through) is a one-step live mutation. destructiveHint (line 109) is advisory annotation. No second factor exists. HOWEVER the docstring's actual safety claim is the protected-sender GATE (lines 3-8, 122-123), which is enforced fail-closed in service.run_triage via audit.assert_no_violations() (service.py:127) regardless of dry_run — a prompt-injected dry_run=False still cannot move a protected sender.
findings.rationale: True that dry_run is a soft, model-controlled barrier; but the file's central anti-injection guarantee is the gate (which holds), not dry_run. dry_run is described as one of two choices, never the sole barrier. Overstated; downgraded high→low.

findings.title: Gmail provider never populates EmailMessage.date, so escalate/pending age logic is a silent no-op
findings.evidence: All three EmailMessage constructions (gmail.py:157-161, 204-211, 286-293) omit date=, which defaults to None (core/models.py:48). The metadata fetches request metadataHeaders=["From","Subject"] (lines 177, 243) — no Date header retrieved. calculate_email_age_hours(None) returns 0 (core/rules.py: 'if email_date is None: return 0'). escalate_by_age then hits 'if email_age_hours < 24: should_escalate=False'. cli.py:883 calls calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date) and cli.py:667 builds the pending age column from msg.date.
findings.rationale: Confirmed end-to-end: Gmail msg.date is always None, so escalate is a silent no-op and pending age always reports 0h.

findings.title: IMAP search passes raw query string with charset=None; Gmail-style queries (label:, has:) are NOT valid IMAP SEARCH and will error or mis-search
findings.evidence: Line 184: `self._connection.uid("search", None, query)` forwards `query` verbatim. cli.py:1014 sets `--query` default="has:nouserlabels" for ALL providers (no per-provider override on the label path), and cli.py:442 passes args.query into run_labeler → list_messages. `has:`/`label:` are not valid IMAP SEARCH keys; line 185-186 raises RuntimeError on a server NO. The report path (cli.py:480-484) guards `f"label:{label}"` behind `if args.provider == 'gmail'`, but the label/escalate/summary paths do not — so the default query reaches IMAP raw.
findings.rationale: Gmail query dialect reaches IMAP SEARCH verbatim with no translation; common-path failure on the default label command.

findings.location: apply_label 279, remove_label 303, star 331, unstar 340, mark_read 364, mark_unread 373, archive 321
findings.title: imaplib uid(STORE/...) errors are exceptions OR ('NO', ...) tuples; success returned True without checking response status
findings.evidence: Verified imaplib.IMAP4._command_complete raises self.error ONLY on typ=='BAD'; a typ=='NO' (label missing, permission, quota, invalid flag) returns ('NO',[...]) without raising. Lines 279-280/303-304/331-332/340-341/364-365/373-374 each call uid('STORE',...) then unconditionally `return True`, catching only exceptions. base.apply_actions (base.py:344-346) records applied_labels from this True return into the audit. The COPY branch (288-289) and ensure_label_exists DO check res, proving the asymmetry.
findings.rationale: Server NO rejections are silently reported as success; primary trust-surface defect (label add) — confirmed against real imaplib semantics.

findings.title: IMAP archive() for Gmail extensions removes \Inbox but does not verify the STORE succeeded; standard-IMAP archive COPY+\Deleted+EXPUNGE is non-atomic data-loss risk
findings.evidence: Part (1) CONFIRMED: gmail-ext branch (315) calls remove_label(message_id,'\\Inbox') which returns True regardless of STORE NO (303-304), so archive reports success even when inbox-removal was rejected. Part (2) FALSE: the standard-IMAP branch (317-326) does COPY then STORE +FLAGS (\Deleted) and `return True` — it does NOT call expunge() at all. The 'mailbox-wide expunge destroys unrelated \Deleted messages' claim does not exist in this code.
findings.rationale: Inbox-removal-unchecked half is real; the expunge-data-loss half is fabricated for this file — overstated.

findings.title: IMAP provider never populates EmailMessage.date; no INTERNALDATE/Date fetched → escalate no-op
findings.evidence: Both EmailMessage constructions (198-202, 261-268) omit date=, and core/models.py:48 defaults date=None. The fetch at 219 is only (BODY.PEEK[HEADER.FIELDS (FROM SUBJECT)]) — no Date header, no INTERNALDATE. cmd_escalate (cli.py:883) computes age via calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date); core/rules.py:1173-1174 returns 0 for None, so escalate_by_age never escalates for IMAP. cmd_pending similarly shows 0h.
findings.rationale: IMAP never populates date; escalate --provider imap is a documented-feature no-op — confirmed end to end.

findings.location: apply_label line 279, remove_label line 303, archive line 314-315
findings.title: Gmail-extension IMAP STORE for label add/remove/archive ignores the IMAP response status and always returns True
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U085: unchecked uid('STORE',...) return on the X-GM-LABELS add/remove path, and archive() = remove_label('\\Inbox') inheriting the same False-success. Narrower scope (label add/remove/archive only) than U085 which also enumerates star/flags.
findings.rationale: Identical defect to U085 (R2 re-discovery of the swallowed STORE status).

findings.title: Standard-IMAP archive expunges the whole mailbox, deleting unrelated messages
findings.evidence: Lines 317-326: standard-IMAP archive does uid('COPY',...) then on res=='OK' uid('STORE', +FLAGS \Deleted) then `return True`. There is NO expunge() call anywhere in archive(). The claim 'archive() does ... then self._connection.expunge()' misreads the code — the standard branch never expunges (that omission is the OPPOSITE defect, captured by U131/U383).
findings.rationale: The whole-mailbox-expunge data-loss claim does not match the actual code, which never expunges.

findings.title: AppleScript injection: message_id and label interpolated unescaped into osascript source
findings.evidence: Every mutating method builds AppleScript via f-string with zero escaping: line 213/265/297/313/362/378 `first message whose id is {message_id}` (unquoted), line 266 `mailbox "{label}"`, line 342 `make new mailbox with properties {{name:"{label}"}}`, line 133/261/332/409 `of account "{self.account}"`. No escape helper exists anywhere in the module (full file read).
findings.rationale: Genuine AppleScript-injection sink; downgraded from high because all sources (message_id from Mail.app's own data, label from static rules/config, account from CLI --account) are locally-controlled, not remote/untrusted input.

findings.title: AppleScript injection via mailbox and account names interpolated into osascript
findings.evidence: Line 133 `of account "{self.account}"` and line 139 `mailbox "{mailbox}"` — same unescaped f-string interpolation. `mailbox` is hardcoded "INBOX" by every caller (cli.py never passes mailbox to list_messages); account from CLI.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause defect (unescaped AppleScript interpolation) as U090; mailbox is not actually caller-controllable in current code, narrowing the claim.

findings.location: get_message_details line 213; apply_label lines 263-270; star/unstar/mark_read/mark_unread (e.g. 295-301, 360-366); ensure_label_exists 335-345
findings.title: AppleScript injection via message_id and label interpolated unescaped into osascript
findings.evidence: Identical claim set to U090 (message_id unquoted at 213/297/362, label-quoted at 266/342). message_id is typed str (EmailMessage.id, models.py:45) and never validated.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U090, restated.

findings.title: Mail.app provider never populates EmailMessage.date; AppleScript fetches no date received → escalate no-op
findings.evidence: EmailMessage at lines 191-197 and 240-247 omit `date=` (defaults None, models.py:48). list_messages AppleScript (line 160) and get_message_details AppleScript (line 226) never query `date received`. cmd_escalate calls calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date) (cli.py:883) and pending uses msg.date (cli.py:667,676); calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for None (rules.py:1173-1174), so escalate_by_age never fires for mailapp.
findings.rationale: Confirmed silent no-op: escalate and age-based pending are inert for mailapp. Severity medium not high — it is a missing-feature/silent-no-op, not data loss or wrong destructive action.

findings.title: AppleScript injection via unescaped label/mailbox/account/message_id interpolation
findings.evidence: Same unescaped label/account/mailbox/message_id interpolation across the same lines as U090.
findings.rationale: Round-2 restatement of the U090 injection cluster.

findings.location: _get_session (196-211) + _api_get/_api_post/_api_patch (213-232); token acquired once in connect() at 234-239
findings.title: Access token never refreshed mid-run; long sweeps fail with 401 once the token expires
findings.evidence: connect() (236) sets self._access_token = self._acquire_token() once. _get_session() (198) short-circuits `if self._session: return self._session`, so the Bearer header (208) is baked in only at first creation and never refreshed. _api_get/post/patch (213-232) call raise_for_status() with no 401-aware refresh/retry. No acquire_token_silent between batches. There is also no in-process token-expiry check.
findings.rationale: Real: a long single-connection sweep will 401 after token TTL with no refresh path; downgraded to medium because the main label loop sleeps 1s/batch and a typical run completes well within a ~60-90min token, so impact is limited to very large mailboxes.

findings.title: Folder lookup fallback can silently return the label string instead of a real folder ID, causing wrong-destination move
findings.evidence: Line 651 `return self._folder_cache.get(label, label)` returns the raw label string when the cache miss occurs. The inner fallback (644-647) only updates the cache when result.get('value') is truthy; an empty value list leaves parent_id/cache unchanged with no exception. apply_label (527-530) then POSTs /move with destinationId=<label string>.
findings.rationale: Confirmed contract violation (returns label not folder id); blast radius bounded because Graph /move with a bogus destinationId 400s and is caught at 535 returning False (fail-ish-safe), so wrong-folder move requires the label to collide with a well-known folder id — narrow. Medium.

findings.title: Broad except swallows all errors (auth, network, 5xx) and degrades to empty/false, hiding real failures
findings.evidence: list_messages (438-440) returns ListMessagesResult(messages=[]) on ANY Exception; cli.py (192-194) treats empty messages as 'No more messages found' and breaks the loop. apply_category (349-350) bare-excepts to current_cats=[]. _init_folder_cache (261) and _init_category_cache (284) log a warning and continue with empty caches. All confirmed verbatim.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: a mid-pagination 401/429/5xx is swallowed into an empty result that the CLI reads as successful completion — silent early termination on a bulk job. High kept.

findings.location: lines 6-8 ("# Optional: Outlook.com support" then msal/requests)
findings.title: `requests` is mislabeled "Optional: Outlook" but is a MANDATORY runtime dependency for Gmail OAuth token refresh
findings.evidence: google.auth.transport.requests does import `requests` at module level (confirmed via inspect.getsource). BUT the finding's load-bearing claim ('requests is NOT installed by google-api-python-client/google-auth-httplib2 by default') is false: `requests` is a HARD transitive dep via TWO paths from the declared Gmail-only deps. pip metadata: google-api-core Requires-Dist includes 'requests<3.0.0,>=2.20.0' (google-api-python-client -> google-api-core), and requests-oauthlib Requires-Dist includes 'requests >=2.0.0' (google-auth-oauthlib -> requests-oauthlib). So a Gmail-only user who skips the explicit `requests` line STILL gets requests installed. `pip show requests` Required-by: includes google-api-core. The claimed ImportError-on-token-refresh does not occur.
findings.rationale: The crash scenario is impossible: requests is a hard transitive dependency of the declared Gmail deps, so it is always installed; only the comment label is imprecise (cosmetic).

findings.title: Bulk-sender router moves protected mail with no protected-sender gate (and unanchored substring match)
findings.evidence: route_bulk_senders.applescript line 9: `if theSender contains ("@" & dom) or theSender ends with dom then` -> line 28 `move msg to bulkMailbox`. There is no protected-sender check anywhere in the 33-line file (grep for protected/is_protected returns nothing). The `ends with dom` branch has no leading-dot boundary, so dom='mailchimp.com' matches a sender ending in 'notmailchimp.com'. Contrast core/rules.py:659-663 `_domain_matches` which intentionally does `domain == entry or domain.endswith('.' + entry)` to kill substring embeds, and core/audit.py:1-33 which states the headline guarantee that a protected sender is NEVER moved out of the inbox. This standalone AppleScript bypasses both the gate and the audit.
findings.rationale: Both claimed defects are real in the current code: no protected-sender gate and an unanchored `ends with` substring match that moves mail out of the inbox; the Python guarantees it violates are present as cited.

findings.title: set -u aborts iCloud step (and whole script) if ICLOUD_IMAP_* env vars are unset
findings.evidence: run_automation.sh:5 `set -euo pipefail`; lines 47-49 expand `$ICLOUD_IMAP_HOST/USER/PASS` with no `:-` defaults. Reproduced under bash: `set -euo pipefail; IMAP_PASS="$ICLOUD_IMAP_PASS" /bin/echo ran || echo guarded-fail` -> `bash: ICLOUD_IMAP_PASS: unbound variable`, the `||` guard did NOT fire, and the following `echo` (banner equivalent) never ran (outer exit=1). Decisively, the real sourced file ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh exports ICLOUD_IMAP_USER/ICLOUD_IMAP_PASS ONLY inside an `if command -v op ...; then ... fi` block (lines 19-29), so on any machine without the op CLI those vars are genuinely unset, triggering exactly this abort.
findings.rationale: set -u aborts on the unguarded ICLOUD_* expansion before the || guard, killing the iCloud step and the completion banner; the op-absent code path in the real env file makes the unset condition concretely reachable.

findings.location: lines 6-15 (packages[].registryType=pypi, identifier=universal-mail-automation, version 0.1.0); cross-ref pyproject.toml (whole file) and scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py gen_server_json lines 82-89
findings.title: server.json advertises a PyPI package that the repo cannot build or publish (no [project]/[build-system] in pyproject.toml)
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml is exactly 3 lines and contains ONLY [tool.pytest.ini_options] (testpaths/pythonpath); no [project] or [build-system]. `ls setup.py setup.cfg MANIFEST.in` -> all 'No such file or directory'; grep '\[project\]|\[build-system\]' across all *.toml returns nothing. server.json lines 6-15 advertise {registryType: pypi, identifier: 'universal-mail-automation', version: '0.1.0', transport: stdio}, generated verbatim by gen_server_json at scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:82-89. So the tree cannot build/publish a PyPI dist of that name; an MCP registry client trusting the entry fails at install.
findings.rationale: Packaging metadata genuinely absent so the advertised PyPI artifact cannot be built from this tree; an mcp_server package WITH __main__.py exists (python -m mcp_server runs locally), so the gap is distribution-only and the repo is an unpublished 0.1.0 PUBLIC_PROCESS demo whose live surface is the Cloudflare worker, not PyPI — downgrade high->medium.

findings.location: whole file (no credit-debit/cap assertion); cf. tests/test_acp.py:117-153 and tests/test_store.py:35-43
findings.title: Test suite asserts credits are ADDED and that consume_credit works as a unit, but no test asserts a triage run ever DEBITS a credit or hits a cap — masking the unwired metering
findings.evidence: Triage path has zero metering: `grep` of api/service.py for consume_credit/entitlements_for/monthly_run_cap/run_credits/debit/cap returns NOTHING; api/app.py `_run` -> `service.run_triage` (service.py:70,109) carries no account/credit context and has no auth/Depends dependency on the triage endpoints. `consume_credit` exists only at api/store.py:235. Every test touching run_credits/consume_credit only ADDS or unit-tests in isolation: test_store.py:35-43 (`test_credits_atomic_no_overdraw` unit-tests consume_credit), test_store.py:84/88 (==100, add), test_acp.py:133 (==200 credited), test_acp.py:153/173 (==100, idempotency). grep across tests/ for run_credits/consume_credit/balance/debit/cap finds NO test that drives /v1/triage and asserts a balance DECREASE or a cap-block.
findings.rationale: Factual core fully verified: metering is unwired in the triage path and no debit-on-run/cap test exists; downgraded to medium because this is a contributory test-coverage gap (the masking), while the severity-bearing item is the unwired production metering itself.

findings.title: Offline sender-check fallback is fail-OPEN: only 3 hardcoded patterns are protected, contradicting the safety promise
findings.evidence: localSenderCheck (line 708) marks protected only for empty / .gov / chase.com / 1password.com; line 753 returns protected:false otherwise, so e.g. alerts@bankofamerica.com renders the green 'Can move' verdict (line 818). True. BUT this fallback ONLY fires when the fetch to /v1/senders/check fails (line 809: ok && data ? data : localSenderCheck). The sole documented/CI deployment (wrangler.toml main=worker.mjs, custom_domain uma.4444j99.dev) serves a same-origin API: worker.mjs senderCheck (lines 82-170) has the SAME protection set, so on the live demo the fetch succeeds and the JS fallback is never reached. API_BASE defaults to '' (no injection site found).
findings.rationale: Real fail-open in the offline mirror, but it is a latent fallback that does not execute on the actual worker-backed deployment; severity downgraded from high and scope corrected (not the live surface).

findings.location: lines 679-704 (SAMPLE_PLANS) and 932-933 (catch -> localPlans() fallback render); cross-ref api/plans.py PLANS pro/business (name="Pro"/"Business", price_display $19/$49) and startCheckout(p.id)
findings.title: Static-demo fallback renders different plan NAMES and prices than checkout actually charges (Inbox safety audit/$29-$49 vs Pro/$19) — buyer deception on the live Cloudflare demo
findings.evidence: SAMPLE_PLANS does use id 'pro' name 'Inbox safety audit' price '$29-$49' and id 'business' name 'Assisted cleanup' '$149-$299' (lines 689-702); plans.py prices Pro $19/mo, Business $49/mo (plans.py:87-125). But the 'STATIC Cloudflare demo with NO API backend' premise is FALSE: wrangler.toml routes uma.4444j99.dev to worker.mjs which serves GET /v1/billing/plans returning $19/$49 'Pro'/'Business' (worker.mjs:24-46,265). The page fetches /v1/billing/plans first (line 900); on the live demo that fetch succeeds, so the worker plans render and SAMPLE_PLANS (catch branch line 933) never executes. Checkout on the worker returns 503 (worker.mjs:269-270), so the page shows 'Billing is not live here' and never reaches Stripe.
findings.rationale: The source divergence is real, but the 'buyer deception on the live demo' claim rests on a wrong deployment model — the live worker serves correct prices and SAMPLE_PLANS is a dead-fallback for a static host that this repo does not deploy.

findings.title: Feed advertises price as integer cents (100/900) under currency 'USD' — ambiguous unit risks a 100x agent-facing mispricing vs the actual cents charge
findings.evidence: feed.py:48 `"price": pack["amount_cents"]` -> 100/900; line 50 `"currency": "USD"`. Charge path is minor-units: models.py:86 `base = int(pack["amount_cents"])`, :94 `"total": subtotal`, grand_total :100-101, router.py:262 `charge(amount=amount, currency=CURRENCY)` and :310 receipt text says '... minor units'. So feed price == charged amount numerically. The 100x hazard hinges entirely on the claim that the ACP product-feed `price` field is conventionally MAJOR units; the OpenAI ACP product-feed spec actually expresses price in integer minor units, and the module docstring claims the feed is 'spec-faithful'. No doc (docs/agent-commerce.md) or code declares the unit either way.
findings.rationale: Real ambiguity on a buyer-facing surface, but the asserted MAJOR-unit convention is unverified/likely wrong for ACP (which uses minor units); the displayed-vs-charged 100x is conditional on a consumer misreading the spec, so severity is overstated.

findings.title: Charge treats only status=='succeeded' as ok, dropping 'requires_action'/'processing' into a permanent failure that strands a real payment
findings.evidence: payment.py:92-97 returns ok=True only when getattr(intent,'status')=='succeeded'; 'processing'/'requires_action' fall to the ok=False/error branch. router.py:267-280 then re-persists status=STATUS_READY and returns 402. Grep confirms NO payment_intent.* webhook anywhere: billing.py webhook (lines 225-272) handles only checkout.session.completed / subscription / invoice events. So a charge that settles asynchronously at Stripe will never trigger fulfillment (router fulfills only on the synchronous result.ok==True path, 290-317).
findings.rationale: Real gap: async-settled ACP PaymentIntent has no reconciliation path, so a 'processing' charge that later succeeds credits no runs. Severity downgraded from high to medium: this SPT/card-only flow with confirm=True and automatic capture almost always returns 'succeeded' synchronously; 'processing'/'requires_action' is the uncommon branch.

findings.title: Synchronous confirm with no return_url: a 3DS/requires_action result is treated as a hard failure, stranding an authorized-but-uncaptured charge
findings.evidence: payment.py:72-88 builds PaymentIntent.create with confirm=True and no return_url, no off_session, no capture_method, no cancellation path. A requires_action (3DS/SCA) result hits the ok=False return (94-97); router (273-280) reverts to READY and returns 402, but the intent persists at Stripe uncancelled. No code path ever voids/cancels it (grep shows the only cancel logic is acp.cancel of the session, not the Stripe intent).
findings.rationale: Distinct correctness angle from U106 (dangling/uncancelled intent that could later capture), so kept separate rather than duplicate. Severity medium: the dangling-intent capture risk is real but the SPT confirm flow rarely returns requires_action, and a held authorization expiring is the more likely outcome than a silent late capture.

findings.location: lines 13, 33 ("price": 100 / "price": 900); source acp/feed.py build_feed line 48 ("price": pack["amount_cents"])
findings.title: ACP product feed emits price as a bare integer of minor units (cents), not major units / decimal — an agent can over-read price by 100x
findings.evidence: Confirmed factually: acp/feed.py:48 `"price": pack["amount_cents"]`; api/plans.py:144-147 pack_100 amount_cents=100, pack_1000 amount_cents=900; product_feed.json:13/33 emit 100/900. BUT the premise is wrong: this is the OpenAI/Stripe ACP spec (acp/__init__.py:19 API_VERSION="2026-04-17"), whose money model is integer minor units, not Google-Merchant major-unit decimals. acp/models.py:8 states verbatim 'All monetary amounts are integer minor units (cents), per spec.' The feed price (cents) is numerically identical to the charged amount: build_line_items (models.py:86-94) uses base=int(pack['amount_cents']) as subtotal/total, grand_total feeds payment.charge(amount=int(amount)) (payment.py:74), and the receipt says '{amount} usd minor units' (router.py:310). Feed and charge agree exactly when read per the ACP convention.
findings.rationale: Real fact (price is bare integer cents) but the 100x-misread conclusion rests on importing the Google Merchant major-unit convention, contradicted by the repo's own minor-units ACP model; hazard exists only for a non-conformant consumer, so downgraded from high to low.

findings.title: Charge is executed BEFORE the fulfillment dedup, so a session that was fulfilled-but-not-marked-completed re-charges; relies entirely on Stripe idempotency_key, which the fail-closed/Null path and any non-Stripe processor do not provide
findings.evidence: Order is: amount (257) -> payment.charge (262) -> fulfill_once (290). If a prior /complete charged+fulfilled but crashed before _persist set COMPLETED (332-337), status is still READY and a retry re-enters charge at 262. Double-charge is prevented ONLY by idempotency_key=f'acp-charge:{session_id}' (265). StripeSPTPaymentClient honors it (payment.py:84-87); NullPaymentClient never charges (returns ok=False), and any future processor that ignores the key would re-charge while fulfill_once returns False.
findings.rationale: The fragile single-processor-contract observation is correct. Severity medium not high: the only configured real processor (Stripe) honors the key, so the double-charge is latent/conditional on a future or misconfigured client.

findings.title: Charge can succeed while fulfillment/persist throws, leaving money captured and session left in ready_for_payment with no completion record
findings.evidence: No try/except wraps get_account_by_api_key/create_account/fulfill_once/sign/save_receipt/_persist/_complete_idempotency (286-338) after a successful charge (262-267). Any raise -> unhandled 500, session not persisted COMPLETED, idempotency left 'processing' (store sets 'done' only via idempotency_complete at 122). Funds already captured. Recovery depends on client retry within IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT=60s (store.py:103) and the acp-charge:{session_id} dedup.
findings.rationale: Charge-captured-but-no-completion-record window is real with no compensating action. Closely related to U018 but broader (any post-charge raise, not just receipt mint). Kept distinct.

findings.title: ACP bearer token is never validated against issued keys — any attacker-supplied token mints/credits an account
findings.evidence: Same code as U016: _gate accepts any non-empty bearer (79-93); complete_session get_account_by_api_key then create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key) on miss (287-289). No allowlist/signature/hashing. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Identical root cause to U016.

findings.title: ACP bearer token is never authenticated — any non-empty string is accepted as the buyer identity
findings.evidence: _gate (80-83) only checks non-empty bearer; complete_session uses ctx.api_key as the account key (287-289). api keys are uma_+token_urlsafe(32) (store.py:110-112) so guessing a victim key is hard, but no auth boundary validates the presented bearer.
findings.rationale: Same unvalidated-bearer root cause as U016.

findings.title: Completed session's created_at preserved but data_json fully replaced each persist — concurrent failed and successful /complete race to last-writer-wins on the whole row including status
findings.evidence: save_session uses INSERT OR REPLACE keyed on id with no compare-and-set on status (store.py:407-413; created_at is preserved at 403-406 but the whole data_json/status/account_id are replaced). Two concurrent /complete calls (one success at 332-337, one failed-payment at 273-280) each call _persist; last writer wins the entire row. A failed-payment _persist (status=READY, account_id=None) committing after the success _persist reverts the row to READY, dropping order/account_id, while fulfill_once (already committed) keeps the credit — making the session re-completable with the order receipt orphaned.
findings.rationale: Mechanism confirmed exactly. Severity medium not high: requires a genuine concurrent success+failure race on the same session within the single-writer-lock window, and the re-completion is charge-deduped by Stripe; data corruption of the durable record is real but narrow.

findings.location: complete_session lines 287-290 + store.fulfill_once line 365-389 (PRIMARY KEY session_id)
findings.title: IDOR at /complete: a second account completing the same session is charged but credited nothing (fulfill_once keyed by session_id, not account)
findings.evidence: acp_fulfillments PRIMARY KEY is session_id (store.py:93); fulfill_once dedups on session_id alone (365-389). With no ownership check (U017), bearer B can /complete account A's READY session: charge at router.py:262 executes against B's delegated token (B pays), but fulfill_once returns False (already fulfilled by A) -> the `elif order is None` branch (323-330) returns 200 completed with 'already_fulfilled' and NO credits/order. B paid, got nothing.
findings.rationale: Distinct money-loss consequence of the IDOR: charge and credit dedup on different keys (payer vs session). Confirmed.

findings.title: subscription.updated with missing/None status silently skips status write, risking stale access grants
findings.evidence: billing.py:248 `status = ... obj.get('status')`; store.py:213 only writes non-None fields, so status=None leaves status column unchanged while plan is updated from price. entitlements_for keys access off status (plans.py:184-186).
findings.rationale: Real but its impact is gated by U027: entitlements_for is never called, so the stale-status risk has no runtime effect today. Stripe subscription objects always carry a status, so the None case requires a malformed payload. Severity high->low.

findings.title: _resolve_account can create a brand-new free account on subscription events, fragmenting customer state
findings.evidence: Same fall-through at billing.py:218. When account_id is None this mints a random acct_ id (confirmed: create_account(account_id=None) -> 'acct_'+token_hex, store.py:155).
findings.rationale: Same root-cause as U028 (orphan account on unresolvable subscription event); adds the legitimate event-ordering trigger (subscription.* before checkout.session.completed).

findings.title: Checkout/portal accept arbitrary account_id with no ownership/auth check — an attacker can attach a subscription to or open a billing portal for someone else's account_id
findings.evidence: create_portal (billing.py:147-167) takes req.account_id (PortalRequest, no auth field), looks up get_account(req.account_id) -> stripe_customer_id (152) and opens a portal session for it. No api_key / ownership check anywhere in the function; grep confirms no auth dependency in billing.py routes.
findings.rationale: Confirmed IDOR/authz gap: any caller who knows/guesses an account_id can open another customer's billing portal. Severity high->medium: account_ids are acct_+token_hex(12) = 96 bits (store.py:155), not enumerable, so exploitation requires leaking a specific id; still a real missing-authorization defect. Not corroborated in this slice.

findings.title: _resolve_account silently creates a NEW free account when metadata account_id and customer mapping both miss — can orphan the real grant
findings.evidence: Same code path billing.py:212-218. account_id present but get_account returns None AND no customer mapping -> create_account(account_id=account_id) (confirmed creates with that explicit id, store.py:155).
findings.rationale: Same root-cause as U028; the 'stale/foreign id orphans the grant' angle is one instance of the orphan-creation defect.

findings.title: checkout.session.completed grants 'active' without verifying payment_status; an unpaid/async checkout can grant access
findings.evidence: billing.py:236 `status='active'` is hard-coded for checkout.session.completed; obj.get('payment_status') is never read (grep of billing.py for payment_status returns nothing).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: a completed-but-unpaid (async payment) session writes status=active. Severity high->medium: impact is bounded because entitlements_for is never consulted (U027), so the granted 'active' has no runtime effect on triage; a later invoice.paid/subscription event would update the row regardless.

findings.title: invoice.paid blindly sets status='active', resurrecting canceled/expired subscriptions
findings.evidence: billing.py:268-269 `if acct: store.set_subscription(account_id=acct['id'], status='active')` — no current-state check, no plan. store.py:213 skips None plan so plan stays at prior value.
findings.rationale: Confirmed mechanically. Severity high->low: the finding itself notes entitlements_for treats active+free as Free floor, AND entitlements_for is never called at all (U027), so the resurrection has no runtime entitlement effect. Out-of-order reactivation is real data corruption but currently dead.

findings.title: Attacker-influenced subscription metadata.account_id is used verbatim as the new account primary key
findings.evidence: billing.py:218 passes meta_account verbatim to create_account, which uses it as the PK only when falsy-check fails (store.py:155 `account_id or 'acct_'+...`). Verified: create_account with an existing id raises IntegrityError(UNIQUE accounts.id) — so it cannot overwrite/squat an EXISTING account; it can only pre-create a not-yet-existing id.
findings.rationale: Mechanical 'metadata used verbatim as PK' is true, but the 'account-id squatting / write access over identity table' framing is overstated: metadata is set server-side at checkout (billing.py:132) and the event must pass signature verification (182-185), so an attacker cannot supply arbitrary metadata without forging a signed event. Same code line as U028 but a distinct (security) angle, so partial rather than duplicate.

findings.title: set_subscription can hit the UNIQUE(stripe_customer_id) constraint, raising 500 and an infinite Stripe retry loop
findings.evidence: store.py:45 stripe_customer_id TEXT UNIQUE. Verified at runtime: set_subscription(account_id=B, customer_id=cus_X) when cus_X already links account A raises sqlite3.IntegrityError(UNIQUE constraint failed: accounts.stripe_customer_id). set_subscription (store.py:217-221) does not catch it; it propagates to billing.py:197 -> HTTP 500; event not marked (203 unreached) -> Stripe redelivers indefinitely.
findings.rationale: Confirmed stuck-event / infinite-retry path. Severity high->medium: requires a specific cross-linking scenario (same customer resolved to two different account ids via metadata vs customer mapping), which is uncommon but reachable via the orphan-create paths.

findings.title: Receipt signs the gate's own self-reported summary, not an independently re-derived one — attests 'server emitted these counts', not 'counts are true'
findings.evidence: signed_body (83) signs triage_result.get('audit') = audit.summary() (service.py 133), and assert_no_violations (audit.py 255) reads the same self.violations list — both populated by record(). The claim 'same in-memory counters' is accurate. HOWEVER core/audit.py 95-114 (_independently_protected) shows record() re-derives protection via is_protected_sender on the RAW sender (178: union with gate flag) and disposition comes from what the provider ACTUALLY did (archived/moved observed, 179-184), NOT from the gate's post-mutation LabelAction. So the summary is NOT 'one component reading its own output' — it is an independent observer's counts.
findings.rationale: Real but materially overstated: the finding frames the signature as attesting only the gate's self-report, but the audit IS the independent re-derivation (the whole point of core/audit.py). The signature legitimately attests the independent observer's counts; the only true gap is 'no THIRD divergent derivation', which is a much weaker claim than 'air of authority it does not have'. Downgraded high->low.

findings.title: fulfill_once credits the account but does NOT verify the account row exists; an INSERT-OR-IGNORE fulfillment + UPDATE on a nonexistent account silently credits nothing yet returns True
findings.evidence: fulfill_once (365-389): INSERT OR IGNORE into acp_fulfillments (rowcount checked at 380), then UPDATE accounts SET run_credits=...+? WHERE id=? (383-387) with NO rowcount check, then returns True (389). Caller router.py:288 creates the account first via create_account if get_account_by_api_key is None, so in the normal ACP path the id exists — but a stale/wrong account_id (e.g. deleted account) credits zero rows yet returns True and mints a receipt claiming runs_credited=N.
findings.rationale: Confirmed logic gap (UPDATE rowcount unchecked, lying receipt possible); mitigated because the ACP caller guarantees the account exists, so medium.

findings.title: 60s stale-claim re-grant of an idempotency key can double-execute a non-idempotent action (acp.create mints a duplicate, orphaned session)
findings.evidence: idempotency_begin lines 337-344 rebind request_hash + created_at and return {'state':'new'} once _now()-created_at > 60s (IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT, line 103). create_session (router.py:167-190) generates a fresh _new_session_id() per 'new' return and is NOT money-deduped (only acp.complete is session-keyed for charge+fulfill). So a slow-but-alive create past 60s can yield a second orphaned session.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the 60s window cannot distinguish slow from crashed, and create/update are not downstream-deduped; real but requires a >60s in-flight request, so medium.

findings.location: mark_event_processed 253-260 + api/billing.py 191-203 (separate commits on shared connection)
findings.title: Webhook dedup mark and the handler's side-effect writes are not in one transaction, so a crash between them re-processes the event (or marks-then-loses it)
findings.evidence: billing.py flow: is_event_processed (191) → _handle_event (196, its own committed set_subscription/create_account writes) → mark_event_processed (203, separate INSERT+commit). No enclosing transaction. _resolve_account (billing.py:218) calls create_account when no customer mapping exists, which is NOT idempotent (can fork a second account on redelivery if the prior run committed its writes but crashed before mark_event_processed).
findings.rationale: Confirmed non-atomic dedup-vs-effect boundary; the account-fork window is narrow (requires crash between the two commits) so medium not high.

findings.title: combined_text space-joins sender and subject, letting subject tokens satisfy sender/domain-anchored patterns (the .gov-anchor root cause)
findings.evidence: core/models.py:58 returns f"{self.sender} {self.subject}".lower(). The cross-file corroboration holds: core/rules.py:1007-1008 builds the identical inline string and _find_best_label (rules.py:1032) does re.search(pattern, combined_text). BUT grep shows combined_text the PROPERTY has zero production callers (only core/models.py:56 defines it; rules.py inlines its own copy) — so hardening the property is moot. Critically, the protected-archive gate does NOT use this string: is_protected_sender (rules.py:797) parses the sender header via _iter_sender_domains and the .gov terminal-label rule operates on the PARSED sender domain, never on combined_text. So a subject-injected 'irs.gov' affects categorization/priority labeling only, not the never-archive gate.
findings.rationale: Real field-conflation in the categorization path, but severity high is inflated: the protected/archive gate is sender-parsed and immune, and the model property is dead code; blast radius is mis-labeling, not mis-archiving.

findings.title: IMAP archive COPY+\Deleted+EXPUNGE per message calls mailbox-wide expunge in a loop; unchecked STORE and expunge can delete unrelated flagged mail or leave duplicates
findings.evidence: Restates the unscoped expunge-in-loop, and additionally notes the STORE result is unchecked (lines 75-77 -> True). The unchecked-STORE portion overlaps U358/U359.
findings.rationale: Primary claim duplicates U006; its unchecked-STORE sub-point is covered by U359.

findings.title: COPY+Delete+EXPUNGE fallback expunges entire mailbox, not just the message
findings.evidence: Same unscoped `imap.expunge()` claim, lines 70-77. Suggested fix `imap.uid('EXPUNGE', uid)` is the correct UIDPLUS remedy.
findings.rationale: Duplicate of U006.

findings.location: triage tool lines 109-127 (dry_run default True, no auth/metering)
findings.title: MCP triage tool exposes destructive mailbox mutation (dry_run=False) with no authentication, metering, or credit consumption
findings.evidence: Same MCP-layer no-auth/no-metering/no-credit gap as U079; explicitly framed as a 'NEW location of the same class.' service.run_triage at server.py:132 with no plan/credit check.
findings.rationale: Self-declared same-class duplicate of U079.

findings.title: Standard-IMAP archive does COPY then unconditional +FLAGS \\Deleted but never EXPUNGE; message remains until expunge and is destructive without confirmation
findings.evidence: Lines 318-323: COPY to '"Archive"', then on OK `STORE +FLAGS (\Deleted)` then `return True` with no expunge(). Message remains in INBOX flagged \Deleted (hidden by many clients, looks gone) yet base.apply_actions records did_leave_inbox=True (base.py:353-355). 'Archive' is a hardcoded literal with no ensure_label_exists — COPY to a nonexistent folder returns NO → returns False safely. Contrast icloud_triage.py:64-76 which uses UID MOVE then COPY+\Deleted+EXPUNGE.
findings.rationale: Real disposition mis-report (no expunge, reports archived) — but no immediate data loss; medium not high.

findings.location: apply_label / move script line 263-270; get_message_details 211-228; star 295-301; unstar 311-317; mark_read 360-366; mark_unread 376-382
findings.title: AppleScript injection / breakage via unescaped message_id interpolated as a raw literal
findings.evidence: Focuses on `whose id is {message_id}` as a bare unquoted token across the mutating methods; msg_id = parts[0] of tab-split osascript output (line 190), typed str, no `^[0-9]+$` validation. Same interpolation sink as U090.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U090, narrowed to the message_id token.

findings.title: AppleScript injection via unescaped mailbox/label/account names with embedded double-quotes
findings.evidence: Double-quote/backslash breaking out of `mailbox "{label}"`, `of account "{self.account}"`, `name:"{label}"`. label_override from config vip_senders does reach apply_label (rules.py:986-987 -> cli.py:237), so labels are config-controllable. Same sink as U090.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U090; the quoted-string-break variant of the same interpolation bug.

findings.location: list_messages, lines 428-434 ($orderby 'receivedDateTime desc' combined with arbitrary $filter)
findings.title: Graph rejects $orderby + $filter on different properties with HTTP 400 — the primary 'isRead eq false' pending/list query fails
findings.evidence: Code always sets $orderby=receivedDateTime desc (431) and, when query non-empty, $filter=query (433-434). cli.py:642 passes query='isRead eq false' for Outlook pending. Whether Graph 400s on $orderby+$filter when the orderby property is not a prefix of the filter is a documented Graph constraint but is API-runtime behavior I cannot execute here.
findings.rationale: Plausible and the code path is exactly as described, but confirming the 400 requires hitting live Graph (the exact rule varies by property/index); the swallow-to-empty consequence (U097) is independently confirmed. needs_runtime.

findings.title: ruff and mypy run advisory-only (set +e) — lint/type regressions can never fail CI
findings.evidence: ci.yml:35 ruff step `set +e`, captures ${PIPESTATUS[0]}, only writes a `::warning::` and step-summary on nonzero — step always exits 0. ci.yml:51 mypy step identical pattern. Comment at lines 46/62 explicitly says 'pytest remains the hard gate'. Combined with the 3.11/3.12-only matrix (U007), no automated gate catches a 3.9 typing/syntax regression.
findings.rationale: Accurate: both lint and type steps are non-blocking by design; pytest on two non-3.9 interpreters is the only hard gate.

findings.title: Cloudflare smoke test validates only a thin happy-path slice and would not catch the method/route/contract divergences
findings.evidence: ci.yml:111-119 smoke test only asserts: /health contains '"status":"ok"', /app/ 2xx, /v1/billing/plans contains '"plans"', and a single PROTECTED sender (courts.ca.gov) returns '"protected":true'. Confirmed in worker.mjs that no /.well-known/agent.json, /server.json, or /openapi.json route exists (grep of pathname checks at lines 226-307 shows none), so those would 404 untested. Worker line 1 defines `const PROTECTED = new Set([...])` but senderCheck (lines 82+) uses `.includes("courts.ca.gov") || .endsWith(".gov")` and `.includes("chase.com")` — the PROTECTED set is indeed unused. No non-protected sender, wrong-method/405, /v1/triage vs preview, or /v1/audit/{run_id} is exercised.
findings.rationale: Coverage claims verify against the worker source; this is a real thin-slice smoke test. Downgraded to low: it is a post-deploy observability smoke test on a demo domain, not a unit/contract gate — limited blast radius.

findings.title: AGENTS.md describes only the legacy Gmail scripts and omits the entire unified CLI / multi-provider / API architecture
findings.evidence: AGENTS.md lines 5-16 name gmail_labeler.py as 'core labeling logic', final_sweep.py, recount.py, and install via `pip install google-api-python-client google-auth-httplib2 google-auth-oauthlib`; line 26 states 'No unit test suite'. On disk the current architecture exists: cli.py (41KB), core/, providers/, api/ directories; tests/ contains conftest.py + test_acp.py/test_api.py/test_audit.py/test_billing.py/test_config.py/test_mailapp.py/test_mcp.py; pyproject.toml has [tool.pytest.ini_options]; .github/workflows/ci.yml references pytest. README.md/CLAUDE.md describe the unified multi-provider CLI. AGENTS.md presents the superseded legacy layout and falsely claims no tests.
findings.rationale: Every concrete claim verified against current disk: AGENTS.md is stale, points agents at wrong entry points, and falsely denies the existing pytest suite/CI gate; impact is documentation/onboarding only (no runtime defect), so medium holds.

findings.title: Doc/install mismatch: CLAUDE.md tells users to check 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler' but deploy installs com.user.mail_automation
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md:104 = 'launchctl list | grep com.user.gmail_labeler'. deploy.sh installs/bootstraps com.user.mail_automation (line 51 LABEL="com.user.mail_automation", line 60 launchctl bootstrap "$AGENT_ID" "$PLIST_DEST" where PLIST is com.user.mail_automation.plist), and line 57 explicitly boots out the OLD com.user.gmail_labeler (confirming rename). deploy.sh:74 prints 'Status: launchctl list | grep mail_automation'. README.md:553 = 'launchctl list | grep mail_automation'.
findings.rationale: Stale label name in CLAUDE.md: scheduler was renamed gmail_labeler -> mail_automation everywhere (deploy.sh, README) but CLAUDE.md line 104 was not updated, so its status grep matches nothing; severity lowered to low since impact is a misleading status check in agent-guidance doc, not a functional break.

findings.title: Dockerfile COPY . . ships logs, state, caches, and any local secrets into the image
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U009: blanket `COPY . .` (line 12) with no .dockerignore. Confirms the additional bloat/state angle — working tree has multi-MB logs (gmail_labeler.log 3.8MB, auto_drain*.log ~1MB), labeler_state.json (this one IS git-tracked, contrary to the implied secrecy), __pycache__, .venv, .git, .ruff_cache/.pytest_cache, .serena all present and would be copied. credentials.json/token caches were NOT found in-tree but would leak if a dev created them locally.
findings.rationale: Real but identical root-cause defect (missing .dockerignore + COPY . .) as U009; merged as duplicate, severity left at medium for the bloat/state framing.

findings.location: line 2 (Python-3.10+ badge) and line 241 (Prerequisites: Python 3.10 or later) vs CLAUDE.md:278 and api/app.py:34 / requirements-mcp.txt:2-3 / Dockerfile:7
findings.title: Three-way contradiction on the minimum Python version (3.9 vs 3.10)
findings.evidence: README.md:2 badge 'Python-3.10+' and :241 'Python 3.10 or later'. CLAUDE.md:278 'Python 3.9+'. api/app.py:33-34 comment 'the core API stays importable on 3.9'; requirements-mcp.txt:2 'the official `mcp` SDK requires Python >=3.10, while the core engine stays importable on 3.9+'; Dockerfile:7 'The core stays 3.9-importable'. pyproject.toml contains ONLY [tool.pytest.ini_options] and [tool.* pythonpath] — no [project] requires-python.
findings.rationale: Genuine three-way doc inconsistency on the Python floor with no authoritative requires-python pin; but it is documentation/version drift, not a runtime defect, so medium is inflated to low.

findings.location: build_feed lines 33-64 (and committed acp/product_feed.json); no test references build_feed
findings.title: No test asserts feed price equals the amount actually charged — a price/unit drift between feed and /complete charge would ship undetected
findings.evidence: grep for build_feed/feed.json/product_feed across tests/*.py returns nothing; tests/test_acp.py only has a mock `charge` (line 26), no assertion tying feed price to grand_total. Two independent paths confirmed: feed emits amount_cents verbatim (feed.py:48), charge derives from amount_cents via build_line_items (models.py:86-94) -> grand_total -> charge (router.py:257-262).
findings.rationale: Claim is literally true (no test covers build_feed or feed/charge price parity), but this is a test-coverage gap not a runtime defect; medium is inflated for a system where feed and charge currently share the same source value.

findings.title: Pydantic body validation runs before the auth/version gate, so malformed bodies bypass the gate and return a non-spec error envelope
findings.evidence: Handlers declare typed body params (models.CheckoutCreate at 167, CheckoutUpdate at 201, CheckoutComplete at 236), which FastAPI validates BEFORE the handler body runs _gate. Only ACPError has a handler (register_acp_handlers, router.py:60); grep confirms no RequestValidationError handler anywhere. So a malformed body returns FastAPI's default 422 {'detail':[...]} (off-spec envelope) and never reaches the Bearer/API-Version gate, echoing the offending input to an ungated caller.
findings.rationale: Both claims (spec non-conformance + validate-before-gate ordering) confirmed. Medium is right: information echoed is the caller's own body, not stored secrets.

findings.title: Only fetches up to SAMPLE_SIZE but messages.list maxResults=2000 exceeds Gmail's 500 cap, silently returning fewer
findings.evidence: Line 175: service.users().messages().list(userId='me', labelIds=[label_id], maxResults=SAMPLE_SIZE).execute() with SAMPLE_SIZE=2000 (line 18). No nextPageToken pagination loop. Gmail API clamps maxResults to 500. Line 98 chunks min(len(messages), SAMPLE_SIZE)=min(~500,2000)=~500. Line 68 prints the actual len(messages), and line 136/151/152 divide by len(msg_data), so percentages use the real (~500) count.
findings.rationale: The under-fetch (no pagination, ~500 cap vs intended 2000) is real, but the 'report claims a 2000-message analysis it never performed' part is false — the report prints the actual count; downgraded to low.

findings.title: Receipts are persisted (and run_ids minted) even for dry_run previews, mixing speculative previews into the durable audit ledger
findings.evidence: triage_preview (app.py:88) calls _run(req, dry_run=True), which unconditionally mints run_id (line 127) and calls receipts.persist(run_id, result) (line 129) with no account_id. persist defaults account_id=None (receipts.py:88-89) and save_receipt stores NULL (store.py:269/278). receipts.py:106 swallows ledger write failures, so growth is best-effort. Because the endpoint is unauthenticated (U001), preview writes are unbounded.
findings.rationale: Real: dry-run previews do persist NULL-account receipts. But it is a low-severity storage/ledger-hygiene issue (parasitic on the U001 auth gap), not medium — and list-can-never-return is moot since no list endpoint is wired (see U269).

findings.title: current_period_end is read but a None/expired value never downgrades entitlements — and _period_end only set on subscription.* events, never on invoice.paid
findings.evidence: invoice.paid (billing.py:268-269) passes no current_period_end. entitlements_for (plans.py:184-193) never reads current_period_end — and is itself never called (U027). _period_end is only passed on subscription.* events (billing.py:261).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: there is no code path where a passed current_period_end revokes access. Severity medium->low: the field is dead data and entitlements are not consulted at all, so no current runtime impact.

findings.title: entitlements_for trusts account['run_credits'] via int(...) without guarding non-int/None stored values, and is dead code on the live path
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U029 (entitlements_for has no caller — confirmed by grep). The finding itself concedes the int() path is 'currently safe' (schema run_credits INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, store.py:51) and that the status lowercasing 'correctly excludes' canceled/past_due. Its substantive claim (resolver exists but no endpoint consults it) is U029.
findings.rationale: Primary claim is identical to U029 (dead entitlements_for); the int()/status sub-claims are self-acknowledged non-issues.

findings.title: monthly_run_cap is consumed only by the marketing-artifact generator, never by runtime enforcement
findings.evidence: grep 'monthly_run_cap' --include='*.py': consumed only at scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:46 (marketing render) and inside public_dict()/entitlements_for() in plans.py. No run-counting comparison exists. Confirmed no usage-accounting storage: store.py schema (41-98) has run_credits and current_period_end but no per-month counter; grep for runs_used/usage_count/monthly_runs/period_start/reset_at across the repo => NO USAGE TABLE FOUND.
findings.rationale: Monthly run cap is advertised in the public catalog but never enforced, and the data needed to enforce it is not recorded anywhere. Real money-path gap; medium is appropriate (advertised limit with no enforcement substrate).

findings.title: Metered per-run billing advertised but no Stripe Meter event is ever emitted
findings.evidence: grep -rn 'meter_event|triage_run|meter_events|billing.meter' --include='*.py' . returns a single hit: the literal 'meter_event_name': 'triage_run' string at plans.py:136. No stripe.billing.meter_events / meter reporting call exists anywhere (service.py, app.py, billing.py, mcp_server). The $0.01/run metered path is declared but no usage is ever reported to Stripe.
findings.rationale: Metered revenue mechanism is unimplemented — runs are never counted or reported to a Stripe meter. Corroborated flag echoed from slice.

findings.location: entitlements_for return dict, line 192 (run_credits) — combined with no caller
findings.title: entitlements_for surfaces run_credits but, being uncalled, the credit balance is read by nothing for any access decision — deeper instance than round 1's consume_credit note
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U029: entitlements_for (the sole reader of run_credits for an entitlement decision, line 192) is uncalled. consume_credit (the debit) is called only by tests/test_store.py:39,42 (confirmed by grep). Both the read (plans.entitlements_for) and debit (store.consume_credit) halves are orphaned.
findings.rationale: Real observation but the same dead-code defect as U029 viewed from the credit-balance read side; not an independent bug.

findings.title: run_triage is the single triage chokepoint and accepts no caller identity — enforcement has no insertion point that is actually used
findings.evidence: run_triage signature (service.py:70-81) has only provider/query/limit/dry_run/remove_label/tier_routing/vip_only/audit_path/provider_factory — no account_id/api_key/entitlements. Both callers pass no identity: api/app.py:97-107 (_run) and mcp_server/server.py:129-138 (_triage). Repo-wide grep: consume_credit (defined api/store.py:235) and entitlements_for (defined api/plans.py:175) have ZERO runtime callers — only tests/test_store.py and the docs findings file reference them. service.py never imports api.store or api.plans.
findings.rationale: The structural premise is real: run_triage is the sole triage chokepoint and exposes no caller identity, so cap/credit checks cannot fire on the live path — the metering functions are genuinely dead. Medium is fair for the structural locus.

findings.title: run_credits is a write-only balance — granted by ACP fulfillment and add_credits, never read for an access decision
findings.evidence: run_credits incremented by add_credits (227) and fulfill_once (384), surfaced by plans.entitlements_for (192) and plans.py:192 in get-account response. grep confirms entitlements_for has NO production caller (only def at plans.py:175 + comment). consume_credit uncalled (see U033). No code reads run_credits to gate a triage run.
findings.rationale: Confirmed write-only balance; this is the read-side counterpart to U033's write-side, both real. Kept distinct (read vs debit) at medium.

findings.title: Single shared connection serialized by one RLock negates WAL read concurrency (docstring claim is false)
findings.evidence: Single self._conn (line 127); _fetch_one (426) and _fetch_all (435) both acquire the same self._lock RLock (129) that every write holds. WAL concurrency requires separate connections, so all access is fully serialized; the docstring at 21-24 ('WAL keeps reads concurrent with the writer') is inaccurate for this design.
findings.rationale: Confirmed throughput/latency claim + false docstring rationale; no correctness/data impact and writes are tiny per the design, so low.

findings.title: No PRAGMA busy_timeout set: any second connection to the DB file fails immediately with 'database is locked'
findings.evidence: __init__ sets only PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL (134); no PRAGMA busy_timeout. SQLite default busy_timeout is 0. A second connection (extra worker, backup tool, CLI, checkpoint contention) would get immediate SQLITE_BUSY.
findings.rationale: Confirmed hardening gap; impact only when a second connection touches the file (not the documented single-conn design), so low.

findings.title: No foreign-key constraints or enforcement: receipts/acp_sessions/acp_fulfillments accept arbitrary account_id, allowing orphaned ledger and credit rows
findings.evidence: _SCHEMA: receipts.account_id (65), acp_sessions.account_id (86), acp_fulfillments.account_id (94) are plain TEXT with no REFERENCES; __init__ never issues PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON. Orphan rows are accepted at the DB layer.
findings.rationale: Confirmed missing referential integrity (schema root of U124); its concrete money impact is already captured by U124, so the schema finding alone is low.

findings.location: build_agent_manifest lines 17-51 (protocols/agentic_commerce, oauth_scopes); cf. api/plans.py METERED_ADDON line 130, gen_commerce_artifacts.py
findings.title: Agent discovery manifest advertises a metered/credit-pack commerce surface and verified-safe paid runs that the engine never meters or debits — false self-description to autonomous agents
findings.evidence: Confirmed: 'triage_run' meter event appears ONLY as a string literal (api/plans.py:136) — grep for triage_run/meter_event/create_meter_event/MeterEvent across api/, mcp_server/, scripts/ finds zero emission sites. consume_credit is defined (api/store.py:235) but has NO callers (grep consume_credit returns only the def). api/service.run_triage (service.py:70-81) and mcp_server/server._triage (server.py:129-135) take no account/credit param and never debit — so the MCP `triage` tool runs free and unlimited. ACP credits the buyer via fulfill_once (acp/router.py:290, store.py:365) but that balance is never spent. gen_commerce_artifacts.py:24,99-104 imports and uses build_agent_manifest/build_llms_txt, so the committed static files share the same false advertisement. The advertised surfaces (manifest lines 33-37 checkout, line 42 /v1/billing/plans -> METERED_ADDON '$0.01/triage run' + CREDIT_PACKS, plans.py:138 'This is how AI agents pay for verified-safe triage') are structurally unmet by the runtime.
findings.rationale: Core integrity defect (unmetered/never-debited commerce surface advertised to agents) is real and corroborated; overstated only on the auth sub-claim — ACP uses an API-key-as-bearer gate (acp/router.py:79-82), not OAuth scopes, and MCP has no auth at all, so 'oauth_scopes:[] understates the auth surface' is loosely framed. Hence partial.

findings.title: Bare try with no on-error swallows all move failures silently
findings.evidence: Lines 16-20 are `try` / if/move / `end try` with no `on error` clause and no logging. Any failure of `move msg to targetMailbox` (missing target, account offline, permission) is silently discarded; the user gets zero diagnostics and believes archiving succeeded.
findings.rationale: Bare try-without-on-error genuinely swallows every move failure with no signal; medium is appropriate for a silent-failure on a destructive op.

findings.title: Secret passed as 1Password CLI argument is exposed in process listing / shell history
findings.evidence: Line 111: cmd = ["op", "item", "edit", item, f"{field}={value}"] — the secret `value` is an argv element passed to subprocess.run (lines 33-38, no shell). The identical pattern exists in gmail_auth.py:57 (_op_item_edit, called by store_token_info at line 154, the live Gmail token write-back). sensitive=True at line 117 only suppresses stderr in error text (line 43-44), it does not affect argv.
findings.rationale: Plaintext token JSON is on the `op` process argv, visible via ps/proc to any local user for the subprocess lifetime; real for the write-back path.

findings.title: Secret value passed as a command-line argument is exposed in process listing / argv
findings.evidence: Same defect described from _run_op's vantage: the argv built by op_item_edit (line 111) embeds the secret and _run_op executes it. Read paths correctly only receive secrets via stdout. Root cause identical to U157.
findings.rationale: Same argv-secret-exposure defect as U157, attributed to the executor rather than the builder.

findings.title: No test coverage for the credential write/store boundary (store_json_secret, op_item_edit, FileNotFound/CalledProcessError paths)
findings.evidence: tests/ contains test_acp/api/audit/billing/config/mailapp/mcp/models/protected_enforcement/receipts/rules/state/store/web.py and no test references `onepassword`, `op_item`, or `store_json_secret` (grep returned only the definition site). The credential write boundary and _run_op error branches are untested.
findings.rationale: Test gap is real, but it is a test-quality/maintainability concern, not a runtime defect — downgraded from medium to low.

findings.location: extract_domain() line 61-64; classify_domain() lines 66-88; callback line 121-129
findings.title: Bare 'except:' swallows all errors in domain extraction and batch callback, plus a too-loose keyword classifier
findings.evidence: Bundles three real issues: (1) bare `except:` at line 63 returning None; (2) callback `if exception: return` at line 122 silently dropping failed-fetch messages; (3) substring keyword classifier with first-dict-order-match-wins (lines 73-76) so 'account' (Finance/Banking, iterated first) beats a later 'verify' (Tech/Security). All verified in source.
findings.rationale: All three sub-claims are accurate. This is a multi-issue bundle whose classifier component overlaps U044/U048 and whose silent-failure component overlaps U533/U535; kept as confirmed (its own composite) at medium.

findings.title: Bare except: swallows all errors and the domain split is fragile against display-name From headers
findings.evidence: Bare `except:` at line 63 confirmed (catches BaseException). The relay-collapse claim is plausible: extract_domain does only `sender.split('@')[-1].strip('>]').lower()` with no MIME/relay decode, so a relay sender keys by the relay domain. But the specific 'icloud Hide-My-Email' example is speculative for a Gmail-only script (TARGET label Misc/Other, gmail_auth) and the parse for the common 'Name <user@bank.com>' case actually works correctly as the description concedes.
findings.rationale: Bare-except smell is real but harmless (str.split won't raise); the relay-bucket misgrouping is a genuine but narrow design limitation, overstated as a protected-sender breach. Low.

findings.title: batchModify and list calls have no error handling, no pagination, and an unchecked remove-label can erase intent
findings.evidence: Confirmed: run_sweep has NO try/except — `service.users().messages().list(...).execute()` (98-102) and `batchModify(...).execute()` (117) will raise HttpError on rate-limit/5xx and abort the sweep mid-rule with no state persistence (no StateManager used here at all). No backoff, unlike GmailProvider. Also confirmed line 76 `results['labels']` direct subscript -> KeyError risk. The bundled infinite-loop-on-None-remove sub-claim is the same overstated corner as U049/U050.
findings.rationale: The error-handling/no-backoff defect is real and accurately stated; the infinite-loop rider is overstated but does not negate the core finding — kept at medium (standalone legacy script, manual invocation).

findings.title: --limit counts built actions, not messages scanned; vip-only/protected/skip runs fetch far more than limit
findings.evidence: Loop guard `while processed_this_run < limit` (183) and `processed_this_run += len(actions)` (293). `actions` excludes protected senders (continue at 225), non-VIP in vip_only (continue at 230), and None details (continue at 207). batch_limit = min(limit - processed_this_run, 100) (185) keeps requesting full batches and page_token advances (298,313), so the loop scans far more than `limit` messages when many are filtered out. Docstring (line 156) says 'Max messages to process per run'.
findings.rationale: Confirmed off-by-semantics: with --vip-only over a large mailbox, processed_this_run only counts built actions, so the run can walk the entire mailbox (extra API calls + 1s sleeps per batch) before reaching limit. Diverges from documented behavior; runaway-cost risk is real but bounded by mailbox size, so medium.

findings.title: health connect/disconnect not in try/finally; failure after connect leaks the connection
findings.evidence: Lines 504-517: `try: provider.connect(); healthy, message = provider.health_check(); provider.disconnect()`. If health_check() raises, control jumps to `except Exception` (515) and disconnect() never runs. Unlike cmd_label/cmd_report/cmd_summary/etc. which use `with provider:` (439, 475, 542) guaranteeing __exit__/disconnect.
findings.rationale: Real: health uses manual connect/disconnect without try/finally, so an exception after connect leaks the IMAP socket / OAuth session. Downgraded medium->low: cmd_health is a one-shot CLI invocation that exits immediately after (process teardown reclaims the socket); the leak window is a single short-lived process, not a long-running service.

findings.title: cmd_pending omits VIP config loading, so pending output's is_vip/tier reflects only built-in VIPs, inconsistent with other commands
findings.evidence: cmd_label (422-423), cmd_summary (523-524), cmd_vip (718-719), cmd_escalate (828-829) each call load_config()+apply_vip_senders_from_config. cmd_pending (622-631) jumps straight to get_provider with no load_config/apply_vip_senders_from_config call. Line 666/674 then compute cat_result.is_vip via categorize_with_tier, which reflects only built-in VIPs without the config-applied ones.
findings.rationale: Confirmed inconsistency: pending's is_vip flag ignores user-configured VIP senders that summary/vip/escalate honor. Real but cosmetic — affects only the displayed VIP star marker on pending items, not any apply action. Low.

findings.title: cmd_escalate trusts age_hours=0 without detecting that the provider supplied no date
findings.evidence: age_hours = calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date) (883); escalate_by_age(email_age_hours=age_hours,...) (886). calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 when date is None (rules.py:1163-1166). Verified gmail (providers/gmail.py:204-209, 286-291), imap (261-266), mailapp (240-245) construct EmailMessage with NO date= kwarg (defaults None, core/models.py:48). Only outlook sets date (outlook.py:464,515). So for gmail/imap/mailapp age_hours is always 0, escalate_by_age never escalates, yet summary prints 'Messages escalated: 0' (937) with no warning.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: for 3 of 4 providers escalate is a guaranteed no-op presented as a successful triage. The command gives false assurance with no 'date unsupported' warning. Medium — escalate is a documented user-facing command and the silent zero is misleading.

findings.title: cmd_pending age column is always 0h and sort key is degenerate for non-Outlook providers
findings.evidence: age_hours = calculate_email_age_hours(msg.date) (667); displayed as '{age_hours:.0f}h' (694, 704). msg.date is None for gmail/imap/mailapp (see U166 evidence), so age_hours==0 -> uniform '0h'. Sort key (x['tier'], -x['age_hours']) (680) reduces to tier-only ordering since -0 is equal for all.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: pending's age column is uniformly 0h and the secondary 'oldest-first' sort is degenerate on the 3 non-Outlook providers. Same root cause as U166 (providers don't populate date) but a distinct symptom in a different command. Display-only, no data harm — low.

findings.title: Type confusion: corrupt state with non-int total_processed crashes run on first batch
findings.evidence: total_processed = state.get_total() (173). get_total() (core/state.py:101-103) returns self.state.get('total_processed', 0) with no type coercion; the default only applies when the key is absent. Line 295 `total_processed += len(actions)` then does str + int -> TypeError if the persisted value was a string. _load (state.py:46-56) does no schema validation, only JSON parse.
findings.rationale: Confirmed mechanically: a hand-corrupted state file with total_processed as a string crashes on the first processed batch. Downgraded medium->low: requires an externally-corrupted state file (the code itself always writes an int via save()), so it is a robustness gap against manual tampering, not a defect in normal operation. The exception is caught at 320-324, state re-saved (re-corrupting), then re-raised as an unhandled traceback.

findings.title: courts.ca.gov protection is spoofable via substring — 'x@courts.ca.gov.evil.com' matches includes() and is wrongly reported protected
findings.evidence: Line 106 value.includes('courts.ca.gov') is an unanchored substring on the full lowercased From. 'phish@courts.ca.gov.attacker.com' contains 'courts.ca.gov' -> returns protected:true, tier 1 Personal/Government. core/rules.py _domain_matches (line ~676) and _gov_protected (terminal label) reject such embeds.
findings.rationale: Confirmed substring-spoof of the configured domain. Over-protect (fail-safe direction for archiving) so medium, not high — a spoof being marked protected keeps mail in inbox rather than archiving it; the real harm is the false 'highest-trust' verdict.

findings.title: chase.com protected only as a substring — 'support@chase.com.phish.net' reported protected, and any display name containing 'chase.com' triggers tier-1 Finance verdict
findings.evidence: Line 128: if (value.includes("chase.com")) -> protected tier-1 Finance/Banking for ANY From containing the substring 'chase.com' (e.g. 'alerts@chase.com.evil.com', display name 'Chase.com Security <x@phish.net>'). No boundary anchoring vs core/rules.py _domain_matches.
findings.rationale: Confirmed live substring-spoofability at line 128 (distinct from the dead line-153 disjunct in U057). Over-protect direction -> medium.

findings.title: /v1/audit returns identical 'protected_held:2, archived:1' counts for every run_id — receipts are not tied to any run and any enumerated id returns a 200 success receipt
findings.evidence: Line 282 parses runId via .split('/').pop() but never looks it up; body (288-304) is a hardcoded constant total:3/protected_held:2/archived:1 for every id. No 404 path, unlike api/receipts.py get_receipt 123-124 which raises HTTPException(404, 'receipt not found') on miss.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: any id returns identical 200 fabricated counts; the surface carries zero information about any real run. Medium (demo surface).

findings.location: triagePreview lines 172-198; reached from /v1/triage and /v1/triage/preview lines 255-263
findings.title: triagePreview fabricates archived/protected/labeled counts unrelated to any mailbox — the live 'triage' endpoints invent results from the limit parameter alone
findings.evidence: triagePreview (172-198) derives all counts from `limit` with no mailbox access (capped=min(limit,50) or 50; total=max(capped,3)). Confirmed numbers are theatre. BUT the finding's arithmetic examples are WRONG: I ran the code — limit:500 -> capped=50 -> total=50 (not 3); limit absent -> Number(undefined)=NaN -> not finite -> capped=50 -> total=50 (not 3). total=3 only occurs if a caller passes limit between 1 and 3.
findings.rationale: Core claim (fabricated counts from limit alone, no mailbox) is confirmed; the specific worked examples ('limit:500 still gets total:3', 'limit absent total=3') are factually incorrect, hence partial.

findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml lines 108-119 (smoke test) vs worker behaviors above
