Verified at runtime: `stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError` resolves to <class 'stripe._error.SignatureVerificationError'> (lazy attribute access works even though `import stripe.error` as a submodule fails). The <16 pin excludes the v16 surface change the finding worries about.
findings.rationale: The attribute exists on every version allowed by the pin; the except tuple (billing.py:183) evaluates fine. No AttributeError occurs. False positive for the actual declared/installed dependency.

findings.title: Event marked processed only after success, but a handler that raises 500 leaves the read-gate open while a redelivery may already be mid-flight
findings.evidence: Same lines 194-203 and same observation that dedup is a separate read not the atomic mark; correctly notes the non-idempotent create_account branch (billing.py:218) as the replay hazard.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause and remediation (make handle+mark one atomic unit) as U025.

findings.title: status from obj.get('status') may be None and is written verbatim; entitlements then treat unknown/None status oddly
findings.evidence: Same status=obj.get('status') (billing.py:248) -> set_subscription(status=None) skip (store.py:213). Reiterates the plan/status non-atomicity.
findings.rationale: Same None-status-skip root-cause as U115; impact dead via U027.

findings.location: _handle_event lines 248-262, plan_id_for_price + 'unknown price -> keep current plan'
findings.title: Unknown Stripe price maps to plan_id=None, which is then written as the account plan, demoting a paying customer
findings.evidence: billing.py:250-251 plan_id=plans.plan_id_for_price(price_id); plan_id_for_price (plans.py:164-172) returns None when no env price matches (stripe_price_id reads os.environ, plans.py:48). store.py:213 then skips the None plan, so the comment 'keep current plan' holds only by accident of None-skip. _checkout_plan_id (billing.py:287-291) also falls back to plan_id_for_price(None)=None, so a real purchase with no metadata plan can write plan=None.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: when STRIPE_PRICE_* env vars are unset, every subscription.updated resolves plan_id=None. Severity medium->low: checkout sets metadata.plan (billing.py:132) so _checkout_plan_id usually returns the metadata plan (line 289); and entitlements_for is never consulted (U027), so a None/stale plan column has no runtime effect today.

findings.title: invoice.paid forces status='active' but never refreshes plan, so a downgraded plan stays downgraded while invoice.paid re-activates
findings.evidence: Same invoice.paid branch billing.py:268-269 setting status=active with no plan; same out-of-order reactivation concern.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause as U120 (invoice.paid forces active without re-reading subscription state).

findings.title: current_period_end stored as raw Stripe epoch int with no timezone/units validation; entitlement code never enforces it
findings.evidence: Same observation: current_period_end stored (billing.py:261, store.py:211) but plans.entitlements_for never consults it (plans.py:175-193).
findings.rationale: Same dead-field root-cause as U145.

findings.title: Webhook _resolve_account silently creates a brand-new free account when metadata account_id does not exist
findings.evidence: Same fall-through billing.py:218 create_account when metadata account_id does not exist and no customer mapping. Correctly notes metadata is server-set at checkout so forgery needs signature bypass.
findings.rationale: Same orphan-account root-cause as U028; stale/deleted-id trigger is one instance.

findings.title: Real subscription on an unconfigured/unmatched price silently leaves the plan unchanged with no log
findings.evidence: Same plan_id_for_price(price_id)->None then set_subscription None-skip path (billing.py:250-251,259; store.py:213). Adds the 'no warning logged' observation and the broad except (KeyError,IndexError,TypeError) at billing.py:283.
findings.rationale: Same unknown-price->plan unchanged root-cause as U273; the missing-log/silent angle does not change the underlying defect. Not corroborated in slice.

findings.title: Stripe status written verbatim with no validation; non-active states (incomplete/paused) keep the paid plan stored
findings.evidence: billing.py:248 status written verbatim; the floor-to-free guard at :253 only covers ('canceled','unpaid','incomplete_expired'). A 'paused'/'incomplete' status keeps the matched paid plan_id in the plan column. The finding correctly notes entitlements_for (plans.py:184-186) treats anything outside {active,trialing} as Free floor, so live entitlement is safe today.
findings.rationale: Confirmed data-inconsistency. Severity medium->low: the finding itself acknowledges no current runtime impact; combined with U027 (entitlements never consulted) the stored-plan inconsistency is presently inert. Hazard is a future direct reader of account['plan'].

findings.location: _handle_event invoice.paid 265-270 and subscription events 240-263; no event ordering guard
findings.title: Out-of-order or replayed events (invoice.paid after subscription.deleted) can re-activate a canceled account
findings.evidence: Idempotency is per-event-id only (store.is_event_processed, billing.py:191); no created/timestamp or current_period_end comparison anywhere in _handle_event. invoice.paid sets active unconditionally (269); a reordered subscription.updated can rewrite plan=pro/status=active after a delete.
findings.rationale: Real ordering gap but overlaps U120/U274 (invoice.paid) and the entitlements are never consulted (U027), so reactivation has no runtime effect today. Kept partial (distinct framing: per-state vs per-event-id idempotency) rather than duplicate. Severity medium->low given no live impact.

findings.title: checkout.session.completed reads obj.get('subscription') which is None for one-time/payment-mode or async-payment sessions, writing subscription_id=NULL while granting active
findings.evidence: billing.py:228 subscription_id=obj.get('subscription'); when None, store.py:213 skips the stripe_subscription_id write while :236 still hard-codes status='active'. Result: active grant with no subscription linkage.
findings.rationale: Confirmed mechanically. Severity medium->low: later customer.subscription.* events resolve by metadata.account_id (billing.py:246-247), not only by subscription_id, and invoice events resolve by customer_id, so the missing subscription_id does not fully break later revocation; and entitlements are never consulted (U027).

findings.title: On cancel/unpaid the handler sets plan='free' but does NOT clear stripe_subscription_id/customer_id, and status may still be a verbatim non-canceled Stripe value
findings.evidence: Same verbatim-status + 3-status floor guard (billing.py:248,253,260). The 'never nulls stripe_subscription_id on cancel' sub-claim is true (store.py only writes non-None fields; nothing passes a clearing sentinel) but rides the same verbatim-status code region.
findings.rationale: Overlapping root-cause with U278 (non-active states keep paid plan / partial state write); the stale-subscription_id linkage is a secondary facet of the same set_subscription None-skip semantics.

findings.title: A handler that partially succeeds then raises leaves a partial DB mutation but the event un-marked, so the full retry re-applies the already-applied partial writes
findings.evidence: billing.py:191-203: dedup is read at 191 (is_event_processed), _handle_event runs at 196, and mark_event_processed is only reached at 203 after a clean return. store.py set_subscription/create_account each commit() per statement (autocommit-per-call), so a mid-sequence raise leaves earlier commits persisted and the event un-marked -> Stripe redelivers and the whole branch re-runs.
findings.rationale: Real partial-commit-then-full-replay window exists, but set_subscription is an idempotent UPDATE and _resolve_account prefers existing customer mapping, so re-creating a distinct orphan requires a narrow ordering; medium severity is overstated to low.

findings.title: invoice.* events with no resolvable customer silently no-op, so a paid invoice for an account whose customer link was never written never activates
findings.evidence: billing.py:266-270 and 272-277: both branches resolve only via store.get_account_by_customer(customer_id), guarded `if customer_id else None`, with no metadata.account_id fallback and no _resolve_account. If customer_id is absent/unmatched the branch silently returns (no log, no state change).
findings.rationale: Accurate: a paid invoice for an account with no customer link is a silent no-op. But subscription.* events (which DO carry metadata and create the link) normally precede invoice.paid, and entitlements are status-gated, so practical blast radius is small -> low.

findings.title: Subscription events read metadata.account_id off the subscription object, but checkout-set metadata lives on subscription_data — orphan-account creation on real flows
findings.evidence: billing.py:246 reads meta_account from obj.get('metadata'); checkout sets it under subscription_data.metadata (billing.py:133) which Stripe propagates to the subscription. A dashboard/portal-created subscription carries no account_id metadata, so meta_account is None and _resolve_account (208-219) falls to get_account_by_customer or, failing that, creates a free orphan (218).
findings.rationale: Correct that metadata is not guaranteed on every subscription event; however the customer_id mapping (set by an earlier checkout.session.completed) normally resolves it, so orphan creation is the fallback-of-fallback -> low.

findings.title: subscription.updated/created can move a Stripe customer_id onto a different account, and resolution prefers metadata account_id over the existing customer mapping
findings.evidence: billing.py:212-213: _resolve_account returns account_id when `account_id and store.get_account(account_id)` BEFORE consulting the customer mapping at 215. set_subscription then writes customer_id onto that account; accounts.stripe_customer_id is UNIQUE (store.py:45), so re-pointing a customer already bound to another account raises IntegrityError -> 500.
findings.rationale: The metadata-over-customer precedence is real, but account_id here is Stripe-controlled subscription metadata, not a buyer-supplied request field, so 'attacker-influenceable' overstates it; the practical effect is a 500 on UNIQUE collision, not a cross-account hijack.

findings.title: Expanded Stripe objects: obj.get('customer'/'subscription') may be a dict (when expanded), used as a string id in get_account_by_customer/set_subscription
findings.evidence: billing.py:227/245/266/273 pass obj.get('customer') directly to get_account_by_customer/set_subscription with no isinstance(str) coercion. store.py binds it as a SQL param; a dict param raises sqlite3.InterfaceError. Tests only ever feed string ids (tests/test_billing.py:64,100,139).
findings.rationale: Whether 'customer' is ever a dict depends on the Stripe API version / expand config on the live webhook endpoint — not statically determinable; default Stripe webhooks deliver an id string, so this is conditional. Duplicate of U428 (same root cause).

findings.location: cross-ref store.run_credits (ledger) vs entitlements_for line 192 — credits exposed but triage never debits
findings.title: entitlements_for returns run_credits (line 192) but no triage path reads or debits it; the number is reported to clients as a balance that never decreases
findings.evidence: grep across the repo: entitlements_for (plans.py:175) has ZERO callers — no endpoint in api/app.py returns it, and service.run_triage/app._run never read run_credits, call consume_credit, or enforce monthly_run_cap. The 'balance' is therefore not advertised to any client today.
findings.rationale: The underlying truth (run_credits is never debited by triage; consume_credit/entitlements_for are dead) is real, but the finding's framing that entitlements_for 'actively advertises this balance to clients' is wrong because nothing calls it — so the contract-violation claim is overstated to info.

findings.title: entitlements_for downgrades to free only on non-(active|trialing) status but never checks current_period_end, and defaults missing status to 'active' — an account row with no/blank status keeps paid plan
findings.evidence: plans.py:184 `status = (account.get('status') or 'active').lower()` does default blank/None to active. But: (a) the schema column is status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active' (store.py:48) so a stored blank/None is the normal default state, and (b) entitlements_for is never called (U029), so this fail-open never affects any live decision. current_period_end indeed not checked here.
findings.rationale: Logic flaw is real in isolation but its blast radius is gated by the dead-code condition — only matters if entitlements_for is ever wired in. Downgraded to low/partial.

findings.title: Account with NULL/unknown subscription status defaults to 'active', so an incomplete/unset-status subscription can be honored as paid
findings.evidence: Confirmed: billing.py:248 `status = ... obj.get('status')` can be None for incomplete subs; the downgrade list at billing.py:253 covers canceled/unpaid/incomplete_expired but NOT None/'incomplete'. store.set_subscription (store.py:206-215) only writes non-None columns, so a None status leaves the column at default 'active' (store.py:48). plans.py:184 then defaults blank to 'active'. The fail-open chain is real.
findings.rationale: Cross-file chain is verified, but the consuming function (entitlements_for) is dead (U029), so today no paid limit is ever granted off this path. Genuine fail-closed-vs-fail-open design flaw, but latent until enforcement is wired — partial/low.

findings.title: Metered price ($0.01/run) and credit-pack prices are inconsistent: pack_1000 charges $9.00 for 1000 runs ($0.009/run) but metered advertises $0.01/run — overlapping price surfaces disagree
findings.evidence: Arithmetic confirmed: metered unit_amount_cents=1 = $0.01/run; pack_100 = 100c/100 = $0.01/run; pack_1000 = 900c/1000 = $0.009/run (a 10% volume discount). The metered-vs-pack discrepancy is a deliberate-looking volume discount, not an error. The 'never charged' half is the genuine defect and duplicates U148.
findings.rationale: The price 'inconsistency' is a normal bulk discount (overstated as a defect); the load-bearing claim (metered never billed) is U148. Downgraded to low. Corroborated echoed.

findings.title: Ephemeral signing key fallback means receipts signed before RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is set verify as valid against a different (random) key, and silently become unverifiable after restart — a tamper-evidence gap
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U031: ephemeral per-process key when RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY unset, signs+verifies in-process, unverifiable after restart/across replicas. Lines 44-57.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U031 (ephemeral-key fallback). Note: the framing 'verify as valid against a different random key' is benign/expected (in-process verify is internally consistent); the real issue is the cross-restart/cross-worker gap already captured by U031.

findings.title: Ephemeral per-process signing key makes receipts unverifiable after restart and differ across replicas
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U031: per-process secrets.token_bytes(32) fallback => receipts unverifiable after restart and divergent across replicas. Adds the ACP-order-receipt angle but it is the same key-rotation defect.
findings.rationale: Same ephemeral-key defect as U031; not a distinct bug.

findings.title: Published verification recipe omits ensure_ascii, breaking independent verification of receipts containing non-ASCII (e.g. the audit's ⚠ violation marker, unicode receipt_line)
findings.evidence: _canonical (63): json.dumps(body, sort_keys=True, separators=(',',':')) — ensure_ascii defaults True, escaping non-ASCII to \uXXXX. The published recipe (137-140) names sort_keys and separators but NOT ensure_ascii. Non-ASCII IS reachable: core/audit.py receipt_line() appends literal '⚠' on a violation (audit.py 272), and that receipt_line flows into the signed body (84). A foreign-language verifier that doesn't ASCII-escape reconstructs different bytes -> false 'tampered'.
findings.rationale: Genuinely incomplete recipe for the exported/open-source verifier. Severity low: a Python verifier using the literal json.dumps recipe matches (ensure_ascii=True is Python's default, so the documented Python call works); only non-Python or non-default reimplementations break, and only on receipts that contain non-ASCII (violation marker / unicode receipt_line). The audit.py:225 ensure_ascii=False reference is a separate JSONL file path, not the canonicalization — no internal contradiction.

findings.title: Receipt persistence is fully best-effort: a ledger write failure means the run returns 200 with a run_id whose GET will 404, with no signal to the client
findings.evidence: persist() (96-108): save_receipt wrapped in 'except Exception ... logger.warning' (106-107), returns signature regardless. app.py _run (127-129): run_id minted, result['run_id'] set, receipts.persist(run_id, result) called with return value ignored, then result returned 200. A transient DB failure => 200 with run_id whose later GET /v1/audit/{run_id} 404s, no signal to client.
findings.rationale: Accurately describes the silent-degradation path. Downgraded medium->low: this is an explicit, documented design choice (safety invariant already enforced upstream at service.py 127; ledger is convenience), the window is a transient DB write failure, and the failure mode is a missing-receipt 404 not data corruption.

findings.title: Two independent receipt signing paths can diverge: ACP order receipt body shape (router 297-311) is hand-built and not produced by signed_body(), risking signature/verify mismatch if signed_body() ever changes
findings.evidence: router.py 297-311 hand-builds order_receipt_body with the same 5 keys (run_id, provider, dry_run, summary, receipt_line) that signed_body (77-85) defines, then receipts.sign(order_receipt_body) (312). get_receipt reconstructs those exact 5 keys from DB columns (125-131). No call to signed_body() from the ACP path; no test asserts cross-path body-shape equivalence (test_acp.py:138 only checks one field of an in-process round-trip). So a future change to signed_body() would silently break ACP-receipt verification.
findings.rationale: Real latent drift risk; today the shapes match so it verifies. Pure maintainability/latent-correctness, no current bug => low. Same underlying duplication as U512.

findings.title: Idempotency claim has a check-then-insert TOCTOU race under the RLock-but-multi-statement design only protects within a process; cross-process it is unsafe, and even in-process the 'new' path is fine but stale-reclaim rebinds without verifying status
findings.evidence: Same two sub-claims as U036/U300: (1) cross-process SELECT+INSERT TOCTOU → uncaught IntegrityError under multi-worker (lines 326-332); (2) stale-reclaim returns 'new' (337-344). In-process the RLock serializes correctly.
findings.rationale: Restates U036's cross-process TOCTOU/IntegrityError and the stale-reclaim race; merged into the idempotency-concurrency cluster.

findings.title: create_account does not handle UNIQUE constraint violations (stripe_customer_id / api_key), surfacing IntegrityError as an unhandled 500
findings.evidence: create_account (145-165) does a bare INSERT with no ON CONFLICT / try-except; accounts.stripe_customer_id and api_key are UNIQUE (schema 45,50). _resolve_account (billing.py:208-219) does not consult get_account_by_api_key before create. Concurrent webhook redeliveries or duplicate api_key inserts raise sqlite3.IntegrityError → unhandled 500.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: UNIQUE-violation path is uncaught; manifests under a race/duplicate-key, so medium.

findings.title: Idempotency 'processing' stale-timeout rebinds the key to a new payload but a slow original request can still complete and overwrite the rebind, allowing two operations under one key
findings.evidence: idempotency_complete (355-362) UPDATEs status='done', response_json=? WHERE key=? with no status/owner guard; combined with stale-reclaim (337-344) a late original overwrites the retry's stored response (last-writer-wins). Same mechanism described by U305.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U305 (unconditional complete clobbers a rebound key); merged.

findings.title: API keys stored in plaintext and matched by direct SQL equality (no hashing, not constant-time)
findings.evidence: Schema line 50: api_key TEXT UNIQUE (plaintext column). get_account_by_api_key (170-175) does SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE api_key = ?. new_api_key (110) stores the full token verbatim. No hashing; data/app.db holds live credentials at rest. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Confirmed plaintext-secret-at-rest; the timing-side-channel sub-claim is negligible, but the at-rest issue is real, so medium.

findings.title: Stale 'processing' idempotency reclaim rebinds the key without resetting status to 'processing', and the 60s timeout can let a still-running original double-execute
findings.evidence: Same stale-reclaim concern as U125/U302: lines 337-344 free a 'processing' key after 60s and return 'new', allowing a slow-but-alive original to be double-executed on the money path; mitigated for /complete by the per-session Stripe key (router.py:265) + fulfill_once.
findings.rationale: Restates the 60s stale-reclaim double-execute race (U125); merged.

findings.location: Store single shared connection + RLock, class-level; idempotency_begin lines 322-353
findings.title: Single global SQLite connection serialized by one RLock makes all DB writes a process-wide bottleneck and INSERT race window in idempotency_begin spans a non-transactional read+insert
findings.evidence: Same multi-worker cross-process TOCTOU + uncaught IntegrityError on idempotency_begin INSERT (326-332) as U036/U296. In-process the RLock makes it atomic; the bug needs >1 process pointing at MAIL_DB_PATH.
findings.rationale: Cross-process idempotency-INSERT race identical to U036; merged.

findings.title: Stale 'processing' idempotency entry is re-claimed as 'new', allowing the protected operation to re-run
findings.evidence: Same stale-reclaim re-run as U125/U300 (337-344). Note: the NullPaymentClient/fake-client sub-claim is about acp/payment.py + tests, not store.py; in store.py the defect is the 60s reclaim itself. Double-charge mitigated by per-session Stripe key (router.py:265).
findings.rationale: Reiterates the 60s stale-reclaim path (U125); the test-client coupling is out-of-file speculation; merged.

findings.title: ACP update_session can mutate a session's items/total without re-binding to the original idempotent create, enabling price/quantity change before complete
findings.evidence: update_session (router.py:200-232) re-derives line_items/total_runs/status from body.items for any non-terminal session and _persist's them; _gate (router.py:72-93) only checks a non-empty Bearer with no ownership tie to the session creator. save_session (store.py:392-413) stores account_id but nothing checks the updater matches it. Any caller with the session_id can rewrite the cart before /complete.
findings.rationale: Confirmed missing authorization tying update to creator; mitigated only by unguessable acp_cs_+token_hex(16) ids, so medium. (Defect lives in router.py, not store.py, but store provides no owner-scoped update.)

findings.title: create_account silently accepts an arbitrary externally-supplied api_key, conflating server-issued credentials with caller-asserted strings
findings.evidence: create_account(api_key=...) (152-163) writes any caller-supplied string into accounts.api_key with no uma_-format validation; new_api_key (110-112) is the only legitimate minter. acp/router.py:289 calls create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key) with the caller's raw bearer, so the store cannot distinguish issued vs caller-asserted keys. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Confirmed: store conflates server-issued and caller-supplied keys; store-level enabler of the auth-bypass, medium.

findings.title: idempotency_complete writes status='done' + response unconditionally — a stale-reclaimed key lets a late original writer clobber the retry's result (last-writer-wins on a rebound key)
findings.evidence: idempotency_complete (355-362): UPDATE idempotency_keys SET status='done', response_json=? WHERE key=? — no guard on current status or claim ownership. After stale-reclaim (337-344) rebinds the key to request B, a late-finishing request A also calls idempotency_complete and overwrites with A's stale response; a later replay returns A's response though B was acknowledged. No fencing token / compare-and-set.
findings.rationale: Confirmed last-writer-wins on a rebound key (no CAS on status); requires the >60s reclaim race to fire, so medium.

findings.title: Default autocommit isolation_level left implicit; explicit per-method commit() means multi-statement methods are NOT atomic if a statement raises mid-way
findings.evidence: Same missing-rollback root as U035: fulfill_once (374-389) runs INSERT OR IGNORE then UPDATE before one commit with no try/except rollback; if the UPDATE raises, the INSERT is pending. Proposes `with self._conn:` — exactly U035's remediation. Note the 'later unrelated commit() flushes the pending INSERT' mechanic overstates sqlite3 behavior (a failed statement's pending tx is discarded on the next implicit BEGIN).
findings.rationale: Same transaction-atomicity/missing-rollback defect as U035 with the same fix; merged.

findings.location: line 337 (if _now() - existing["created_at"] > IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT) with _now()=time.time() at line 106-107
findings.title: Idempotency 'processing' abandonment window uses non-monotonic wall clock (time.time), so a clock step can prematurely free or permanently wedge a key
findings.evidence: _now() = time.time() (106-107, wall clock); created_at stored as REAL; the 60s abandonment check at line 337 computes _now()-created_at. An NTP step / clock adjustment can prematurely free or never-expire a 'processing' key. A monotonic source would be correct for an interval decision.
findings.rationale: Confirmed wall-clock-for-interval anti-pattern; impact requires a clock step and is bounded by the already-mitigated reclaim path, so low.

findings.title: idempotency_complete silently no-ops if the key row is absent (rowcount unchecked), so a lost claim leaves the key permanently un-finalized
findings.evidence: Lines 355-362: `UPDATE idempotency_keys SET status='done', response_json=? WHERE key=?` followed by commit(); cur.rowcount is never read. acp/router.py:120-122 _complete_idempotency calls it unconditionally and assumes success. If the key row is absent, UPDATE matches 0 rows and commits cleanly.
findings.rationale: Code-true: rowcount is genuinely unchecked, so a missing key silently no-ops. But all production callers INSERT the key in idempotency_begin earlier in the same request (state 'new'), and rows are never deleted, so the 'absent row' precondition cannot occur on any current path — severity is latent/low, not medium.

findings.title: idempotency stale-reclaim updates request_hash/created_at but NOT status, so a crashed 'processing' key can be reclaimed yet still read as processing by a racing third request
findings.evidence: Lines 338-343: the stale-reclaim UPDATE sets only request_hash and created_at, never status (stays 'processing'); returns {'state':'new'} (line 344). The SELECT-then-UPDATE (323-344) is not an atomic compare-and-set, though it runs entirely under self._lock (322).
findings.rationale: The status-not-reset observation is code-true. The 'two requests both pass >timeout and both get new' sub-claim is FALSE for the in-process case: RLock serializes the whole begin, so the second caller sees the rebound created_at and the fresh row is not stale. Real but narrow (only matters cross-process or if the lock is bypassed) — low.

findings.title: Manifest/llms.txt reflect the raw request base_url (Host header) — Host-header spoofing makes the self-describing surface advertise attacker-controlled URLs
findings.evidence: well_known.py:80,85 pass str(request.base_url) straight into the builders, which embed it as absolute checkout_url/mcp url/receipt_verification (lines 29,35-36,41-43,68,72). Grep for TrustedHost/allowed_hosts across api/ returns nothing; app.py (lines 56-165) installs NO middleware at all (no TrustedHostMiddleware, no CORS), and no configured PUBLIC_BASE_URL exists. Dockerfile:19 launches uvicorn with no --forwarded-allow-ips/proxy config. Starlette derives base_url from the Host header, so a forged Host makes the served manifest advertise attacker-controlled commerce/MCP/receipt URLs to trusting agents.
findings.rationale: Host-header reflection into a B2A discovery surface with zero host validation is present in current code; medium severity is justified for an agent-trusted checkout/receipt path.

findings.title: Discovery endpoints reflect request base_url into manifest URLs with no Host allowlist (cache-poisoning / link-spoofing)
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U310: str(request.base_url) reflected into advertised checkout_url/mcp url/receipt_verification with no Host allowlist. Both findings cite the same lines (78-85 / 17-51) and the same Host-header injection mechanism on the same file.
findings.rationale: Same defect, same file, same mechanism as U310 — the 'cache-poisoning/link-spoofing' framing is just an alternate label for the host-reflection issue (the actual cache angle is separately raised in U434).

findings.title: Mutating inbox collection while iterating skips messages (move during repeat-over-messages)
findings.evidence: Line 8 `set inboxMessages to messages of inbox` binds a live Mail.app reference, and the loop `repeat with msg in inboxMessages` (L15) calls `move msg to targetMailbox` (L18). Moving a message out of inbox shifts remaining indices in the live collection, so a single pass skips intervening matches. Standard Mail.app behavior; fix is to snapshot to a static list or iterate in reverse by index.
findings.rationale: Mutate-while-iterating a live `messages of inbox` reference is a real correctness defect that leaves roughly half of matching messages un-archived per run; medium fair.

findings.title: Resolving Archive mailbox can throw before the loop with no handling
findings.evidence: Lines 9-13 resolve `set targetMailbox to mailbox targetMailboxName` (default "Archive", L4) / `mailbox ... of account ...` with no surrounding try. If no top-level mailbox literally named "Archive" exists (default targetAccountName is "", L5), this raises an unhandled AppleScript error before the repeat loop, aborting the run.
findings.rationale: Statically the resolve is outside any error handler and the default depends on a not-guaranteed mailbox name; real but config-conditional, so medium with the understanding it only fires when 'Archive' is absent.

findings.title: HttpError handler breaks the category loop on a transient error, silently skipping remaining work
findings.evidence: Lines 128-130: `except HttpError as e: logger.warning(f"API Error: {e}"); break`. The `break` exits the inner `while True` for the current category; the outer `for category` loop continues. A single transient 429/5xx ends archiving for that label after only a WARNING log, with no retry/backoff. Partial work is reported without an error-level signal.
findings.rationale: The transient-error break with warning-only logging and no retry is exactly as described in current code.

findings.location: senders_for() callback lines 61-68; archive_loop pagination lines 92-131
findings.title: Archive pagination re-runs the same query and breaks on len(ids)<1000, so >1000 protected-heavy pages can loop or miss messages; HttpError aborts the whole category
findings.evidence: Bundles the same pagination/infinite-loop claim (lines 112-115, no pageToken) as U041 plus the HttpError-aborts-category claim (U314). The added note that senders_for's batch.execute() raising would propagate into the per-category HttpError handler is correct: senders_for() is called at line 107 inside the try, and batch.execute() (line 79) has no local try/except. The fail-closed empty-From behavior is correctly observed (is_protected_sender('') returns True, core/rules.py line 797-798).
findings.rationale: Primary defect is the same infinite loop as U041; remaining sub-claims duplicate U314/U317. No new root cause.

findings.location: archive_loop lines 90-130 (query line 90); senders_for batch.execute line 79
findings.title: Gmail query uses unescaped category in label: and batch.execute has no error handling
findings.evidence: Line 90: `query = f"label:{category} label:INBOX"`. The query-injection/escaping concern is purely hypothetical — ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES (lines 30-50) is a static developer-controlled list; some entries already contain a space ('AI/Data Exports') which is a real Gmail-query quoting concern, not an injection vector. The infinite-loop and uncaught batch.execute claims duplicate U041/U317. The static-list escaping angle is not a live bug.
findings.rationale: Real underlying defects are the (duplicated) infinite loop and batch error handling; the framing around query escaping/injection is speculative and not exploitable with a static list, so severity downgraded to low/partial.

findings.title: Unhandled batch.execute() exception aborts protected-sender gate before INBOX removal
findings.evidence: Line 79: `batch.execute()` has no try/except, confirmed. It is called from line 107 inside archive_loop's try, whose `except HttpError` (line 128) catches HttpError but NOT non-HttpError exceptions (socket timeout, SSLError, BatchError) — those would propagate and crash the run. However the googleapiclient batch.execute() surfaces HTTP/transport failures as HttpError in the common case, so the typical failure is caught and handled (abandons category, = U314). The fail-closed correctness claim is verified: ids that never get a callback are absent from `out`, and `senders.get(i, '')` -> '' -> is_protected_sender('') == True (core/rules.py line 797-798), so they are protected (safe).
findings.rationale: The missing try/except is real but the dangerous case (non-HttpError crash, partial batch) is narrow; the fail-closed default holds, so the safety claim is fine and severity is overstated at medium.

findings.title: Plaintext secret passed as a command-line argument to `op` (visible via process listing)
findings.evidence: Same finding as U157: token (incl. refresh_token) serialized and passed as `field=value` argv element in both auth/onepassword.py:111 and gmail_auth.py:57. Confirmed identical pattern in both files.
findings.rationale: Restates the U157 argv-secret leak; same root cause across the two files.

findings.title: op item edit assignment arg breaks when field name contains '=' or value contains '='
findings.evidence: Line 111 builds f"{field}={value}". The '/'-joined field claim is correct (parse_op_ref line 137 joins parts[2:] with '/'), but value containing '=' is NOT a problem: `op` splits only on the first '=', so the full secret JSON (which may contain '=') is preserved. The wrong-field-write concern only triggers for sectioned op:// refs with >3 path segments routed through store_json_secret — which is dead code (see U446).
findings.rationale: Real edge case for multi-segment fields, but the value-contains-'=' portion is wrong, and the only triggering path (store_json_secret) has no callers; needs the right config to manifest.

findings.location: op_item_edit, line 111; op_item_get, line 85; op_read, line 60
findings.title: User/env-derived item names or values starting with '-' can be parsed by op as flags (argv option injection)
findings.evidence: Confirmed no `--` end-of-options sentinel is added before the item/ref positionals (lines 60, 85, 111). subprocess.run uses argv (no shell), so an item/ref/field beginning with '-' could be parsed by `op` as a flag. Inputs are operator-controlled env/config (OP_*), not network-derived.
findings.rationale: Genuine missing-`--` hardening gap, but exploitation requires a self-inflicted dash-prefixed item/field name; low real-world blast radius. Not the high-confidence injection vuln implied.

findings.title: parse_op_ref maps op:// fields with extra '/' segments into a single slash-joined field, which op edit cannot address as a nested field
findings.evidence: Line 137 field = "/".join(parts[2:]) confirmed. Read/write asymmetry for sectioned refs is the same defect as U319's multi-segment-field claim, and the write path (store_json_secret) is dead code (U446).
findings.rationale: Same sectioned-ref field-path mismatch as U319; only manifests via the unused store path.

findings.title: Gmail `from:{domain}` query may not match the raw extracted domain, causing zero moves (and feeding the infinite-loop)
findings.evidence: Query is `f"from:{domain} label:{TARGET_SOURCE}"` (line 157) with domain from `sender.split('@')[-1].strip('>]').lower()` (line 62). Gmail's `from:` is tokenized/fuzzy and a subdomain like 'email.marketing.brand.com' can match differently than a bare extracted token; trailing junk (only '>' and ']' stripped, not '"' or whitespace) can corrupt the token. Plausible cause of zero-match → reinforces U043 no-progress.
findings.rationale: Real edge case (incomplete stripping + Gmail from: fuzziness) but confidence is medium and the dominant 'Name <addr>' path parses fine; mostly a contributing factor to the loop concern (U043), not an independent high-impact bug. Low.

findings.title: batchModify failure only sleeps 5s and continues, double-counts moves, and pagination uses a fresh search each loop causing potential infinite loop
findings.evidence: Confirms the inner-loop pagination/failure defect: HttpError except (181-183) sleeps 5s without break/retry; the failed batch's messages retain the source label so the same `from:domain label:Misc/Other` list (161-163, no pageToken) refetches them → indefinite spin. moves_performed += len(batch_ids) (179) only in success branch. No max-iteration guard.
findings.rationale: Accurate description of the same inner-loop infinite-spin defect as U047. Same root cause.

findings.title: Gmail search-query injection via sender-derived domain
findings.evidence: domain is interpolated unescaped into the Gmail search query (line 157). extract_domain (line 62) strips only '>' and ']', not spaces — so a From with a domain like 'x in:anywhere' could in principle survive. However the domain is `sender.split('@')[-1]` so it is the substring AFTER the last '@'; injecting Gmail operators requires a crafted From with '@operator-text' as the final segment, and `.split('@')[-1]` plus `.strip('>]')` constrains it. Result IDs are batchModified, so a broadened search could mis-move mail.
findings.rationale: A genuine but low-likelihood injection: it is search-query (not shell/SQL) injection, attacker must control a sender's From header, and impact is label moves (not archive/delete). Real-but-narrow → low.

findings.location: drain_loop lines 100-199 (outer while True), 159-187 (inner while True), callback line 121
findings.title: Potential infinite loop and dropped errors in inbox drain
findings.evidence: Restates (1) inner-loop infinite spin from same-query refetch on batchModify failure (lines 159-186) and (2) callback `if exception: return` dropping messages (line 122) feeding moves_performed==0 outer spin. Also notes `service` never closed (true — no close()). All verified.
findings.rationale: Composite of the inner-loop infinite-loop (U047/U323) and the silent callback drop (U533); same underlying defects, no new root cause.

findings.location: run_sweep line 91 (`if not add_id`) vs lines 88-89, 114
findings.title: Missing 'remove' label is silently tolerated, leaving source label attached forever
findings.evidence: Confirmed: line 91 `if not add_id:` guards only add_id; remove_id (line 89) is never guarded. Line 114 `"removeLabelIds": [remove_id] if remove_id else []` silently sends [] when remove_id is None, so the source label is never stripped and no warning is logged (contrast line 92 for add_id).
findings.rationale: Real asymmetry / missing warning, but blast radius is small: a missing Misc/Other label also means the label-scoped query matches nothing, so the practical effect is a no-op rule, not data loss; downgraded to low.

findings.title: SWEEP_RULES target label names diverge from core taxonomy ('Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/RealEstate' not in LABEL_RULES)
findings.evidence: Verified against core/rules.py: it defines `Dev/Infrastructure` (line 89, NO Work/ prefix) and has NO `Work/RealEstate` (grep found only Dev/GitHub, Dev/Infrastructure, Dev/GameDev). SWEEP_RULES use `Work/Dev/Infrastructure` (lines 30,37,66) and `Work/RealEstate` (line 60) — divergent. `Education/Research` (line 54) DOES exist (core/rules.py line 353), so that part of the finding is wrong. get_label_id returns None for nonexistent labels -> line 91 skips with a warning, making Notion/Backblaze/Termius/Majestic sweeps no-ops unless those exact labels were hand-created.
findings.rationale: Taxonomy divergence for Work/Dev/Infrastructure and Work/RealEstate is real (Education/Research portion is a false sub-claim); effect is silent no-op rules, not corruption, so low.

findings.title: SWEEP_RULES route by bare 'from:<domain>' with no protected-sender gate; safe today but a one-line edit ships unprotected mutations
findings.evidence: The finding admits the current code is safe (relabel-only, no INBOX removal) and frames the concern as a hypothetical future edit. The module docstring (lines 6-9) ALREADY states this explicitly: 'relabel-only; does NOT remove INBOX and does NOT enforce the protected-sender gate ... Do NOT extend it to archive ... without adopting that gate first.' is_protected_sender exists in core/rules.py (line 787) but this legacy standalone script is documented as intentionally not wiring it. No current defect.
findings.rationale: Speculative future-edit risk on code that already documents the exact constraint; not a present bug — false positive.

findings.location: default --query 'has:nouserlabels' (label_parser line 1014) used for all providers
findings.title: Gmail-specific default query passed verbatim to IMAP/Outlook/Mail.app providers
findings.evidence: label_parser --query default='has:nouserlabels' (1014), help text 'default: has:nouserlabels for Gmail' (1015). cmd_label passes args.query straight into run_labeler (443) -> provider.list_messages(query=...) for ALL providers; get_provider/run_labeler do no per-provider remapping. The other commands (escalate 1089, summary 1131, vip 1176) default to '' which is provider-safe.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: `cli.py label --provider outlook` (no --query) sends the Gmail-only operator 'has:nouserlabels' to Outlook/IMAP/Mail.app, which is not a valid query for those backends. Likely matches nothing or errors per provider. Medium — affects the primary 'label' command's default behavior for non-Gmail providers.

findings.title: Global --verbose flag is defined on the top-level parser only and is shadowed/unreachable when a subcommand is given
findings.evidence: Tested: `cli.py label --provider gmail --verbose` -> 'error: unrecognized arguments: --verbose' (exit nonzero). `cli.py label -v ...` -> 'error: unrecognized arguments: -v'. Only `cli.py -v label ...` (prefix position) is accepted. -v/--verbose is added to the root parser (968-972) before subparsers (1004) and is not on provider_group, so it is unrecognized after a subcommand.
findings.rationale: Confirmed by execution: the intuitive `label --provider gmail --verbose` errors out; verbose is only usable in prefix position. Real usability defect but downgraded to low — it is a logging-verbosity flag, not a correctness/data issue, and a workaround exists.

findings.title: pending uses sender-side 'is:starred' query for gmail but 'isRead eq false' for outlook, then filters on msg.is_starred — outlook path yields zero pending items
findings.evidence: gmail query='is:starred' (640), outlook query='isRead eq false' (642), else query='' (644). Post-filter is `if msg.is_starred` for ALL providers (665). Verified outlook maps is_starred = (flag.flagStatus=='flagged') (providers/outlook.py:449,466,491,518), NOT isRead. So the outlook branch fetches UNREAD messages then keeps only FLAGGED ones — two orthogonal attributes — almost always yielding an empty/wrong pending list.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: outlook's query (unread) and post-filter (flagged) are uncorrelated, so cmd_pending --provider outlook discards nearly everything it fetched. The else-branch (non-gmail/non-outlook) fetches all then keeps starred, which matches the filter but ignores any 'due' semantics. Medium — pending is a user-facing triage command that is effectively broken for Outlook.

findings.location: run_labeler, lines 172, 186-190 (page_token = state.get_token(); passed to provider.list_messages)
findings.title: Malformed page token from state is replayed unvalidated to the provider
findings.evidence: page_token = state.get_token() (172) -> state.py:97-99 returns self.state.get('next_page_token') with no type check; passed verbatim to provider.list_messages(page_token=...) (186-190). is_resumable() (state.py:131-133) checks only `is not None`. No string validation. But save() (state.py:84) only ever writes the provider-returned next_page_token (a str or None), so the code never produces a non-string token itself.
findings.rationale: Real but overstated/narrow: the unvalidated-replay only triggers on an externally hand-edited state file storing a non-string next_page_token. In normal operation the token is always a provider-issued string or None. Robustness gap against tampering, not a live defect — low, partial.

findings.title: Worker .gov protection uses endsWith('.gov') on the full From value, accepting spoofs the real API rejects
findings.evidence: Line 106 protects when value.includes('courts.ca.gov') || value.endsWith('.gov') on the full From string, not a parsed domain. 'courts.ca.gov.evil.com' contains 'courts.ca.gov' -> protected. 'attacker@evil.gov' ends with '.gov' -> protected. core/rules.py _gov_protected: domain.split('.')[-1]=='gov' on the recovered domain only ('irs.gov.attacker.com'->False, 'service.gov.uk'->False).
findings.rationale: Confirmed parsed-domain-vs-raw-string divergence for the .gov path. Over-protect direction (medium). The finding's own hedging text acknowledges some examples balance out; the substring courts.ca.gov.evil.com pass-through is the solid instance.

findings.title: Overwrites live Apple Mail plist with only a single .backup that is clobbered on every run
findings.evidence: Same defect as U336 — overwritten fixed `.backup` (lines 18, 103) plus in-place rewrite (lines 123-124). The 'no validation that rewritten plist is well-formed' sub-claim is also true (no round-trip check), but it is the same read-modify-write root cause.
findings.rationale: Identical root-cause defect in the same file as U336 (overwritten backup + unvalidated in-place rewrite).

findings.title: Non-atomic plist write can corrupt SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist on interrupt
findings.evidence: Lines 123-124 open the live PLIST_PATH 'wb' and plistlib.dump directly; no temp+os.replace (confirmed by grep). This is the non-atomic-write half of U336.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause as U336 (non-atomic plist write); narrower framing of the same cluster.

findings.title: Plist read-modify-write not atomic; backup overwritten each run
findings.evidence: Line 103 `shutil.copy2(PLIST_PATH, BACKUP_PATH)` runs at the start of EVERY invocation, and BACKUP_PATH (line 18) is a fixed `.backup` suffix (not timestamped) — so a second run overwrites the prior backup. Lines 123-124 `with PLIST_PATH.open('wb') as fh: plistlib.dump(new_list, fh)` write directly onto the live plist with no temp-file + os.replace; grep confirms no tempfile/os.replace/dry_run anywhere in the file. A crash/Ctrl-C mid-dump truncates the live SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist, and the only backup may already be the corrupted copy.
findings.rationale: Both halves verified in current code: backup is clobbered each run AND the write is non-atomic onto a real Mail data file; this is the primary statement of the cluster.

findings.title: Protected-sender disposition over-reported as protected_held even when message left the inbox (count vs violation mismatch)
findings.evidence: Lines 181-184: `if is_protected: disposition = PROTECTED_HELD; if left_inbox: self.violations.append(message_id)`. counts[PROTECTED_HELD] is incremented (line 194) even for a violated message. summary() (245) reports protected_held from that count, and receipt_line (267) labels it 'protected held in inbox'. There is no distinct 'protected_violated' disposition. tests/test_audit.py:144 confirms a violated protected message returns disposition PROTECTED_HELD.
findings.rationale: Real internal contradiction: the held count and the persisted disposition both claim 'held in inbox' for a message recorded as having left it; only the parallel violations list signals the breach. Root cause is the disposition assignment at line 182.

findings.title: _append catches only OSError; a serialization error in json.dumps escapes into the apply path
findings.evidence: Lines 218-225 wrap both the file ops AND json.dumps(entry,...) (line 225) in one try; the except at line 226 catches only OSError. A non-serializable value in entry would raise TypeError, escaping the apply path against the 'must NEVER raise' invariant (comment 227-231). However every entry field is controlled: ts/provider/message_id/disposition/domain/sender are str|None, the bool fields are bool, and labels_added=list(labels_added or []) is populated by callers from action.add_labels / info['add_labels'] (typed Sequence[str]).
findings.rationale: The narrow except is a genuine deviation from the stated invariant, but with current callers every field is JSON-serializable, so the escaping TypeError is not reachable in practice. Real-but-overstated; broadening to except Exception would be a cheap hardening.

findings.title: Audit silently downgrades to non-protected when rules import/parse fails
findings.evidence: Lines 110-114: bare `try: from core.rules import is_protected_sender; return bool(...) except Exception: return False` with NO logging. Line 178: `is_protected = bool(protected) or _independently_protected(sender)`. If core.rules fails to import, the cross-check returns False for every sender silently and the audit collapses to trusting only the gate's `protected` flag — the exact 'gate stopped recognizing a sender' scenario the module docstring (20-38) sells becomes uncatchable, with no warning.
findings.rationale: Real silent degradation of the headline independence guarantee; a one-time logger.warning on the import/compute failure would surface it. Confirmed at the cited lines.

findings.title: The independent audit's protection re-derivation swallows ALL exceptions and returns False (fail-OPEN for the cross-check)
findings.evidence: Same code as U339: _independently_protected (110-114) bare-except returns False on ANY exception (fail-open for the cross-check). U340 reframes U339's 'import failure' as the broader 'any exception / fail-open vs fail-closed' philosophy, but the defect and the lines are identical (the bare except at 113-114 feeding the union at line 178).
findings.rationale: Same root-cause defect as U339 (the swallow-and-return-False in _independently_protected). The _domain_of swallow is acknowledged cosmetic by the finding itself; the protection-relevant claim is the same code U339 cites.

findings.location: summary() line 250 -> api/receipts.py signed_body 83 / persist 94-105 -> GET /v1/audit/{run_id} 114-142 (unauthenticated)
findings.title: Receipt body schema is designed to carry raw violation message_ids to an unauthenticated endpoint; only the fail-closed assert keeps it empty
findings.evidence: audit.py:250 summary() returns 'violations': list(self.violations) (raw message_ids appended at line 184). api/service.py:133 sets result['audit']=audit.summary(); receipts.signed_body (receipts.py:83) copies triage_result.get('audit') verbatim into 'summary' (comment claims 'counts only, no senders' — true for senders, but violations carries ids), persisted (94-105) and served by the UNAUTHENTICATED GET /v1/audit/{run_id} (114-142). CONFIRMED that today this is masked: service.py:127 audit.assert_no_violations() raises BEFORE _run returns, and app.py:112-122 converts it to a 500 so receipts.persist (app.py:129) is never reached when violations is non-empty.
findings.rationale: The schema genuinely carries the raw-id array to an unauthenticated endpoint; the only thing keeping it empty is the upstream fail-closed raise (single point of defense). Real defense-in-depth gap, but no live leak today, so severity is low; the receipts.py:83 'counts only, no senders' comment is technically accurate (ids != senders) which further bounds impact.

findings.location: _independently_protected lines 110-114 and _domain_of lines 84-92 (both import core.rules and both bare-except)
findings.title: A single core.rules import failure collapses BOTH the cross-check AND the receipt's domain field at once, with the receipt still emitted as if trustworthy
findings.evidence: Both functions independently `from core.rules import ...` inside bare `except Exception` (110-114 and 84-89). A core.rules import failure trips BOTH for the same message: the cross-check degrades to one source (line 178) AND _domain_of falls back to the divergent rpartition('@') parser (line 92). entry/summary carry no 'independent re-derivation degraded' signal, so the record is written and summary() reports 'no violations' with full confidence.
findings.rationale: Real compound observation, but the protection-collapse half is the same defect as U339; the genuinely new content is the simultaneous signal-less corruption of the domain field + absence of any degradation flag. Marked partial (overlaps U339's root cause) rather than a wholly independent confirmed finding.

findings.title: No type validation of YAML values before assigning to typed config fields
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U186 (scalar fields assigned without type coercion). Additionally asserts custom_rules/vip_senders assigned wholesale without isinstance guard (249-250, 259-260) — verified true; apply_vip_senders_from_config:313 calls .items() so a non-dict vip_senders would AttributeError. The isinstance(dict) guard is only on provider sub-blocks (200,211,226,237).
findings.rationale: Overlapping no-type-validation finding; the vip_senders/.items() AttributeError aspect is real and shared with U343's framing, but the scalar-coercion core duplicates U186. Marked duplicate of U186.

findings.title: VIP entry missing 'pattern' is silently dropped without warning
findings.evidence: Lines 313-314: `for key, vip_data in config.vip_senders.items(): if isinstance(vip_data, dict) and "pattern" in vip_data:`. Entries failing the guard (missing/typo'd 'pattern') fall through with no else branch and no logger.warning — silently dropped. Only the success path logs (324) and increments count.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: malformed VIP entries are silently skipped with no diagnostic. Severity low — affects only misconfigured entries, and a VIP not being registered fails open toward normal categorization, not toward data loss.

findings.title: Broad except Exception swallows YAML parse errors as empty config
findings.evidence: Lines 124-126: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...); return {}`. Any parse/IO failure is swallowed and the system continues on pure defaults. yaml.YAMLError and OSError are not distinguished; a malformed config silently degrades to default Gmail behavior.
findings.rationale: Confirmed broad-except silent fallback. Whether a malformed config 'should' be fatal is a design judgment; low severity — logged at warning, no crash, but custom rules/VIPs silently vanish.

findings.title: merge() silently merges actions for DIFFERENT message_ids; only the docstring says 'assumed'
findings.evidence: core/models.py:97-98 hardcodes message_id=self.message_id with no equality check; docstring line 96 only says 'same message_id assumed'. Confirmed merge() has NO production caller — grep '\.merge(' finds it only in tests/test_models.py:100-126. The defect (silent adoption of self.message_id) is real as written.
findings.rationale: Accurate read of the code, but severity medium is overstated for an unguarded public method with zero callers; it is a latent API-hardening gap, not an active money/data-path bug.

findings.title: merge() can resurrect a non-empty sender from `other`, weakening the fail-closed protected-sender guarantee
findings.evidence: core/models.py:99 computes sender=self.sender or other.sender and line 102 archive=self.archive or other.archive. The fail-closed protected gate is confirmed: rules.py:797-798 ('if not sender or not sender.strip(): return True') and base.py:296-297 ('A blank sender fails closed (protected)'). So coalescing a blank sender to a non-blank one across a merge could theoretically un-protect. BUT merge() has no production caller (tests only), so the inverse-fail-closed scenario is purely hypothetical today.
findings.rationale: The fail-closed invariant and the coalesce logic are both real and the interaction is correctly identified, but with no caller the security severity is latent — downgrade to low.

findings.title: VIP tier and tier_config can disagree when VIP tier is outside 1-4, yielding wrong folder/star/inbox behavior
findings.evidence: Reproduced: add_vip_sender('boss', pattern='boss@x', tier=5) -> categorize_with_tier('boss@x.com','hi') yields result.tier=5 but result.tier_config.number=1 (line 983 PRIORITY_TIERS.get(5, PRIORITY_TIERS[1]) falls back to tier-1 config). add_vip_sender (900-925) does not validate tier.
findings.rationale: Real divergence between result.tier and tier_config for out-of-range VIP tier. Low: requires a user misconfiguring a VIP tier outside 1-4 (no default VIPs ship); blast radius is downstream code trusting tier vs tier_config inconsistently.

findings.location: lines 1176-1182 (calculate_email_age_hours: local import + replace(tzinfo=utc)) vs line 8 module-level import
findings.title: calculate_email_age_hours assumes naive provider dates are UTC, but providers populate naive LOCAL dates (e.g. email Date: header parse), inflating/deflating age by the UTC offset
findings.evidence: Lines 1180-1182 confirmed: naive datetime -> replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc) (assume-UTC). test_rules.py:325 documents test_naive_datetime_assumed_utc as intentional. The 'providers populate naive LOCAL dates' premise is hypothetical: only outlook.py sets date= and it uses 'received' (Graph returns tz-aware ISO8601); gmail/imap/mailapp set no date at all.
findings.rationale: The skew is real ONLY if a future provider supplies a naive local timestamp. No current provider does (Outlook dates are tz-aware; others are None). Confirmable only with a provider/config that emits naive local dates -> needs_runtime, low.

findings.title: Failed state save is swallowed; run continues believing progress is persisted
findings.evidence: Same swallowed-save-exception defect as U073: except Exception logs and returns None; callers get no signal; broad except masks non-serializable errors.
findings.rationale: Identical root cause to U073.

findings.title: _load does not validate that loaded JSON is a dict with expected keys
findings.evidence: Same missing isinstance(dict) validation as U071; recommends merge against _default_state(). No new defect.
findings.rationale: Same no-schema-validation root cause as U071.

findings.title: Overly broad except in _load masks non-JSON load errors and silently discards prior state
findings.evidence: core/state.py:54-55 `except Exception as e: logger.error(...)` falls through to `return self._default_state()` (line 56). A transient read failure (permission/lock) on an otherwise-valid file silently discards saved progress rather than being distinguished from genuine corruption.
findings.rationale: Distinct from U071 (over-broad catch vs no shape-check). Real but low impact — only a transient I/O failure on an existing file discards resumable state, and re-processing is idempotent.

findings.title: RESET_STATE default 'true' wipes GmailLabeler resume state, and save(None,0,{}) may not match how run() reads token
findings.evidence: final_sweep.py:9 RESET_STATE defaults 'true'; line 12 calls app.state_manager.save(None, 0, {}) unconditionally, which writes the shared GmailLabeler state file. BUT gmail_labeler.py:40 defines STATE_FILE = "labeler_state.json" (NOT 'gmail_state.json' as the finding claims; gmail_state.json is a separate file used only by core/config.py and is absent on disk — labeler_state.json is the one that exists). Critically, run() only reads/writes the page token when query == "has:nouserlabels" (gmail_labeler.py:285-286, and the saves at 307-308/321-322 are all guarded by that same condition). For query="label:Uncategorized" the sweep starts fresh (page_token=None) and never touches the saved token, so there is NO cross-query contamination via the token; the sweep run itself cannot corrupt or be corrupted by the main pass's token.
findings.rationale: Real mechanism (RESET_STATE=true clobbers the main has:nouserlabels resume token in labeler_state.json) but the finding mislabels the file ('gmail_state.json'), invents a non-existent race/cross-contamination (no concurrency; the sweep query never reads/writes the token), and the run() guard means the only true effect is the line-12 reset overwriting a prior pass's token — a benign re-scan, not data loss. Severity overstated.

findings.title: Headless/hosted credential acquisition silently blocks on a local-server OAuth flow
findings.evidence: Line 177 `creds = flow.run_local_server(port=0)` is reached unconditionally when no valid/refreshable token exists, with no interactive/headless guard. Hosted path is reachable: api/service.py:99-100 `prov=factory(provider); prov.connect()` -> providers/gmail.py:87 build_gmail_service -> get_credentials. run_triage default provider='gmail' (service.py:72). HOWEVER service.py:98-105 wraps connect() in `except Exception -> ProviderUnavailable (503)`, so a *raised* error already maps cleanly to 503 — only a genuine *blocking hang* (run_local_server awaiting a redirect that never comes) defeats that guard.
findings.rationale: Real: no headless guard and the path is reachable; but the 'opaque 503/hang' framing is overstated — raised errors are already caught; only the blocking-hang sub-case escapes. Hence partial.

findings.title: OAuth refresh-token written to 1Password via command-line argument 'field=value' — secret exposed in process args
findings.evidence: Line 57: `cmd = ['op', 'item', 'edit', item, f'{field}={value}']` — the value (compact token JSON incl. long-lived refresh_token, built at store_token_info lines 151-152) is passed as a process argv, visible via ps/proc for the subprocess lifetime. Corroborated by the identical pattern at auth/onepassword.py:111 `cmd = ['op','item','edit', item, f'{field}={value}']`.
findings.rationale: Confirmed argv secret-exposure of a long-lived refresh token; corroboration flag holds (matching code in auth/onepassword.py).

findings.title: Page-token resumption with a draining/destructive query can skip messages or resume against a shifted result set
findings.evidence: Lines 285-286 load a saved page token only for query=='has:nouserlabels'; lines 321-322 persist nextPageToken for that same default query. Labeling unlabeled mail removes it from the 'has:nouserlabels' match-set, so a resumed nextPageToken indexes into a shifted/shrunken result set. The author's own comment (272-282) calls page tokens 'dangerous' for draining queries, yet the default query IS a draining query and the code still persists+reuses its token. Gmail can skip messages between the consumed pages and the saved token's offset.
findings.rationale: Real silent-incomplete-coverage hazard on resume for the default draining query, exactly contradicting the in-file warning; medium is fair.

findings.title: Legacy categorize_email uses unguarded header['name']/['value'] and a catch-all label 'Uncategorized' that is never created elsewhere consistently
findings.evidence: Two claims bundled. (1) Hard subscript header['name']/['value'] at lines 346/348 and email_data['payload']['headers'] at line 342: confirmed, and KeyError is swallowed by `except Exception` at line 441 which prints+continues. But the get() at lines 417-424 always requests From/Subject in metadata format, so KeyError only on a malformed/partial API response - not a normal path. (2) 'Uncategorized' vs core 'Misc/Other' split-brain: confirmed (same as U203), sweep targets label:Misc/Other only.
findings.rationale: Both sub-claims are real but each is overstated/conditional and the catch-all half duplicates U203. Downgraded medium->low: KeyError needs a malformed response and is caught; the file is unwired and relabel-only.

findings.title: COPY failure returns False but message is left in place — but STORE/EXPUNGE results unchecked on success path
findings.evidence: Lines 75-77: after a successful COPY, `imap.uid('STORE', uid, '+FLAGS', r'(\Deleted)')` and `imap.expunge()` run with no return-code check, then `return True` unconditionally. If STORE fails, the message is COPIED to Archive but left in INBOX -> duplicate counted as 'moved'.
findings.rationale: Real unchecked-result/duplication path, but only on the COPY fallback (not default MOVE) and only on STORE failure; severity low. Same defect as U359.

findings.title: If COPY succeeds but STORE/EXPUNGE step errors, message is duplicated or the function returns True without confirming deletion
findings.evidence: Lines 72-77: COPY checked (returns False on non-OK), but STORE (75) and expunge (76) results are unchecked and the function unconditionally `return True` (77). On STORE/expunge failure the source stays in INBOX while a copy exists in Archive, yet moved+=1 (line 163).
findings.rationale: Accurate description of unchecked STORE/EXPUNGE on the COPY fallback; low severity (COPY path only). U358 is the same defect.

findings.title: Folder/label names interpolated unquoted/unescaped into IMAP commands (folders with spaces or quotes break or inject)
findings.evidence: core/rules.py defines real labels with spaces: line 135 'AI/Data Exports' and line 494 'Awaiting Reply'. imaplib._command does NOT quote arguments (CPython source: data = data + b' ' + arg, no _quote call), so ensure_label line 98 imap.create('Awaiting Reply') sends raw 'CREATE Awaiting Reply' where the space is an arg separator and the name is misparsed. The double-quote/backslash injection in apply_label (lines 106/109) is latent (fixed taxonomy).
findings.rationale: The space-in-create defect is CURRENT and triggers on two existing labels (verified imaplib does not auto-quote); the unescaped-quote injection part is latent. Confirmed but partially overstated -> medium.

findings.title: Messages are marked \Seen as a side effect of labeling, and STORE/COPY results are never checked
findings.evidence: Line 110 imap.uid('STORE', uid, '+FLAGS', r'(\Seen)') runs unconditionally for every non-dry-run message; default --query is 'ALL' (line 116) on default mailbox INBOX (line 115), so a routine apply run marks the whole inbox read. Return tuples from STORE (106/110) and COPY (109) are never assigned/checked.
findings.rationale: Both sub-claims verified against current code: unconditional \Seen on ALL/INBOX, and STORE/COPY results discarded so failures are silent. Medium is appropriate (only on non-dry-run; default DRY_RUN=1).

findings.title: apply_label ignores IMAP command status and force-marks \Seen on every processed message
findings.evidence: Same two defects as U361: unchecked STORE/COPY return values + unconditional \Seen (line 110). Adds the ensure_label 'NO treated as success' observation, which overlaps U804. Confirmed on its merits but same root cause as U361.
findings.rationale: Same silent-failure + force-\Seen root cause as U361 in this file; deduplicated to U361.

findings.title: Advertises /v1/audit/{run_id} 'receipt verification' that the live worker returns unsigned
findings.evidence: Same mis-attribution as U208. The surface serving this llms.txt (api/app.py via api/receipts.py router) returns a genuinely signed, independently verifiable receipt: GET /v1/audit/{run_id} (receipts.py:114-142) returns signed_body + signature + algorithm='HMAC-SHA256' + explicit verify instructions, with the key derived from RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY (receipts.py:44-57). An agent verifying against this endpoint is NOT misled. The unsigned hardcoded /v1/audit body cited is in the separate worker.mjs:281-305 demo, which advertises a different llms.txt.
findings.rationale: The /v1/audit/{run_id} on the FastAPI surface this file describes returns a real HMAC-signed, re-derivable receipt; the 'unsigned' claim belongs to the demo worker, a different surface.

findings.title: Unbounded while-loop relies on each batchModify shrinking the result set; no nextPageToken and no progress guard
findings.evidence: Lines 43-64: `while True:` lists maxResults=1000 with no pageToken, batchModify removes UNREAD (line 57), breaks only on empty messages (49) or len(ids)<1000 (63). No max-iteration cap, no try/except. Confirmed no pageToken and no error guard.
findings.rationale: The loop structure is as described, but the non-termination scenario requires batchModify to silently fail to remove UNREAD or persistent index lag returning exactly >=1000 stale ids — a narrow edge; medium overstated, downgraded to low.

findings.title: Pagination loop never advances pageToken — relies on side-effect of removing UNREAD to terminate
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U364: 'pagination loop never advances pageToken; relies on UNREAD removal to terminate; len(ids)<1000 only guard'. Same lines 43-64.
findings.rationale: Same defect (no-pageToken/loop-termination heuristic) described in U364; merged.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS entries with a URL scheme produce malformed origins (http://https://host) and never match
findings.evidence: Lines 51-53 treat each entry as a bare host: hosts += entries; origins = [f'http://{h}'...] + [f'https://{h}'...]. SDK _validate_host/_validate_origin do exact match. If an operator supplies MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='https://app.example.com', the host entry never equals a real Host (hostname[:port]) so 421, and origins become 'http://https://app.example.com' which never match an Origin so 403. No scheme normalization exists.
findings.rationale: Real foot-gun under a plausible mis-entry, but it requires operator misconfiguration and the correct format (bare host) is documented in DEPLOY.md / the docstring; low severity.

findings.location: check_protected_sender line 94 (sender[:4096], subject[:4096]) vs api/schemas.py SenderCheckRequest max_length=4096
findings.title: MCP check_protected_sender truncates to 4096 chars silently rather than rejecting, diverging from the API's validation contract
findings.evidence: server.py:94 passes sender[:4096], subject[:4096] to service.check_sender — silent truncation. schemas.py:29-30 bounds the HTTP SenderCheckRequest with max_length=4096 which REJECTS (422) longer input. So an over-length sender yields 422 on REST but a (truncated) protected verdict on MCP.
findings.rationale: Real divergence; truncation could in theory drop a trailing protected-domain anchor. But From-header local-part/domain that matters is at the start, real headers are far under 4096, and is_protected_sender matches anywhere — so the wrong-verdict risk is marginal. Low.

findings.title: INBOX strip in _drop_if_protected matches only 'INBOX'/'\\INBOX' but remove path matches the same; a lowercase or aliased inbox label could leak past the gate
findings.evidence: Both the gate (base.py:303 `lbl.upper() not in ("INBOX", "\\INBOX")`) and the leave-inbox detection (base.py:351 `label.upper() in ("INBOX", "\\INBOX")`) call `.upper()`, so a lowercase 'inbox' IS matched/stripped — the finding's 'lowercase ... leak' premise is wrong. The only realizable concern is a differently-named/localized inbox in remove_labels, but the sole folder provider (Outlook) has remove_label() as a hard no-op (outlook.py:539-542: logs warning, returns False); its inbox departure runs through archive() (outlook.py:544 → /move), and _drop_if_protected sets action.archive=False (base.py:301). cli.py:274-275 only appends the user-supplied --remove-label category (e.g. 'Misc/Other'), never an inbox name. No code path lets a protected message leave the inbox via an aliased remove_labels entry.
findings.rationale: Lowercase claim is refuted by the .upper() normalization; the aliased-inbox path is unreachable in current code because the one folder provider departs via archive() (which IS gated), not remove_label.

findings.title: remove_label falls back to raw label NAME as label ID on cache miss, sending an invalid removeLabelIds value
findings.evidence: remove_label line 317: `label_id = self._label_cache.get(label) or label`. apply_actions line 413: same fallback. _label_cache is populated only at connect()/ensure_label_exists. A user label not cached this session (e.g. 'Misc/Other' created elsewhere) falls back to sending the display name as removeLabelIds; Gmail requires the opaque Label_XX id for user labels, yielding HttpError 400 (caught/logged at 328-330 → returns False; or counted as chunk error at 469-472). System labels (INBOX/STARRED) work because id==name.
findings.rationale: Confirmed cache-miss fallback sends an invalid label id for user labels; this is the primary instance of the remove_label-fallback defect.

findings.title: apply_label single-path and apply_actions batch-path enforce protected gate inconsistently
findings.evidence: True that the protected gate lives only in apply_actions/_drop_if_protected, and archive()->remove_label('INBOX') (line 332-334) has no protected check. BUT a repo-wide grep for non-provider callers of .archive(/.remove_label(/.apply_label(/.star( returns ZERO results — every mutation funnels through apply_actions (cli.py:284, 925). No code path bypasses the chokepoint today.
findings.rationale: Real invariant-locality gap, but the bypass is purely hypothetical: no actual caller invokes archive()/remove_label() directly, so severity downgraded.

findings.title: remove_label falls back to using the raw label string as a label ID when not in cache, which can no-op or error silently
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U369: `label_id = self._label_cache.get(label) or label` at line 317 sending raw label name on cache miss. (Minor overstatement: Gmail returns a 400 for an unknown removeLabelIds id rather than a silent no-op, so 'returns True silently' is partly wrong — the modify raises HttpError caught at 328-330 returning False.)
findings.rationale: Same defect as U369; the 'silent success' framing is slightly inaccurate but the underlying bug is the same.

findings.title: Gmail batchModify is treated as all-or-nothing, but invalid ids within a chunk fail the whole call, marking valid messages in the chunk as errored
findings.evidence: Lines 452-472: each chunk of up to BATCH_MODIFY_SIZE=1000 ids is one batchModify call. The except at 469-472 increments error_count by len(chunk) and leaves `succeeded` unchanged for the whole chunk. There is no per-id isolation or smaller-chunk fallback. Gmail returns a single 400 for the entire batchModify if any id is invalid, so one bad id strands up to 999 valid messages as error/unapplied; audit (482-495) then records labels_added=[] for all of them.
findings.rationale: Confirmed poison-pill: a single invalid id fails the whole 1000-id chunk with no isolation.

findings.location: apply_actions pending dict lines 434/484-487 (keyed by action.message_id)
findings.title: Gmail audit recording keyed by message_id collides on duplicate actions, dropping audit records for all but the last action on a given id
findings.evidence: pending is keyed by action.message_id (line 434); a second LabelAction for the same id overwrites the first's recording intent. The post-execution loop (483-495) iterates the full actions list but reads pending.get(action.message_id), so it emits one record per action all reading the last-written pending entry — under/mis-recording when two distinct actions share an id. Note LabelAction.merge exists (core/models.py:95) so callers can produce per-message coalesced actions, but cli.py merges before dispatch; same-id collisions are possible only if a caller does not coalesce.
findings.rationale: Real audit-collision on duplicate message_ids, but contingent on a caller emitting un-merged same-id actions; severity downgraded to low.

findings.title: IMAP SEARCH query not encoded/quoted; multi-word criteria and non-ASCII break or inject extra search tokens
findings.evidence: uid('search', None, query) passes a single positional query string; imaplib splits the arg by spaces internally (it does tokenize space-separated args), so 'FROM example.com SUBJECT "x"' is not sent as one literal token in the way claimed. The genuine residual is CHARSET=None for non-ASCII search terms. Note: this path is largely moot because U084 shows the query is Gmail-dialect and fails before quoting matters.
findings.rationale: CHARSET-for-non-ASCII concern is real but the tokenization claim misreads imaplib; low and overlapping with U084.

findings.title: Pagination offset math is inconsistent between the slice and the next_token, causing skipped/overlapping pages
findings.evidence: Confirmed by reproduction: window(total=50,start=60,limit=10) returns uids[0:-10] = 40 wrong-end messages instead of empty. next_token (206) is guarded `next_start < total` so the provider never EMITS a bad token; a stale persisted token (cli.py:172 state.get_token()) after the inbox shrinks is the realistic trigger. The 'window size only correct when start is a multiple of limit / diverges if limit changes' framing is largely the same fragility.
findings.rationale: Real fragility under stale/external tokens, but only off-the-happy-path; low severity, and same cluster as U376/U381/U640.

findings.title: Slice stop index total-start can become negative when start>total, silently yielding wrong/empty window
findings.evidence: Line 194 clamps the START index with max(0,...) but NOT the STOP index `total - start`. Reproduced: total=50,start=60,limit=10 → stop=-10 → uids[0:-10] returns 40 messages (wrong end) rather than an empty page. Triggered by a stale page_token (start>total) replayed from state (cli.py:172).
findings.rationale: Genuine negative-stop-index bug; clearest statement of the pagination cluster's root cause.

findings.title: fetch HEADER parsing assumes data[0] is a 2-tuple; raw/atom responses raise TypeError unhandled
findings.evidence: Line 221 guards `data[0] is None` but line 224 indexes data[0][1] without isinstance(data[0], tuple) — unlike the X-GM-LABELS (238) and FLAGS (254) parsing which DO guard the tuple shape. A non-tuple FETCH response would raise TypeError. base.batch_get_details (base.py:151) would need to be the catcher; it is not per-item-guarded for this.
