However real IMAP servers return a tuple for BODY[...] fetches, so this is an edge case.
findings.rationale: Real inconsistency vs the guarded parses below, but unlikely on conforming servers; low.

findings.title: X-GM-LABELS value not IMAP-quoted/escaped; labels with spaces, quotes, or backslashes corrupt the STORE command
findings.evidence: Lines 279/303 interpolate `f'"{label}"'` with no escaping of embedded quotes/backslashes. archive() (315) passes literal "\\Inbox" → STORE value `"\Inbox"`. Labels are from a static taxonomy today (LABEL_RULES keys, no embedded quotes), so practical breakage is limited; the \Inbox quoting works for Gmail's X-GM-LABELS in practice.
findings.rationale: Real lack of escaping but inputs are currently controlled; correctness risk is latent — low.

findings.title: X-GM-LABELS parsing regex `\(([^)]*)\)` is wrong for labels containing parentheses or quoted spaces; mis-splits labels
findings.evidence: Lines 243-247: re.search(r'\(([^)]*)\)', raw_str) then .split() on whitespace and strip('"'). For labels containing spaces inside quotes this shatters them; for labels containing ')' the [^)]* stops early. Real parsing weakness. But this only affects derived labels/is_starred detection (read-only enrichment), not any mutating operation, so blast radius is small.
findings.rationale: Genuine fragile parse, read-only impact on label/star detection only; severity downgraded to low.

findings.title: IMAP command/argument injection via unescaped label and mailbox names
findings.evidence: Confirmed: f'"{label}"' quoting (279/288/303/319) without escaping, create(label) raw (352), select(mailbox) raw (158), message_id passed raw to STORE/COPY/FETCH. CRLF/quote injection is theoretically possible. BUT label names come from the static LABEL_RULES taxonomy and message_id is server-issued UIDs; no attacker-controlled string reaches these paths in current code. The 'folder name synced from server' vector is not implemented here.
findings.rationale: Injection surface exists but is not reachable with attacker-controlled input today; severity overstated — partial/low.

findings.title: IMAP pagination slice can drop/duplicate messages and mis-handle offsets
findings.evidence: Same negative-stop-index-on-stale-token defect as U376; also restates that next_token=start+limit is computed independent of returned count (line 205-206).
findings.rationale: Same pagination root cause as U376.

findings.title: IMAP STORE/COPY return code not checked; broad except returns success/failure inconsistently
findings.evidence: Restates the unchecked-STORE-return defect (star/unstar/mark_read/mark_unread return True regardless of NO) = U085, plus the standard-archive no-expunge observation = U131. Two already-tracked root causes bundled.
findings.rationale: STORE-status half duplicates U085; the no-expunge half duplicates U131.

findings.title: Standard-IMAP archive sets \Deleted without EXPUNGE and reports success, leaving the message in the inbox flagged-deleted
findings.evidence: Same defect as U131: standard archive does COPY then +FLAGS \Deleted with no EXPUNGE, returns True, base.apply_actions records did_leave_inbox/archived=True (base.py:353-355,381). Hardcoded 'Archive' target. Verbatim the same disposition-mis-report.
findings.rationale: Same no-expunge / false-archived root cause as U131.

findings.title: IMAP folder/label names interpolated unquoted-or-naively into IMAP commands (command injection / breakage)
findings.evidence: Same unescaped-label-into-IMAP-command concern as U380 (X-GM-LABELS/COPY via f'"{label}"', CREATE raw at 352). Inputs are the static taxonomy; not attacker-reachable today.
findings.rationale: Same injection/quoting cluster as U380.

findings.title: 'first message whose id is' searches only current/default mailbox context, may target wrong or missing message
findings.evidence: Line 213/265 `first message whose id is {message_id}` is unscoped (no mailbox/account container), while list_messages scopes to a specific mailbox/account (line 139). Whether Mail.app resolves an unscoped `message` against an ambiguous default and whether ids collide across accounts is AppleScript runtime behavior.
findings.rationale: Plausible wrong-target/missing-message concern, but only confirmable by executing against Mail.app; cannot verify statically. Severity lowered (apply_label re-derives via ensure_label_exists; failure returns False, message stays put).

findings.title: Mailbox marked as created even when AppleScript fails, caching a false 'exists' state
findings.evidence: Line 350 `self._created_mailboxes.add(label)` runs inside try after _run_applescript (so a RuntimeError skips the add — the finding's own self-correction). The hierarchical-name concern (Mail.app treats 'Work/Dev/GitHub' as a literal name, not nesting; line 342 vs move at 266) is real in code but its effect is Mail.app runtime behavior.
findings.rationale: Static portion (add only on success) is benign as the finding admits; the '/'-not-nested mismatch is a real latent concern requiring Mail.app to confirm. Downgraded to low.

findings.title: Per-page O(n) message indexing causes quadratic cost and re-fetch of full message list each page
findings.evidence: Line 140 `set allMsgs to messages of targetMailbox` then line 146 `item i of allMsgs` per iteration, re-run each page (line 144 repeat). The materialization/indexing cost is AppleScript-runtime-dependent; the timeout->empty->silent-truncation chain is real (line 172-175).
findings.rationale: Performance characterization (O(N) per access / O(N^2) sweep) is plausible but a runtime claim about AppleScript collection access; the silent-truncation consequence is the verifiable part. Real but overstated/needs runtime to quantify.

findings.title: get_message_details TSV parse mis-handles sender/subject containing tabs (same delimiter-collision as list_messages)
findings.evidence: Line 226 joins sender & subject & flags with "\t"; line 235 `output.split("\t")`; line 236 `if len(parts) < 5`; line 239 `parts[:5]`. A tab in sender/subject shifts is_read/is_flagged/mailbox to wrong tokens.
findings.rationale: Same delimiter-collision class as U216, distinct method (get_message_details). Confirmed correctness bug.

findings.location: apply_label line 266, ensure_label_exists lines 338/342, list_messages account_filter line 133/261/332/409
findings.title: AppleScript injection: label/account/message_id interpolated unescaped into osascript
findings.evidence: Same unescaped interpolation lines. Verified the API-path claim: run_triage (api/service.py:70) reaches mailapp, but does NOT pass attacker-controlled label/account/mailbox — those come from server config, and `query` is unused by mailapp list_messages.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U090; the 'reachable from API path' note is technically true but does not introduce remote untrusted control of the interpolated values.

findings.title: AppleScript tab/linefeed-delimited parsing breaks on senders/subjects containing tabs or newlines
findings.evidence: Same tab/newline delimiter-collision in list_messages (179-197) as U216, plus the `---TOTAL:` sentinel-collision note (line 182: `if line.startswith("---TOTAL:")` would mis-handle a subject literally starting with '---TOTAL:').
findings.rationale: Same root-cause delimiter-collision defect as U216 in the same method; the sentinel-collision is the one incremental detail but same parsing bug.

findings.title: 30s osascript timeout with no recovery; TimeoutExpired loses partial output and orphan process risk
findings.evidence: timeout=30 (line 75) -> RuntimeError (line 82) -> list_messages returns empty (line 173-175). The 'partial mutation with no record' concern applies to move/flag scripts which do not catch into empty-result; but the silent-truncation core is the U217 mechanism.
findings.rationale: Same 30s-timeout/silent-empty-result defect as U217; the partial-mutation angle is a plausible extension of the same root cause.

findings.title: Inner find-folder failure raises a misleading 'Failed to create or find folder' for the whole label, not the failing segment
findings.evidence: Inner try/except (635-649): the RuntimeError at 649 fires only when _api_get itself throws; when it succeeds but returns empty value (644 false), no error is raised and the loop continues with stale parent_id. So the genuine 'not found' path is silent and the transient-GET-error path is fatal — inverted relative to intent.
findings.rationale: Confirmed inverted error semantics. Low severity (correctness nuance; combined effect already captured by U096). corroborated=false echoed.

findings.title: $top passed unbounded to Graph; values >1000 are rejected/clamped, and large $top with $orderby+$filter can 400
findings.evidence: Line 429 `"$top": limit` with no clamp. Main label loop caps batch_limit at 100 (cli.py 'min(limit - processed_this_run, 100)'), but cmd_escalate (cli.py 855-858) calls list_messages(limit=args.limit) directly with default 500 and user-overridable --limit (1093-1097). 500 < Graph's 1000 cap; >1000 only via explicit --limit 5000.
findings.rationale: The unbounded-$top defect is real (no clamp), but the >1000-rejection only fires on a user passing --limit>1000; default paths stay <=500. The $orderby+$filter sub-claim overlaps U134/U093 (needs_runtime). Partial, low.

findings.title: Unbounded recursive child-folder fetch with no depth limit or cycle/duplicate-name guard; deep trees risk perf/stack and name collisions overwrite cache entries
findings.evidence: Recursion at 272 with no depth cap; full_name key built as parent_name/child (270) so identical path strings overwrite in _folder_cache; $top:100 with no nextLink follow (268). All present.
findings.rationale: Real but overstated: Outlook folder hierarchies are not cyclic (tree-structured), so no infinite recursion; duplicate path-string collisions across distinct trees are rare for personal mailboxes. The pagination-truncation point duplicates U220. Partial, low.

findings.title: Token cache file written/read with default (world-readable) permissions — OAuth refresh token at rest is not protected
findings.evidence: Line 167 `open(self.token_cache_path, "w")` with no mode/chmod; repo-wide grep for chmod/0o600/umask in providers/ and auth/ returns nothing. Read at 153-154 has no try/except so a truncated/corrupt cache raises uncaught in _get_msal_app. No atomic temp-then-rename.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: refresh token persisted with inherited umask (commonly 0644) and non-atomic write. Low: single-user macOS home dir is the documented target; not a missing-dependency issue (msal IS in requirements.txt). corroborated=false echoed. Duplicate cluster with U398/U401/U403.

findings.title: acquire_token_silent error result not surfaced; falls through to interactive prompt in headless/automated runs
findings.evidence: Lines 177-187: if acquire_token_silent returns an error dict (no 'access_token'), the `if result and 'access_token' in result` (178) is false and execution falls through to acquire_token_interactive (184-187) which opens a browser. run_automation.sh runs headless via launchd per CLAUDE.md; no device-code/silent-only mode.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: a headless scheduled run whose silent refresh fails will invoke interactive auth and hang/fail rather than logging a clean re-auth-needed error. Medium for the documented daily automation use case. corroborated=false echoed.

findings.title: tier_routing target_folder is never honored by Outlook move path — tier folder routing is effectively a no-op for the actual move
findings.evidence: Verified base.apply_actions (342-345): it iterates ONLY action.add_labels and calls apply_label(message_id, label); it never reads action.target_folder. cli.py:257 sets action.target_folder = tier_config.folder but add_labels still holds the categorization label (cli.py:243). For Outlook, apply_label moves to ensure_label_exists(label)=the category folder, NOT the tier Action/* folder.
findings.rationale: Confirmed cross-file gap: target_folder is set but never consumed by any provider's apply path, so Outlook tier 'Action folders' moves are a no-op (only the category color is applied). Medium — documented feature partially non-functional. corroborated=false echoed.

findings.title: MSAL OAuth token cache written to disk with default (potentially world-readable) permissions
findings.evidence: Same `open(path,'w')` with no chmod 0600 (167) and grep-confirmed absence of chmod/umask. NOTE: the parenthetical 'undeclared'/dependency framing is moot — msal IS declared in requirements.txt:7.
findings.rationale: Same world-readable-token-cache root cause as U395. Duplicate.

findings.title: Folder create/find fallback can silently return the parent or raw label as the destination id, risking a move to the wrong folder
findings.evidence: Combines the raw-label-return-as-destination (651) and the unescaped OData $filter (642) — both already adjudicated under U096 and U218.
findings.rationale: Same root causes as U096 (return label as id) + U218 (OData escaping). Duplicate.

findings.title: apply_category swallows the read-current-categories error and proceeds with empty list, silently dropping existing categories
findings.evidence: Same read-fails->current_cats=[]->PATCH overwrites existing categories defect at 347-359.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U219. Duplicate.

findings.title: Token cache file written without restrictive permissions and silent failure modes around interactive auth in a server context
findings.evidence: Bundles the interactive-auth-in-headless-context (184) with the token-cache permissions (167) and uncaught corrupt-cache deserialize (153-154). The interactive-hang is the substantive part = U396; the permissions part = U395.
findings.rationale: Composite of U396 (headless interactive auth) and U395 (cache permissions). Duplicate.

findings.title: Bare/broad except: pass swallows folder-fetch and create errors silently
findings.evidence: Same `except Exception: pass` at 273-274 plus the ensure_label_exists generic-RuntimeError loses-cause point (already in U392).
findings.rationale: Same bare-except-pass folder-cache root cause as U222 (with U392 overlap). Duplicate.

findings.title: OAuth token cache written world-readable with no restrictive permissions
findings.evidence: Same world-readable token cache via plain open() at 167, no lock; grep-confirmed no chmod/0o600.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U395. Duplicate.

findings.title: Outlook silently swallows date-parse failures, falling back to None and disabling escalation for that message
findings.evidence: Lines 453-458 and 504-509: datetime.fromisoformat(...replace('Z','+00:00')) inside try/except ValueError that on failure leaves received=None with no log. msg.date=None then yields age 0 in the escalate path (calculate_email_age_hours), disabling escalation for that message silently.
findings.rationale: Confirmed silent date-drop with no warning log. Low: under normal Graph responses fromisoformat succeeds; the no-log degradation is the concrete defect. The Python-version/fractional-second sub-claim is U405.

findings.title: datetime.fromisoformat on Graph receivedDateTime rejects 7-digit fractional seconds on Python <3.11, silently dropping the date
findings.evidence: Code path confirmed (454-456/505-507). The Python<3.11 7-digit-fractional-second limitation is a real CPython behavior, and CI only tests 3.11/3.12 (.github/workflows/ci.yml:15 python-version ['3.11','3.12']), so it would be invisible in CI. But CLAUDE.md only STATES 'Python 3.9+'; whether a deploy actually runs 3.9/3.10 AND whether Graph returns 7-digit fractions for these mailboxes is environment-dependent.
findings.rationale: Real version-specific failure class but conditional on a 3.9/3.10 runtime that I cannot confirm is used (local is 3.14, CI is 3.11/3.12); narrows the round-1 U404 swallow to a specific cohort. Partial, low. corroborated=false echoed.

findings.title: response.json() on 202/204 (empty body) move/patch responses raises JSONDecodeError, turning a successful move into a reported failure
findings.evidence: Lines 218/225/232 all do `return response.json()` unconditionally after raise_for_status(). apply_label (533) and archive (551) call _api_post on /move; the response is discarded (`self._api_post(url, data)` return value unused), but json() runs first. Graph /move returns 201 with the moved-message body normally, yet PATCH/POST mutations can return 202/204 with empty bodies, on which response.json() raises JSONDecodeError. That exception is caught by the broad `except Exception` in each mutator (e.g. 535-537, 553-555, 585-587) and converted to return False.
findings.rationale: Real success-as-failure misclassification path on empty-body 2xx responses, but Graph move/patch normally return bodies; impact is occasional, swallowed, and metadata-only — low blast radius.

findings.title: datetime.fromisoformat fails on Graph timestamps with sub-second precision beyond 6 digits or fractional 'Z', silently disabling escalation
findings.evidence: Lines 454-458 and 505-509 do `datetime.fromisoformat(msg['receivedDateTime'].replace('Z','+00:00'))` inside try/except ValueError: pass. On Python 3.14 (this env) fromisoformat accepts 7-digit fractions (verified: parses '...123456+00:00'), but CLAUDE.md documents a 3.9+ floor and there is no requires-python pin (checked pyproject.toml). On 3.9/3.10 fromisoformat rejects >6 digit fractions, which Graph (.NET DateTimeOffset, 7-digit ticks) can emit, raising ValueError that is silently swallowed to date=None, disabling age escalation for those messages.
findings.rationale: Real only on the documented 3.9/3.10 floor (false on the 3.11+ runtime actually installed); silent escalation-disable, not data loss — overstated severity, version-conditional.

findings.title: Token bound into the requests.Session Authorization header at first use is never refreshed even though MSAL silent-refresh exists
findings.evidence: connect() (236) calls _acquire_token() once. _get_session() (206-210) bakes `Bearer {self._access_token}` into self._session.headers on first call and caches the Session; subsequent _api_* reuse it. Nothing re-invokes _acquire_token or refreshes the header mid-run; only disconnect() (243-246) clears both. A run exceeding the ~1h access-token lifetime would 401, caught by broad excepts → per-item False.
findings.rationale: Mechanism is accurately described and real, but only bites runs longer than the token lifetime (rare for triage batches capped at 1000) — low practical blast radius.

findings.title: Outlook folder lookup builds an OData $filter via f-string with an unescaped folder name (OData injection / breakage)
findings.evidence: Line 641-643: params={'$filter': f"displayName eq '{part}'"} where `part` is an unescaped label segment. A single quote in the segment breaks the OData literal (Graph 400) or could alter filter semantics; the surrounding except (648) then raises RuntimeError('Failed to create or find folder').
findings.rationale: Genuine OData-injection/breakage on the folder-find fallback, but label segments come from a fixed internal taxonomy today, so practical exposure is low. Duplicate root-cause with U852.

findings.title: $top=limit passed verbatim to Graph with no per-page cap or pagination loop
findings.evidence: Line 429 `params['$top'] = limit`. API schema (api/schemas.py:16,47) sets MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT=1000 and Field(le=1000), so limit can reach 1000 — Graph's documented $top cap, so no 400 in normal use. The method returns only one page (next_page_token from @odata.nextLink, 469-472); the CLI loop drives pagination (cli.py 183-298). There is no clamp to a smaller page size, but the loop already passes min(...,100).
findings.rationale: Single-page-per-call is by design (caller loops); $top up to 1000 is within Graph's cap, so the 'returns 400' claim is largely false. Real residual: no defensive clamp. Overstated → partial/low.

findings.title: MCP server's documented standalone HTTP run command needs uvicorn/starlette that requirements-mcp.txt does not declare
findings.evidence: requirements-mcp.txt has only `mcp>=1.2,<2` (line 6); uvicorn[standard]>=0.29 and fastapi (Starlette) live in requirements-api.txt. server.py:20 docstring documents `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app`. BUT the file header (lines 3-5) instructs `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt`, and EVERY real install path installs all three: Dockerfile RUN line installs all three, ci.yml:30 installs all three, render.yaml uses the Docker runtime. The CMD itself runs `uvicorn api.app:app` (the API entrypoint, which has uvicorn from requirements-api.txt and mounts /mcp), not the standalone `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app`.
findings.rationale: Real gap only under a hypothetical MCP-only install (requirements.txt + requirements-mcp.txt alone) that no instruction or deploy path actually performs; overstated as a deployability break.

findings.title: `requests>=2.28.0` floor permits known-vuln versions (CVE-2023-32681 proxy cert leak, CVE-2024-35195 verify=False persistence)
findings.evidence: Confirmed `requests>=2.28.0` (line 8) permits 2.28.x-2.31.x: CVE-2023-32681 fixed in 2.31.0, CVE-2024-35195 fixed in 2.32.0, so the floor admits versions vulnerable to both. However the floor only PERMITS vulnerable versions; a fresh resolve picks latest (the local .venv resolved requests 2.34.2). No pin forces an old version.
findings.rationale: Real floor-hygiene issue (should be >=2.32.0) but exposure is conditional on a resolver/environment choosing an old version; not an active vulnerability in a fresh install. Severity reduced to low.

findings.title: `ends with dom` matching is unreliable and `contains` can over-match, risking wrong-folder moves
findings.evidence: Lines 6-14 define is_bulk_sender with `contains ("@" & dom) or theSender ends with dom`. The `ends with dom` branch is unreliable because `sender of msg` is typically 'Name <user@host>' ending in '>' (confirmed unparsed in providers/mailapp.py:190,193), so it rarely ends in a bare domain. `contains` is a substring test that can over-match short configured domains. Moves (line 28) are wrapped in a bare `try` (lines 25-30) with no `on error`, so misroutes are silent. Default list line 2 includes placeholder 'newsletter.yourfav.com'.
findings.rationale: All component claims verified against the file; this is the round-1 correctness framing of the same matching logic flagged more sharply by U099/U413 — kept as confirmed.

findings.title: Default bulkDomains ships an example domain 'newsletter.yourfav.com' and runs unedited
findings.evidence: Line 2: `set bulkDomains to {"mailchimp.com", "substack.com", "newsletter.yourfav.com"}`. First two are live real domains; third is a placeholder. The file contains no dry-run guard or confirmation prompt (no occurrence of dry-run/dryRun in the 33 lines), and line 28 unconditionally `move msg to bulkMailbox`. Run unedited, it immediately moves all mailchimp.com and substack.com inbox mail out to Newsletters.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: shipped defaults contain live domains and the script applies destructive moves with no dry-run, so running unedited acts on real mail.

findings.title: set -e plus `source` of op env file aborts whole run if any `op read` fails
findings.evidence: The real sourced file ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh wraps every `op read` substitution with `... 2>/dev/null) || true` (lines 26-28) and guards the whole block with `if command -v op &>/dev/null; then ... fi`. A failing `op read` therefore returns 0 via `|| true` and does NOT trip `set -e`. The finding's premise (an unguarded op lookup aborting the run at the source line) does not hold against the actual env file.
findings.rationale: The env file already defends against set -e abort with `|| true` on each op read; no unguarded failure path exists as described.

findings.title: Test never exercises tamper-detection on GET, and verification only passes because sign and verify share the same in-process ephemeral key
findings.evidence: api/receipts.py: verify(body,sig) -> sign(body) -> _signing_key(), which returns env RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY or a module-global _EPHEMERAL_KEY (secrets.token_bytes(32) cached per process). So in-process sign and verify ALWAYS share the same key by construction — the cross-restart/rotation failure mode named in the docstring ('Receipts signed now will not verify after a restart') cannot be exercised by these tests. The GET endpoint get_receipt (lines 114-141) returns rec['signature'] verbatim with NO server-side recompute/verify, so a tampered stored row is not rejected by the endpoint; the test asserts tamper-detection only via in-process receipts.verify at test_receipts.py:52, never against the server. grep confirms RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY is never set anywhere in tests/, so the durable-key path is uncovered. test_sign_then_verify_roundtrip (11-17) likewise only proves in-process sign/verify symmetry plus a body-mutation flip.
findings.rationale: Every factual claim is accurate against current code, but this is a test-coverage/over-confidence gap (production code is unchanged and correct for the in-process case), not a code defect — blast radius is limited to false test confidence, so medium is overstated; downgraded to low.

findings.title: No test covers negative-n on consume_credit/add_credits (overdraw-via-negative money bug uncovered)
findings.evidence: consume_credit (store.py:239-246) binds int(n) to both the SET (run_credits - ?) and the WHERE (run_credits >= ?). For n=-5 the guard becomes run_credits >= -5 (always true on a non-negative balance) and the arithmetic becomes run_credits-(-5)=+5 — a free-credit path. add_credits (store.py:223-233) does run_credits + int(n) with no floor, so n=-100 drives the balance negative. No upstream n>0 validation exists (no validation in service.py/app.py). test_credits_atomic_no_overdraw (35-43) only feeds positive n (5,3,5), so negative/zero is uncovered.
findings.rationale: The sign-abuse arithmetic and the test-coverage gap are real, but no production caller currently passes negative n (fulfill_once passes pack quantities), so the practical blast radius is a latent/test-only gap rather than a live exploit — severity downgraded to low.

findings.title: Live audit counts interpolated into innerHTML without escaping (DOM-XSS sink)
findings.evidence: a.protected_held/archived/moved/labeled/kept are interpolated into innerHTML with only `?? 0` (lines 866-870), no escapeHtml. Confirmed unescaped. However both real backends type-coerce these to int: FastAPI AuditSummary (schemas.py:54-61, all `int`) and worker.mjs triagePreview emits numeric literals. Exploit requires API_BASE pointed at a hostile origin; API_BASE = window.__MAIL_AUTOMATION_API_BASE__ || '' (line 677) with no injection site found anywhere in the repo, so it is same-origin on the only deployment.
findings.rationale: Real missing-escape on numeric sinks but blocked by int-typed schemas on both shipped backends; only exploitable under an attacker-controlled API_BASE that the deployment never sets — defense-in-depth, not a live vuln.

findings.title: categorization.tier interpolated into innerHTML unescaped; server schema does not coerce it
findings.evidence: Confirmed asymmetry: line 812 `const tier = cat.tier != null ? \`tier ${cat.tier}\` : ""` is raw, while line 813 escapes cat.label via escapeHtml; both flow into out.innerHTML (816/818). SenderCheckResponse.categorization is Optional[dict] (schemas.py:36) — NOT coerced, so a non-numeric tier would pass through. But the categorization engine emits integer tiers (PRIORITY_TIERS 1-4; worker.mjs emits numeric tier 1/3/4), and the value is server-derived from rules, not user input. Exploit needs the engine/backend to emit HTML in tier, or a hostile API_BASE (never set).
findings.rationale: The label-escaped/tier-unescaped inconsistency is a real latent sink worth a one-line fix, but tier is server-controlled and numeric in practice; not exploitable on the shipped surfaces.

findings.title: Checkout redirect assigns server-supplied URL to window.location with no host validation
findings.evidence: Line 888: `if (ok && data && data.url) { window.location.href = data.url; }` with no scheme/host allowlist — confirmed unvalidated redirect sink. Reachable only on a 2xx /v1/billing/checkout response carrying `url`. On the only deployment the worker returns 503 for /v1/billing/checkout (worker.mjs:269-270), so this branch never fires on the demo; on the FastAPI backend data.url is a server-built Stripe Checkout Session URL, not attacker-supplied. Requires a compromised backend or hostile API_BASE (never set) to weaponize.
findings.rationale: Real missing host-allowlist on a money-path redirect (good hardening target), but not reachable on the demo and the legit backend supplies a trusted Stripe URL; severity downgraded from medium.

findings.title: Charge treats only status=='succeeded' as ok, but a PaymentIntent in 'requires_capture' or 'processing' is neither charged-final nor refunded — fulfillment is skipped while funds may be held/captured later
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U106: only status=='succeeded' returns ok=True (line 92); 'processing'/'requires_action' return ok=False with no async-completion handler (confirmed: no payment_intent.* webhook). U421 adds 'requires_capture' but the code uses default automatic capture (no capture_method in params 73-83), so requires_capture is not reachable here.
findings.rationale: Identical defect and location as U106 (status-gate drops async settlement into permanent failure). Marked duplicate; the requires_capture variant is not reachable given automatic capture.

findings.title: ACP product feed 'price' is emitted in cents (100 / 900) which may be misread as dollars by an ACP consumer
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U108: feed 'price' = pack['amount_cents'] (acp/feed.py:48) yielding 100/900. The finding itself hedges ('Confirm the ACP 2026-04-17 feed spec's price unit; if it is major units this is a money/pricing bug, if minor units it is correct but undocumented') — and the repo answers that question: minor units (acp/models.py:8). Same defect, lower-severity framing.
findings.rationale: Same price-unit observation as U108 on the same fields; self-hedged to the very spec question the codebase resolves in favor of correct-but-undeclared.

findings.title: A session can be re-priced via update after being READY, and amount charged at /complete is recomputed from line_items — but update does not re-validate that items still map to known packs against a tampered stored session
findings.evidence: Restates the no-re-derivation-from-CREDIT_PACKS-at-completion concern: amount=grand_total(stored line_items) (257) and credited total_runs from row (246) are taken from persisted state, not re-validated against the catalog at charge time. Same line_items/repricing root cause as U259; the total_runs-vs-line_items angle overlaps U266.
findings.rationale: Same defect family as U259 (charge derived from mutable stored line_items, no catalog re-validation at /complete).

findings.location: complete_session() lines 273-280 vs 332-337 (status persistence on failure)
findings.title: Payment-failed branch persists session as STATUS_READY but the response includes only the failure message; a concurrent successful /complete and a failed one both persist, last-write-wins can resurrect READY over COMPLETED
findings.evidence: save_session is INSERT OR REPLACE (store.py:407-412) with no compare-and-set on status. Two concurrent /complete (different Idempotency-Keys, so both pass _begin_idempotency as 'new'): A succeeds → persists COMPLETED (337); B declines → persists STATUS_READY (273-278). If B's write lands after A's, the row reverts to READY for an already-fulfilled+credited session. A later GET shows READY; another /complete re-enters charge (Stripe dedups, fulfill_once returns False) leaving an incoherent state machine. No atomic status transition guard exists.
findings.rationale: Last-write-wins on a non-CAS INSERT OR REPLACE confirmed; concurrent mixed-outcome completes can resurrect READY over COMPLETED. Requires concurrency with distinct idempotency keys, so medium.

findings.title: No guard against amount <= 0 before charging / fulfilling
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U260 (no amount<=0 guard before charge/fulfill; relies on catalog never holding a zero/negative price; NullPaymentClient always fails per payment.py:55-56). Same lines 257-266.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U260.

findings.title: On a charge-success-but-fulfill_once-False replay, the session is re-persisted bound to whichever bearer last called /complete, rewriting account_id ownership
findings.evidence: router.py:337 always _persist(..., account_id=account['id']) with the CALLER's account, with no ownership check that this bearer matches the original fulfillment recipient. fulfill_once may return False (already credited to a different account) yet the success branch still runs and rewrites acp_sessions.account_id via INSERT OR REPLACE (store.py:407-412). acp_fulfillments.account_id (store.py:92-97) records the true recipient, so the two can disagree.
findings.rationale: Confirmed ownership rewrite on re-complete by a different bearer; impact is audit/reconciliation correctness (true recipient preserved in acp_fulfillments), so low.

findings.title: Checkout creates a brand-new free account when account_id absent but the created account_id is only returned in the JSON, not bound to any session/cookie
findings.evidence: billing.py:121-123 creates a free account when req.account_id is falsy and returns its id only in the JSON body (143). No cookie/session binding and no idempotency key on checkout; repeated POSTs each mint a fresh account + Stripe session.
findings.rationale: Real UX/orphan-accumulation gap; the actual grant still rides client_reference_id back through the webhook, so the front-end must persist the returned account_id. Low, as the finding itself rates it.

findings.title: Expanded Stripe 'customer'/'subscription' object (dict) would be used as a customer id, causing wrong lookups or a 500
findings.evidence: Same code as U286: billing.py:227/245/266/273 assume obj.get('customer') (and obj.get('subscription') at 228) are string ids with no isinstance guard. A dict would mis-lookup (orphan create) or raise sqlite3.InterfaceError in store.py -> uncaught -> 500 -> unmarked -> redelivery loop.
findings.rationale: Plausible only under an expand/API-version config that returns expanded objects, which cannot be confirmed statically; default webhooks send id strings. Root-cause duplicate of U286.

findings.title: Receipt verification recomputes sign(body) from a body reconstructed out of the DB; type coercion on round-trip (dry_run, summary) could break signature match for some receipts
findings.evidence: Sign-time summary is triage_result['audit'] = audit.summary() (audit.py 241-251): str keys, ints, and violations=list(self.violations) of str ids — all JSON-round-trip-stable scalar types. test_receipts.py:48-52 actually does GET after a real DB persist and asserts receipts.verify(rec['signed_body'], rec['signature']) is True, proving the round-trip is byte-faithful for the current shape. The claimed float/tuple mismatch requires a summary type that audit.summary() does not currently emit.
findings.rationale: Mechanism is correct (verification couples to JSON round-trip fidelity) but the concrete failure is not reachable with the current summary shape — a passing test confirms it. Latent hazard only; confidence-low finding correctly. Downgraded medium->low.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite3 connection with autocommit-per-statement and no transaction grouping means multi-statement money operations are not atomic if an exception occurs between statements
findings.evidence: Each write method calls self._conn.commit() at its end with no try/except/rollback (e.g. fulfill_once 374-389, set_subscription 217-221). Python sqlite3 default isolation_level='' means DML opens an implicit txn. No BEGIN/COMMIT wraps multiple store methods.
findings.rationale: True that there is no cross-method transaction and no explicit rollback-on-error. But each single method's multi-statement work (INSERT+UPDATE in fulfill_once) is committed atomically in one commit(), and an exception before commit() leaves the implicit txn to be rolled back by the next commit/close — sqlite3 does not 'inadvertently commit' prior aborted statements. Overstated; the money-path atomicity within a method is actually intact. Low.

findings.title: idempotency_complete updates status='done' even if the key row was reclaimed by another request, with no compare-and-set on the original claim
findings.evidence: idempotency_complete (355-362) UPDATEs by key with no compare-and-set against the original claim's created_at/request_hash. A slow original request that was stale-reclaimed (begin rebinds request_hash at 339-342) can still complete and overwrite the new claimant's response_json.
findings.rationale: Code-true last-writer-wins on the stored idempotent response. Requires the >60s stale-reclaim window to actually fire (charge p99 << 60s per U739), so low blast radius. Distinct enough from U308 (missing-row) to stand, but same method.

findings.title: Replay of a key whose request completed without storing a response returns None as the replay body, which router returns as a JSON 'null' 200 response
findings.evidence: Line 352: `json.loads(existing['response_json'] or 'null')` yields None when response_json is NULL on a 'done' key; router returns state['response'] (router.py:117). Same defect described verbatim by U742.
findings.rationale: Identical root cause to U742 (None-response-on-done-key conflated with proceed/null-body). The 'done with NULL response' state is also not reachable on any current path since idempotency_complete always writes json.dumps(response). Duplicate.

findings.title: save_receipt uses INSERT OR REPLACE — an id collision silently overwrites a prior signed receipt
findings.evidence: Lines 282-289: `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO receipts (run_id PRIMARY KEY ...)`. run_id is 'run_'+token_hex(12) (api/app.py:127) for triage and 'order_'+token_hex(12) (acp/router.py:296) for ACP — different prefixes make cross-collision impossible, as the finding itself concedes.
findings.rationale: Real OR-REPLACE-clobbers-signed-receipt observation, but same defect and same location as U525. 96-bit random ids and disjoint prefixes make collision practically unreachable; severity low. Duplicate of U525.

findings.location: build_agent_manifest lines 17-51 / agent_manifest line 78-80 (no Cache-Control, reflects request.base_url)
findings.title: Agent manifest/llms.txt reflect Host into absolute URLs AND set no Cache-Control, so a poisoned response can be cached and served to other agents
findings.evidence: Confirmed the responses set no Cache-Control: JSONResponse(...) line 80 and PlainTextResponse(...) line 85 pass no headers, and no middleware adds cache directives (app.py installs none). The underlying Host reflection is the same as U310. However, the cache-poisoning amplification requires an intermediary/CDN that caches keyed on path-only while the body varies by Host — that infrastructure is not present in the repo and is purely hypothetical here.
findings.rationale: The no-Cache-Control fact is true, but the persistent-poisoning impact is conditional on an unverified CDN/cache layer; it amplifies U310's root cause rather than standing alone, so downgraded to low/partial.

findings.title: apply_category/remove_category operate on /me/messages/{id} but the categorize+move pipeline may move the message first, leaving categories applied to a now-stale-folder message id
findings.evidence: Ordering confirmed in base.apply_actions (base.py 344 apply_label/move → 357 star → 359 apply_category). apply_category (343) and remove_category (377) PATCH /me/messages/{message_id} with the ORIGINAL id. Whether Graph invalidates that id after a /move is the load-bearing premise and cannot be confirmed statically.
findings.rationale: Ordering hazard is real in code, but the defect hinges on Graph's move-id semantics (Graph typically preserves the message id across moves), which is unverifiable without a live mailbox — needs runtime confirmation; low if id is preserved.

findings.title: CI never exercises the documented 3.9 core floor nor the stripe/mcp lower version bounds
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U007/U241: matrix ["3.11","3.12"] (ci.yml:15) + combined install (ci.yml:30). Lower bounds (stripe>=15.2 in requirements-api.txt, mcp>=1.2 in requirements-mcp.txt, fastapi>=0.110, pydantic>=2.0) are never validated because pip resolves to latest allowed. 3.9 core-only path untested.
findings.rationale: Accurate but is the same defect as U007 (missing 3.9 floor) plus the U241 install-coupling point, just severity-rated 'low'. Marking duplicate of U007 (same-file root cause: CI never exercises the 3.9 floor).

findings.title: Smoke test runs AFTER deploy with no rollback, so a broken worker is already live before the test can fail
findings.evidence: ci.yml:100-106 'Deploy Worker' (wrangler deploy publishes immediately) precedes the smoke test at 108-119 in the same job with no rollback step anywhere in the workflow. On smoke failure the job simply goes red; the broken worker is already live on uma.4444j99.dev.
findings.rationale: Correct: smoke is post-hoc, not a gate, and there is no rollback. Low blast radius (a public demo domain, manual revert available). Same family as U240/U485 but distinct angle (no-rollback vs no-retry).

findings.title: CHANGELOG omits all of the commerce/API/MCP/ACP/Cloudflare work — stale vs current repo state
findings.evidence: CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] (lines 10-13) lists only 'Platinum Sprint: CI/CD workflow, standardized badge row, ADR documentation' and 'Initial CHANGELOG'. The tree contains substantial unrecorded features: api/ (app.py, billing.py, store.py, plans.py, schemas.py, well_known.py), mcp_server/, acp/ package (router.py, feed.py, models.py, payment.py = Agentic Commerce Protocol), wrangler.toml (Cloudflare share Worker), Stripe billing, signed-receipt ledger (tests/test_receipts.py), pricing tiers (api/plans.py). requirements-api.txt and requirements-mcp.txt exist. None of this work appears in the changelog.
findings.rationale: Real documentation-staleness gap: changelog materially understates the project; verified by directory listing and grep showing the commerce/API/MCP/ACP/Cloudflare surfaces all exist on disk but are absent from CHANGELOG. Low severity is correct (docs only, no runtime/correctness impact).

findings.title: GEMINI.md contains only auto-generated ORGANVM system boilerplate — no project-specific guidance, contradicting AGENTS.md which calls it the 'overview'
findings.evidence: GEMINI.md is entirely the auto-generated block: ORGANVM:AUTO:START (line 1) through ORGANVM:AUTO:END (line 114) covering System Context, Active Directives, Trivium, Logos, plus a Google prompting note (line 71: '**Prompting (Google)**: context 1M tokens') and a generic Conductor OS Integration section (lines 119-124). Grep confirms zero mention of the project's own surfaces (no 'CLI', 'provider', 'gmail_labeler', 'rules', 'categoriz', or 'email' in the file). AGENTS.md:9 verbatim: '- Reference docs: `GEMINI.md` (overview) and `PROCESS.md` (rule-mapping plan).'
findings.rationale: AGENTS.md cites GEMINI.md as the project 'overview', but the file holds only system-wide ORGANVM boilerplate with no project-specific guidance, so the cross-reference is misleading; documentation-only, no code blast radius.

findings.title: Inconsistent minimum Python version across docs (3.10 vs 3.9)
findings.evidence: Same README lines (2, 241) and same CLAUDE.md:278 '3.9+' vs README '3.10+' contradiction as U140, citing the same api/app.py:33-34 and requirements-mcp.txt:2-3 corroboration. Both are the identical Python-version-floor doc-drift defect.
findings.rationale: Identical root cause to U140 (Python version floor disagreement across README/CLAUDE.md); merge.

findings.title: ACP feed hardcodes the spec version literal instead of importing API_VERSION — version drift risk
findings.evidence: acp/__init__.py:19 `API_VERSION = "2026-04-17"` (with __all__ exporting it, line 15). acp/router.py:32 `from acp import API_VERSION`. acp/feed.py has no API_VERSION import (imports only `from api import plans`) and hardcodes `"version": "2026-04-17"` at line 60. Same literal duplicated.
findings.rationale: Verified duplicate-literal drift risk: router uses the single source of truth, feed hardcodes it; a future bump in acp/__init__.py would diverge silently. Low severity, maintainability.

findings.title: Value-score penalty keys on bare 'off' and '%' substrings, over-penalizing legitimate subjects
findings.evidence: Line 48: any(x in subject_lower for x in ['digest','summary','log','alert','notification','receipt','order','off','%']). Substring 'off' matches 'office','offer','kickoff','cutoff','offsite'; '%' matches any percent-bearing subject. Penalty is -20 (line 49).
findings.rationale: Bare-substring matching of 'off' and '%' over-penalizes legitimate subjects; confirmed logic flaw in an advisory heuristic, low severity is correct.

findings.location: line 28 (from cli import get_provider, run_labeler) -> cli.py:48 logging.basicConfig
findings.title: Importing the FastAPI app triggers logging.basicConfig() as an import-time side effect
findings.evidence: service.py:28 does module-scope `from cli import get_provider, run_labeler`. cli.py:48-51 calls logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s') at module top level. api/app.py:28 imports api.service, so importing the app triggers cli's import-time basicConfig as a side effect.
findings.rationale: Verified import-time root-logger reconfiguration; classic library side-effect anti-pattern. Low severity is correct (best fixed by moving basicConfig into cli's __main__).

findings.title: idempotency_keys and webhook_events grow unbounded — no TTL/cleanup, monotonic disk growth and ever-slower scans
findings.evidence: No DELETE/cleanup of idempotency_keys or webhook_events anywhere (grep for DELETE in api/store.py returns nothing). Rows are inserted per ACP request (327-331) and per webhook (254-258) and never pruned. No created_at index, no wal_autocheckpoint.
findings.rationale: Confirmed unbounded monotonic growth with no retention/vacuum. Pure operability slow-burn, no correctness impact; severity low as stated.

findings.title: add_credits() is dead production code — ACP fulfillment inlines its own UPDATE, nothing else credits
findings.evidence: grep across acp/, api/, mcp_server/ finds add_credits only in tests/test_store.py:38. The live credit path acp/router.py:290 calls store.fulfill_once, which inlines `UPDATE accounts SET run_credits = run_credits + ?` at store.py:383-387, never add_credits.
findings.rationale: Verified dead production code; the documented crediting helper diverges from the actual crediting site. Low severity (latent risk if a future caller uses it outside fulfill_once's dedup).

findings.title: store_json_secret is dead code (no callers) — write-back actually goes through gmail_auth._op_item_edit, a duplicate implementation
findings.evidence: grep for store_json_secret across *.py returns only auth/onepassword.py:233 (the def). The live token write-back is gmail_auth.store_token_info (line 144) -> gmail_auth._op_item_edit (line 56), a near-identical private duplicate of op_item_edit. auth/__init__.py exports only load_secret/load_json_secret, not the store/op_* helpers.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: store_json_secret has zero callers and the write path runs through a parallel gmail_auth implementation; any security fix must be applied twice.

findings.title: Bare `except:` swallows all errors in extract_domain
findings.evidence: Line 63 `except:` is bare. The body `sender.split('@')[-1].strip('>]').lower()` cannot raise for a str, so the except is dead defensiveness; it would also swallow KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit. Unparseable senders → None → dropped via `if dom:` (line 128).
findings.rationale: Accurate low-severity code-smell. Same bare-except concern as U161/U448 but this is the cleanest, most-scoped statement of it.

findings.title: Bare except swallows all errors and returns None domain
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U447: bare `except:` at line 63 catching BaseException, returning None, downstream `if dom:` drop (line 128). Recommends `except Exception` or None-guard.
findings.rationale: Correct but duplicates U447 (same line, same defect).

findings.title: Audit disabled silently when --dry-run, so dry-run apply previews bypass receipt unless audit object passed; _record_dry_run_intent only runs if audit is not None
findings.evidence: _make_audit returns None when getattr(args,'dry_run',False) (365-366). cmd_label (437) and cmd_escalate (845) call _make_audit, so audit is None during dry-run. In run_labeler the dry-run recorder is gated `if actions and dry_run and audit is not None:` (289), which is never True from the CLI since dry_run implies audit is None. Thus _record_dry_run_intent (55-89) and the gate at 289-290 are unreachable via the only CLI caller.
findings.rationale: Confirmed dead code under current CLI wiring: _record_dry_run_intent can only fire if a caller passes a non-None audit together with dry_run=True, which cmd_label never does (audit is forced None in dry-run). The documented 'preview records intended disposition' feature is unreachable from the CLI. Low — dead feature, not a runtime fault.

findings.location: cmd_summary markdown lines 696 / cmd_pending markdown lines 693-696 / cmd_vip markdown lines 797-800, 793
findings.title: Markdown table/code-span injection from unescaped sender, subject, and VIP pattern
findings.evidence: cmd_pending markdown row: `print(f"| {item['tier']} | {vip}{item['sender'][:30]} | {item['subject'][:30]} | {age_str} |")` (696) — raw sender/subject, no '|' escaping. cmd_vip: `print(f"Pattern: \`{vip.pattern}\`")` (793) and `print(f"| {m['date']} | {m['sender'][:25]} | {m['subject'][:30]} |")` (800) — raw interpolation into table cells / code span. A subject containing '|' breaks table columns; a backtick in a pattern breaks the code span; markdown links pass through.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: markdown output paths interpolate untrusted sender/subject/pattern without escaping pipes/backticks/link syntax. Real but low — these are local CLI reports printed for the operator's own mailbox; the 'injection' risk is rendering corruption / link smuggling if the operator pastes the report elsewhere, not code execution.

findings.title: PROTECTED set is dead code and protected-sender logic is duplicated/inconsistent in the fallthrough branch
findings.evidence: PROTECTED Set (line 1) is never referenced (grep of file confirms). Actual decision is inline: .gov broad (106), chase (128), fallthrough value.includes('chase.com')||value.includes('1password.com') (153) where chase is unreachable. Declared set != computed protection.
findings.rationale: Confirmed dead constant + duplicated/inconsistent fallthrough. Correctly rated low (maintainability/correctness hazard). This is the round-1 root finding that U057/U176 re-derive.

findings.location: /v1/triage handler lines 260-263 vs /v1/triage/preview lines 255-258; cross-ref api/app.py real triage semantics
findings.title: /v1/triage (the apply path) returns dry_run:true and is byte-identical to /v1/triage/preview — the worker erases the preview-vs-apply distinction the whole product is built on
findings.evidence: Lines 255-258 and 260-263 both call triagePreview(body.provider||'demo', Number(body.limit)); triagePreview always returns dry_run:true. api/app.py 91-95: /v1/triage -> _run(dry_run=req.dry_run) -> service.run_triage (live apply path).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the apply path is byte-identical to preview on the worker. Same root defect as U059; low because this is the explicitly non-canonical demo surface.

findings.title: Marketing classifier matches bare substrings 'deal' and 'news' anywhere in the sender — 'newsletter@irs-deals.example', 'jdealer@gmail.com' misclassified
findings.evidence: Line 150: const marketing = value.includes('deal') || value.includes('news'). Substring on whole lowercased From -> 'idealist.org', 'jdealer@...', 'newson@...', any 'newsletter' match -> classified Marketing tier 3.
findings.rationale: Confirmed substring over-matching in the JS classifier. Low — affects only the cosmetic category label on a demo; no archiving consequence (Marketing tier-3 vs Misc tier-4 both unprotected, both archivable in the demo's verdict).

findings.location: senderCheck lines 154-165 fall-through tier_config color always 'amber'
findings.title: Fall-through tier_config.color is hardcoded 'amber' for BOTH Marketing (tier 3) and Misc/Other (tier 4) — Reference should be 'green' per the engine tier table
findings.evidence: Fall-through tier_config: name = marketing?'Delegate':'Reference', but color is hardcoded 'amber' (line 161) for both. Empty-sender branch (line 95) uses color:'green' for the same tier-4 Reference. So tier-4 Reference returns 'amber' here vs 'green' there — inconsistent within one function.
findings.rationale: Confirmed internal color inconsistency for identical tier. Low cosmetic defect.

findings.title: Worker returns wrong tier color 'amber' for Reference (tier 4) and Delegate (tier 3); README/core define green and blue
findings.evidence: Line 161 hardcodes color:'amber' for both tier-3 Delegate and tier-4 Reference. README.md tier table (verified lines 190-191): Tier 3 Delegate = Blue, Tier 4 Reference = Green. The .gov/chase branches correctly use 'red' for tier 1.
findings.rationale: Confirmed documentation/implementation drift (Delegate should be blue, Reference green). Low cosmetic. Same code locus as U454; echoing corroborated:false.

findings.title: /v1/audit (no trailing slash) does not match the audit handler and silently serves static assets
findings.evidence: Line 281: `url.pathname.startsWith("/v1/audit/")` requires the trailing slash. A GET to `/v1/audit` (no slash) does not match, falls through to `serveApp` (line 320) -> `env.ASSETS.fetch(request)`, serving static assets instead of JSON. The API (api/app.py:8) exposes `GET /v1/audit/{run_id}` which would JSON-404.
findings.rationale: Code literally requires the trailing slash; `/v1/audit` falls through to ASSETS, a real contract divergence (low impact, demo-only fabricated bodies).

findings.title: Worker /llms.txt diverges from the canonical builder and advertises non-existent worker routes
findings.evidence: Worker lines 307-318 hardcode an llms.txt that does NOT match build_llms_txt (well_known.py:54-75): the canonical version lists MCP, ACP checkout, product feed; the worker version lists only Health, Sender check, Triage preview, Pricing, Audit receipt. The worker version does NOT advertise mcp/acp (the description's claim it 'advertises the MCP/ACP surfaces' is the inverse of reality — the canonical one does). gen_commerce_artifacts.py (lines 99-100) writes root llms.txt but never touches worker.mjs, so drift is real.
findings.rationale: Drift is real and confirmed, but the finding's specific claim that the worker advertises non-existent mcp/acp routes is backwards — the worker omits them; the canonical builder includes them. Overstated/misread, hence partial.

findings.title: Marketing categorization is a naive substring on 'deal'/'news' producing false categorizations and an always-amber tier_config color
findings.evidence: Line 150: `const marketing = value.includes("deal") || value.includes("news")` — naive substring (matches dealer@, idealist@, newsletter, newsroom, etc.). Line 161: `color: "amber"` is hardcoded for BOTH tier 3 and tier 4 branches. core/rules.py PRIORITY_TIERS defines tier 3=blue (line 50), tier 4=green (line 58), so a tier-4 'Reference' record carrying color 'amber' is internally inconsistent vs the canonical taxonomy.
findings.rationale: Both sub-claims verified: substring matching is naive, and amber is hardcoded across tiers that should be blue/green per core/rules.py.

findings.title: Hardcoded Mail version path 'V10' will silently fail on other macOS Mail versions
findings.evidence: Line 17 hardcodes `~/Library/Mail/V10/MailData/SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist` with no V* discovery (grep shows no globbing of the Mail dir). But lines 99-100 `if not PLIST_PATH.exists(): raise SystemExit(...)` exit LOUDLY with a clear message — it does NOT 'silently fail' as the title claims.
findings.rationale: Hardcoded version-specific path is real (portability limitation), but the 'silently fail' characterization is wrong — the script SystemExits with a clear error.

findings.location: record() lines 181-184 and entry build 197-210 (entry['archived']/['moved'] vs entry['disposition'])
findings.title: Violation JSONL line is internally contradictory: disposition=protected_held while archived/moved=true on the same record
findings.evidence: For is_protected and left_inbox, line 182 sets disposition=PROTECTED_HELD while the same entry writes entry['archived']=bool(archived) (203) and entry['moved']=bool(moved) (204), one of which is True in this exact case. So the persisted JSONL line carries disposition=protected_held AND archived/moved=true with no line-level 'violation:true' flag to disambiguate.
findings.rationale: Real per-line self-contradiction; distinct from U337 (which is about summary counts / receipt). Low severity: the data needed to reconstruct the breach (archived/moved booleans + the violations list) is present, so it degrades clarity not correctness of downstream re-derivation.

findings.title: Docstring claims 'CLI > env > config file' precedence but load_config never applies CLI args
findings.evidence: Module docstring line 5 and load_config docstring 153-156 promise 'CLI > env > config file > defaults', but the body (166-179) only does _apply_yaml_config then _apply_env_config. There is no config_path-vs-CLI args parameter for overrides; load_config() takes only config_path and env_prefix. CLI flags are handled separately via argparse in cli.py.
findings.rationale: Confirmed doc/implementation mismatch — load_config never applies a CLI tier despite the documented contract; low severity (documentation accuracy, no runtime defect).

findings.title: frozen=True gives a false immutability guarantee: mutable Set fields can be mutated in place
findings.evidence: core/models.py:25 @dataclass(frozen=True) with docstring line 30 'Immutable'; labels (line 49) and categories (line 53) are Set[str] via default_factory=set. frozen blocks attribute reassignment only — msg.labels.add(...) is not prevented. test_immutability (tests/test_models.py:59-63) only asserts that reassigning msg.id raises, never tests set mutation, so the shallow guarantee is unverified by the suite.
findings.rationale: Accurate: frozen is shallow and the docstring overstates immutability; severity low is appropriate (no confirmed in-place mutation of a cached instance in the current providers, but the hazard is real).

findings.location: line 1163 (def calculate_email_age_hours(email_date: Optional["datetime"]) -> float) with no `from __future__ import annotations` at top (line 8) and `datetime` imported only locally at line 1176
findings.title: Forward-ref annotation Optional["datetime"] is unresolvable — NameError if type hints are ever introspected
findings.evidence: Confirmed: no `from __future__ import annotations` in core/rules.py (grep returned nothing); datetime imported only locally at line 1176; parameter annotated Optional["datetime"] (string forward-ref). grep for get_type_hints across the repo returned zero hits, so it is latent.
findings.rationale: Real but latent: string annotation does not crash at def time (import test passes), but get_type_hints/pydantic/schema introspection would NameError. Nothing in-repo introspects it. Low is correct.

findings.title: com.user.gmail_labeler.plist is orphaned — committed but never installed by deploy.sh (only booted out)
findings.evidence: deploy.sh:9-10 set PLIST_SRC/DEST to com.user.mail_automation.plist only; :48 copies only that; :57 references com.user.gmail_labeler ONLY to bootout. `git ls-files` shows com.user.gmail_labeler.plist is tracked and it exists on disk (970 bytes). No grep hit installs/copies it. The plist itself (line 6: <string>com.user.gmail_labeler</string>) invokes the same run_automation.sh as mail_automation.
findings.rationale: The gmail_labeler plist is committed and on-disk but has no installer path in deploy.sh (only a bootout), making it orphaned dead weight; hygiene/low is accurate.

findings.title: agent-commerce.md says 'Five endpoints' but then lists six ACP routes
findings.evidence: Line 61 reads 'Five endpoints implement the verified Agentic Checkout spec:' but the single contiguous fenced block (lines 64-69) lists SIX routes: POST /acp/checkout_sessions (create), GET /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} (retrieve), POST /acp/checkout_sessions/{id} (update), POST .../complete, POST .../cancel, and GET /acp/feed.json (product feed discovery). There is no separator line distinguishing the five checkout_sessions endpoints from the feed.json discovery endpoint, so the prose count 'Five' does not match the six routes presented beneath it.
findings.rationale: Genuine numeric/prose inconsistency: 'Five' matches only the checkout_sessions endpoints while the block visually presents six routes with no separation; pure documentation accuracy issue with no code blast radius, so low severity stands.

findings.title: Second frontend (docs/pitch) is an orphan: not deployed and not linked from anywhere, so its stale copy will drift unnoticed
findings.evidence: wrangler.toml lines 9-10: [assets] directory = "./web". ci.yml's only deploy job (cloudflare-share-deploy, lines 78+) runs wrangler-action deploy on that worker. web/ contains only index.html (40KB, the served surface). Repo-wide grep for 'docs/pitch' returns ONLY the bug-sweep findings.json self-references — zero references from any workflow, doc, or served HTML. So docs/pitch/index.html is a git-tracked auto-generated artifact with no consumer.
findings.rationale: Orphan/dead-surface claim verified end to end; it is the load-bearing mitigant that lowers U198/U199 severity. Correctly rated low.

findings.title: Legacy labeler start/finish banners stamp naive local time while audit.py stamps UTC — same local-vs-UTC inconsistency as state.py
findings.evidence: Confirmed: lines 390 and 472 use naive datetime.now().strftime(...); core/audit.py:198 uses datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(). BUT gmail_labeler_legacy.py does NOT import or call audit.py at all - these are console print banners, not entries written to the same receipt log. The 'mixed in one run log, cross-correlation wrong by UTC offset' premise does not hold because the two timestamps never land in the same artifact.
findings.rationale: The naive-vs-UTC observation is factually true but the stated impact (mixing in a run log with audit receipts) is incorrect for this file - these are ephemeral console banners. Downgraded low->info; cosmetic.

findings.title: IMAP connection leaked on exception; logout only on success path
findings.evidence: main() opens imap = connect_imap(...) at line 131 and imap.logout() is only at line 158, with no try/finally; early returns at lines 137 and 142 and any exception in select/search/fetch/apply skip logout. Cross-file claim verified: icloud_triage.py uses try (line 102) / finally (line 167) with imap.logout() (line 169).
findings.rationale: Real resource leak on exception/early-return; low severity (per-run socket leak, only matters for repeated/scheduled failures).

findings.title: IMAP connection not closed on early return / exception (resource leak) and logout-only cleanup
findings.evidence: Identical defect to U468: connect at line 131, logout only at line 158, no try/finally, early returns at 137/142 leak the socket. Same icloud_triage.py contrast.
findings.rationale: Same connection-leak root cause as U468.

findings.title: Runtime state file with per-account mailbox statistics is committed to the repo
findings.evidence: Same file and same root cause as U078: labeler_state.json is git-tracked (git ls-files), contains total_processed=36470 plus the per-category breakdown, and is not covered by .gitignore (git check-ignore exit=1; .gitignore ignores only *.log, credentials.json, data/, audit/*.jsonl, etc.). Note the 'secrets' category is a misclassification — the data is mailbox metadata, not a credential.
findings.rationale: Identical defect to U078 on the same file; consolidated under U078 with adjusted severity medium (its 'secrets'/'low' framing both mis-rate the actual publicly-exposed-metadata leak).

findings.location: module docstring line 3 vs query line 41
findings.title: Docstring says '>30 days' but the query uses older_than:7d (and inline comment says 7 days)
findings.evidence: Same '>30 days' docstring vs older_than:7d query mismatch (lines 3, 40, 41) as U210.
findings.rationale: Identical doc/behavior threshold mismatch finding; merged into U210.

findings.title: Gmail label_counts recorded before batchModify runs — over-counts on batch failure or protected-suppressed actions
findings.evidence: Lines 445-446: `for label in action.add_labels: result.add_label_stat(label)` runs unconditionally in the build loop, before any batchModify. base.py:344 calls add_label_stat only after a successful apply_label. So on a failed/poison-pill chunk (469-472) or for protected-suppressed actions, label_counts overstates what was applied while the base provider does not.
findings.rationale: Confirmed build-time over-count divergent from the base provider; report-only impact, low severity.

findings.title: O(labels) reverse lookup per label id per message — quadratic label resolution on large mailboxes
findings.evidence: Lines 198-202 and 280-284: nested loop `for lid in label_ids: for name,id_ in self._label_cache.items(): if id_==lid` is O(K*L) per message, repeated for every fetched message. A one-time reverse {id:name} dict would make it O(K).
findings.rationale: Confirmed quadratic reverse-lookup; pure perf smell on the common path, low severity.

findings.title: No requires-python / [project] metadata — nothing authoritatively enforces the Python floor at install time
findings.evidence: Same underlying fact as U226: pyproject.toml has no [project] table and no requires-python (confirmed: file is 3 lines, only pytest config). The python-floor split is documented in requirements-mcp.txt:2 (mcp SDK needs >=3.10) and README.md:2,241 (3.10+), never enforced via requires-python.
findings.rationale: Strict subset of U226 — the requires-python/version-floor point is already explicitly named inside U226's description; identical root cause (missing [project] metadata).

findings.title: total_archived double-counts overlapping labels and miscounts because Gmail messages carry multiple labels
findings.evidence: Line 12 includes UNREAD in relevant_labels: `... or l['id'] in ['INBOX', 'TRASH', 'UNREAD']`. Line 41 only excludes by NAME 'INBOX'/'TRASH', so the UNREAD system label (name 'UNREAD') passes and its messagesTotal is added at line 42. Gmail messages carry multiple labels, so summing messagesTotal across user labels + INBOX + UNREAD genuinely over-counts; 'TOTAL ORGANIZED ARCHIVE' (line 45) is not a real message count.
findings.rationale: Both claims (multi-label double-counting and UNREAD inclusion in the total) are exactly what the current code does; low severity is correct for a reporting-only script.

findings.title: httpx is a test-only dep shipped into the production image
findings.evidence: grep for 'import httpx|from httpx|httpx.' across api/, acp/, mcp_server/ and the whole tree returns ZERO hits in production .py files. Outbound HTTP in production uses `requests` (providers/outlook.py). httpx is consumed only by `fastapi.testclient.TestClient` (tests/test_api.py:3, test_billing.py:8, test_acp.py:5, test_receipts.py:3, test_web.py:3). Dockerfile L8-9 `COPY requirements.txt requirements-api.txt requirements-mcp.txt ./` + `RUN pip install ... -r requirements-api.txt` bakes httpx into the prod image. No requirements-dev.txt exists (only requirements.txt, requirements-api.txt, requirements-mcp.txt).
findings.rationale: Test-only dependency (httpx via TestClient) is installed wholesale into the production image; real hygiene smell, severity low is accurate.

findings.title: test_immutability asserts the wrong (overstated) guarantee and never exercises set-content mutation
findings.evidence: core/models.py declares labels: Set[str] = field(default_factory=set) (line 49) and categories (line 53) on a @dataclass(frozen=True). frozen=True blocks attribute rebinding (msg.id = ...) but not in-place mutation, so msg.labels.add('x') succeeds silently. test_immutability (test_models.py:57-63) only reassigns msg.id and asserts AttributeError; it never tries an in-place set mutation, leaving the real mutability hole uncovered.
findings.rationale: Accurate test-coverage gap: the test validates only attribute-rebind immutability while the mutable Set fields remain mutable in place and untested.

findings.title: Test coverage gap: 'corrupt' test only exercises invalid JSON, never valid-JSON-wrong-shape
findings.evidence: test_state.py:23-29 writes 'NOT VALID JSON {{{' which only triggers the json.JSONDecodeError branch at core/state.py:52. core/state.py _load() (lines 46-56) performs NO schema/type validation: valid JSON that is a list/str/null is returned verbatim and stored as self.state, after which self.state.get(...) (e.g. line 99/103) or defaultdict(int, self.state.get('history', {})) in get_history() (line 112) would crash on a wrong-typed value. No test in the file exercises valid-JSON-wrong-shape; the only other negative test is test_missing_file_returns_defaults (lines 31-34).
findings.rationale: Accurately describes both the test and the unvalidated load path in core/state.py; a genuine test-quality coverage gap, severity 'low' is correct.

findings.title: Store tests run against ':memory:' where WAL is silently ignored — the concurrency/durability properties the store advertises are never exercised by tests
findings.evidence: _store() at lines 6-8 always constructs Store(':memory:'). store.py:131-132 comment confirms WAL is 'silently ignored' on :memory:, and store.py:134 sets PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL which therefore no-ops in tests. No test in tests/ spawns threads against the store (grep of test_store.py shows purely sequential single-connection calls). test_idempotency_stale_processing_is_reclaimed (91-98) is single-threaded and only checks the post-timeout reclaim, not a live-holder race or idempotency_complete clobber race.
findings.rationale: Accurate: all store tests run single-threaded on :memory: where WAL/busy_timeout/file-locking are inert, so the store's headline concurrency/durability guarantees are unverified — a genuine but low-severity test-quality gap.

findings.title: Offline categorization uses naive substring match ('deal'/'news') that over-matches
findings.evidence: Lines 755-758: `value.includes("deal") || value.includes("news")` → Marketing/tier-3. Bare substring match; news@idealcorp.com contains 'deal' (within 'i-deal-corp') and 'news', mislabeling as Marketing. worker.mjs:159 has the identical bare-substring defect, so it reproduces on the live demo too, not only the offline fallback.
findings.rationale: Genuine over-match in the client JS (and mirrored in the worker); low impact — it only sets a display label/tier on a no-credentials demo, never moves mail.

findings.title: Green design tokens were referenced ~13 times but never defined until this dirty diff (pre-existing broken branding)
findings.evidence: git show HEAD:web/index.html: --green/--green-2/--green-soft are NOT defined in :root (only --accent* at lines 18-21) yet referenced at HEAD lines 79,245,266,271,277,278,285,287,297, etc.; `grep -- '--green:'` on HEAD returns nothing (exit 1). The dirty diff adds the three definitions (diff lines 20-22). So the committed HEAD shipped with ~13 undefined var(--green*) references resolving to invalid/inherited values.
findings.rationale: Verified against HEAD: the green tokens were undefined in the committed page and are fixed only by the uncommitted diff — a real (now-being-fixed) branding regression.

findings.location: line 52 (`mypy . --ignore-missing-imports`) with no mypy config in repo
findings.title: mypy invoked with no config and no exclude — scans everything from repo root, advisory result is near-meaningless
findings.evidence: ci.yml:52 runs `mypy . --ignore-missing-imports`. Verified no mypy.ini and no setup.cfg (ls returned nothing); pyproject.toml contains only [tool.pytest.ini_options], no [tool.mypy]. So mypy scans the whole tree from root with missing-import errors suppressed. (Local .venv is python3.14 — in CI runner deps install to site-packages so a local venv isn't scanned, matching the finding's own caveat.)
findings.rationale: Accurate on the config facts. Impact is limited because the mypy step is already advisory (U135/U873) — a near-meaningless advisory output is a real but minor smell.

findings.location: line 31 (`pip install pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy`) and line 30 (`pip install -r requirements*.txt`)
findings.title: Tooling (pytest/ruff/mypy) installed unpinned alongside unpinned app deps — non-reproducible toolchain
findings.evidence: ci.yml:31 installs `pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy` with zero version pins; ci.yml:30 installs three requirements files that are overwhelmingly `>=` (requirements.txt google-api>=2.0.0 etc; requirements-api.txt fastapi>=0.110, uvicorn>=0.29, httpx>=0.27, pydantic>=2.0, stripe>=15.2,<16; requirements-mcp.txt mcp>=1.2,<2). No lockfile present.
findings.rationale: Factually correct — toolchain is non-reproducible. Low severity: standard hardening gap, and ruff/mypy drift only affects advisory output (they can't fail the build), so blast radius is mostly pytest/coverage variance.

findings.title: codecov-action@v4 used without CODECOV_TOKEN — coverage upload can fail/skip silently on this private-org repo
findings.evidence: ci.yml:71 uses codecov/codecov-action@v4 with no `token:` input and no CODECOV_TOKEN referenced anywhere; ci.yml:74 `fail_ci_if_error: false` so an upload failure is swallowed. README.md:8 shows a static 'coverage-pending' badge. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Config facts confirmed, but the 'will fail/skip' core claim is needs_runtime — tokenless v4 upload can succeed for public repos and the actual pass/fail depends on Codecov's plan/ratelimit at runtime. Real observability gap, severity low (no functional impact). Marking partial (overstated certainty).

findings.title: Post-deploy smoke test hits a live domain immediately after deploy with no retry/propagation wait — flaky failures and a non-blocking deploy
findings.evidence: ci.yml:108-119: smoke step runs after wrangler deploy (100-106), under `set -euo pipefail` with `curl -f`, no retry/backoff. Job only runs on push to main (gated by needs:test + if at line 82). A transient edge failure turns a successful prod deploy into red CI with no rollback.
findings.rationale: Correct and is the same root-cause defect as U240 (no retry/propagation wait) and overlaps U437 (no rollback). Marking duplicate of U240 (same-file: post-deploy smoke with no retry → flaky).

findings.title: .gitignore omits already-tracked PII artifacts (labeler_state.json, mail_export.tsv) and any *_state.json
findings.evidence: Same root-cause gap as U008 (missing *_state.json and mail_export.tsv patterns), confirmed by the same git check-ignore results. Additional claims verified: labeler_state.json and mail_export.tsv ARE already tracked (git ls-files); there is NO .dockerignore but a Dockerfile DOES exist in the repo (git ls-files | grep Dockerfile -> 'Dockerfile'). No tracked *token_cache* file exists (~/.outlook_token_cache.json lives in $HOME per CLAUDE.md, outside the repo), so the *token_cache*.json suggestion is precautionary, not a current leak.
findings.rationale: Same .gitignore coverage gap as U008 for the load-bearing claim; correctly notes the two artifacts are already tracked and that a Dockerfile exists with no .dockerignore counterpart. Kept at low: the extra recommendations (token_cache pattern, .dockerignore) are hardening, not active defects.

findings.location: lines 9-10 (pip install -r ...) — no constraint/hash file, no pip version pin
findings.title: Docker build installs unhashed, unpinned dependencies with `pip install` and no `--require-hashes`, allowing supply-chain drift between identical source builds
findings.evidence: Lines 9-10: `pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt` with no constraints file and no --require-hashes. All three requirements files use `>=` floors (google-api-python-client>=2.0.0, fastapi>=0.110, uvicorn[standard]>=0.29, pydantic>=2.0, mcp>=1.2,<2; stripe>=15.2,<16). render.yaml has autoDeploy:true + runtime:docker, so a rebuild of the same commit resolves newer transitive versions. Only stripe and mcp have ceilings; fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/google-*/urllib3 float freely.
findings.rationale: Factually accurate supply-chain non-reproducibility; low severity is appropriate (best-practice gap, not an active exploit).

findings.title: README documents the non-standard ~/System/Logs path, propagating the convention error rather than ~/Library/Logs
findings.evidence: README.md:261 'Create the log directory at ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/', :544 'logs written to ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/', :556 'tail -f ~/System/Logs/mail_automation/launchd.stdout.log'. plist:15,17 use /Users/4jp/System/Logs/mail_automation/. deploy.sh:11 LOG_DIR="$HOME/System/Logs/mail_automation". So README accurately documents the code's actual (non-standard) path.
findings.rationale: Real convention nit — ~/System/Logs is non-standard vs ~/Library/Logs — but README is faithfully documenting the code, not introducing the error; the 'grows unbounded' harm is minor/speculative, so info not low.

findings.title: README 'First Run' Outlook/IMAP examples inherit a Gmail-only default query
findings.evidence: cli.py:1014 default="has:nouserlabels" with help (:1015) 'Query to filter messages (default: has:nouserlabels for Gmail)'. README First Run :286 'python3 cli.py label --provider outlook' and :289 '--provider imap --host imap.mail.me.com' show no --query override, so they inherit the Gmail-specific search syntax against Outlook Graph / IMAP.
findings.rationale: Accurate: README presents non-Gmail first-run commands that silently inherit a Gmail-only default query the help text itself scopes to Gmail; low-severity doc accuracy issue.

findings.title: Product image_url points to the dashboard HTML page (/app/), not an image
findings.evidence: feed.py:47 `"image_url": f"{base}/app/"` is identical to `"url": f"{base}/app/"` (line 45); /app/ is the SPA dashboard HTML route, not an image asset. Reflected in committed product_feed.json lines 12/32 ("image_url": "https://mail.example.com/app/").
findings.rationale: Confirmed: image_url resolves to an HTML page, which validating feed consumers may reject; minor correctness/cosmetic issue, downgraded from medium.
