findings.title: Payment client is cached at module level keyed on first-call env; a STRIPE_SECRET_KEY set after first call (or rotated) is never picked up
findings.evidence: payment.py:106-110 memoizes _CLIENT on first call from os.environ at that instant. If the process resolves before STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is set, NullPaymentClient is cached and every /complete returns 402 'payment is not configured' until set_payment_client(None) or restart. Contrast billing.py:68 (_client reads key per call). set_payment_client exists (113-117) but is test-only.
findings.rationale: Confirmed caching/late-key divergence. Severity low: deploy-ordering edge case, fail-closed direction (no free credits), recoverable by restart.

findings.title: Payment client is cached process-wide on first resolution; a STRIPE_SECRET_KEY set after first call (or rotated) is never picked up
findings.evidence: Verbatim same defect and location as U491: _CLIENT cached on first call (107-109), late/rotated key ignored, diverges from billing.py:68 per-call read. Same fix (resolve per call / invalidate on key change).
findings.rationale: Same root cause and location as U491; merged.

findings.location: lines 100-110 (_CLIENT module global, get_payment_client lazy init)
findings.title: Module-global payment client lazy-init has a read-check-set race under the FastAPI threadpool (no lock)
findings.evidence: payment.py:107-109 is an unsynchronized `if _CLIENT is None: ... _CLIENT = ...` with no threading import at all (the file imports only logging, os, dataclass, typing). store.py:441-460 guards the identical pattern with _STORE_LOCK = threading.Lock() + double-check. FastAPI dispatches sync endpoints on a threadpool, so two concurrent first-requests can both see None and each construct a client; second assignment wins, first is orphaned.
findings.rationale: Confirmed distinct concurrency defect (not the env-caching angle of U491). Severity low: tiny race window, both candidate clients are functionally equivalent, the orphan causes no money error — purely a missing-lock inconsistency vs the sibling get_store().

findings.title: Static product_feed.json bakes the placeholder host mail.example.com into committed buyer-facing product/image/checkout/seller URLs
findings.evidence: product_feed.json hardcodes https://mail.example.com in checkout_url (line 4), url (10/30), image_url (12/32), seller_url (20/40). acp/feed.py:72-73 write_static_feed defaults base_url="https://mail.example.com"; the live route feed() (feed.py:67-69) resolves host from request.base_url, but the COMMITTED artifact carries the placeholder. A static host (Cloudflare demo) serving this file points an agent at a non-existent host for the checkout entrypoint.
findings.rationale: Placeholder host is genuinely baked into the committed buyer-facing artifact including checkout_url; low severity is correct since it is a regenerable artifact and the live FastAPI route self-resolves.

findings.title: Feed image_url points to an HTML app page (/app/), not an image asset
findings.evidence: acp/feed.py:47 `"image_url": f"{base}/app/"` -> product_feed.json:12,32 image_url = https://mail.example.com/app/. api/app.py:162 `app.mount("/app", StaticFiles(directory=str(_WEB_DIR), html=True), name="web")` serves /app/ as an HTML dashboard (index.html via html=True), not an image asset. A feed consumer fetching image_url for a thumbnail gets text/html.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: image_url resolves to an HTML page, not an image; minor feed-quality/spec smell, low severity is accurate.

findings.title: Shipped ACP product feed and discovery artifacts hardcode mail.example.com placeholder URLs
findings.evidence: Within product_feed.json this restates U494's placeholder-host defect (checkout_url/url/image_url/seller_url = https://mail.example.com) plus U495's image_url-is-HTML smell. The cross-artifact half (.well-known/agent.json, server.json, llms.txt) is true (grep confirms all carry mail.example.com; scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:28 BASE defaults to https://mail.example.com) but lives in other files outside this file's scope. For acp/product_feed.json the root cause is the same as U494. Severity claimed medium overstates the low blast radius (regenerable artifact, live routes self-resolve via well_known.py/feed.py request.base_url).
findings.rationale: Same product_feed.json placeholder-host root cause as U494 (and folds in U495); severity downgraded from medium to low to match the regenerable-artifact blast radius.

findings.title: On payment failure the failed response is stored via idempotency_complete, so a legitimate retry with the SAME Idempotency-Key replays the FAILURE instead of re-attempting payment
findings.evidence: On result.ok False the branch builds a 402 response, _persist's it, then _complete_idempotency(ctx, resp) (router.py:279) which calls idempotency_complete (store.py:355-362) marking the key status='done' with the failure body. A retry reusing the SAME Idempotency-Key hits idempotency_begin replay (store.py:348-353) and gets the stored 402 back (router.py:241-242), never re-attempting payment. Caller must use a fresh key.
findings.rationale: Storing a transient failure as a terminal idempotent result is confirmed; unusual but recoverable with a new key, so low.

findings.title: GET /checkout_sessions/{id} returns the stored session to ANY valid-format bearer with no ownership check
findings.evidence: get_session (router.py:194-197) runs _gate(require_idempotency=False) — which only checks API-Version + a non-empty Bearer prefix (router.py:72-83) — then returns row['data']['response'] for the id with no ownership/api_key binding check. acp_sessions has an account_id column (store.py:86) but it is never consulted on retrieval. Response includes buyer name/email and order/permalink. Confidentiality rests on the 128-bit session id (router.py:134 token_hex(16)).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: any valid-format bearer can read any session by id; exposure requires a leaked id (unguessable otherwise), so low.

findings.title: STATUS_EXPIRED is defined but never assigned; sessions never expire and remain completable indefinitely
findings.evidence: models.py:22 STATUS_EXPIRED='expired' is referenced only in update_session's terminal-state guard (router.py:211-212). grep across api/ and acp/ shows no assignment of STATUS_EXPIRED to any session status, no TTL/created_at expiry sweep, and no created_at age check in complete_session. acp_sessions has created_at (store.py:88) but nothing reads it for expiry. A READY session is completable indefinitely.
findings.rationale: Confirmed dead/unenforced lifecycle state; sessions never expire. Informational/correctness, low.

findings.location: complete_session lines 304-310 (receipt summary 'currency': CURRENCY ('usd')) vs acp/feed.py:50 / product_feed.json ('USD') and api/billing.py:102 ('usd')
findings.title: Currency casing is inconsistent across the money surfaces: charge+receipt+billing use 'usd', the product feed uses 'USD' — a consumer keying on exact case sees two currencies
findings.evidence: router.py:39 CURRENCY='usd' used in the charge (263) and signed receipt summary (306,310). billing.py:101 'currency': 'usd'. feed.py:49 'currency': 'USD'; acp/product_feed.json lines 14,34 'currency': 'USD'. A consumer matching currency codes by exact case sees the advertised feed currency ('USD') differ from the receipt/charge currency ('usd').
findings.rationale: Confirmed case inconsistency across money surfaces; ISO codes are case-insensitive by convention so impact is limited to strict exact-string matchers — low.

findings.title: Receipt account_id is the auto-provisioned bearer account, not the buyer email on the session — receipt audit trail attributes the purchase to an unidentifiable orphan
findings.evidence: save_receipt is called with account_id=account['id'] (router.py:316), where account is the bearer-resolved/auto-created orphan (287-289, plan='free', no email/customer). The session carries a buyer dict (body.buyer) but it is not used for the receipt's account_id. list_receipts(account_id) (store.py:300-310) for the buyer's real billing account will never surface this receipt. Compounds U258 (disjoint identity spaces).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the ledger row is filed under the orphan bearer account, not the buyer/billing identity. Audit-correctness, low. Closely related to U258 but a distinct surface (receipt ledger vs credit balance), so kept separate.

findings.location: _links lines 125-130 / _shape line 144 — terms_of_use & privacy_policy URLs point at {base}/terms and {base}/privacy which are not routed
findings.title: ACP session links advertise /terms and /privacy endpoints that the app does not serve (404)
findings.evidence: _links (router.py:127-130) emits {base}/terms and {base}/privacy on every session. grep for /terms and /privacy across api/, acp/, web/ returns only these two router.py lines — no route serves them. app.py registers routers (billing/receipts/acp/feed/well_known, lines 134-138), a '/' handler (158), and mounts StaticFiles only at /app (162). well_known.py serves /.well-known/agent.json and /llms.txt only. So both legal links 404.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: advertised terms/privacy links resolve to no route. Spec-faithfulness/compliance polish, low.

findings.title: Batch callback registered twice (on the batch AND per-request) causing each callback to fire twice — double-counting
findings.evidence: Line 101: batch = service.new_batch_http_request(callback=callback); line 103: batch.add(..., callback=callback). In google-api-python-client the per-request callback supersedes the batch-level one, so each callback fires exactly once. No double-counting occurs at runtime.
findings.rationale: Finding itself concedes it fires once; this is redundant/misleading code, not a defect that double-counts. Downgraded to info.

findings.title: Division by zero / statistics.mean on empty list crashes report if all batch fetches fail
findings.evidence: Callback returns early on exception (lines 82-83), so msg_data/value_scores stay empty if all GETs fail. Line 146 statistics.mean(value_scores) raises StatisticsError on empty; lines 151-152 divide by len(msg_data) -> ZeroDivisionError. Pre-fetch guard at line 178 only covers the list() result, not fetch failures.
findings.rationale: Real crash path on total batch-fetch failure with no post-fetch empty guard; severity low because it requires every GET in every chunk to error.

findings.location: _run lines 127-129 (run_id minted + receipts.persist) — preview path via triage_preview line 88
findings.title: triage_preview persists a durable receipt and mints a run_id for a no-op preview, polluting the audit ledger (REST preview path)
findings.evidence: Same defect as U144: triage_preview (app.py:86-88) calls _run(req, dry_run=True) which mints run_id and persists a receipt (lines 127-129). MCP triage_preview (server.py:105) is analogous. The write-amplification/availability angle is the same root cause (unbounded unauthenticated preview persistence) already captured by U144.
findings.rationale: Restates U144's dry-run-receipt-persistence with an availability framing; same root cause, same _run lines.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status='active' unconditionally, but does not re-write current_period_end, so a past_due->active flip leaves a stale/expired period_end that nothing re-validates
findings.evidence: billing.py:269 calls set_subscription(account_id, status='active') with no current_period_end; store.py:206-215 writes only non-None fields, so a prior (possibly expired) current_period_end is retained while status flips active.
findings.rationale: Accurate, but the finding itself notes entitlements_for never reads current_period_end (confirmed: plans.py:184-193 ignores it), so today this is latent/inert -> low.

findings.title: subscription.created/updated with status=None writes nothing for status but still forces current_period_end and may demote plan, producing a row whose plan changed without a corresponding status change
findings.evidence: billing.py:248 status=obj.get('status') can be None; 251 derives plan_id from price (can be None via plan_id_for_price). store.py:213 skips None columns, so plan is rewritten while status is left untouched -> a decoupled (plan,status) pair from one event.
findings.rationale: Real internal-consistency gap; benign under the current status-gated entitlement resolver. Overlaps U717 (same None-status root cause) but adds the plan-rewritten-while-status-skipped angle. Low.

findings.title: Per-run price is inconsistent across the catalog: metered is $0.01/run but pack_1000 sells runs at $0.009/run, and neither price is ever charged
findings.evidence: Identical arithmetic claim to U290/U905: metered $0.01, pack_100 $0.01, pack_1000 $0.009, and metered never metered/charged. Same source lines.
findings.rationale: Same per-run price-ladder observation as U290; volume discount is intentional and the billing gap is U148.

findings.title: run_credits coerced with int(account.get('run_credits',0)) can raise ValueError/TypeError on a non-numeric stored value, 500-ing the entitlement check
findings.evidence: int(account.get('run_credits', 0)) at plans.py:192 would raise on a non-numeric stored value. But schema enforces run_credits INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 (store.py:51) and add_credits/consume_credit write int() values (store.py:229,242), so a non-int is not reachable via any code path. Additionally entitlements_for is never called (U029), so the exception cannot occur at runtime today.
findings.rationale: Plausible fail-mode but doubly gated: schema prevents non-int storage AND the function is dead. Genuine hardening note, very narrow — low.

findings.title: Receipt ledger write failure swallowed (acceptable) but the API never surfaces that the run has no verifiable receipt
findings.evidence: Same defect as U294: persist() swallows save_receipt exception (106), app.py ignores the outcome and returns run_id, so a failed ledger write yields a 404 on later GET with no client signal.
findings.rationale: Identical silent-failure root cause as U294 (same lines, same call site).

findings.title: No minimum-length/strength validation on RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY — a weak/short key is accepted silently
findings.evidence: _signing_key() (47-49): env = os.environ.get('RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY'); if env: return env.encode('utf-8') — used directly as HMAC key with no length/entropy check, while the ephemeral fallback uses 32 bytes (51). RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY='x' is accepted silently.
findings.rationale: True: no minimum-strength guard on the operator-supplied signing key. Low severity — it is a config-hardening gap requiring operator misconfiguration; HMAC with a short key is weak but the operator chose it.

findings.title: ACP order receipts are hand-built instead of reusing signed_body(), so the two receipt schemas can silently drift
findings.evidence: Same duplication as U295: ACP /complete re-implements the signed-body shape inline (router 297-311) instead of calling receipts.signed_body(); shapes match today, drift would break ACP-only verification with no shared test.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U295 (forked signed-body builder). Maintainability/latent only.

findings.title: prov.disconnect() exception is silently swallowed with bare except: pass, hiding resource-cleanup failures
findings.evidence: service.py:120-124: `finally: try: prov.disconnect() except Exception: pass`. No logging in the except, unlike the connect() failure at lines 101-102 which logs with exc_info=True.
findings.rationale: Accurate: disconnect failures are silently swallowed with no observability on a resource-cleanup path. Low severity stands.

findings.title: Broad 'except Exception' around categorize_with_tier silently degrades categorization to None with no logging
findings.evidence: service.py:63-66: `try: categorization = _jsonable(categorize_with_tier(...)) except Exception: categorization = None` with comment 'never block the protect-check' — no logger call in the handler.
findings.rationale: Accurate: every categorize_with_tier exception is swallowed to None with zero logging, so a recurring rules-engine bug would be invisible while the endpoint returns 200/categorization:null. Low severity stands.

findings.title: Provider disconnect failure is swallowed with bare 'except: pass', and assert_no_violations runs AFTER disconnect so a disconnect that masks state is invisible
findings.evidence: The disconnect-swallow portion duplicates U513 (lines 120-124). The escape-path claim is real: run_labeler is wrapped only in try/finally (108-124), connect is the only thing in try/except (98-105), and _run (app.py:108-122) catches ONLY ProviderUnavailable and AuditInvariantError — so a generic run_labeler exception propagates uncaught. HOWEVER the 'possibly sensitive message' sub-claim is wrong: FastAPI is created without debug=True (app.py:56-67), so Starlette's ServerErrorMiddleware returns a fixed 'Internal Server Error' body, not the exception text.
findings.rationale: Real-but-overstated and overlaps U513: the uncaught-generic-500 path is genuine, but the sensitive-message-leak claim is false (no debug, FastAPI masks the message), and the disconnect-swallow root cause is already U513.

findings.title: Broad 'except Exception' silently nulls categorization, hiding rules-engine regressions on the public trust surface
findings.evidence: Same lines (service.py:63-66) and same defect as U514: broad `except Exception: categorization = None` with no logging. The added re.error/VIP-regex angle (a bad config-loaded VIP pattern raising re.error inside categorize_with_tier) is just one instance of the same swallowed-exception path; it does not change the root cause or location.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause defect at the same location as U514; merge.

findings.location: current_period_end column (49); set_subscription (191-221); entitlements_for in plans.py (184-186)
findings.title: Subscription expiry (current_period_end) is stored but never checked — an expired-but-active row keeps paid limits
findings.evidence: api/plans.py:184-186: entitlements_for resolves active purely from `status in ('active','trialing')`; current_period_end (stored at store.py:49, written via set_subscription 211 and billing.py:261) is never read. grep confirms entitlements_for has zero callers repo-wide.
findings.rationale: Code-true gap: expiry stored but unchecked. Latent today because entitlements_for is uncalled (enforcement not wired), so no current over-grant. Low as stated.

findings.title: consume_credit always debits exactly the requested n with no cap on n and no zero/negative guard at the caller boundary
findings.evidence: Lines 240-244: `UPDATE ... SET run_credits = run_credits - ? WHERE id=? AND run_credits >= ?` with the same int(n) bound to both the subtraction and the guard. A negative n passes `run_credits >= n` (always true) and `- (negative)` increases the balance, returning rowcount>0 (True). No n>0 assertion in consume_credit or add_credits.
findings.rationale: Arithmetic is exactly as described. consume_credit has no production caller (only tests), so unreachable from request input today — low severity, but a genuine latent money-path hazard once enforcement is wired.

findings.title: WAL pragma failure is silently swallowed — store can silently run in rollback-journal mode on restricted/networked filesystems
findings.evidence: Lines 133-136: `try: self._conn.execute('PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;') except sqlite3.Error: pass`. The exception is swallowed and the returned mode row is never inspected; no logging on downgrade.
findings.rationale: Code-true silent swallow of the WAL-set exception. Single-writer correctness does not depend on WAL here (RLock serializes), so the consequence is reduced observability — low.

findings.title: Module-singleton Store connection is never closed in production and not coupled to FastAPI lifespan — connection/handle leak across reloads
findings.evidence: get_store (445-452) builds a process-wide Store with an open connection (line 127); api/app.py lifespan (48-53) only enters the MCP session manager, never calls store.close(). set_store(None) in tests/conftest.py:26,33 replaces the singleton without closing the prior connection. grep finds Store.close() called nowhere in production.
findings.rationale: Verified: no close() in app lifecycle and conftest abandons connections. FD/WAL-sidecar leak under reloads and in the test suite. Low (no data risk).

findings.title: WAL is never checkpointed and the connection is never closed in production, so the -wal/-shm sidecar files grow unbounded
findings.evidence: Same facts as U520: WAL enabled (line 134), no wal_autocheckpoint set, single immortal connection never closed (get_store 445-452, no lifespan shutdown). The -wal/-shm-growth consequence is a corollary of the same never-closed-connection defect.
findings.rationale: Same root cause and location as U520 (connection never closed); the WAL-sidecar-growth angle is a consequence, not an independent defect. Duplicate.

findings.title: int() coercions on caller-supplied counts can raise ValueError/TypeError mid-transaction, becoming an unhandled 500 and (with the no-rollback bug) poisoning the connection
findings.evidence: int(n) at 229/243, int(limit) at 306, int(runs) at 378 and 386 are all inside the locked section. In fulfill_once, int(runs) at 378 is evaluated while building args for the FIRST execute (the INSERT) and at 386 for the UPDATE — both before their respective execute() calls.
findings.rationale: TypeError/ValueError-on-int() and the no-rollback observation are real. But the specific claim that int(runs) at 386 'runs AFTER the INSERT' leaving a dangling open txn is overstated: the 386 cast feeds the second execute; if it raised, the prior INSERT (378) is uncommitted and rolled back on next op — no money applied. acp/router.py:303 passes total_runs from build_line_items (always int), so unreachable today. Real-but-overstated, low.

findings.title: Reads run inside whatever transaction the shared connection currently holds, so a read can observe an uncommitted in-flight writer's data or a stale snapshot
findings.evidence: All reads use self._conn (the same connection writes use), serialized by self._lock (426,435). No write method calls a _fetch_* helper between its INSERT and commit, so the 'read partially-applied state' window is not exercised by any current code path.
findings.rationale: Technically true that reads share the writer's connection/transaction, but the RLock fully serializes calls and no method interleaves a read between write statements, so there is no observable defect today. The docstring's 'WAL keeps reads concurrent' is aspirational, not load-bearing. Downgrade to info — architectural note, not a bug.

findings.location: __init__ 133-136 (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL try/except) + return value never checked
findings.title: journal_mode=WAL return value is never verified, so on a filesystem that silently downgrades to another mode (NFS, some FUSE) the store runs without WAL while the docstring assumes WAL
findings.evidence: Lines 133-136 execute PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL and never read the returned mode row; this is the same code site U519 flags. U524 emphasizes the no-exception silent-downgrade case vs U519's swallowed-exception case, but both describe the unverified WAL result at lines 133-136.
findings.rationale: Same location and same underlying defect (WAL outcome never verified/logged). Complementary framing, but one fix addresses both. Duplicate of U519.

findings.title: save_receipt uses INSERT OR REPLACE on run_id, so a colliding run_id silently overwrites a prior signed receipt
findings.evidence: Line 282: `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO receipts (run_id ...)` with run_id as PRIMARY KEY (62-63). A second write under the same run_id silently replaces the prior signed receipt_line/signature/summary with no error.
findings.rationale: Code-true: REPLACE on an append-only audit ledger silently overwrites. 96-bit random ids with disjoint prefixes (run_/order_) make collision practically unreachable, and triage receipt writes are best-effort try/except (receipts.persist), so realistic blast radius is low.

findings.title: Agent manifest advertises /mcp and ACP checkout unconditionally even when the MCP app failed to mount
findings.evidence: build_agent_manifest unconditionally sets protocols.mcp.url = f'{base}/mcp' (line 29) and llms.txt always advertises {base}/mcp (line 65). In api/app.py the /mcp mount is guarded: _MCP_AVAILABLE is set False when the mcp SDK import fails (app.py:39-45, comment notes the SDK needs Python>=3.10), and the mount only happens 'if _MCP_AVAILABLE' (app.py:141-143). On a deployment without the mcp dep / on Python 3.9, /mcp is absent (404) yet the manifest still advertises it.
findings.rationale: Genuine discovery/runtime mismatch on optional-dependency deployments; low severity is appropriate since on a fully-provisioned deploy /mcp is present and the manifest is accurate.

findings.title: Age cutoff uses local 'current date' with no timezone normalization; boundary off-by-one vs the Python age engine
findings.evidence: Line 2 computes cutoffDate from `(current date)` (Mail.app local time) and line 17 compares `date received of msg < cutoffDate`. The Python age engine core/rules.py calculate_email_age_hours (verified L1176-1184) uses datetime.now(timezone.utc) and assumes UTC for naive dates; core/audit.py L198 also stamps UTC. The two 90-day boundaries can differ by the local UTC offset.
findings.rationale: Local-vs-UTC boundary disagreement is real but bounded by the UTC offset and only matters at the 90-day edge; low severity stands.

findings.title: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES references labels that do not exist in the core taxonomy (Work/Dev/*, Work/RealEstate)
findings.evidence: ARCHIVE_CATEGORIES lines 34-37 list 'Work/Dev/Infrastructure', 'Work/Dev/GitHub', 'Work/Dev/Code-Review', 'Work/RealEstate'. grep of core/rules.py shows LABEL_RULES defines these as 'Dev/Infrastructure' (line 89), 'Dev/GitHub' (line 71), 'Dev/Code-Review' (line 83) with NO 'Work/' prefix, and `grep -n 'RealEstate|Work/'` returns nothing — no 'Work/RealEstate' rule exists. If the account taxonomy matches the core rules engine, the query `label:Work/Dev/...` returns 0 messages and those classes are never archived (silent no-op).
findings.rationale: Label-name mismatch between this script and the core taxonomy is real; impact is a silent functional gap, not corruption, so low severity holds.

findings.title: 1Password read failures are swallowed to a warning and fall through to default/None
findings.evidence: Lines 179-180 and 190-191 catch RuntimeError, logger.warning, and fall through to `return default` (line 193, often None). A transient op failure (locked vault, not signed in) is thus indistinguishable from 'not configured' to callers.
findings.rationale: Accurately described silent-degradation behavior; low severity as it is observability/diagnosability, not a correctness break.

findings.title: Secret value passed on 1Password CLI argv (visible in process list)
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U157: cmd = ['op','item','edit',item,f'{field}={value}'] (line 111), secret on argv, sensitive=True only suppresses stderr. The finder rated it 'low' but the blast radius matches U157.
findings.rationale: Same argv-secret-exposure defect as U157; upgraded to medium to match the canonical for consistency.

findings.title: Warning logs include the full RuntimeError detail, which embeds op stderr that may contain ref/item/field identifiers
findings.evidence: Lines 180/191 log the RuntimeError `e`, which on the read path is built by the non-sensitive _run_op branch (line 45-46) interpolating exc.stderr verbatim plus description f"reading secret {ref}" / f"reading field {field} from item {item}" (lines 64, 91). Reference/item/field/vault identifiers land in WARNING logs. Read paths are not sensitive=True.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: topology identifiers (not secret values) reach logs; low-severity info-disclosure/recon surface.

findings.title: store_json_secret silently prefers op_ref over item/field and silently no-ops on partial config edge cases
findings.evidence: Confirmed control flow: a present-but-unparseable ref (parse_op_ref returns None at lines 131/134) falls through; if item/field env vars also unset, line 274 raises the generic 'Token storage not configured' even though a (malformed) ref was set. Accurate. However store_json_secret is dead code (U446), so no live path exercises this.
findings.rationale: Diagnosability concern is real as described, but it sits on the unused store_json_secret path; low impact.

findings.title: Batch callback silently swallows per-message exceptions
findings.evidence: Line 122 `if exception: return` in the batch callback silently drops any message whose metadata GET failed, with no logging or counter. The drop changes which domains/subjects feed classify_domain and the sorted_domains volume ranking, with zero operator visibility.
findings.rationale: Accurate silent-failure. Low impact for an analysis-only sampling step (the bulk move still operates on the live `from:domain` query, not the sample).

findings.title: Analysis batch.execute() has no error handling; a failure aborts the whole drain with traceback and the print('\r') progress can hide errors
findings.evidence: Line 135 `batch.execute()` has no try/except — an HttpError on the analysis fetch propagates and aborts drain_loop with no state/resume. Line 180 uses `print(..., end='\r')` which can visually clobber an interleaved `logger.warning` from the except branch (182). Mixed print()+logging to the same stream confirmed (StreamHandler at line 31).
findings.rationale: Both sub-claims accurate; low because it is an observability/robustness nit, and an abort is arguably safer than the silent loop. Progress-print interleave overlaps U888.

findings.title: Batch callback swallows fetch exceptions silently and batch.execute has no error handling
findings.evidence: Combines the callback silent-drop (line 122) and unguarded batch.execute() (line 135), plus notes direct `h['name']`/`h['value']` indexing (lines 124-125) instead of .get() which would KeyError on a malformed header dict.
findings.rationale: Same two defects as U533 (callback drop) and U534 (unguarded execute); KeyError sub-point is real but Gmail metadata format guarantees those keys. Duplicate of U533/U534 cluster.

findings.title: Comment claims label auto-creation but code just `continue`s, dropping the domain when target label missing
findings.evidence: Line 149 comment 'Create label if missing (shouldn't happen with our standard set, but safety)' but line 150 body is just `continue` — no creation. classify_domain can return any of the 9 KEYWORDS keys plus 'Notification'; if a target label was never created, target_id is None (146), the domain is skipped, and moves_performed contribution is lost (feeding U043 no-progress).
findings.rationale: Accurate misleading-comment + silent-skip. Low; the configured taxonomy normally has these labels so the skip path is uncommon.

findings.title: get_label_id uses results['labels'] without .get(), KeyError if no labels key
findings.evidence: Confirmed: line 76 `for l in results['labels']:` direct subscript, raising KeyError if 'labels' absent. Verified other code is defensive: gmail_labeler.py line 102 and recount.py line 6 both use `results.get('labels', [])`. Also confirmed get_label_id is invoked twice per rule (lines 88-89) re-fetching the full label list each time — N round trips inefficiency.
findings.rationale: Real but minor robustness gap; Gmail labels().list practically always returns a 'labels' key for a normal account, so low.

findings.title: get_label_id accesses results['labels'] without .get(), KeyError if labels key absent
findings.evidence: Identical claim and identical location as U537: line 76 `results['labels']` direct subscript vs `.get('labels', [])` used elsewhere. Same root cause.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U537 in the same file/lines.

findings.title: report command issues a separate API list call per label with no pagination/cost guard and swallows query into broad except
findings.evidence: Lines 478-487: loops every LABEL_RULES key, for gmail runs `provider.list_messages(f"label:{label}", limit=1)` (481), count=result.total_estimate (482). Verified gmail.list_messages passes q=query verbatim to the API (providers/gmail.py:148) with no quoting; total_estimate=resultSizeEstimate (gmail.py:166). Catch is generic `except Exception as e` printing 'Error ({e})' (486-487).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: one list call per label (no batching/cost guard), hierarchical labels like 'Work/Dev/GitHub' passed unquoted to Gmail search (may mis-match), and resultSizeEstimate presented as a count. Low — report is an informational command; counts being approximate/occasionally wrong is a quality issue not a data-integrity one.

findings.title: Broad except re-raises after saving but the surrounding cmd_label has no handler — provider __exit__ runs but process exits with traceback, no exit code mapping
findings.evidence: run_labeler `except Exception as e:` logs, saves state, `raise` (320-324). cmd_label calls run_labeler inside `with provider:` (439-450) with no try/except, so the exception propagates out of `return args.func(args)` (1201) and crashes with a traceback + default exit 1, bypassing the 0/1/2 mapping at 455-457. KeyboardInterrupt branch (316-319) does NOT re-raise — falls through to `return result` (332), so Ctrl-C is reported as a normal (partial-success) run.
findings.rationale: Confirmed both halves: a mid-run Exception escapes as an unstructured traceback (no clean exit-code mapping), and KeyboardInterrupt is silently treated as a successful partial run rather than an interruption. Inconsistent error semantics. Low — these are exit-code/UX inconsistencies; the state is still saved before the crash, so no data loss.

findings.title: VIP attribution re-runs regex over all VIP patterns and attributes a message to the FIRST matching pattern only; overlapping VIPs undercounted, import re inside loop
findings.evidence: Lines 761-772: after cat_result.is_vip, iterates vip_senders, `import re` (764) inside the loop body, `re.search(vip.pattern, msg.sender, re.IGNORECASE)` then `break` on first match (765-772). First-match-only attribution; uncompiled unvalidated pattern; no try/except. If two patterns match a sender, only the first dict-iteration match is credited.
findings.rationale: Confirmed and slice marks corroborated=true. Real but low-impact: first-match-only undercounts overlapping VIPs in a display-only report; in-loop import is harmless. The missing-error-guard portion overlaps U548 (same call site).

findings.location: run_labeler lines 197-201, cmd_summary 552-556, cmd_pending 655-659, cmd_vip 750-754, cmd_escalate 866-870
findings.title: batch_get_details detection via hasattr but fallback dict-comprehension calls get_message_details per id which may return None and partially populate; no error isolation
findings.evidence: All commands do `if hasattr(provider,'batch_get_details'): details = provider.batch_get_details(msg_ids) else: details = {m.id: provider.get_message_details(m.id) for ...}` (e.g. 198-201). In the fallback, a get_message_details raising aborts the whole comprehension. But gmail.batch_get_details (providers/gmail.py:213-258) already isolates per-message errors via a callback collecting failed_ids and retries them individually, and consuming loops iterate details.items() (not msg_ids), so a dropped id is skipped, not a KeyError.
findings.rationale: Partial: the fallback-path 'one error kills the page' claim is real (dict comprehension has no per-item isolation). But the 'missing keys cause silent drops vs the if-not-msg check' framing is overstated — loops iterate details.items(), so omitted ids are simply absent, never KeyError, and only the non-Gmail fallback lacks isolation (Gmail's batch path isolates). Low.

findings.location: run_labeler line 311 time.sleep(1.0) inside while loop
findings.title: Fixed 1s throttle per batch is unconditional even in dry-run and even on the last (break) iteration
findings.evidence: Line 311 `time.sleep(1.0)` is unconditional, executes before the `if not page_token: break` check (313-314), and is not gated on `not dry_run`. A 10k-message dry-run paginating 100 at a time sleeps ~100s for no API reason; the final page also sleeps before breaking.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: fixed throttle fires even in dry-run and on the terminal iteration. Minor perf/UX waste, no correctness impact. Low.

findings.title: Provider-specific flags (--host/--user/--password/--gmail-extensions/--account) accepted for every provider but only used by imap/mailapp; silently ignored otherwise
findings.evidence: provider_group defines --host/--user/--password/--account/--gmail-extensions for all subcommands (982-1002). cmd_label passes them all into get_provider (425-432). get_provider's gmail branch returns GmailProvider() (116) and outlook returns OutlookProvider() (133), ignoring host/user/password/account/gmail-extensions entirely. No validation warns the user.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: provider-specific flags are accepted globally and silently ignored for gmail/outlook; e.g. `--provider gmail --host imap.example.com` silently ignores host while using Gmail OAuth. Low — confusing UX, but no security/data harm (the ignored flags can't redirect the connection).

findings.location: cmd_pending sort line 680 key uses x['age_hours'] which is 0 for messages with no date
findings.title: Messages with missing date get age_hours=0 (calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for None), sorting them as 'newest' regardless of true age
findings.evidence: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 when date is None (rules.py:1163-1166, verified). cmd_pending sorts by (tier, -age_hours) (680). escalate uses the same age at line 883. Providers gmail/imap/mailapp never set date (None), so age_hours is 0 for them.
findings.rationale: Confirmed but largely a duplicate symptom of U167/U166: the None-date -> age 0 root cause means undated messages mis-sort as newest and never escalate. Distinct framing (None-handling of the age function), so kept as confirmed rather than duplicate, but low severity and overlaps the U166/U167 cluster.

findings.title: Negative or zero --limit is passed unvalidated straight to provider.list_messages
findings.evidence: argparse --limit is type=int with no bound (e.g. 1093-1097, 1133-1138). summary/pending/vip/escalate pass args.limit straight into provider.list_messages(limit=args.limit) (545, 648, 746, 857). gmail.list_messages does page_size = min(limit, LIST_PAGE_SIZE) (gmail.py:144), so a negative limit yields a negative maxResults sent to the Gmail API; limit=0 fetches nothing while reporting a clean run.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: no lower-bound validation on --limit for the read-only triage commands; negative -> provider-dependent error/UB, 0 -> silent empty run. Low — requires deliberately passing a nonsensical value; worst case is an API error or an empty report, not data corruption.

findings.title: report command presents Gmail resultSizeEstimate as an authoritative label count
findings.evidence: Line 482 `count = result.total_estimate or 0` for gmail; total_estimate = resultSizeEstimate (providers/gmail.py:166). For non-gmail count = 'N/A' (484), printed under '## Label Counts' (477). The list thus mixes estimated ints and the literal string 'N/A' under a precision-implying heading.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: Gmail's documented-approximate resultSizeEstimate is presented as an exact label count, and ints/'N/A' strings are mixed. Overlaps U539 (same report command, estimate concern) but framed on the estimate-precision angle. Low — informational report.

findings.location: cmd_vip lines 763-772 (import re inside loop) and 765 re.search(vip.pattern, ...)
findings.title: cmd_vip re-runs unvalidated user VIP regex with no try/except, crashing the command on a bad pattern
findings.evidence: Line 764 `import re` inside the for loop; line 765 `re.search(vip.pattern, msg.sender, re.IGNORECASE)` with no try/except. vip.pattern originates from user YAML via apply_vip_senders_from_config (cmd_vip 718-719). An invalid regex raises re.error here, aborting the whole vip command with a traceback.
findings.rationale: Confirmed and slice marks corroborated=true: this call site re-runs the user-supplied pattern unguarded, a second crash site for the untrusted-regex class. Distinct from U541 (which is the first-match/attribution angle) but same lines; could be seen as overlapping. Low — only triggers on a malformed user-configured regex pattern.

findings.title: `.gov` suffix match over-protects every government TLD address, not just configured senders
findings.evidence: Line 106: `if (value.includes("courts.ca.gov") || value.endsWith(".gov"))` protects any address ending `.gov`. CI smoke test (.github/workflows/ci.yml:116-119) posts `clerk@courts.ca.gov` and asserts `protected:true` — which is satisfied by the broad `.gov` branch, so the test passes regardless of explicit-list membership.
findings.rationale: Broad `.gov` over-protection and test-masking confirmed; erring toward protection keeps severity low (safe direction for this safety-product).

findings.location: senderCheck line 84 empty-sender branch returns protected:true with label 'Misc/Other'
findings.title: Empty/missing sender is fail-closed protected but mislabeled Misc/Other tier-4 — protected verdict paired with an archive-eligible tier_config (keep_in_inbox:false)
findings.evidence: Lines 84-104: empty sender returns `protected: true` with `categorization.tier_config.keep_in_inbox: false`, `star: false`, label 'Misc/Other' tier 4. Per CLAUDE.md tier table, protected/Critical items keep_in_inbox=true; here a protected verdict is paired with an archive-eligible tier_config, an internally contradictory record.
findings.rationale: Accurately describes the code; top-level `protected` flag is authoritative so impact is cosmetic — low/info-tier inconsistency, correctly rated low.

findings.location: fetch() dispatch lines 244-320; serveApp fall-through line 320
findings.title: Unknown POST paths and wrong-method requests fall through to serveApp/ASSETS instead of 404/405 — POST /v1/senders/check via GET, or POST to any unrouted path, silently serves static assets
findings.evidence: Each route gates on pathname AND method (e.g. line 250 requires POST, line 265 requires GET). A GET /v1/senders/check or PUT /v1/triage matches no branch and falls through to `serveApp` (line 320) -> ASSETS.fetch, returning HTML/asset (or SPA) instead of 405/404. The FastAPI app returns proper 405/404.
findings.rationale: Method/route fall-through to ASSETS is confirmed in the dispatch chain; low impact on a demo host.

findings.title: startsWith('/v1/audit/') with split('/').pop() mis-handles trailing-slash and nested paths — '/v1/audit/' yields run_id 'demo', '/v1/audit/a/b' yields 'b'
findings.evidence: Line 282: `runId = url.pathname.split("/").pop() || "demo"`. For `/v1/audit/` pop() returns '' -> defaults to 'demo'; for `/v1/audit/a/b` pop() returns 'b'. Loose parsing as described.
findings.rationale: Path-parsing behavior verified exactly; low impact because the returned body is a fabricated constant, not a real lookup.

findings.title: Malformed/empty request body silently becomes {} so senderCheck/triage proceed with undefined sender — no 400 for invalid JSON
findings.evidence: Lines 216-222: `readJson` catches any parse error and returns `{}`. Line 252 calls `senderCheck(body.sender)` -> undefined -> empty branch (protected:true Misc/Other). Lines 257/262 call triagePreview with `body.provider || "demo"`. A malformed/empty body yields a 200 fabricated result, never a 400.
findings.rationale: Silent {} fallback and resulting 200-instead-of-400 behavior confirmed; low for a demo surface.

findings.location: PLANS lines 3-59 vs api/plans.py free/pro/business definitions
findings.title: Plan catalog is a hardcoded duplicate of api/plans.py with no shared source — prices/caps drift silently between the live demo and the real billing engine
findings.evidence: worker PLANS (lines 3-59) hardcode id/name/price_cents/monthly_run_cap/retained_receipt_days/features duplicating api/plans.py:68-126; web/index.html:679 SAMPLE_PLANS is a third copy. No shared source feeds worker.mjs (gen_commerce_artifacts.py writes pricing.md/llms.txt/agent.json/server.json — never worker.mjs). CI smoke test (ci.yml:115) only asserts the literal '"plans"' string, so price/cap drift is uncaught. Note: worker /v1/billing/plans also omits the API's metered/credit_packs/currency fields (billing.py:97-102) — an additional shape divergence.
findings.rationale: Hardcoded fourth copy with no shared source and weak CI assertion all verified; classic fix-bases-not-outputs drift, low severity (demo).

findings.title: Wildcard CORS (access-control-allow-origin:*) lets any origin read the unsigned audit receipts and sender-classification API from the browser
findings.evidence: Line 63: `"access-control-allow-origin": "*"` in COMMON_HEADERS, spread into every json() response (line 71). Applies to /v1/audit/* (281-305) and /v1/senders/check (250-253). No credentials are involved, and audit bodies are currently fabricated constants (no real ledger wired), so today there is no victim data to read cross-origin.
findings.rationale: Wildcard CORS on audit/sender endpoints is real, but the 'read a victim's audit receipts' consequence is speculative/conditional on a future real-ledger wiring — confirmed-but-narrow; low.

findings.title: Worker inlines a hand-written llms.txt that can drift from the canonical root llms.txt / gen_commerce_artifacts.py output
findings.evidence: Worker lines 307-318 inline the /llms.txt body. Root llms.txt is regenerated by scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py (lines 99-100, via build_llms_txt) per DEPLOY.md:77-79; that script never writes worker.mjs. ASSETS dir is ./web (wrangler.toml:9-10) which contains only index.html — root llms.txt is not an asset, so the worker's inline copy is its only /llms.txt and will silently diverge.
findings.rationale: Inline-vs-canonical drift verified; same root cause as U457 but framed as deployment/fix-bases — both confirmed, distinct emphasis.

findings.title: Worker senderCheck is a divergent re-implementation of the Python protected-sender gate (share-demo only)
findings.evidence: Worker senderCheck (PROTECTED set line 1; .gov/chase/1password substring checks lines 106-153) reimplements the gate in JS rather than calling core/rules (service.py:22,61 uses is_protected_sender/categorize_with_tier). Lines 151-153: final branch sets `protected: value.includes("chase.com") || value.includes("1password.com")` while categorizing as Misc/Other tier 4 — a protected:true paired with a non-protected tier the Python gate would not emit. Note the `1password.com` in the PROTECTED set (line 1) is only ever checked in this final fall-through.
findings.rationale: Divergent JS re-implementation and the 1password protected+tier-4 inconsistency confirmed; low for a marketed demo, but a genuine correctness/drift risk.

findings.title: serveApp special-cases /app but lets any other path hit ASSETS, so /docs, /openapi.json, /server.json resolve to the SPA fallback or a bare 404 with no API semantics
findings.evidence: serveApp (lines 224-236) special-cases `/` (302) and `/app`,`/app/` (index.html); everything else is `env.ASSETS.fetch(request)`. wrangler.toml [assets] (lines 9-12) sets only directory+binding — no `not_found_handling`/`html_handling`. The actual response for `/openapi.json` or `/server.json` depends on the Cloudflare assets default behavior, which is not statically determinable from this repo.
findings.rationale: The code path is real but the concrete outcome (404 asset vs SPA-200) depends on undocumented Cloudflare asset defaults at runtime — needs_runtime.

findings.title: Root redirect uses 302 to /app/ and constructs the target with new URL('/app/', url), which works but the 302 (temporary) plus lost query/fragment is a minor divergence
findings.evidence: Line 227: `Response.redirect(new URL("/app/", url), 302)` constructs the target from only the `/app/` path, dropping any query string on `/`. billing.py:129 sets success_url to `/app/?...` directly (bypassing `/`), so the documented Stripe flow is unaffected; only a hypothetical `/?billing=...` link would lose its query.
findings.rationale: Query-drop on `/` redirect is technically correct, but the finding itself concedes the real Stripe flow targets /app/ directly — impact is a hypothetical edge; downgraded to info.

findings.title: OPTIONS preflight returns Allow-Methods GET,POST,OPTIONS for ALL paths including ones that only accept one method, and never reflects requested headers
findings.evidence: Lines 240-242: OPTIONS returns COMMON_HEADERS unconditionally for every path. Line 64: `access-control-allow-methods: GET,POST,OPTIONS` advertised even for single-method routes; line 65: `access-control-allow-headers` is the fixed string `content-type`, so requested headers like authorization are not reflected.
findings.rationale: Accurately describes a blanket preflight; cosmetic for a credential-free demo (no auth headers used), so info rather than low.

findings.title: Worker billing webhook returns 503 JSON for POST /v1/billing/webhook, masking the fail-closed 400 'invalid signature' contract of the real webhook
findings.evidence: Worker lines 269-279 stub checkout, portal, AND webhook all to 503 'billing is not configured on this share demo'. api/billing.py:170-185 shows the real webhook is fail-CLOSED: unverified payload -> 400 'invalid signature' (line 185); missing secret -> 503 (line 176). The worker returns 503 unconditionally, masking the 400 signature-rejection contract.
findings.rationale: Contract divergence on the webhook stub verified; impact is low since the demo never processes real Stripe events, but the finding is accurate.

findings.title: RunAtLoad=true on gmail_labeler plist would trigger a full multi-provider run immediately on load/login, in addition to the 9 AM schedule
findings.evidence: Lines 25-26 of com.user.gmail_labeler.plist: '<key>RunAtLoad</key>\n<true/>'. diff confirms sibling com.user.mail_automation.plist has '<false/>' at the same line. So if THIS plist were manually loaded, run_automation.sh (full multi-provider mutating run) would fire immediately at load/login in addition to the 09:00 StartCalendarInterval. The true-vs-false inconsistency across two otherwise byte-identical plists is real.
findings.rationale: RunAtLoad=true is factually present and the immediate-on-load side-effect is real; severity stays low because deploy.sh never bootstraps this file (it boots it out, line 57), so the trigger requires a manual mis-load — the finding's own 'if gmail_labeler were ever loaded' framing is the accurate scope.

findings.title: Plist provides no PATH/EnvironmentVariables; job depends entirely on run_automation.sh sourcing the 1Password env and on /bin/bash existing
findings.evidence: Plist has no EnvironmentVariables key (confirmed — dict at lines 4-27 contains only Label, ProgramArguments, WorkingDirectory, StandardOutPath, StandardErrorPath, StartCalendarInterval, RunAtLoad). Mitigation confirmed: run_automation.sh:19-24 sources $HOME/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh and run_automation.sh:11 uses the absolute venv python ($REPO_DIR/.venv/bin/python). No secrets embedded in plist (good, confirmed). Whether the op env-fetch fails under launchd's minimal PATH depends on where the `op` CLI lives and whether the op env script hardcodes its path — not statically determinable from the files in scope.
findings.rationale: Accurately describes launchd's minimal-environment behavior and the implicit PATH dependency; correctly self-rates low/medium. Confirmability requires inspecting ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh (out of repo, not visible) and running under launchd, so needs_runtime; severity held at low given the finding itself concedes it is mostly mitigated.

findings.location: SELF_NAME default line 23 + PERSONAL def lines 38-40
findings.title: Default SELF_NAME 'your-name' creates a PERSONAL smart mailbox matching the literal substring 'your-name'
findings.evidence: Line 23 `SELF_NAME = os.environ.get('SELF_NAME', 'your-name')`; line 39 uses it as the PERSONAL mailbox's From ContainsString expression. No guard checks whether the placeholder is still in effect (grep found no warning/abort on the default). Running without setting the env var installs a PERSONAL smart mailbox that matches the literal substring 'your-name'.
findings.rationale: Verified footgun: unset env var silently writes a non-functional placeholder rule into live Mail config; minor blast radius (cosmetic/garbage rule), hence low.

findings.title: Public package API omits names that consumers actually use (apply_vip_senders_from_config, AuditLog, AuditInvariantError)
findings.evidence: Facts confirmed: apply_vip_senders_from_config is defined at core/config.py:300 and imported via deep path `from core.config import load_config, apply_vip_senders_from_config` at cli.py:44 (used at lines 423/524/719/829). core/audit.py exists with class AuditInvariantError (line 69) and class AuditLog (line 117); api/service.py:21 does `from core.audit import AuditInvariantError, AuditLog`, and cli.py:24,367 likewise. None of these three names appear in core/__init__.py's import block or __all__ (lines 42-78). So the re-export gap is real.
findings.rationale: Real omission but overstated as a 'defect': deep imports work correctly, nothing crashes, and core.audit is plausibly an intentionally separate surface; this is a curation/style observation, not a bug — adjusted to info.

findings.location: receipt_line() lines 264-273 (esp. 'protected held in inbox' at 267 vs 'leave inbox' at 268)
findings.title: Human receipt_line reports a violating (protected-but-departed) message as 'protected held in inbox', misstating the breach in the headline number
findings.evidence: Line 267 prints `{s['protected_held']} protected held in inbox`; because a violated protected message is counted into PROTECTED_HELD (record line 182 / summary line 245), the headline figure includes a message that actually left the inbox. The only departure signal is the appended '⚠ GATE VIOLATION on N protected message(s)!' (line 272); the main sentence still affirmatively states those are 'held in inbox'.
findings.rationale: Real, and the human-facing surface variant of U337 (distinct line: receipt_line vs summary count). Low severity because the violation warning is appended and is loud; in API contexts assert_no_violations raises before any receipt is shown.

findings.location: record() line 178 (_independently_protected re-runs is_protected_sender per message) cross-ref providers/base.py:299/gmail.py:403 (_drop_if_protected already ran it)
findings.title: Audit re-runs the full protected-sender regex check on every recorded message, duplicating the gate's work per-message
findings.evidence: Line 178 unconditionally calls _independently_protected(sender), which lazily imports and runs the full is_protected_sender regex/normalize pipeline (rules.py:787, iterating every address via _iter_sender_domains) for every record() call — the same sender the provider gate already evaluated. The union `bool(protected) or _independently_protected(sender)` could short-circuit when protected is already True, since Python `or` would skip the second call only if the first operand is truthy — but here `protected` is the gate flag and the second eval is exactly what's wanted when it's False; the redundancy is on the common protected-path where protected=True.
findings.rationale: Correctly identified efficiency smell on the hot apply loop. Note: Python's `or` ALREADY short-circuits when bool(protected) is True (line 178), so the redundant call only happens when protected=False OR when bool(protected) evaluates True it is skipped — meaning the doubled work is actually on the NON-protected common path, not the protected one as the finding states. Still a real per-message cost; low severity (correctness fine).

findings.title: Audit JSONL 'domain' field uses normalize_sender (first address only), under-reporting multi-address senders
findings.evidence: record() line 206 stores domain=_domain_of(sender); _domain_of (84-92) calls normalize_sender which returns only the FIRST/primary address's domain (rules.py:740-742 docstring: 'for the FIRST/primary address'). The gate is_protected_sender unions over EVERY address (rules.py:800 _iter_sender_domains). So for a multi-address From where the protected sender is second, the receipt's domain field shows a non-protected domain while disposition is protected_held.
findings.rationale: Real: the displayed/redacted domain can misidentify which sender was protected on multi-address headers. Counts and violations unaffected. Low severity — evidentiary/display fidelity only.

findings.title: IMAP_HOST/IMAP_USER env vars ignore the configured env_prefix, inconsistent precedence
findings.evidence: Lines 282-285 read IMAP_HOST/IMAP_USER bare; 294-297 read OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID/OUTLOOK_TOKEN_CACHE bare — no f'{prefix}' applied. All other overrides use the prefix (266,268,270,272,276,278,286,290). So passing env_prefix='MYAPP_' would not namespace IMAP/Outlook host/user/client vars.
findings.rationale: Confirmed inconsistency — these four vars ignore env_prefix (likely intentional reuse of the legacy IMAP_*/OUTLOOK_* names documented in CLAUDE.md, but it does contradict the docstring's MAIL_AUTO_* framing); low severity.

findings.title: Sample config file written without atomicity or overwrite guard
findings.evidence: Lines 419-423: `if path: path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True); with open(path, "w") as f: f.write(sample)` — unconditional 'w' open, no exists() check, no backup. An existing config at that path is overwritten silently.
findings.rationale: Confirmed no overwrite guard. Severity low — create_sample_config is an explicit user-invoked helper to scaffold a sample, not part of automated runs; grep shows it's not called from cli.py automation paths.

findings.title: Double os.getenv call (TOCTOU-style redundancy) on every env override
findings.evidence: Each override calls os.getenv twice, e.g. 266-267, 268-269, 276-277. Truthiness guard means an explicitly-empty env var is treated as unset (real behavior). The TOCTOU angle is not meaningful single-threaded (env is process-local).
findings.rationale: Confirmed as a style/redundancy smell (double getenv + empty-string-skip); the TOCTOU framing is overstated. Info severity — no correctness defect, just verbosity.

findings.title: custom_rules and vip_senders YAML are assigned wholesale with no validation, then VIP patterns are fed to re.search unguarded
findings.evidence: Restates U063 (vip pattern -> re.search unguarded, rules.py:885) and U189 (custom_rules dead). The ReDoS angle is the only novel framing: a catastrophic-backtracking pattern in user config would be evaluated by re.search per sender — plausible but requires the user to author the malicious pattern in their own config.
findings.rationale: Combines two already-filed root causes (VIP-regex U063 + custom_rules-dead U189). De-duplicated to U063; the self-authored-ReDoS framing doesn't elevate it given the threat model is the user's own config file.

findings.title: MAIL_AUTO_BATCH_SIZE parsed with bare int() — a non-numeric env value crashes config load
findings.evidence: Same defect as U062/U185: line 273 `config.batch_size = int(os.getenv(prefix+'BATCH_SIZE'))` with no try/except, contrasted against the tolerant DRY_RUN parse at 271.
findings.rationale: Third report of the BATCH_SIZE bare-int crash; de-duplicated to U062.

findings.title: Frozen EmailMessage holds mutable Set fields — frozen guarantee is shallow
findings.evidence: Same defect as U462: frozen=True on core/models.py:25 with mutable Set fields (lines 49, 53). The added hashability claim is itself wrong as a concern — frozen dataclasses with a mutable field generate __hash__ but the field set() is unhashable, so EmailMessage cannot actually be put in a set/dict (TypeError on hash); the 'corrupts the container' scenario can't arise. Core claim is identical to U462.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U462 (shallow frozen); the extra hashing sub-claim is incorrect, so it does not warrant a separate confirmed finding.

findings.title: merge() routes ordered List[str] label fields through set(), destroying order and any intended duplicate/sequence semantics
findings.evidence: core/models.py:100-101 do list(set(self.add_labels + other.add_labels)) — set() does discard insertion order and collapse dups; fields are declared List[str] (lines 86-87), so contract/impl disagree (confirmed). HOWEVER the corroboration is partly a misread: base.py:340-348 does NOT 'first move wins' — the loop sets did_label_move=True (an audit flag) on every LABEL_IS_MOVE apply and does NOT break, applying all add_labels; for folder providers (outlook.py:102, mailapp.py:55 LABEL_IS_MOVE=True) the LAST applied move determines final folder, so order matters but not via the mechanism cited. merge() also has no production caller.
findings.rationale: Order-loss and List-vs-set contract mismatch are real, but the cited 'first-match-wins' control flow misreads base.py and there is no caller; low/latent.

findings.title: verizon (a telecom) is in Finance/Banking, mislabeling carrier mail as a Critical financial-alert
findings.evidence: Line 146 lists r'verizon' under Finance/Banking (tier 1 Critical, in PRIORITY_LABELS line 511). Reproduced: 'bill from verizon' -> Finance/Banking tier 1. Verizon is a telecom, not a bank.
findings.rationale: Real misplacement that inflates the Critical/starred bucket with carrier mail. Low (taxonomy quality, no data loss; arguably intentional to treat carrier bills as financial alerts).

findings.location: AI/Services patterns 113-124 (r'claude', r'openai', r'anthropic', r'ollama', r'perplexity'); AI/Grok r'grok' (129)
findings.title: Bare-substring AI/brand patterns over-match unrelated mail
findings.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('art@museum.com','Claude Monet art print') -> AI/Services tier 3. Lines 114-123 are bare case-insensitive substrings (r'openai', r'anthropic', r'claude', r'perplexity', r'ollama') with no host/word boundary, unlike the (^|@|\.)...\b anchoring used for meta.com (line 121) and the finance/travel domains.
findings.rationale: Real inconsistent anchoring -> AI/Services false positives. Low (tier 3 Delegate, no archival/critical impact).

findings.location: Shopping patterns: r'uber' (243), r'target' (247), r'square' (252)... Personal r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' (488)
findings.title: Many bare-substring keyword rules over-match common English words and brand prefixes
findings.evidence: Reproduced: 'tuberculosis update' -> Shopping (r'uber' line 243); 'retargeting strategies' -> Shopping (r'target' line 247). Note: r'square' at line 250 (squarespace) not 252, r'discover' at 185 (Finance/Payments tier 2), r'mom'/r'dad'/r'family' at 488 (Personal tier 1). The bare unanchored substrings do over-match common English/brand prefixes as claimed.
findings.rationale: Real, broad pattern of bare-substring over-matching; some push into elevated tiers (Personal tier1, Finance/Payments tier2). Low individually but collectively erodes accuracy. Minor line-number drift in the finding does not change the verdict.

findings.title: Negative email age (future-dated email) bypasses every escalation guard and is treated as fresh
findings.evidence: Reproduced: calculate_email_age_hours(now+10h) -> -9.999... (line 1184 returns raw (now-email_date)/3600, no clamp). Negative age satisfies email_age_hours<24 at line 1122 (never escalates, silently). cli.py:680 sort key uses -age_hours, so a negative age becomes a large positive sort key, pushing future-dated items to the top of the pending list.
findings.rationale: Real for future-dated/clock-skewed mail. Low: requires a future receivedDateTime (only Outlook populates date, and Graph dates are normally sane); cosmetic triage-ordering distortion, no data loss.

findings.location: line 616 (_local_domains, _local_selfs = _load_local_protected()) executed on import of core
findings.title: Safety-gate dataset is populated by a filesystem read executed at import of the core package
findings.evidence: Line 616 calls _load_local_protected() at module top level; core/__init__.py imports core.rules. Confirmed import-time file I/O building PROTECTED_SENDERS (line 619). Path resolved relative to module's grandparent dir (lines 593-595), not CWD. Snapshot frozen at first import.
findings.rationale: Real design fragility (import-time I/O side effect on the safety path; frozen snapshot; no refresh hook). Low: works correctly for the normal single-process run; the only sharp edge (non-UTF-8 crash) is already captured as U067.

findings.title: Tech/Google bare substrings 'workspace'/'gcp' over-match unrelated mail
findings.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('rent@coworkspace.com','rent coworkspace downtown') -> Tech/Google tier 2. Lines 232-233 list bare r'gcp' and r'workspace'; both over-match ('coworkspace', 'workspaces').
findings.rationale: Real bare-substring over-match elevating unrelated mail to Tech/Google tier 2 Important. Low (taxonomy noise, no data loss). Same class as U577/U578/U582 but distinct patterns.

findings.location: Finance/Payments r'plaid' (172); Finance/Banking r'chime' (149); Professional/Jobs r'monster' (380); Travel r'kayak' (321); Shopping r'nike'/'nordstrom'/'zara'/'macys' (250-264)
findings.title: Additional bare-brand substrings collide with common English / unrelated products
findings.evidence: Reproduced: 'plaid shirts on sale' -> Finance/Payments tier 2 (r'plaid' line 175); 'Your monster energy rewards' -> Professional/Jobs tier 2 (r'monster' line 380); 'wind chime sale' -> Finance/Banking tier 1 Critical (r'chime' line 149). Note: r'plaid' is line 175 not 172, but the pattern and effect are as described.
findings.rationale: Real bare-brand substring over-match; r'chime' into tier-1 Critical is the worst as the finding notes. Low (false-positive inflation, no data loss). Minor line-number drift only.

findings.title: escalate_by_age does not validate current_tier range/type
findings.evidence: Reproduced: escalate_by_age(0,100,True) -> should_escalate=True, escalated_tier=1 (bypasses the current_tier==1 guard at 1113); escalate_by_age(5,50,True) -> escalated_tier=2 (treats tier 5 as escalatable). No range/type validation on current_tier (1091-1095).
findings.rationale: Real: out-of-range tiers (reachable via the U348 VIP-tier path) are silently mis-escalated; a non-int would TypeError on comparisons. Low (requires upstream misconfig; escalation is opt-in).

findings.title: _find_best_label scans rules in dict order and cannot recover from any same-priority misorder
findings.evidence: Same code (line 1033 strict '<', line 1036 break) and same root cause as U064. Priority-tie histogram confirmed (ties at 3,8,9,11,12,14,17). This finding generalizes the gov/marketing case to all tied groups but is the identical defect.
findings.rationale: Same dict-insertion-order tie-break defect as U064. Listed as the structural generalization; severity inherited as high (the priority-17 tied group is the harmful one).

findings.location: Notification patterns lines 414-422 + Tech/Storage/Notification priority interplay
findings.title: Notification catch-all keywords 'notification'/'alert'/'reminder' shadow more specific tier-1 categories below them
findings.evidence: Reproduced: categorize_with_tier('news@x.com','Reminder: your passport photo discount') -> Notification tier 3. Notification priority 16 (line 424) < Personal/Government priority 17 (line 480), and Notification's bare r'reminder' (line 416) matches first, demoting government/passport mail to Delegate.
findings.rationale: Real and distinct from U064 (this is lower-priority-wins, 16<17, not a same-priority tie). Government/Critical mail with a 'reminder'/'notification' subject is silently shadowed to tier 3 Delegate. Medium: more impactful than the other low-severity regex noise since it demotes a Critical category.

findings.title: VIP label_override is not validated against LABEL_RULES; a bogus override yields default time_sensitive=True silently
findings.evidence: Reproduced: add_vip_sender(label_override='NotARealLabel') -> categorize_with_tier returns label='NotARealLabel', time_sensitive=True. Line 987-988: label=vip.label_override verbatim; LABEL_RULES.get(label,{'time_sensitive':True}) silently defaults. No validation against LABEL_RULES keys.
findings.rationale: Real: an unvalidated VIP label_override propagates as the result label (and, per the finding, into action.add_labels), potentially creating a junk label/folder. Low: requires user-supplied VIP config (no default VIPs); the runtime claim about provider apply is plausible but not verified in this slice.

findings.title: Label selection iterates dict insertion order, so a higher-priority rule defined later than a same-text earlier rule can lose the tie incorrectly
findings.evidence: Describes the identical line-1033 strict-'<' + insertion-order tie-break for the priority-8 (Finance/Tax vs Finance/Payments) and priority-9 (Tech/Security vs Tech/Google vs Personal/Health) ties. Same code, same root cause as U064/U584.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U064; this instance focuses on the lower-stakes non-gov tied groups, hence the finding's own low severity is apt, but it is the same root cause.

findings.title: calculate_email_age_hours returns 0 for a None date, which escalate_by_age treats as a fresh (<24h) email — undated mail never escalates
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U190: line 1173-1174 returns 0 for None date; escalate_by_age line 1122 treats age 0 as <24h -> no escalation. Confirmed gmail/imap/mailapp never set EmailMessage.date.
findings.rationale: Same None-date -> age-0 -> escalation-no-op defect as U190 (U190 is the same finding from R1; U588 is its R2 restatement).

findings.title: clear() resets in-memory state before removing the file, then swallows removal errors — leaves stale file
findings.evidence: core/state.py:124-129 sets self.state = self._default_state() first, then os.remove inside `except Exception -> logger.error`. If remove fails, the function returns normally but the file persists; a later StateManager(filename) reloads the stale token.
findings.rationale: Confirmed silent failure to reset, but clear() is a rare/explicit reset operation (not on the hot path) and the failure mode requires a locked/unwritable file. Low.

findings.location: line 87 (last_run = datetime.now().isoformat()) and the absence of any reader
findings.title: state.py last_run is written as a naive-local ISO string that no code ever parses back, so the isoformat round-trip is untested and would break a naive-vs-aware comparison if a reader is added
findings.evidence: grep confirms no code calls fromisoformat on last_run — only get_last_run (state.py:114) and tests read it; providers/outlook.py:454/505 parse a different field (received). So last_run is write-only dead state today.
findings.rationale: Accurate as a latent-landmine observation, but it is explicitly write-only/dead right now (the distinct claim vs U195). No live defect — informational. Closely related to U195/U779.

findings.title: State file written non-atomically — a crash mid-write corrupts the resume token and loses all progress
findings.evidence: Restates the non-atomic-write/_load-reset defect (U070/U194/U196). The 'dry-run-polluted state' aside references a different file's finding, not state.py.
findings.rationale: Same non-atomic-write root cause as U070.

findings.title: Implemented conditions do not match documented intent (no OR / no Label conditions)
findings.evidence: Comment lines 8-9 describe 'Label is "Awaiting Reply" OR "To Do" OR Label is Personal AND Unread'. Implemented conditions (lines 11-12) are only sender-contains + message-is-unread, no match-type property (Mail.app defaults conditions to ALL/AND) and no label/mailbox condition. Built semantics diverge from the documented design.
findings.rationale: Documented-intent vs implementation divergence is real and statically verifiable; low severity appropriate for an unwired manual script.

findings.title: No idempotency — re-running duplicates smart mailboxes
findings.evidence: Three unconditional `make new smart mailbox` calls at lines 10, 17, 23 with no name-existence check; only the group at lines 3-5 is wrapped in try. Mail.app does not dedupe smart mailboxes by name, so re-running produces duplicate '1. Needs Action' / '2. Finance (Recent)' / '3. Dev Alerts' entries.
findings.rationale: No idempotency guard is genuinely present; low severity correct for a one-time convenience script the user runs by hand.

findings.title: launchctl bootstrap not idempotent / no error handling; can fail on re-deploy
findings.evidence: Line 4 is `set -euo pipefail`. Lines 54 and 57 append `2>/dev/null || true`; line 60 `launchctl bootstrap "$AGENT_ID" "$PLIST_DEST"` has NO `|| true` guard. Under set -e a non-zero bootstrap (e.g. label already loaded after a raced/failed bootout) aborts the script. The cp at :48 has already executed, so the state is half-applied (new plist on disk, not loaded).
findings.rationale: Accurate: bootstrap is the one unguarded launchctl call under set -e and can abort mid-deploy after the file copy; low severity matches the local-deploy blast radius.

findings.title: deploy.sh boots out old jobs then bootstraps, but never reloads gmail_labeler — re-running deploy after a rename leaves only mail_automation, fine, but bootout failures are swallowed
findings.evidence: The bootout `|| true` swallowing (lines 54, 57) is real. But the core claim that deploy.sh 'reports LaunchAgent installed (line 62) unconditionally even if bootstrap failed' is FALSE: line 60 bootstrap is NOT guarded by `|| true` and `set -e` (line 4) is active, so a failed bootstrap aborts the script BEFORE reaching the echo at line 62. The 'failed install reported as success' scenario cannot occur for a bootstrap failure.
findings.rationale: Half right (bootout errors are swallowed) but the headline 'reports success on failure' misreads set -e behavior on the unguarded bootstrap; overstated.

findings.title: Demo doc lists /v1/triage/preview as verified but omits that POST /v1/triage is a silent no-op that always returns dry_run:true
findings.evidence: worker.mjs lines 255-258 and 260-263 both route to triagePreview(); triagePreview() (lines 172-198) always returns `dry_run: true` (line 177) and `run_id: "demo_preview"` (line 196). Doc 'Verified Demo Endpoints' (lines 18-22) lists `POST /v1/triage/preview` but NOT `/v1/triage`, and never states /v1/triage is a no-op preview. However the audit values are NOT fixed 'regardless of input': total=Math.max(capped,3), protected_held and archived derive from the `limit` body field (lines 173-183), so the '3/2/1 regardless of input' claim is overstated — those are the values only when limit<=1 or unset.
findings.rationale: Real documentation accuracy gap (/v1/triage is wired but undocumented and silently a dry-run preview), but the 'fabricated fixed audit regardless of input' characterization misreads code where audit fields are input-derived — hence partial.

findings.title: Nav-dot active-state indexing relies on data-section values being a 0-based dense sequence matching DOM order
findings.evidence: Lines 260-265 build one <a> per section[data-section] in DOM order; lines 270-272: const idx = e.target.dataset.section; ... if (dots[idx]) dots[idx].classList.add('active'). idx is the raw attribute string ('0'..'7'); JS array indexing coerces it. Works only because sections are authored data-section="0".."7" in DOM order (lines 136,148,161,172,183,195,210,237). Reordering/skipping/non-numeric values would highlight the wrong/no dot; the if-guard prevents a crash but the mapping silently misbehaves.
findings.rationale: Latent attribute-to-array-index coupling is real as described; cosmetic, generator-controlled, no current misbehavior — low is accurate.

findings.location: lines 5-11 (delivery web_app status: not_started; revenue subscription status: planned) vs implemented api/billing.py + web/index.html + acp/router.py
findings.title: ecosystem.yaml status fields are stale — claims web_app not_started and subscription revenue merely planned while both are implemented
findings.evidence: ecosystem.yaml lines 5-11: `delivery: - platform: web_app / status: not_started` and `revenue: - platform: subscription / status: planned / priority: critical`. All three claimed implementations exist and are non-trivial: api/billing.py (12KB) implements GET /v1/billing/plans (line 94-95), POST /v1/billing/checkout (105-106, calls stripe checkout.sessions.create at 139), POST /v1/billing/portal (146-147), POST /v1/billing/webhook (170-171, signature-verified fail-closed); web/index.html is 979 lines / 40KB and is mounted at /app via api/app.py:162 (`app.mount('/app', StaticFiles(...))`); acp/router.py (14KB) exists; stripe>=15.2,<16 is declared in requirements-api.txt; docs/plans/2026-06-02-handoff-cloudflare-ci-domain.md confirms the worker deploys to Cloudflare (CI job 'Deploy Cloudflare share demo' succeeded).
findings.rationale: The machine-readable manifest reports the commerce surface as not_started/planned while checkout, portal, webhook, plans catalog, a 979-line dashboard, and Cloudflare deployment are all built — confirmed stale; severity stays low (data/reporting drift, no runtime or security impact).

findings.title: File handle leaks on error: close access is outside any try and the open path may not exist
findings.evidence: Line 4 `set outFile to open for access (outPath as POSIX file) with write permission`; the matching `close access outFile` is at line 41, outside any try. The `tell application "Mail"` block (lines 11-39), `set boxName to name of mbox` (line 16), and the outer `repeat with mbox` (line 15) are NOT inside a try — only the inner per-message body (25-36) and the `messages of mbox` access (18-22) are guarded. An error in `name of mbox` or the Mail tell terminates the script before line 41, leaking the open file-access handle (AppleScript requires explicit `close access` to release it). Separately, outPath is `~/mail_automation/mail_export.tsv` (lines 1) and there is no mkdir/folder-existence step, so `open for access` at line 4 fails if `~/mail_automation` does not exist.
findings.rationale: Both sub-claims hold against current code: close access sits outside all error handling and the outer Mail/mbox-name path is unguarded, plus no directory is created before opening; low severity is correct for a single-user local export utility.

findings.title: TSV delimiter/newline injection corrupts output rows
findings.evidence: Line 33 builds `rowText` by concatenating `boxName`, `message id`, `senderAddress`, `subjectText`, `dateText`, etc. joined with literal `tab`, and line 35 writes `rowText & linefeed`. Sender display names and subjects are written raw with no escaping/sanitization of embedded tabs or CR/LF, so any subject/sender containing a tab or newline shifts columns or injects phantom rows into the TSV.
