findings.rationale: Raw field values are written into a tab/linefeed-delimited file with zero escaping; subjects/senders routinely contain those characters, so downstream TSV parsing can break — genuine correctness defect, correctly rated low.

findings.title: Hardcoded placeholder sender list ships as-is (no-op or wrong matches if run unedited)
findings.evidence: Line 1 literally: set importantSenders to {"boss@company.com", "admissions@university.edu", "familymember@example.com"} — template placeholders. No guard/warning that the list is unconfigured. Run unedited it flags nothing real (or over-matches via the line-5 `contains`).
findings.rationale: Placeholder list ships as-is with no unconfigured-warning; low severity appropriate for a manual-edit AppleScript helper.

findings.title: Iterating live inbox messages with bare try swallows errors; no progress/logging
findings.evidence: Lines 13-18: bare `try` wrapping `set s to sender of msg` and `set flagged status of msg to true` with no `on error` clause; no logging or count output anywhere. Same swallow pattern as archive_old_inbox.applescript (line 16) and route_bulk_senders.applescript (line 25).
findings.rationale: Bare try silently discards per-message read/flag failures with no signal; non-destructive op so low severity is correct.

findings.title: No scope verification on loaded token; a narrower stored token is treated as valid
findings.evidence: Line 162 `creds = Credentials.from_authorized_user_info(token_info, scopes=scopes)` sets the *requested* scopes onto the object; line 164 only checks `creds.valid` (expiry/validity), never `creds.has_scopes(scopes)`. A stored token granted narrower scopes than now requested still yields creds.valid=True, so no refresh/re-consent fires and downstream mutate calls 403 at request time.
findings.rationale: Accurate read of google-auth semantics (from_authorized_user_info does not verify granted scopes); low severity is appropriate — edge case requiring a scope change between sessions.

findings.title: Gmail OAuth token passed as 1Password CLI argv (process-list exposure)
findings.evidence: Same defect as U355: line 57 `cmd = ['op','item','edit', item, f'{field}={value}']` places the token JSON on op's argv. Identical root cause and identical lines as U355.
findings.rationale: Exact same argv-leak defect in the same file/lines as U355; deduplicated.

findings.title: Interactive run_local_server(port=0) silently triggered on a headless/automated run
findings.evidence: Same code defect as U354: line 177 `flow.run_local_server(port=0)` with no non-interactive guard. U605 only reframes the unattended launchd/run_automation.sh path instead of the MCP/REST path, but the underlying defect (no headless detection before launching interactive flow) is identical and in the same lines.
findings.rationale: Same missing-headless-guard defect as U354, different invocation context; deduplicated to U354.

findings.title: No timeout on op subprocess invocation
findings.evidence: gmail_auth.py:21-27 calls subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, text=True, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE) with no timeout=. auth/onepassword.py:33-38 calls subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, text=True, capture_output=True) also with no timeout=. Neither _run_op passes a timeout, so a hung `op` invocation (e.g. awaiting biometric/desktop approval) blocks the caller indefinitely.
findings.rationale: No-timeout claim is literally true in both current files; blast radius is low because these are interactive local-auth utilities (flow.run_local_server is inherently interactive), and the 'ties up a hosted request worker' angle is speculative/overstated but does not negate the underlying missing-timeout defect.

findings.title: Label auto-creation in _init_labels has no error handling; a create failure aborts the entire run before any processing
findings.evidence: Lines 101-113 call labels().list and labels().create() with NO try/except and NOT via _execute_with_backoff (confirmed: only try/except blocks in the file are at 82, 171, 257, 290 — none wrap _init_labels). Any create failure (429/limit/collision) propagates out of __init__ (line 76) and aborts before processing. HOWEVER the finding's supporting claim that a 'legacy get_or_create_label' exists and wraps creation is false — grep shows no get_or_create_label/execute_with_retry anywhere in gmail_labeler.py.
findings.rationale: Core defect (unguarded, non-backoff label creation aborts the whole run) is real; the comparison-to-legacy-helper justification is fabricated, so partial. Low severity (startup-only, fail-loud).

findings.title: Messages that fail BOTH batch-get and one-by-one retry are silently dropped from categorization (never labeled/archived)
findings.evidence: Retry at 170-181 catches HttpError and only logs (181); on failure msg is never inserted into batch_results, and the modifications loop iterates batch_results only (line 194). So a message failing both batch-get and single retry is neither labeled nor archived this pass. For the default query the advancing page-token may re-surface it; for a custom (fresh-start) query it is simply skipped silently.
findings.rationale: Silent under-processing confirmed for a tool that advertises 'exhaustive' labeling; correctly scoped as low (logged, often eventually retried on default query).

findings.title: max_emails limit is only enforced between pages, not within a page — can overshoot by up to a full batch
findings.evidence: Confirmed by structure: the inner per-message loop (415-443) processes up to len(messages) (batch_size up to 500) BEFORE the max_emails check at line 445. So max_emails=10 with batch_size=500 processes ~500 before breaking. HOWEVER the only caller, main() at line 516, passes max_emails=None, and no other code in the repo calls label_all_unlabeled_emails with a max value (grep confirms). The overshoot is dormant.
findings.rationale: Logic defect is real in the function's API but never exercised by any wired caller (max_emails=None everywhere). Downgraded low->info.

findings.title: execute_with_retry has two redundant except blocks and can return None on exhaustion, propagating None to .get()/['id'] callers
findings.evidence: Lines 304-309 (except HttpError,OSError,socket.error) and 310-315 (except Exception) have byte-identical bodies; the first is fully subsumed by the second since every exception is an Exception. There is no `return` after the for-loop, so retries=0 would return None and get_or_create_label line 330 does created_label['id'] (TypeError on None). The function retries ALL exceptions including non-retryable 4xx. Confirmed structurally.
findings.rationale: Redundant-except and implicit-None defects are real; but with default retries=4 the loop always returns or raises (the None path needs retries=0, which no caller passes). Quality/robustness issue, not an active bug. Low.

findings.file: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md
findings.location: iCloud Mail Filtering Rules - Complete Guide.md:23-24,70-71,216-217
findings.title: Committed guide has line-broken email addresses corrupting the documented rules
findings.evidence: Lines 23-24: '• service@american' then 'express.com' on next line (should be service@americanexpress.com). Lines 70-71: bare bullet '•' then 'email@zara.com'. Lines 216-217: bare bullet '•' then 'scholar-alerts-noreply@google.com'. All three split points match the finding exactly.
findings.rationale: Tracked markdown guide has three soft-wrapped/line-broken sender addresses that copy-paste into malformed iCloud rule conditions; doc-hygiene defect, not executable code, so low severity is accurate.

findings.location: main() lines 161-166; per-message archive with no rate-limit/error backoff
findings.title: Apply loop has no per-message error backoff and EXPUNGEs after every message; large applies are slow and a mid-loop server error aborts the rest
findings.evidence: Lines 161-165 have no try/except, so an imaplib exception in archive_uid propagates and aborts remaining moves (finally only logs out, 167-171). True that COPY fallback expunges per message. But the claim 'Moved/failed can never report a non-fatal failure for the MOVE path' is overstated: archive_uid returns False whenever COPY returns non-OK (73-74), which IS counted as failed (164-165); only raised exceptions escape the count.
findings.rationale: Real (no in-loop error isolation) but partly overstated about the failure counter.

findings.location: main lines 131-158 (no try/finally around imap session)
findings.title: IMAP connection leaked on exception — logout only on the happy path
findings.evidence: Same defect: IMAP4_SSL opened at line 131, logout only at line 158, no try/finally; exception in select/search/fetch_headers/ensure_label/apply_label leaks the session.
findings.rationale: Same connection-leak root cause as U468; third restatement.

findings.title: 1Password failure falls through to interactive getpass even in non-interactive/automation context, and swallows all errors
findings.evidence: Line 71 'except Exception as e: print(...)' swallows the op error and falls through to getpass.getpass() at line 74; the inner try/except (73-76) returns in both branches, making the final 'return ""' at line 77 unreachable. In a TTY-less launchd/cron context getpass raises EOFError (caught at 75 -> returns '') and main() bails at line 127 with a generic message, masking the real 1Password failure.
findings.rationale: All claims hold against current code (broad except hides op error; unreachable trailing return; non-interactive fallthrough). Low severity (degraded diagnostics, not data loss).

findings.title: Unguarded dict indexing on labels list and header lookups can KeyError/crash
findings.evidence: Line 8: `label_id = next((l['id'] for l in results['labels'] if l['name'] == 'Misc/Other'), None)` directly subscripts results['labels'] (no .get). Inside cb (line 26): `headers = response['payload']['headers']` and lines 27-28 iterate `h['value']`/`h['name']`. A message returned by messages().get(format='metadata') without a 'payload' key (e.g. some drafts/chat shapes) raises KeyError inside the batch callback. Contrast with line 16 (`.get('messages', [])`) which IS guarded.
findings.rationale: Direct unguarded subscripting on lines 8/26/27/28 is present as described; read-only diagnostic so low blast radius is correct.

findings.title: Mail export TSV (PII sink: sender/subject/message_id columns) is committed
findings.evidence: Same defect as U209: mail_export.tsv is git-tracked (git ls-files confirms), header-only (no data rows), not in .gitignore. Same proposed fix (add to .gitignore alongside audit/*.jsonl and protected_senders.local.txt). Same overstated 'output target of export_mail_snapshot' claim, which actually writes to ~/mail_automation/ outside the repo.
findings.rationale: Identical root cause and remediation to U209 in the same file; the only difference is round/severity labeling (R1 low vs R3 medium).

findings.title: No error handling on list/batchModify; pagination relies on label removal and len<1000 heuristic, can loop
findings.evidence: No try/except anywhere in mark_read_loop; list().execute() (line 44-46) and batchModify().execute() (line 60) are unguarded. Any HttpError aborts with traceback. No backoff (contrast gmail_labeler.py _execute_with_backoff at line 78).
findings.rationale: Confirmed lack of error handling on Gmail API calls; severity appropriately low since it is read-state-only legacy.

findings.title: No error handling on Gmail list/batchModify; service never closed
findings.evidence: Same 'no try/except on list/batchModify' claim as U617; adds 'service never closed'. The Gmail discovery client has no required close() — that sub-claim is a non-issue.
findings.rationale: Error-handling root cause duplicates U617; the 'service never closed' addendum is not a real defect for the googleapiclient resource object.

findings.title: Pagination re-queries first page; termination on len(ids)<1000 is unreliable, can loop or under-process
findings.evidence: Combines the pagination-loop concern (U364) and the no-error-handling concern (U617). Verified gmail_labeler.py:78 does define _execute_with_backoff, so the contrast claim is accurate.
findings.rationale: No new defect beyond U364 (loop/pagination) + U617 (no backoff); merged into U364 as the loop is its primary claim.

findings.title: Marks-read targets reference label names that diverge from the core taxonomy (Work/Dev/* present, but core uses Dev/*)
findings.evidence: mark_rot_read.py lines 28-29 use 'Work/Dev/GitHub'/'Work/Dev/Infrastructure'. core/rules.py:71 defines 'Dev/GitHub' and :89 'Dev/Infrastructure'. organize_labels.py:16,18 maps Dev/GitHub->Work/Dev/GitHub. So these two queries match nothing unless the rename has been applied.
findings.rationale: Taxonomy drift is real and verified against both source files; impact is a silent no-op (no data loss), so low is correct.

findings.title: Heavy MCP objects (FastMCP server + full ASGI app) are constructed at module import time, and MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS is read at import
findings.evidence: _transport_security() (which reads os.environ['MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS'], line 48) is called during FastMCP(...) construction at module scope (line 79); http_app = mcp.streamable_http_app() runs at import (line 151). So env is snapshotted at import and the ASGI app is built eagerly. api/app.py:38-45 wraps the import in try/except, but `python -m mcp_server` (main, 154-160) and `uvicorn mcp_server.server:http_app` do not.
findings.rationale: Accurate description of import-time construction + env freeze; standard pattern for FastMCP module servers and the file even documents the uvicorn target. Genuine but minor operational footgun — low.

findings.location: triage() provider param line 111; triage_preview() provider line 99; _triage line 132 vs api/schemas.py:45 (provider max_length=64)
findings.title: MCP provider argument is length-unbounded, bypassing the HTTP layer's documented anti-DoS max_length=64
findings.evidence: provider is a free `str` on both MCP tools (lines 99,111) passed to service.run_triage->factory(provider)->cli.get_provider with no length bound; schemas.py:45 bounds the HTTP TriageRequest.provider to max_length=64. _clamp_limit only touches limit.
findings.rationale: Real third unbounded field, but provider goes through get_provider's allowlist dispatch (unknown → clean ProviderUnavailable/503), so an oversized provider string is rejected fast with negligible work. Lowest-impact of the input-validation trio — low.

findings.title: readOnlyHint=True on triage_preview masks a credentialed, quota-consuming scan of up to 1000 messages with a per-batch sleep
findings.evidence: triage_preview is annotated readOnlyHint=True (line 97) yet calls _triage->service.run_triage with real mailbox credentials (prov.connect(), service.py:99-100) and pages up to MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT=1000 messages with time.sleep(1.0) per 100-msg batch (cli.py:311; schemas.py:12-16 documents exactly this DoS vector). No metering on the MCP surface.
findings.rationale: Accurate: readOnlyHint (mailbox-unchanged is true) understates the credentialed quota/connection cost, encouraging free repeated calls. Real but low-severity hinting/quota concern.

findings.title: MCP error mapping collapses provider-unavailable and gate-violation to generic RuntimeError, losing the HTTP layer's status semantics and is-retriable signal
findings.evidence: _triage re-raises both ProviderUnavailable (line 139) and AuditInvariantError (line 142) as bare RuntimeError. HTTP path maps ProviderUnavailable→503 (retriable) and AuditInvariantError→500 (api/app.py:108-122), preserving distinct status/severity. FastMCP surfaces any tool exception as a generic isError, so the agent loses the retriable-vs-do-not-retry distinction. (The critical gate message text IS preserved in the AuditInvariantError RuntimeError, line 143-144.)
findings.rationale: Real: the two error classes collapse to one type with no machine-distinguishable retriability signal on the MCP surface. Minor agent-UX/contract gap — low.

findings.title: base.apply_actions wraps the entire per-message body in one try/except, so a failure in apply_label aborts remove/archive/star for that message but still counts it as one processed
findings.evidence: base.py:340-367 wraps ensure_label_exists/apply_label/remove_label/archive/star/apply_category in one try/except. A raise mid-body skips the remaining ops, increments error_count (366), and the audit.record at 383-390 still fires with partial applied_labels/did_leave_inbox state. This is real. However the partial-audit risk is bounded: applied_labels only contains labels whose apply_label() actually returned True before the raise (line 344-346), and did_leave_inbox/did_label_move are likewise set only from operations that completed — so the audit reflects what genuinely ran, not phantom operations. The 'recording a message as labeled/moved when the operation actually errored' wording overstates it: it records the ops that succeeded before a LATER op raised.
findings.rationale: Genuine broad-try/except partial-failure pattern, but the audit captures only completed side effects, so it is not the unreliable phantom-record described; severity correctly low.

findings.location: apply_actions lines 356-363 (star/category for protected senders on folder providers)
findings.title: Protected-sender gate suppresses label-moves but still applies star and category to protected mail
findings.evidence: For protected senders, move_via_label=True (base.py:334) skips add_labels (341), but base.py:356-363 still call self.star() and self.apply_category() unconditionally. On Outlook (folder, LABEL_IS_MOVE=True), star() is a PATCH adding flagStatus (outlook.py:557-587, no /move) and apply_category() is a PATCH adding a categories array (outlook.py:323-362, no /move). Neither ejects from inbox, so the never-archive safety invariant holds — but a protected sender the user may treat as do-not-touch still gets flagged/categorized, contradicting the 'neutralize every out-of-inbox operation' framing only at the policy level, not the safety level.
findings.rationale: Code confirms star/category run for protected senders; correctly scoped as a policy/UX decision, not a safety-invariant breach — low severity stands.

findings.title: list_messages caps maxResults at `limit` but caller paginates expecting full pages, conflating per-page limit with total limit
findings.evidence: Line 144 `page_size = min(limit, LIST_PAGE_SIZE)` passes maxResults (a per-page cap) as the value of `limit`, while base.py documents limit as 'Maximum messages to return per page' (base.py:126). The CLI (cli.py) loops on next_page_token so the two interpretations align in practice, but a single-call caller passing limit>500 gets only one page.
findings.rationale: Confirmed API-contract ambiguity; benign for the actual CLI caller, low severity.

findings.title: Backoff has no jitter and a high 10s base, compounding to ~310s worst case while holding the call serialized
findings.evidence: BASE_BACKOFF_SECONDS=10 (line 33), delay*=2 (line 124) over max_retries=5 gives 10+20+40+80+160=310s of synchronous time.sleep (line 123). No jitter, no Retry-After parsing, and only 403/429+tag are retried — grep for 'jitter|Retry-After|backendError|503|random' in gmail.py returns nothing, so 503/500 are re-raised immediately.
findings.rationale: Confirmed resilience gap: long jitterless serialized backoff, no transient-5xx retry; low/medium.

findings.title: O(n*m) reverse label lookup per message and silent drop of labels not in cache
findings.evidence: Same nested loop as U473 (perf), PLUS the correctness angle: line 198-202 only adds names found in _label_cache; any label id not in the cache (label created post-connect by another client, or system labels like CATEGORY_PERSONAL/IMPORTANT never pre-cached — _init_label_cache caches whatever labels().list returns) is silently dropped, so msg.labels under-reports.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the silent-drop-of-uncached-labels correctness angle is distinct from the pure-perf U473; low/medium.

findings.title: Unconditional time.sleep(2.0) after every batch of 20 makes large fetches very slow and is not configurable
findings.evidence: Line 249 `time.sleep(2.0)  # Throttle between batches` runs unconditionally after every BATCH_GET_SIZE=20 chunk regardless of rate-limit state. 1000 messages = 50 chunks = ~100s pure sleep. Hardcoded, not tied to observed throttling; combined with the 0.5s per-chunk sleep at line 475.
findings.rationale: Confirmed hardcoded unconditional throttle; perf smell, low severity.

findings.title: connect() with injected service sets _connected but never initializes label cache, leaving _label_cache empty
findings.evidence: connect() lines 82-84: when `self._service is not None`, it sets _connected=True and returns early WITHOUT calling _init_label_cache() (only the OAuth path at line 89 calls it). So an injected service leaves _label_cache empty: apply_label treats every label as missing and creates it, remove_label falls back to raw names, reverse-lookup returns nothing.
findings.rationale: Confirmed divergence between injected-service and OAuth paths; primarily a test-path latent issue, low severity.

findings.title: Rate-limit detection by string-matching the exception message is brittle and may mask non-rate-limit 403s as fatal or vice versa
findings.evidence: Lines 111-117: rate_limited requires status in (403,429) AND substring of 'rateLimitExceeded'/'userRateLimitExceeded'/'quotaExceeded' in str(e). 503/500 transient errors are not retried (re-raised at 126). Matches on rendered message string rather than the structured errors[].reason. Same backoff family as U628.
findings.rationale: Confirmed brittle string-match detection plus no transient-5xx retry; low/medium.

findings.title: remove_label falls back to using raw label name as label ID when not cached
findings.evidence: Same `self._label_cache.get(label) or label` fallback at lines 317 and 413 already covered by U369. (Note: for a non-matching removeLabelIds id Gmail returns 400 caught as failure, not a silent success, so the 'silently fail / returns True' framing is partly inaccurate.)
findings.rationale: Identical root cause to U369.

findings.location: apply_actions audit loop, lines 434 (pending keyed by message_id) and 482-495
findings.title: Duplicate message_ids in the action list collide in the pending dict, losing/mis-recording audit receipts
findings.evidence: Same pending-dict-keyed-by-message_id collision as U373 (line 434 write, 483-495 read). The duplicate-receipts angle is the same defect viewed from the read side.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U373.

findings.title: apply_label resolves a stale/missing label id and never re-validates the cache after ensure creates it
findings.evidence: The 'new angle' is the asymmetry between apply_label (resolves via ensure_label_exists at 297-299, always gets a real id) and remove_label (raw-name fallback at 317). The defect being reported is still the remove_label fallback covered by U369; apply_label itself is confirmed correct here.
findings.rationale: The actionable defect is the same remove_label fallback as U369; the asymmetry framing adds no new bug.

findings.title: 403 non-rate-limit errors (insufficientPermissions/forbidden) are only retried if message text matches, else immediately raised — but a 403 quota error whose body lacks the exact tag is treated as fatal
findings.evidence: Lines 111-117 confirm the tag set is exactly {rateLimitExceeded, userRateLimitExceeded, quotaExceeded}. A 403 'dailyLimitExceeded' (a rate condition) does NOT contain any of these substrings, so it falls through to re-raise (line 126) as fatal rather than backing off. This is a concrete instance within the U632 string-match family.
findings.rationale: Real concrete sub-case of U632's string-match brittleness (dailyLimitExceeded treated as fatal); overlaps U632 but adds a specific reason tag — partial/low.

findings.title: Rate-limit backoff sleeps up to 10+20+40+80+160s synchronously, blocking the request thread with no jitter or cap
findings.evidence: Same 310s synchronous time.sleep total as U628 (lines 104,123-124,33). The added angle — running on FastAPI's threadpool and exhausting workers — is plausible but needs the API service code; the static fact (long synchronous jitterless sleep with no overall deadline) is confirmed.
findings.rationale: Confirmed synchronous blocking backoff; threadpool-exhaustion impact is the same backoff-family root as U628, low severity.

findings.title: COPY-to-folder does not verify ensure_label_exists succeeded; create('NO') swallowed so COPY to nonexistent folder fails silently-ish
findings.evidence: ensure_label_exists (352-355) treats both 'OK' and 'NO' from create() as success and caches into _created_folders. A genuine CREATE failure (invalid name/permission) returning NO is cached as created, so a retry short-circuits at line 348-349 and never re-attempts; the subsequent COPY (288) returns NO → apply_label returns False. The mis-classification of NO is real, though 'NO' most commonly does mean 'already exists'.
findings.rationale: Real NO-as-success caching defect; low impact (apply_label still returns False on the failed COPY).

findings.title: Decoded header parts joined with space can corrupt RFC2047 multi-encoded-word values and ignores decode errors silently
findings.evidence: Tested: truly adjacent encoded-words (no separator) are returned by decode_header as a SINGLE part, so ' '.join inserts no spurious space in that case — the headline claim is not reproduced. The residual real issues: ' '.join can mis-join an encoded-word part adjacent to a plain-text part, and errors='ignore' (line 34) silently drops undecodable bytes.
findings.rationale: Main 'spurious space between adjacent encoded words' claim is wrong per decode_header behavior; only minor residual — low.

findings.title: IMAP pagination slice math can return overlapping or out-of-order pages and uses reverse-from-end indexing that breaks when total < limit
findings.evidence: Same negative-stop-index pagination defect as U376. Additionally notes the doc/comment mismatch: line 193 comment says 'most recent first (reverse order)' but the tail slice returns ascending UIDs within the window (not reversed) — minor doc bug, confirmed by reading 193-194.
findings.rationale: Pagination root cause duplicates U376; the ordering-comment nit is real but cosmetic.

findings.title: IMAP connection leaked if login() fails
findings.evidence: Lines 139-140: self._connection is assigned the IMAP4_SSL object, THEN login() is called. If login raises, _connection stays set with an open socket and the exception propagates. disconnect() (143-153) does guard `if self._connection` and will logout, so the `with` path (base.py:82-87) recovers; direct callers that don't use the context manager and don't call disconnect leak the socket.
findings.rationale: Real but narrow leak (only non-context-manager direct use); context-manager path self-heals — low.

findings.title: IMAP self._connection used without None-check (AttributeError if not connected)
findings.evidence: _connection defaults to None (line 89) and is set only in connect(). Calling a method before connect() raises AttributeError on .uid/.select rather than a clear 'not connected'. However base.health_check (base.py:409-413) wraps list_messages in try/except and returns (False, str(e)), and the CLI always uses `with provider:` (base.py:82 calls connect). So no real crash path in current usage.
findings.rationale: Edge-case robustness only; no reachable crash given context-manager + try/except — low.

findings.title: ensure_label_exists swallows mailbox-create failure but still caches the name and returns it, so a later move silently targets a nonexistent mailbox
findings.evidence: Finding itself notes RuntimeError path does NOT add to _created_mailboxes (correct — add at line 350 is before any return, inside try, so a raise skips it). On success the add is unconditional (line 350). apply_label move failure is caught at 275 returning False; message stays in inbox.
findings.rationale: Largely self-refuting on the static claim; the residual 'create-failure masked from result counts, message left in inbox' is real but low impact (inbox-safe by design, as the finding concedes).

findings.title: Per-call limit not enforced when paginating via @odata.nextLink; can over-fetch beyond requested limit
findings.evidence: When page_token is set, lines 417-420 force params=None, so the caller's smaller `limit` is ignored on every continuation; the nextLink carries Graph's original $top. cli.py:185 shrinks batch_limit=min(limit-processed,100) but the while-guard `processed_this_run < limit` (183) and `processed_this_run += len(actions)` (293) only bound the run loosely — a single page can return more than limit-processed, overshooting --limit by up to (page_size-1).
findings.rationale: Accurately describes real over-processing on the page_token path; minor, bounded by next loop iteration; low severity confirmed.

findings.title: Category create-conflict fallback re-fetches but still raises generic RuntimeError, and masks non-conflict errors as 'race'
findings.evidence: Lines 314-321: any _api_post failure (auth/throttle/invalid-color/network) is caught, logged at DEBUG (316), assumed a race, cache re-inited (318), and on still-miss raises RuntimeError without chaining `from e` (321). The original cause is only at debug level.
findings.rationale: Real diagnosability gap and over-broad 'race' assumption with no backoff on 429; metadata path only — low. Closely related to U650/U850 but distinct emphasis.

findings.title: Folder-name resolution depends on a cache that is empty when connect() was skipped or folder caching failed; labels silently missing
findings.evidence: Lines 494-500 resolve parentFolderId by linear scan of self._folder_cache. _init_folder_cache (252-262) swallows failures leaving the cache empty/partial; _fetch_child_folders only fetches $top=100 per parent (268) with no nextLink follow, so folders beyond 100 are absent. A miss leaves labels empty silently.
findings.rationale: Genuine correctness gap for folder/label state when cache is incomplete; read-side only, low impact.

findings.title: Corrupt/invalid token cache file aborts auth with an unhandled exception
findings.evidence: Lines 152-154: `with open(...) as f: cache.deserialize(f.read())` has no try/except. _save_token_cache (167-168) writes non-atomically (open 'w' + write), so an interrupted write yields truncated JSON; deserialize then raises and propagates through _acquire_token → connect, aborting the run with no fall-back-to-fresh-cache.
findings.rationale: Real crash path with a real non-atomic-write enabler; rare (requires interrupted write/corruption), recoverable by deleting the cache — low severity.

findings.title: Due-date formatting truncates time to midnight and forces UTC, mis-stating the due date across timezones
findings.evidence: Line 574 `due_str = due_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z')` hard-codes midnight and a 'Z' literal regardless of due_date.tzinfo or time, then sets timeZone='UTC' (577). core/models.py declares due_date: Optional[datetime] with no tz normalization, so a tz-aware local datetime is rendered as its naive Y-M-D at 00:00 UTC, shifting the To Do due date by up to a day.
findings.rationale: Genuine date/tz bug; affects only the optional To Do due-date display, cosmetic-ish — low severity confirmed.

findings.title: Pagination via @odata.nextLink loses $orderby/$select consistency only if server omits them, but more importantly limit/$top is silently dropped on every subsequent page
findings.evidence: Same defect as U644: params forced to None on the page_token path (417-420) so the caller's limit is structurally un-enforceable on any continuation page. Identical root cause and same lines.
findings.rationale: Restates U644's limit-not-enforced-on-pagination finding with different emphasis; no new code defect.

findings.title: ensure_category_exists ignores the requested color on the already-exists path, so tier color is silently wrong when category pre-exists
findings.evidence: Lines 298-299: if name in self._category_cache, return cached id immediately — no color check. apply_category (341) passes the tier color, but a pre-existing master category with a stale color is never reconciled (no PATCH/update path on masterCategories anywhere in the file).
findings.rationale: Real cosmetic correctness gap (Eisenhower color coding can be wrong if category pre-exists with another color); visual-only — low.

findings.title: Unbounded recursive child-folder fetch with no depth guard or visited-set
findings.evidence: Lines 264-274 recurse via self._fetch_child_folders(folder['id'], full_name) (272) with no depth guard, no visited set, one HTTP call per folder, and $top=100 (268) with no nextLink follow (folders >100 per parent silently dropped). Runs on every connect() (259).
findings.rationale: Real connect-time fan-out / no-depth-guard / 100-child truncation; mailbox folder trees are finite/shallow in practice, so low severity, but the missing-folders-beyond-100 piece is a genuine correctness gap.

findings.title: acquire_token_interactive on a headless/hosted server blocks forever (opens a local browser) instead of failing fast
findings.evidence: Lines 184-187 fall back to app.acquire_token_interactive(prompt='select_account') when no cached account. api/service.py:100 calls prov.connect() server-side; app.py:111 maps a raised ProviderUnavailable to 503, but an interactive flow that blocks (browser/local redirect server) does not raise — it stalls the worker thread.
findings.rationale: Accurate: on a headless server the interactive fallback can hang rather than fail fast; gated behind 'no cached token' so it only bites unconfigured/expired-cache server deployments — low.

findings.location: lines 3-7 (httpx>=0.27 with comment "required by fastapi.testclient ... and for tests")
findings.title: httpx is declared as a runtime dependency but is only used by the test suite (TestClient), bloating the production image
findings.evidence: Same root-cause as U476: httpx is test-only (TestClient) but installed via requirements-api.txt into the prod image. The claim that outbound calls go through `requests` is confirmed (providers/outlook.py is the only `requests` importer; httpx unused in production). U476 makes the identical point with higher self-rated confidence.
findings.rationale: Identical defect to U476 (httpx test-only dep shipped to prod image); collapse into one.

findings.location: header comment lines 1-6 ("Optional, ISOLATED") vs CI/Dockerfile which always install it
findings.title: `mcp` is framed as "Optional" but is mandatory for the advertised `/mcp` product surface on the deployed (3.11) image
findings.evidence: Header lines 1-3 say "Optional, ISOLATED from the core requirements." Dockerfile (FROM python:3.11-slim) installs requirements-mcp.txt; ci.yml:30 installs it; api/app.py:12 advertises `/mcp` and mounts it at app.py:141-143. The try/except at app.py:39-45 degrades with `logger.info("MCP server not mounted (%s)", e)`. So on the 3.11 deploy target, mcp is effectively required for the advertised /mcp surface.
findings.rationale: Accurate documentation-nuance observation (the 'optional' label is relative to the 3.9 core engine, not the 3.11 deploy), but the lazy-import/degrade is intentional design, not a code defect — not actionable as a bug.

findings.location: lines 1-15 (whole file: no upper bounds on google-*/requests/pyyaml; floors as low as >=0.5.0)
findings.title: All core deps have unbounded version ceilings and very low floors (google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0), giving non-reproducible installs and major-version drift
findings.evidence: Same underlying defect as U228: unpinned floors + no lockfile -> non-reproducible installs. Adds the google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0 4-year-old-floor angle and the 0.x->1.x breaking-change note, but the root cause (no ceilings / no lockfile) is identical to U228. Confirmed: requirements.txt:2-4,8,11 floor-only, no constraints/lock file on disk.
findings.rationale: Distinct framing but same root-cause defect (unbounded floors, no lockfile) already adjudicated in U228.

findings.location: lines 13-15 (mypy/types-requests commented out) vs .github/workflows/ci.yml:31 (pip install ... mypy)
findings.title: requirements.txt comments out mypy/types-requests as dev deps, but CI installs an unpinned mypy and runs it with no stub packages — type-check is dependency-incomplete
findings.evidence: requirements.txt:14-15 comment out `mypy>=1.0.0` and `types-requests>=2.28.0`. CI ci.yml:31 does `pip install pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy` (unpinned, no stubs). grep for types-requests across .github/workflows/ returns nothing (exit 1). mypy invoked with `--ignore-missing-imports` (ci.yml:52) under `set +e` (ci.yml:51), so the type check is advisory-only and stub-incomplete.
findings.rationale: All claims verified: unpinned mypy, no types-requests install path, defanged advisory run. Real but the type-check is non-gating and the gap is low-impact tooling completeness.

findings.title: Move-during-iteration over live inbox messages skips messages (same Mail.app pattern)
findings.evidence: Line 23 `set inboxMessages to messages of inbox`; line 24 `repeat with msg in inboxMessages`; line 28 `move msg to bulkMailbox` inside the loop. Mutating the inbox collection (move-out) during a forward enumeration over a live `messages of inbox` reference is the classic AppleScript Mail.app index-shift hazard that skips subsequent messages; failures are also swallowed by the bare `try` (lines 25-30, no `on error`).
findings.rationale: Confirmed pattern: moving messages out of the inbox while iterating the live inbox collection skips messages; low severity since it under-routes rather than corrupts.

findings.title: Resolving Newsletters mailbox is unguarded and can abort the whole script
findings.evidence: Lines 16-21 resolve `set bulkMailbox to mailbox bulkMailboxName` (and the account variant) with no enclosing `try`/`on error` (the only `try` in the file is lines 25-30 inside the loop). Line 3 comment: 'create this mailbox in Mail first'. If the 'Newsletters' mailbox does not exist, the `mailbox bulkMailboxName` reference raises an unhandled error and the script aborts with no graceful message.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: mailbox resolution is outside any error handler and the file warns the mailbox must pre-exist, so a missing mailbox aborts the run.

findings.title: PUBLIC_BASE_URL is not validated; an unset/typo env var bakes a placeholder host into committed machine-readable artifacts
findings.evidence: Line 28: BASE = os.environ.get("PUBLIC_BASE_URL", "https://mail.example.com").rstrip("/") — no validation/warning if unset. {BASE}/mcp is baked into server.json (line 91) and consumed by build_llms_txt/build_agent_manifest. BUT lines 26-27 explicitly document the placeholder as intentional ('real URLs are resolved live by the served routes... The committed files are declarations'), and DEPLOY.md:79 documents passing PUBLIC_BASE_URL.
findings.rationale: Real (no guard on the env default) but overstated: the placeholder is a deliberate, documented design choice and outputs are git-tracked artifacts reviewed before commit, so an accidental example.com publish surfaces as a visible diff.

findings.title: Credit-pack price rendered via float division of cents (amount_cents / 100) — float-rounding idiom on the published price page
findings.evidence: Line 62-63: f"${pk['amount_cents'] / 100:.2f}". The only CREDIT_PACKS values are amount_cents=100 and amount_cents=900 (api/plans.py:144-147), both rendering exactly ($1.00 / $9.00). No value in the current catalog is misformatted; the rounding concern is hypothetical for future/added amounts.
findings.rationale: The float-division-for-money idiom is genuinely present, but the actual blast radius is zero at every current value and the output is a reviewed-before-commit markdown page; severity inflated from low to info.

findings.title: Org name in seed.yaml (labores-profani-crux) disagrees with actual git remote (a-organvm)
findings.evidence: seed.yaml:1 comment and seed.yaml:8 `org: labores-profani-crux`. git remote -v => https://github.com/a-organvm/universal-mail--automation.git. server.json:3 => "io.github.a-organvm/universal-mail-automation". CLAUDE.md:320 => Org: `organvm-iii-ergon`. Three distinct org identifiers confirmed on disk.
findings.rationale: The factual drift across the four surfaces is real and corroborated; severity stays low because it is metadata/governance drift, not a code defect, and no in-repo consumer of seed.yaml's org field was found to actually misroute (that impact claim is speculative).

findings.location: lines 14-17 (produces: 'Email workflow automation and scheduling' artifact; consumes: [])
findings.title: seed.yaml produces/consumes does not describe the implemented commerce product (subscriptions, ACP credit packs, MCP)
findings.evidence: seed.yaml:14-17 declares a single generic artifact and `consumes: []`. On disk the commerce surfaces exist: api/billing.py (imports stripe), acp/router.py + acp/product_feed.json (ACP credit packs), mcp_server/server.py (MCP tool server). None are represented in the seed produces list.
findings.rationale: The under-described produces contract is a real, verifiable drift between seed.yaml and the implemented revenue-bearing surface; severity low because it is contract-completeness drift with no proven downstream breakage (no in-repo seed.yaml graph consumer found).

findings.title: Org identifier is inconsistent across surfaces: seed.yaml says 'labores-profani-crux', remote/server.json say 'a-organvm', CLAUDE.md says 'organvm-iii-ergon'
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U661: seed.yaml:8 org=labores-profani-crux vs git remote a-organvm vs server.json:3 io.github.a-organvm vs CLAUDE.md:320 organvm-iii-ergon. Same root-cause org-identifier inconsistency within the same file under review.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U661 (R1) re-reported in R3 with the same surfaces; merge into U661.

findings.title: MCP remote URL points to placeholder mail.example.com
findings.evidence: server.json:19 `"url": "https://mail.example.com/mcp"` is a literal example.com placeholder; the real live host is uma.4444j99.dev (README.md:89, wrangler.toml:6 custom_domain route). Generator scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py:28 defaults BASE to 'https://mail.example.com' and line 91 emits f'{BASE}/mcp', so the committed artifact bakes the placeholder unless PUBLIC_BASE_URL is set at gen time.
findings.rationale: Placeholder remote URL confirmed verbatim; same placeholder-host class as the .well-known/agent.json findings, a discovery/config-drift defect with no runtime crash, so low severity stands.

findings.title: Fixture's broad except binds _payment_mod=None and can skip payment-client reset, leaking state between tests
findings.evidence: tests/conftest.py L27-31: `try: from acp import payment as _payment_mod; _payment_mod.set_payment_client(None) except Exception: _payment_mod = None` — both import AND reset are in one try; L34-35 teardown is guarded by `if _payment_mod is not None`. But acp/payment.py L113-117 set_payment_client is just `global _CLIENT; _CLIENT = client` — it cannot raise. The setup call at L29 also clears any prior client before EVERY test (autouse), so a leak would be re-cleared next test.
findings.rationale: Code structure is exactly as described (real broad-except smell coupling import+reset and masking import errors), but the claimed cross-test state leak/order-dependence is unreachable with the current non-raising set_payment_client — it is purely refactor-contingent, and the autouse setup reset further nullifies any leak.

findings.title: Test fake payment client ignores idempotency_key, so the per-session charge-dedup claim is never actually exercised
findings.evidence: _OKPay.charge (26-27) ignores idempotency_key. test_complete_is_idempotent (141-153) relies on the status guard + fulfill_once. BUT router.py:248-251 returns early when status==COMPLETED BEFORE reaching charge() — so on a second /complete the real code never re-calls charge anyway; the per-session Stripe key (router.py:265) is crash-recovery belt-and-suspenders, not the primary dedup. The charge-layer key is genuinely never exercised, but its blast radius is smaller than implied.
findings.rationale: Factually true that charge-key dedup is untested, but overstated: the status guard short-circuits before charge, so this is a minor secondary safety net, not the main double-charge protection.

findings.title: Gmail-override safety tests stub _execute_with_backoff/_drop_if_protected so the override path is partly mocked away
findings.evidence: test_audit.py:289-290 stub gp.ensure_label_exists = lambda label: label and gp._execute_with_backoff = lambda fn, _desc: fn(); line 333-336 do the same plus gp._drop_if_protected = lambda action: False. Production _execute_with_backoff (providers/gmail.py:97-126, real retry/backoff loop) and real ensure_label_exists (gmail.py:344-369) are indeed not exercised by THESE two tests. The stub signature (fn, _desc) matches the real 2-positional-arg signature, so the stub is faithful.
findings.rationale: Accurate coverage observation, but no production defect — the stubbing is necessary to avoid live API calls and the finding itself self-classifies as 'coverage note rather than a definite defect'; framing it as actionable overstates it, hence partial/info.

findings.title: Test asserts _independently_protected(12345) is False, locking in 'non-str silently swallowed' as correct
findings.evidence: core/rules.py:797 `if not sender or not sender.strip()` — 12345 is truthy so it reaches 12345.strip(), which raises AttributeError; core/audit.py:113-114 `except Exception: return False`. So _independently_protected(12345) returns False (fail-OPEN), while the gate fails CLOSED for blank/None (rules.py:798 returns True; audit.py:837? confirmed via _independently_protected('') is True at test line 437). The asymmetry the finding describes is real and the test (line 438) does cement it.
findings.rationale: Real, narrow fail-open asymmetry confirmed, but the contract types sender as str (gate is the real enforcement; audit.py docstring explicitly 'declines to assert a protection it cannot compute'); only triggers if a non-str sender reaches the helper, so low not actionable-high.

findings.title: Tests only exercise valid numeric/string inputs; no coverage for the int() crash or invalid provider/log_level
findings.evidence: core/config.py:273 `config.batch_size = int(os.getenv(f"{prefix}BATCH_SIZE"))` is unguarded — non-numeric env value raises ValueError; the only test (test_config.py:170) feeds clean "500". No validation of default_provider/log_level exists (config.py:188-191, 266-269 assign raw values; no checks), and tests only feed valid values. `custom_rules` is stored at config.py:249-250 but a repo-wide grep shows it is never read/consumed anywhere (only the dataclass field def, the assignment, and a YAML comment), confirming the '(it never does)' sub-claim; extra_priority_labels/extra_keep_in_inbox are likewise dead. No test asserts custom_rules is applied.
findings.rationale: Every sub-claim checks out against current source: unguarded int(), no provider/log_level validation, custom_rules populated-but-never-consumed, and happy-path-only coverage. Severity stays low — these are test-coverage gaps, not a live production crash.

findings.location: tests/test_mailapp.py lines 9-25 (only star+due_date test); providers/outlook.py:557-578; providers/mailapp.py:293-294
findings.title: No test exercises the one provider that actually consumes due_date (Outlook); the sole due_date star test targets Mail.app which ignores it
findings.evidence: Grep across tests/ shows the ONLY due_date-driving test is test_mailapp.py:19 (datetime(2026,6,1)). It targets MailAppProvider, whose star() at providers/mailapp.py:293-294 is documented 'due_date is ignored by Mail.app'. The sole provider that consumes due_date is Outlook (providers/outlook.py:572-578: due_str = due_date.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z") with timeZone:UTC), and grep confirms no test calls OutlookProvider.star() or instantiates it for star — so the strftime/dueDateTime formatting path has zero test coverage. The companion UTC defect is real: strftime zeroes the time to T00:00:00 and ignores the original time/tzinfo, then hardcodes timeZone:UTC, so a naive datetime is silently stamped midnight-UTC.
findings.rationale: Coverage gap is real and exactly as described: the one due_date test exercises a provider that ignores due_date, leaving the only consuming provider (Outlook) untested. Test-quality, low blast radius.

findings.title: MCP destructive/readonly hint assertions silently skip on the Python 3.9 CI floor
findings.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 sets `python-version: ["3.11", "3.12"]` (no 3.9 lane), and line 30 runs `pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt` (requirements-mcp.txt contains `mcp>=1.2,<2`). So `mcp` is installed in every CI lane and `pytest.importorskip("mcp")` at test_mcp.py:11 does NOT skip; the safety assertions at lines 39-41 (readOnlyHint/destructiveHint) and 34 (outputSchema) execute on every CI run. mcp_server/server.py:87/97/109 confirm the annotations being asserted exist.
findings.rationale: The claimed '3.9 floor that silently skips the safety assertions' does not exist in CI — the matrix is 3.11/3.12 and CI explicitly installs the mcp SDK, so these assertions run every build; the 3.9 mention is only a docstring rationale for dep isolation, not a CI lane.

findings.title: merge tests normalize with set()/sorted(), so they cannot detect order loss or the List-vs-Set contract violation
findings.evidence: merge() at core/models.py:100 does add_labels=list(set(self.add_labels + other.add_labels)) — set() loses insertion order. test_merge_combines_labels asserts set(merged.add_labels) == {'A','B'} (test_models.py:101) and test_merge_deduplicates asserts sorted(merged.add_labels) == ['A','B','C'] (line 108). Both normalize with set()/sorted() before comparing, so they cannot detect the order-loss in the List[str] contract.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the assertions are invariant under reordering, so they assert nothing about the declared ordered-list semantics and cannot fail on the order-loss defect.

findings.location: missing coverage for merge() message_id / sender
findings.title: No test covers merge() mismatched message_id or the sender coalescing rule
findings.evidence: TestLabelAction (test_models.py:74-127) contains no test passing two actions with different message_id (merge docstring 'same message_id assumed', core/models.py:96 — unguarded invariant) and no test for sender=self.sender or other.sender coalescing (core/models.py:99). The LabelAction docstring (lines 71-74) ties sender to a fail-closed protected-sender gate, making the coalescing security-relevant yet untested.
findings.rationale: Verified absence: both the mismatched-message_id path and the security-relevant sender coalescing rule are public-method behaviors with zero test coverage.

findings.title: No test for state-file write failure or concurrent/partial-write corruption
findings.evidence: core/state.py:91-95 — `try: with open(self.filename, 'w') as f: json.dump(self.state, f, indent=2) except Exception as e: logger.error(...)` swallows every exception, returns None (signature `-> None`, line 74), surfaces nothing to the caller. The open mode 'w' truncates the file before json.dump runs, so a crash mid-dump leaves a partial/unparseable file (non-atomic). The save tests (test_state.py:38-71) only assert happy-path writes; no test covers an unwritable path or a truncated/partial write.
findings.rationale: Claims about save()'s exception-swallowing and non-atomic truncating write match core/state.py exactly, and no save-failure/atomicity test exists; legitimate coverage gap. Independent root cause from U478 (save-side vs load-side), so not a duplicate; severity 'low' is honest for a test-quality finding.

findings.title: Partial-update test never exercises subscription_id, leaving the stripe_subscription_id no-clobber path uncovered
findings.evidence: set_subscription (store.py:191-221) accepts subscription_id and conditionally writes stripe_subscription_id (store.py:208). The test at lines 24-32 sets customer_id/plan/status/current_period_end and asserts plan/stripe_customer_id/status/current_period_end survive a partial update, but never passes subscription_id and never asserts got['stripe_subscription_id']. So the no-clobber contract is unverified for exactly that column.
findings.rationale: Verbatim true: one of the five no-clobber columns (stripe_subscription_id) is never exercised by the test that exists to prove the no-clobber guarantee; a regression dropping it on a partial event would pass.

findings.title: Stale-processing reclaim test reaches into private _conn and sets created_at=0 — brittle and only covers the extreme stale case
findings.evidence: The test (line 95) does s._conn.execute("UPDATE idempotency_keys SET created_at = 0 ..."), reaching into the private connection and using created_at=0 (epoch, ~55y ago). The timeout logic at store.py:337 is `_now() - existing['created_at'] > IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT` with TIMEOUT=60.0 (store.py:103). No test exercises the now-59 vs now-61 boundary, so an off-by-sign/units bug in that comparison would escape, and coupling to s._conn ties the test to the private schema.
findings.rationale: Accurate: the reclaim test only proves the extreme far-past case and reaches into private state, leaving the actual 60s timeout boundary uncovered — a real but low-severity boundary/brittleness gap.

findings.title: No Content-Security-Policy and an overridable global API base, amplifying the innerHTML sinks
findings.evidence: No <meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy"> in the file; multiple innerHTML insertions (816,818,843,858,862,911,941); API_BASE is a window-global override (line 677). worker.mjs sets COMMON_HEADERS but no CSP/Content-Security-Policy is present (grep finds none). So the page ships with no CSP and an overridable request origin.
findings.rationale: Factually accurate defense-in-depth gap, but downgraded to info: it only amplifies the other sinks, all of which are themselves blocked by escaping/int-coercion/same-origin on the shipped surfaces.

findings.title: Limit input max=1000 is not enforced before sending to the unauthenticated preview endpoint
findings.evidence: Line 838 sends `parseInt($("limit").value,10) || 50` with no clamp to the max=1000 declared at line 594 — confirmed no client-side cap. But the server enforces the real bound: schemas.py TriageRequest.limit = Field(..., ge=1, le=MAX_TRIAGE_LIMIT=1000) (schemas.py:16,47), so an over-large value is rejected (422) by FastAPI, and worker.mjs caps internally (triagePreview Math.* on Number(limit)). The client offers no mitigation but the server is the authoritative gate.
findings.rationale: Real that the HTML max is advisory and unclamped, but the unmetered-cost concern is mitigated server-side by the le=1000 schema bound; client-only nit, info severity.

findings.title: Email validation regex is trivially permissive
findings.evidence: Line 882: `if (!email || !/.+@.+\..+/.test(email))`. This regex is trivially permissive (accepts 'x@y.z', and HTML-ish junk). The email then posts to /v1/billing/checkout (line 887). The finding itself notes the address is escaped where rendered, so it is a data-quality issue, not XSS.
findings.rationale: Accurate but minor: it is a known-weak client-side email check; the real validation/normalization belongs server-side at Stripe customer creation. Info severity.

findings.title: postJSON collapses all transport and JSON-parse errors to a single indistinguishable {ok:false,status:0} / {data:null}
findings.evidence: postJSON returns {ok:false,status:0,data:null} on fetch throw (line 791) and sets data=null on JSON parse failure (line 788), collapsing network-down, CORS-blocked, non-JSON-200, and real-503 into indistinguishable states. The preview handler treats `status===503 || !ok || !data` identically (line 841) and shows 'Static share mode' for all of them, so a transient network blip is misreported as static mode.
findings.rationale: Verified: error states are conflated and a real backend failure is masked behind the benign 'static share mode' notice; low UX/observability defect, no security impact.

findings.title: wranglerVersion pinned only to major "4" — non-reproducible builds, supply-chain drift
findings.evidence: ci.yml:102 `uses: cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4` (floating major tag) and ci.yml:105 `wranglerVersion: "4"` (floating major). Both can change between identical commits; the step holds CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN (env line 84) and deploys to production.
findings.rationale: Accurate hardening observation. Low/info-grade: floating major tags are extremely common practice; the security upside of SHA-pinning is real but the realistic risk is low. Overlaps U682 (which covers all actions including this one).

findings.title: Third-party actions pinned to mutable major tags instead of commit SHAs
findings.evidence: ci.yml:19 actions/checkout@v4, :22 actions/setup-python@v5, :71 codecov/codecov-action@v4, :88 actions/checkout@v4, plus :102 cloudflare/wrangler-action@v4 — all mutable major-tag pins. The cloudflare-share-deploy job has CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN in env (line 84).
findings.rationale: Correct: no SHA pinning anywhere. Standard supply-chain hardening gap; severity low (theoretical re-tag attack). Subsumes U681's wrangler-action point but is a broader distinct finding.

findings.title: pip cache keyed on default lockfiles may miss requirements-api/mcp changes
findings.evidence: ci.yml:23-25 setup-python uses `cache: 'pip'` with no `cache-dependency-path`. The install step (ci.yml:30) installs requirements.txt + requirements-api.txt + requirements-mcp.txt. setup-python's default pip cache hashing resolves requirements*.txt globs in some versions, but the key is not explicitly scoped to all three files.
findings.rationale: Plausible config nit. Note: actions/setup-python's default pip cache-dependency hashing actually globs `requirements*.txt`/`*.lock` style patterns, so all three MAY be hashed — the precise behavior is version-dependent. Real-but-low; the worst case is a stale-cache install, not incorrect dep resolution (pip still resolves against the files). Severity low.

findings.location: lines 16-21 (agentic_commerce.spec_version "2026-04-17") and product_feed.json line 1 ("version": "2026-04-17") vs acp/payment.py STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION line 28 ("2026-04-22.preview")
findings.title: Advertised ACP spec_version (2026-04-17) trails the Stripe SPT preview version the charge path actually requires (2026-04-22.preview)
findings.evidence: agent.json:17 `"spec_version": "2026-04-17"` and product_feed.json:2 `"version": "2026-04-17"` match acp/__init__.py:19 API_VERSION (enforced exactly by acp/router.py:74 `_gate`). acp/payment.py:28 `STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION = "2026-04-22.preview"` is used only at payment.py:86 as the server-side `stripe_version` option on the internal PaymentIntent create. The two version numbers are real and distinct.
findings.rationale: Both facts verified, but the harm theory is overstated: the Stripe SPT preview version is an internal server-side header on a delegated-token PaymentIntent the agent never constructs; it is not part of the ACP 2026-04-17 checkout spec the manifest advertises (acp/__init__.py docstring explicitly separates the two axes), so no agent-buildable token shape depends on it. No public-surface conflation harm.

findings.location: lines 22-28 (api block + oauth_scopes: []) vs api/app.py triage/preview/audit endpoints which require no auth (and acp surfaces accepting any bearer)
findings.title: agent.json declares oauth_scopes [] and no auth requirement, faithfully mirroring an API that enforces no auth — the manifest normalizes the unauthenticated surface
findings.evidence: agent.json:28 `"oauth_scopes": []`. grep for `Depends|Bearer|Authorization|require_` in api/app.py returns nothing — /v1/triage (app.py:91), /v1/triage/preview (:85), /v1/senders/check (:79), GET /v1/audit/{run_id} require no auth. acp/router.py:80-83 `_gate` accepts ANY non-empty bearer without validating it against any store. The manifest accurately mirrors this unauthenticated contract.
findings.rationale: The finding concedes the manifest is 'technically consistent with the code'; agent.json correctly describes the real (unauthenticated) surface, so this is not a defect IN the JSON file but a restatement of a code-side auth concern that lives in api/app.py and acp/router.py — out of scope for this artifact.

findings.title: DEPLOY.md Fly.io instructions are generic and unverifiable; no fly.toml ships and secrets list is Gmail-only
findings.evidence: DEPLOY.md:29-33 Fly.io block sets only GMAIL_OAUTH_OP_REF/GMAIL_TOKEN_OP_REF, but lines 52-54 explicitly document all commerce/agent surfaces as fail-soft ('absent the relevant secret, the catalog/pure endpoints still work and the money/charge endpoints return a clean 503 billing is not configured'). The full secrets table is present in the SAME file at lines 56-65, and the Credentials section (35) and Commerce section (50-65) both follow the Fly.io block.
findings.rationale: Documented intended fail-soft behavior with the secrets table in the same doc; the 'no pointer back' claim misreads the file. Not a defect.

findings.location: line 19 (CMD uvicorn ...) relies on uvicorn CLI from requirements-api.txt
findings.title: Container start command `uvicorn api.app:app` has no dependency-presence guard; a requirements-api.txt install failure yields a non-obvious 'uvicorn: not found' rather than a build failure
findings.evidence: CMD (line 19) is `uvicorn api.app:app ...`; uvicorn comes only from requirements-api.txt (uvicorn[standard]>=0.29). There is no `python -c 'import uvicorn,fastapi,...'` smoke import in the build. However, lines 9-10 install all three requirements files in a SINGLE RUN layer — if requirements-api.txt resolution fails, the entire RUN fails and the build fails (pip is all-or-nothing per invocation). The 'partial success' scenario requires a future edit that splits the installs or reorders them; it is not reachable with the current single-RUN Dockerfile.
findings.rationale: The missing smoke-import guard is real, but the 'partially-successful pip layer yields a runnable image that can't start' premise does not hold for the current single-RUN install; overstated, downgraded to info.

findings.title: Currency case mismatch between feed ('USD') and checkout/charge ('usd')
findings.evidence: feed.py:50 `"currency": "USD"` (uppercase). router.py:39 `CURRENCY = "usd"`, used in build_session (143/152) and charge (263) and receipt (305). Two different casings across discovery vs transaction surfaces.
findings.rationale: Verified case inconsistency; harmless to Stripe (charge uses correct lowercase) but a strict cross-surface validator keyed on exact case sees two currencies. Low.

findings.location: build_feed line 48 (price) and line 47 (image_url) vs ACP product-feed spec
findings.title: Product feed emits integer-cents price and a non-image image_url, likely failing strict ACP feed validation
findings.evidence: Same price-unit claim as U105 (feed.py:48 integer cents under currency USD) plus the image_url-not-an-image claim already raised as U490 (feed.py:47). No new root-cause defect in this file.
findings.rationale: The price-unit half duplicates U105's root cause and the image_url half duplicates U490; consolidating under U105.

findings.location: feed() line 67-69 (JSONResponse, no Cache-Control) + build_feed reflects base_url
findings.title: Product feed reflects Host and is served with no Cache-Control, enabling cached poisoning of checkout_url/seller_url
findings.evidence: feed.py:67-69 returns plain `JSONResponse(...)` with no Cache-Control/no-store header set (verified by reading the handler). base_url reflection confirmed (same as U242). The persistent-poisoning escalation requires an actual path-keyed shared cache in front of the API, which is not present/configurable in this repo.
findings.rationale: The missing Cache-Control is real, but it only escalates U242 (same Host-reflection root cause) and the persistence claim is conditional on an unseen caching layer; overlaps U242 and is hypothetical, so partial/low.

findings.title: An update/create with an item whose id is valid but quantity drives subtotal has no per-line or grand-total overflow/sanity cap beyond Item.quantity<=1000; many distinct valid items multiply unboundedly
findings.evidence: Same claim as U243/U245: Item.quantity le=1000 (line 27), items unbounded (line 43), no max_items, no grand_total ceiling. Recommends adding max_items + grand_total cap — identical root cause to U243.
findings.rationale: Same unbounded-aggregate-charge defect already adjudicated as U243; the finding even self-rates low confidence.

findings.title: build_line_items indexes it['id'] with [] though Item guarantees id; but build_line_items is also called with raw model_dump dicts — a missing key would KeyError 500 instead of a clean invalid_items
findings.evidence: models.py:81 pack = packs.get(it['id']) uses subscript on it['id'], while line 85 uses it.get('quantity', 1). The function is typed List[dict] (line 70). Real callers (router.py:174,218) pass [i.model_dump() for i in body.items] of validated Item models, so 'id' is always present today; the KeyError->500 only occurs for a future/other caller passing a dict without 'id'.
findings.rationale: Genuine subscript-vs-.get inconsistency on a public List[dict] helper; harmless under current callers (Item guarantees id), so correctly low and theoretical.

findings.title: build_line_items indexes it['id'] and trusts pack['amount_cents']/['runs'] with bracket access; KeyError/TypeError on malformed input
findings.evidence: Restates U692 (it['id'] subscript KeyError) and adds pack['amount_cents']/pack['runs'] subscript (models.py:86,96) raising KeyError/TypeError if the CREDIT_PACKS catalog (plans.py:143-148) ever omits a field. Same none-handling/defensive-access root cause as U692.
findings.rationale: Same defensive-dict-access defect as U692, merely extended to the pack fields; not an independent bug.

findings.title: build_line_items reads it['id'] via dict access and it.get('quantity') without validating against the Pydantic-bounded model in the update path consistently
findings.evidence: Identical to U692: it['id'] subscript vs it.get('quantity') on a List[dict] helper; KeyError->500 instead of clean invalid_items for any future caller lacking 'id'. Recommends defensive .get('id').
findings.rationale: Verbatim restatement of U692's it['id'] KeyError concern; duplicate.

findings.title: Item quantity read via it.get('quantity',1) but Pydantic Item already bounds qty to 1..1000; raw dict path in router bypasses bound on update with current line_items reuse
findings.evidence: router.py:222-224: when body.items is None on update, line_items=current['line_items'] and total_runs=row['data']['total_runs'] are reused with no re-derivation against the catalog. router.py:257 charges amount=grand_total(current['line_items']) — trusting persisted subtotals verbatim. No re-validation against CREDIT_PACKS at charge time.
findings.rationale: Real: complete trusts persisted line_item subtotals without re-validating against the pack catalog. Only exploitable if a future code path or direct store write tampers with stored line_items, so low/theoretical.

findings.location: build_line_items line 85 (int(it.get('quantity',1))) on create path with raw model_dump dicts
findings.title: build_line_items re-derives qty via int(it.get('quantity',1)) ignoring the pydantic-validated value, so any non-Item caller bypasses the 1..1000 bound
findings.evidence: models.py:85 qty=int(it.get('quantity',1)) re-reads from the dict rather than trusting Item's validated bound (ge=1, le=1000, line 27). The function signature is List[dict] (line 70). A raw-dict caller with negative quantity yields negative subtotal/total (line 87) and negative grand_total/charge; the 1..1000 bound lives only on the Pydantic model, not in the money-computing function. Current callers (router.py:174,218) pass validated Item dumps, so the bound holds in practice.
findings.rationale: Accurate: the quantity bound is enforced only at the Pydantic layer, not where multiplication happens; negative/oversized qty only reachable by a non-Item caller, so correctly low and currently unreachable.

findings.title: All charge exceptions are collapsed to a generic 'charge failed' that the session reports as retriable, masking permanent (non-retriable) declines
findings.evidence: Same bare-except collapse as U247 (payment.py:89-91 returns generic 'charge failed' for every exception). router.py:267-280 reverts to ready_for_payment + 402, so the agent retries even on terminal declines (card_declined, consumed SPT). No retriable/terminal distinction.
findings.rationale: Same root cause and location as U247 (no error-category mapping on the bare except). Merged; the agent-retry-loop framing is the same underlying defect.

findings.title: No guard that amount > 0 before calling Stripe; a zero/empty line-item session reaching charge would error from Stripe rather than be caught early
findings.evidence: payment.py:74 passes int(amount) straight to PaymentIntent.create with no >0 guard. grand_total (models.py:100-101) sums line-item totals; an empty line_items session yields 0. In normal flow such a session is STATUS_NOT_READY and blocked at router.py:252, so amount is positive — but no defensive floor exists at the payment seam; a future zero-priced pack or state edit would send amount=0 and Stripe would reject it into the generic 'charge failed' loop.
findings.rationale: Confirmed missing precondition; only triggers under a hypothetical zero-amount-ready session that the router currently prevents. Severity low (defensive-only).

findings.title: Charge currency is passed verbatim from CURRENCY='usd' while the feed/receipt advertise 'USD' — no normalization, and amount is trusted to already be minor units with no unit assertion
findings.evidence: payment.py:74-75 forward int(amount) and currency verbatim with no minor-unit assertion or lowercase-code validation. Current callers are correct: router.CURRENCY='usd' (router.py:39) and grand_total returns cents (models.py:100-101). But feed.py:49 advertises 'USD' (uppercase) — confirmed mismatch in casing across the system, with no single normalized source at this boundary.
findings.rationale: Confirmed boundary trusts inputs entirely; harmless today (correct callers) but the 'usd' vs 'USD' inconsistency is real. Severity low (no current defect, future-caller hazard).

findings.title: If STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is empty-string (set but blank), NullPaymentClient is selected and every ACP completion silently fails closed with no operator signal
findings.evidence: payment.py:109 `StripeSPTPaymentClient(key) if key else NullPaymentClient()` — an empty-string STRIPE_SECRET_KEY ('') is falsy, so a blank-but-present env var silently selects NullPaymentClient, which returns ok=False 'payment is not configured' for every /complete (NullPaymentClient.charge, lines 55-56). router.py:267-280 surfaces this only as a per-request 402 — no startup/aggregate config signal. Combined with the module-level cache, the blank key is locked for the process lifetime.
findings.rationale: Confirmed empty-string-falsy fail-closed behavior with no operator signal. Severity low: fail-closed (no free credits), and ops would notice 100% 402s; framed as silent-config not money-loss.

findings.title: Charge passes currency unchanged but ACP currency constant is lowercase 'usd' while feed advertises 'USD' — no normalization before Stripe
findings.evidence: Same currency-normalization gap as U699: router.CURRENCY='usd' passed verbatim (router.py:39 -> payment.py:75) vs feed.py:49 'USD', no single normalized source. Also restates the no-amount>0 guard (overlaps U698). Confirmed but same root causes.
findings.rationale: Currency angle duplicates U699; amount angle duplicates U698. Merged into U699 (the currency-normalization finding it most closely matches).

findings.location: StripeSPTPaymentClient.charge line 74 (int(amount)) — no upper bound / no >0 guard before Stripe call
findings.title: Charge passes int(amount) to Stripe with no minimum or maximum bound, so a desynced/huge amount is sent verbatim
findings.evidence: payment.py:74 forwards int(amount) with no min/max bound. The >0 portion duplicates U698. The NEW angle (no upper ceiling) is real: the seam enforces no per-charge maximum, so a desynced/inflated line_items total flows to a real Stripe charge. However the '1,000,000-run / $9,000 ceiling' is enforced upstream in plans/models (the pack catalog bounds quantities), so a 'maliciously-inflated' total is not freely attacker-controlled at this seam.
findings.rationale: Partial: the missing upper-bound at the payment seam is real (defense-in-depth), but the threat is overstated — upstream pack validation already bounds the amount; the seam being 'the last place' is not the only place. Severity low.

findings.title: ACP sessions are never expired; STATUS_EXPIRED is defined and checked but no code ever transitions a session to expired, so a READY session with a stale price can be completed indefinitely
findings.evidence: Same defect as U499: STATUS_EXPIRED defined+checked (router.py:211-212) but never assigned; no TTL sweep or created_at age check; a stale-priced READY session is completable indefinitely because /complete charges grand_total(stored line_items) (257) rather than re-deriving from CREDIT_PACKS.
findings.rationale: Same never-expired-session defect as U499; the repricing tail overlaps U259.

findings.title: On update without items, total_runs is read from row['data']['total_runs'] but line_items kept from current['line_items']; if persisted shapes ever diverge the charge amount and credited runs desynchronize
findings.evidence: Identical to U266: on items-less update, line_items=current['line_items'] (response) and total_runs=row['data']['total_runs'] (data wrapper) are read from two persisted structures (router.py:223-224); written together by _persist so consistent today; charge later uses grand_total(line_items) while credits use total_runs.
findings.rationale: Same two-source-read latent desync already filed as U266.

findings.title: Auto-created account on /complete is plan='free' with no email/customer link; credited runs attach to an orphan account unreachable via billing
findings.evidence: Same defect as U258: create_account(api_key=ctx.api_key, plan='free') (router.py:289) yields an orphan account with no email/stripe_customer_id, unreachable via billing flows that key on account_id/customer_id (billing.py _resolve_account 208-219). If the bearer is lost, credits are stranded. allow-secret
findings.rationale: Same orphan-account-from-unvalidated-bearer root cause as U258.

findings.title: Idempotency replay can return a stored response of JSON null, and gate parses body before idempotency claim allowing partial side effects
findings.evidence: store.py:355-362 idempotency_complete is the ONLY writer that sets status='done', and it writes response_json=json.dumps(response) in the SAME UPDATE. Every router handler calls _complete_idempotency(ctx, resp) (router.py:122) with a real dict; no path passes None. Therefore a row with status='done' AND response_json NULL/'null' (which would deserialize to None and skip the `if replay is not None` branch) is unreachable. The body-validation-before-gate ordering observation is true but the finding itself calls it 'generally fine'.
findings.rationale: The harmful re-run scenario requires a state (done + null response) that no code path can produce; the benign ordering note is acknowledged as non-defect.

findings.title: Idempotent already-completed return does not re-shape order/permalink and can return a session without the order on a fresh Idempotency-Key
findings.evidence: router.py:248-251 returns `current` verbatim. `order = current.get('order')` (line 293); the 'already_fulfilled' info branch (323-330) only fires when fulfilled=False and order is None, which happens when a concurrent /complete in another request minted+persisted the order while THIS request's `current` snapshot predates it and fulfill_once returns False. That order-less completed response then becomes the polled canonical result, so the agent has no session-level pointer to its permalink (the receipt still exists in the ledger).
findings.rationale: Genuine narrow race/observability gap on the session->order pointer; receipt is not lost, so low severity.

findings.title: Bearer parsing does not strip the scheme case-insensitively and ignores leading whitespace nuances; malformed-but-intended tokens slip through as opaque keys
findings.evidence: router.py:80 `auth.startswith('Bearer ')` is case-sensitive and assumes exactly one space; the parsed tail (line 83) is used verbatim as the account identity key in complete_session (ctx.api_key -> get_account_by_api_key, line 287). 'bearer x' is rejected (401); 'Bearer  x' (.strip() at line 83) collapses to the same key as 'Bearer x'.
findings.rationale: Accurate: brittle scheme parsing on a header used as identity key, but self-consistent passthrough keeps impact low.

findings.title: On payment failure the buyer field can be silently dropped/overwritten and the session is re-persisted as READY without re-validating items
findings.evidence: router.py:273-280 re-shapes with status=READY using current['line_items'] and re-persists with the unchanged total_runs (line 278), with no re-derivation against plans.CREDIT_PACKS. The next /complete retry charges grand_total(current['line_items']) (line 257) and credits row['data']['total_runs'] (line 290) from the persisted values.
findings.rationale: Real: completion trusts the persisted session rather than re-resolving the catalog; low because CREDIT_PACKS is static.

findings.title: amount=0 (empty/zero-priced READY session) is charged and the response treated as fulfillable
findings.evidence: A charge only runs when status==STATUS_READY (router.py:252). READY requires valid=True. models.build_line_items sets valid=bool(items) and clears it for any unmatched id (models.py:79-84); plans.CREDIT_PACKS has only pack_100 (100c) and pack_1000 (900c) — no zero-cost pack (plans.py:143-148). An empty-items update yields valid=False -> NOT_READY (router.py:217-221). So a READY session always has non-empty line_items with amount>=100; grand_total can never be 0 at the charge site. The idempotency-key-poisoning consequence is gated behind an unreachable amount=0 precondition.
findings.rationale: amount=0 reaching the charge is statically unreachable given the catalog and the valid=bool(items) guard.

findings.title: update_session with items=None keeps line_items but re-reads total_runs from stored row, while complete recomputes amount from line_items — divergence risk if persisted shapes drift
