findings.evidence: router.py:223-224 reads line_items and total_runs as two independent stored values; complete charges grand_total(line_items) (257) but credits total_runs (290). However every write goes through _persist(session_id, resp, total_runs) (149-155) which always saves both together; no current code path mutates one without the other, and save_session is a single INSERT OR REPLACE. The desync requires a hypothetical future partial-save or external row tampering.
findings.rationale: Real structural fragility (no invariant binds charge-basis to credit-basis) but no active divergence in current code.

findings.title: Saved ACP session 'response' embeds links built from a per-request base_url, so a replay/get returns URLs from whichever Host first created the session
findings.evidence: _links (router.py:125-130) builds terms/privacy URLs from request.base_url; complete_session builds permalink_url from request.base_url (line 292,321). _shape embeds these and _persist saves the full response (line 154); get_session returns row['data']['response'] verbatim (line 197). well_known.py similarly trusts str(request.base_url) (the Host header). So a session created with a forged Host stores attacker-controlled URLs served to later readers.
findings.rationale: Real in-app Host-header trust amplification; blast radius depends on upstream proxy Host validation (not visible here), so low.

findings.location: complete_session lines 296-317 vs api/receipts.py get_receipt 125-131 + store.save_receipt 287
findings.title: ACP order-receipt summary contains an int amount/runs that round-trips through JSON; re-served signed_body may not re-verify if any value type coerces
findings.evidence: Signed body keys (router.py:297-311): run_id, provider, dry_run, summary, receipt_line. get_receipt reconstructs the SAME key set (receipts.py:125-131). summary round-trips json.dumps (store.py:287) / json.loads (store.py:296); dry_run signed as Python False (router.py:300) and reconstructed as bool(0)=False (store.py:297). All current values (ints, strs, bool) are JSON-stable, so _canonical(sort_keys) re-verification SUCCEEDS today. The finding concedes 'these match' and only posits failure for hypothetical future Decimal/None-vs-absent values.
findings.rationale: Round-trip mechanism is real but re-verifies correctly for all current value types; defect is speculative-future, not present.

findings.title: Payment-failed branch re-persists the session preserving the OLD total_runs/line_items without re-validating them against CREDIT_PACKS before the next retry
findings.evidence: Same defect as U709: the payment-failed branch (router.py:273-278) re-persists current['line_items'] and the unchanged total_runs without re-deriving against plans.CREDIT_PACKS, so a retry trusts the persisted session. Identical root cause and overlapping line range (273-280).
findings.rationale: Same root-cause and location as U709; no distinct new defect.

findings.title: current_period_end parsed with int() but no validation it is a sane epoch; partial events can leave period_end stale
findings.evidence: billing.py:297-299: `val = subscription.get('current_period_end'); if val: return int(val)` is NOT wrapped in try/except, unlike the item-level fallback at 300-303. A truthy non-numeric current_period_end (e.g. a string) raises ValueError that escapes _period_end into _handle_event and becomes a 500 (200-202).
findings.rationale: Confirmed asymmetry: top-level int() is unguarded while the fallback is guarded. Robustness gap on a malformed-but-signed payload; low blast radius.

findings.title: Broad except over stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError attribute access can itself raise AttributeError on some stripe SDK versions
findings.evidence: billing.py:183 references stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError. requirements-api.txt pins stripe>=15.2,<16. Stripe SDK not installed in this env (ModuleNotFoundError), so the v15 module layout cannot be runtime-verified here.
findings.rationale: stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError is the documented path on v15 (it is still exported for back-compat), so the AttributeError scenario is unlikely under the pin, but I cannot execute to confirm — needs_runtime against the installed SDK.

findings.title: status from Stripe passed straight into set_subscription; an unexpected/None status can leave a paid plan with no status update while plan stays paid
findings.evidence: billing.py:248 status=obj.get('status') verbatim; 253 only forces plan='free' for canceled/unpaid/incomplete_expired. A None status skips the status column (store.py:213) while the paid plan is still written -> paid plan with stale status. entitlements_for (plans.py:184-186) re-checks status, limiting impact.
findings.rationale: Real latent inconsistency; entitlement re-check keeps access safe. Same root cause as U507/U719 (None/verbatim status); kept as confirmed-low since it is the originating R1 observation.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status=active without re-checking subscription validity; period_end parsing swallows malformed values to None
findings.evidence: billing.py:265-270 invoice.paid sets status='active' unconditionally with no subscription re-check; webhooks are unordered so a late/duplicate invoice.paid can override a past_due set by subscription.updated. _period_end (302) swallows malformed values to None, and store.py:213 won't clear a stale period_end with None.
findings.rationale: Accurate out-of-order/override concern; entitlements re-check status live so access risk is bounded. Low.

findings.title: Subscription status written verbatim from Stripe without normalization; non-active statuses other than the canceled set leave paid plan in place
findings.evidence: billing.py:248-254: statuses like past_due/incomplete/paused are written verbatim with the resolved paid plan_id still set (only canceled/unpaid/incomplete_expired force free). plans.py:185 treats anything outside active/trialing as Free floor, so limits are safe but the stored plan label stays paid.
findings.rationale: Confirmed two-place encoding of entitlement; safe today via the live resolver, latent risk for any future plan-only reader. Duplicate-ish of U717 but distinct enough (non-None non-cancel statuses). Low.

findings.title: Checkout can create an orphan free account that is never linked to the resulting Stripe customer
findings.evidence: billing.py:119-133 create_account(plan='free') then uses id as client_reference_id/metadata; grant depends entirely on the webhook's checkout.session.completed (docstring 9, 'access-grant source of truth'). No idempotency on checkout.
findings.rationale: Same defect family as U427/U722 (orphan free account on the checkout path); confirmed but low. duplicate_of U427 by root cause but kept confirmed since it adds the missed-webhook angle.

findings.title: Unhandled DB exceptions from the event handler 500 and wedge the event into a permanent retry loop
findings.evidence: billing.py:194-203 marks processed only after a clean _handle_event return (203). tests/test_billing.py:88-130 confirms a handler failure is NOT marked and a redelivery re-grants. A deterministic failure (e.g. recurring UNIQUE collision) therefore 500s on every redelivery with no dead-letter handling.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: correct for transient failures, but no poison-event distinction. Low — Stripe retries are bounded in practice and the alternative (marking failed events done) would be worse.

findings.title: Orphan account created by webhook may be left without its customer link if set_subscription then fails, leaking unreferenced rows
findings.evidence: billing.py:218 create_account commits (store.py:158-164) and returns; the customer-link write happens later in set_subscription (231/255), a separate committed transaction. If set_subscription raises (UNIQUE collision / dict param) the orphan account row persists unlinked with a live api_key (store.py creates api_key by default at 156).
findings.rationale: Real non-atomic create+link leaving an unreachable account with a usable api_key. Low frequency (requires a failing second step) and the orphan has no plan/customer so no entitlement granted; low.

findings.title: invoice.paid sets status='active' without touching plan, producing plan='free'+status='active' on accounts created by the orphan path
findings.evidence: billing.py:265-270 resolves only via get_account_by_customer and calls set_subscription(status='active') with no plan. A late/duplicate invoice.paid on a previously-canceled (plan='free') account yields plan='free'+status='active'.
findings.rationale: Confirmed and explicitly benign under current entitlements (free plan = free limits regardless of status); inconsistent for support tooling. Overlaps U506/U718 invoice.paid analysis. Low.

findings.title: Webhook uses bracket indexing on the verified event without guarding malformed-but-signed payloads
findings.evidence: billing.py:187-188 read event['id'] and event['type'] via bracket subscription BEFORE the try/except at 195-202; 196 reads event['data']['object'] inside the try. construct_event validates signature, not schema, so a signed payload missing 'id'/'type' raises KeyError at 187/188 -> uncaught -> 500 instead of the intended 400.
findings.rationale: Confirmed unguarded shape assumption; requires knowledge of the webhook secret (same trust boundary), so blast radius is low. The 'data'/'object' access at 196 is inside the try and would 500 anyway via the handler path.

findings.title: checkout.session.completed hard-codes status='active' regardless of actual subscription/payment state
findings.evidence: billing.py:231-237 always sets status='active' on checkout.session.completed regardless of the real subscription state (delayed-payment / incomplete sessions can complete while the subscription is incomplete). plan_id is bounded via metadata.plan (_checkout_plan_id, 287-291).
findings.rationale: Confirmed assumption of active-on-completion; a subsequent customer.subscription.* event corrects it, so the brief mis-grant window is small. Low.

findings.location: lines 294-303 (_period_end) feeding store.current_period_end (INTEGER) at billing.py:261, store.py:49/199-211
findings.title: current_period_end stored as a bare Stripe epoch int with no semantic check; any consumer comparing it must use UTC fromtimestamp, but no such consumer/normalization exists
findings.evidence: billing.py:298-302 returns int(current_period_end) with no seconds-vs-ms sanity check; stored as raw INTEGER (store.py:49). No consumer compares it (entitlements_for ignores it, plans.py:184-193), so no fromtimestamp/UTC seam exists yet.
findings.rationale: Accurate that there is no normalization seam, but with zero current consumers this is a forward-looking note, not a live defect -> info.

findings.title: construct_event ValueError on malformed JSON and SignatureVerificationError both map to 400, but a missing stripe-signature header yields empty string -> still a verification error, never a distinct 'no signature' path
findings.evidence: billing.py:180 `request.headers.get('stripe-signature', '')` defaults to '' when absent; construct_event('', secret) raises SignatureVerificationError caught at 183 -> 400 'invalid signature' (185), with no distinct 'no signature header' path and no echoed reason.
findings.rationale: Confirmed operational conflation: a header-stripping proxy and active forgery both produce generic 400s, masking a silent webhook outage. Fail-closed behavior is correct; this is observability/diagnosability -> low.

findings.title: Reverse price->plan mapping is O(n) over plans reading env each iteration; if two plans share/misconfigure the same price env value, first match wins silently
findings.evidence: plan_id_for_price (plans.py:169-172) iterates PLANS in insertion order and returns the first stripe_price_id match, reading os.environ each iteration via the stripe_price_id property (line 48). If STRIPE_PRICE_PRO and STRIPE_PRICE_BUSINESS were set to the same id, 'pro' (inserted first) would win. The function IS used live (billing.py:251,291). No uniqueness guard exists.
findings.rationale: Behavior is correctly described and the function is on the live webhook path, but the trigger is a deploy misconfiguration (duplicate price env), not a code defect. O(n)/env-read-per-iter is a non-issue at 3 plans. Low.

findings.location: list_plans/public_dict: price_cents (plans.py:56), unit_amount_cents (plans.py:134), amount_cents (billing.py:100 credit packs)
findings.title: Public billing catalog returns three differently-named integer-cent fields (price_cents, unit_amount_cents, amount_cents) with no per-amount unit declaration
findings.evidence: billing.py list_plans (94-102) returns plans (price_cents), metered (unit_amount_cents), credit_packs (amount_cents from CREDIT_PACKS at plans.py:144-147), under a single top-level 'currency': 'usd'. Three different field names for integer USD minor units with no per-amount unit marker. Confirmed by reading the endpoint.
findings.rationale: Heterogeneous cent-field naming on the public catalog is real; it is an API clarity/consistency issue, not a functional bug. Low.

findings.title: GET /v1/audit/{run_id} is unauthenticated and run_ids/order_ids enumerate the receipt ledger
findings.evidence: router.get('/v1/audit/{run_id}') (114) takes no auth dependency; the response (132-142) exposes provider, dry_run, exact disposition counts, created_at, and the signature. IDs are high-entropy: triage run_id = 'run_' + secrets.token_hex(12) (app.py 127) and ACP order_id = 'order_' + secrets.token_hex(12) (router.py 296) = 12 bytes = 96 bits, so enumeration is impractical as the finding concedes. signed body is documented PII-free (counts only).
findings.rationale: Endpoint is genuinely unauthenticated, but the finding itself concedes 96-bit capability-URL entropy makes enumeration infeasible and the body is PII-free; leak is low-value business-volume metadata to a permalink holder. Low; flag for an explicit access decision, not a defect.

findings.title: Receipt ledger write failure is swallowed to a warning, so a Pro/Business customer paying for 'retained receipt history' can silently lose receipts
findings.evidence: Same swallow-and-continue in persist() (106-107) as U294/U510; this finding reframes the consequence as 'paid retained-receipt-history feature silently loses receipts'. Same code, same silent-failure mechanism.
findings.rationale: Duplicate of U294's root cause; the billing-feature framing adds a product angle (worth a metric/alert) but not a distinct defect.

findings.title: Ephemeral HMAC signing key fallback silently breaks receipt verification across restarts (integrity fallback)
findings.evidence: Same ephemeral-key-rotation-across-restart defect as U031 (lines 44-57), restated as a config concern.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U031; this instance is the lowest-confidence restatement.

findings.title: verify() treats a missing/empty signature as a normal non-match rather than distinguishing 'no signature present'
findings.evidence: verify() (74): hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature or '') — a None/empty stored signature returns False, indistinguishable from a tampered one. store.py schema (69) declares 'signature TEXT' (nullable, no NOT NULL), so a NULL signature is storable and would verify as 'tampered' rather than 'unsigned'. get_receipt never recomputes (U030), so a client can't distinguish.
findings.rationale: True semantic muddiness, but in practice persist() (95) and the ACP path (312) always pass a real hex signature, so a NULL row is not produced by current code. Cosmetic trust-semantics gap => info.

findings.location: get_receipt, lines 122-141 (with core/audit.py summary 241-251 and api/store.py save_receipt 280-290 / get_receipt 292-298)
findings.title: Stored summary round-trips through JSON (SQLite) so signed body bytes may differ from re-served body, breaking verification even without tampering
findings.evidence: Same JSON-round-trip-fidelity coupling as U429: signature over in-memory audit.summary(), persisted via json.dumps(summary) (store.py 287) and re-read via json.loads (store.py 296), re-served in get_receipt body (130). Current shape (str keys, ints, list of str) round-trips faithfully — proven by test_receipts.py:48-52. Future tuple/set/float/NaN field would break it.
findings.rationale: Same latent round-trip-stability hazard as U429; not a distinct defect. Both correctly note no current failure.

findings.title: Ephemeral key lazy-init is not thread-safe (read-check-set race on _EPHEMERAL_KEY under FastAPI threadpool)
findings.evidence: _EPHEMERAL_KEY (41) is set via non-atomic check-then-set (50-51) with no lock, while store.py guards its singleton with _STORE_LOCK double-check (442, 449-451) and FastAPI sync endpoints dispatch on a threadpool (store.py 124). Two concurrent first-requests could both observe None and assign different keys; the receipt signed with the losing key would not re-verify.
findings.rationale: Technically real but extremely narrow: only on the ephemeral-key path (already a production misconfiguration per U031) AND only in the startup-race window before the first assignment wins. Compounds an already-flagged misconfig. Downgraded to info.

findings.title: Provider query string is length-bounded but not otherwise validated; passed straight to provider.list_messages enabling provider-side query abuse
findings.evidence: api/schemas.py:46 `query: str = Field(default="has:nouserlabels", max_length=2048)` — bounded length only, no content allowlist. api/app.py:97-107 `_run` passes `query=req.query` straight into `service.run_triage(...)`, which forwards it to the provider's native query (list_messages). So the factual claim (query content is unvalidated and flows to the provider) is accurate.
findings.rationale: The code does pass an unvalidated, length-bounded provider-native query through to list_messages exactly as described, but this is intended passthrough design (each provider must accept its own search syntax), not a code defect; the real risk hinges entirely on the unauthenticated-endpoint concern owned by the app.py finding, so this is real-but-overstated as a standalone schema bug.

findings.title: get_receipt does json.loads(summary_json) and pops it, but if summary_json is somehow NULL/invalid it raises uncaught
findings.evidence: Lines 296: `row['summary'] = json.loads(row.pop('summary_json'))` with no try/except. summary_json is NOT NULL by schema (line 67) and save_receipt always writes json.dumps(summary) (line 287), so a malformed value is not producible by current code.
findings.rationale: The unguarded json.loads is real, but the schema NOT NULL constraint plus json.dumps-on-write makes a corrupting value unreachable; the finding itself rates confidence low. Robustness nit only — info.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite3 connection with check_same_thread=False relies solely on an RLock; any code path that touches self._conn outside _lock corrupts serialization, and there is no transaction isolation across multi-statement methods
findings.evidence: Describes the single shared check_same_thread=False connection + RLock + per-method commit + no-rollback (same as U430) and folds in the swallowed-WAL-pragma point (same as U519). Its own text concedes the fulfill_once INSERT is rolled back if the UPDATE raises ('acceptable').
findings.rationale: Compound finding that restates U430 (atomicity/no-rollback) plus U519 (WAL swallow). No new defect; the finding even self-defeats the atomicity concern. Duplicate of U430.

findings.title: Stale idempotency claim is re-bound after 60s even mid-charge, allowing a duplicate /complete to proceed concurrently with a slow-but-alive original
findings.evidence: Lines 337-344: a 'processing' entry older than IDEMPOTENCY_PROCESSING_TIMEOUT (60s, line 103) is reclaimed and returns 'new', letting a retry proceed. The finding itself notes double-charge is prevented by the per-session Stripe key (router.py:265) and double-credit by fulfill_once INSERT-OR-IGNORE (376-382).
findings.rationale: The 60s reclaim window is real, but the strong money invariants (single charge, single credit) hold by the finding's own admission; the residual is only confusing concurrent receipt-minting state. Real but mostly self-mitigated — low.

findings.title: Stale-claim reclaim updates created_at but never resets status or clears a prior response_json, leaving a window where a re-claimed key could already hold a response
findings.evidence: Lines 338-343: reclaim UPDATE sets request_hash/created_at, not status, and does not clear response_json — the same stale-reclaim-does-not-transition-status defect U309 raises.
findings.rationale: Same code site and same observation as U309 (status untouched on reclaim); U740 adds a speculative 'stale response_json' angle that no current path can produce. Duplicate of U309.

findings.title: Stale-claim window uses wall-clock time.time(); a backward clock step (NTP correction) can make a fresh 'processing' claim appear stale or never expire
findings.evidence: Line 337 compares _now() (time.time(), 106-107) minus stored created_at (time.time() at insert, 331) against the 60s timeout. Both are wall-clock; no monotonic source. A backward/forward NTP step makes the delta unreliable.
findings.rationale: Code-true use of non-monotonic wall-clock for the staleness window. Requires a clock anomaly to manifest and the money invariants still hold (per U739), so low. monotonic-across-restart caveat the finding raises is itself valid, making it a genuine design fragility.

findings.title: Replay of a key whose response_json is NULL silently returns response=None, which the router forwards as a JSON 'null' body with HTTP 200
findings.evidence: Line 352: `json.loads(existing['response_json'] or 'null')` returns None for a 'done' key with NULL response_json; router.py:117 returns state['response'] and JSONResponse(None) would emit body `null` with 200.
findings.rationale: The None->'null' 200-body path is code-true IF a 'done' key ever has NULL response_json, but no current path produces that state: idempotency_complete always writes json.dumps(response) (line 360), and status only becomes 'done' there. Real code shape, unreachable trigger — low/latent.

findings.location: add_credits lines 223-233 / consume_credit 235-246 (run_credits ledger) vs acp/router.py fulfill_once crediting total_runs (router 290)
findings.title: run_credits balance and the dollar amount charged are tracked in unrelated units with no linkage — a credited-runs vs amount-paid mismatch cannot be detected
findings.evidence: fulfill_once credits total_runs to run_credits (store.py:383-387) while the dollar amount lives only in the receipt summary (acp/router.py:301-308). Both total_runs and amount derive from the same session's build_line_items output (router.py:175,257), so they are consistent at write time with no stored cross-invariant.
findings.rationale: True that runs and dollars are stored in unrelated units with no reconciliation row. But both come from the same priced line_items at completion, so a desync requires a separate mispricing bug; this is a defense-in-depth/auditability gap, not an active defect. Low.

findings.title: json.dumps of caller-supplied dicts can raise TypeError on non-JSON-serializable values, raising inside the lock with no rollback
findings.evidence: json.dumps appears at save_receipt 287, idempotency_complete 360, save_session 410 with no default=str fallback. In save_receipt and save_session the dumps is in the args tuple evaluated before execute(); in idempotency_complete before the UPDATE.
findings.rationale: True that a non-JSON-serializable value would raise TypeError with no default=. But all dicts originate from Pydantic-validated request models / json-derived structures (router.py model_dump, receipts summaries), so non-serializable values are not producible today, and the dumps raises before any execute (no dangling txn). Real-but-overstated, low.

findings.title: close() leaves the singleton pointing at a closed connection; any later call raises 'Cannot operate on a closed database' on every method
findings.evidence: close() (140-142) calls self._conn.close() but does not clear the module singleton _STORE (441) nor null self._conn; get_store (445-452) returns the same instance, and no method guards against a closed connection. A later call would raise sqlite3.ProgrammingError('Cannot operate on a closed database').
findings.rationale: Code-true: close() leaves the singleton pointing at a dead connection with no reconnect/guard. Distinct from U520 (never-closed). Production path never calls close(), so latent — low. Tests use set_store(None) (which drops the ref without close), so the trigger requires someone to call store.close() while keeping the singleton.

findings.location: save_session lines 392-413 (INSERT OR REPLACE) vs fulfill_once 365-389
findings.title: save_session uses INSERT OR REPLACE keyed only on session id with no status/account guard — a concurrent cancel can overwrite a completed session
findings.evidence: store.py:407-413 save_session uses `INSERT OR REPLACE INTO acp_sessions` keyed on `id`, preserving only created_at, with no status/CAS guard. acp/router.py:352 cancel_session rejects only when current['status']==STATUS_COMPLETED at read time; with concurrent execution a cancel that read a READY row persists STATUS_CANCELED (router:357) after the complete path persisted STATUS_COMPLETED (router:337). fulfill_once writes a separate acp_fulfillments table (store.py:376) and is never reverted, so the stored session status can disagree with actual fulfillment.
findings.rationale: Real last-writer-wins TOCTOU between complete and cancel; narrow (single process, genuinely concurrent requests) and only the displayed status desyncs — credits/charges are not reverted, so low severity holds.

findings.title: Single shared sqlite connection with check_same_thread=False relies entirely on RLock; any code path touching _conn without the lock corrupts the connection
findings.evidence: Every _conn access in store.py is wrapped in `with self._lock` (e.g. 158, 183, 217, 239, 253, 280, 322, 356, 374, 402) or routed through _fetch_one/_fetch_all which acquire the lock (425-437); _fetch_one_nolock (429) is only called from inside already-held lock blocks (idempotency_begin:323, save_session:403). The finding itself concedes 'low severity given current usage' and names only hypothetical FUTURE callers.
findings.rationale: No current code path touches _conn outside the lock; this is a speculative architectural-fragility note about code that does not exist, not a defect in the real code.

findings.location: add_credits lines 223-233 + consume_credit lines 235-246 (no overflow / SQLite INTEGER semantics)
findings.title: run_credits INTEGER accumulates unbounded via add_credits/fulfill_once; a 1M-run pack repeatedly fulfilled has no ceiling and consume_credit's n is uncapped
findings.evidence: grep for consume_credit outside tests/definition returns nothing — its only callers are tests/test_store.py:39,42; production credit flow (fulfill_once router:290, add_credits) credits but never debits. The overflow half is moot: finding admits SQLite INTEGER is 64-bit, and total_runs is capped at 1000 packs * 1000 runs/pack = 1,000,000 (acp/models.py:27 quantity ge=1 le=1000; api/plans.py:144-146 runs=100/1000), so 'unbounded/millions' has a hard ceiling and is positive.
findings.rationale: Overstated: no numeric overflow and a real (if large) input ceiling exists, so the overflow framing is info; the genuinely true sub-claim is that consume_credit is unwired in production (credits sold/credited but never spent).

findings.location: fulfill_once line 378 int(runs) — negative/huge runs accepted verbatim into balance
findings.title: fulfill_once trusts the runs argument with no >=0 guard, so a negative or oversized total_runs is credited (or debited) verbatim
findings.evidence: total_runs flows from models.build_line_items (acp/models.py:96 `total_runs += int(pack['runs']) * qty`). qty is Pydantic-validated `quantity: int = Field(default=1, ge=1, le=1000)` (models.py:27) and pack['runs'] are positive literal constants 100/1000 (api/plans.py:144-146). There is no validated-input path that yields a negative or oversized total_runs; max is +1,000,000. router.py:290 passes this validated total_runs to fulfill_once.
findings.rationale: The negative-debit scenario is unreachable through validated input; only a manually corrupted stored session could produce it (not a code defect). Finding self-reports corroborated=false.

findings.title: Field name with a dot is reinterpreted by `op` as section.field; unescaped assignment
findings.evidence: Same unescaped f"{field}={value}" assignment-parsing concern as U319 (op interprets [section.]field on the LHS). Field names come from env config, not network; finder itself rated confidence low.
findings.rationale: Same assignment-misparse root cause as U319/U321, dot-separator variant; low likelihood.

findings.title: op stderr echoed into RuntimeError can leak vault/item names into error surfaces
findings.evidence: Lines 45-46 append exc.stderr.strip() to the RuntimeError for non-sensitive ops (op_read/op_item_get). Same stderr-into-error-surface disclosure as U531, viewed at the _run_op layer rather than the load_secret log site.
findings.rationale: Same op-stderr-in-error-message disclosure as U531; identifiers (not secrets) may leak. Low severity.

findings.title: Read paths do not set sensitive=True, so op CLI stderr is interpolated into error messages
findings.evidence: Confirmed op_read (line 64) and op_item_get (line 91) call _run_op without sensitive=True, so the non-sensitive branch (line 45-46) interpolates exc.stderr. Same disclosure surface and inconsistency-with-write-path observation as U531/U751.
findings.rationale: Same read-path-not-sensitive stderr disclosure as U531; restated from the read-function side.

findings.title: Inconsistent/undocumented op CLI flag ordering for edit vs get; --vault placement after positional assignment
findings.evidence: Confirmed flag ordering differs: op_item_get places --field/--account/--vault after the item positional (lines 85-90); op_item_edit places the field=value assignment before --account/--vault (lines 111-116). Whether `op` mis-parses --vault after a positional is entirely dependent on the installed op CLI version's argparse behavior, which cannot be determined statically.
findings.rationale: The ordering difference is real but the claimed wrong-vault failure depends on op CLI version behavior; requires running the actual CLI to confirm.

findings.title: Empty-string secret is treated as 'not present' and silently falls through to next source
findings.evidence: Line 170 `if value:` treats an empty-string env var as falsy/unset and proceeds to 1Password sources; lines 187/270 `if item and field` likewise skip empty strings. Accurate description of the unset-vs-explicitly-empty conflation.
findings.rationale: Behavior is exactly as described, but treating empty env as unset is conventional and harmless in practice; downgraded to info.

findings.title: Credential write-back has no audit/success logging, so a wrong-field or wrong-vault write is undetectable
findings.evidence: Confirmed op_item_edit returns None with no success log (lines 94-117) and store_json_secret returns silently (lines 263/272). No INFO confirmation of which item/field/vault was written. However store_json_secret is dead code (U446); the live write path is gmail_auth.store_token_info, which also lacks success logging.
findings.rationale: Observability gap is real but minor and sits partly on dead code; pure enhancement suggestion, info-level.

findings.title: batch.execute() unguarded; header lookup by exact-case 'From'/'Subject' may miss
findings.evidence: batch.execute() (line 135) unguarded — same as U534/U535. Header lookup `h['name'] == 'From'` (line 124) is case-exact and uses indexing not .get(); a header dict missing 'name' would KeyError inside the callback.
findings.rationale: Real but minor robustness, and Gmail returns canonical 'From'/'Subject' so the case/KeyError paths are practically unreached. Overlaps the U533/U534/U535 callback+execute cluster.

findings.location: cmd_summary line 564; cmd_pending line 680; cmd_summary tier_counts init line 535
findings.title: tier_counts indexed by cat_result.tier can introduce out-of-range tiers / KeyError-free silent growth
findings.evidence: tier_counts = {1:0,2:0,3:0,4:0} (535); line 564 `tier_counts[cat_result.tier] = tier_counts.get(cat_result.tier, 0) + 1` creates a new key for any out-of-range tier. get_tier_config(tier) falls back to PRIORITY_TIERS[4] (rules.py:1049) mislabeling it 'Reference'. categorize_with_tier currently derives tier from LABEL_RULES (default 4), so out-of-range is not reachable today.
findings.rationale: Confirmed mechanically but the slice itself rates confidence 'low' and notes 'low likelihood given current rules'. categorize_with_tier cannot currently return a tier outside 1-4, so this is a latent unvalidated-trust note, not an active defect. Downgraded to info.

findings.title: escalate: when no actions built (e.g., all dry-run) result.success_count is set to escalated_count, but with dry_run escalated_count>0 and actions empty conflates 'previewed' with 'succeeded'
findings.evidence: Lines 902-930: actions are appended only `if not args.dry_run` (902). In dry-run actions is empty, so the else branch `result.success_count = escalated_count` (930) reports escalations as successes even though apply_actions never ran. Same dry-run-reports-success pattern as cmd_label's else branch (291).
findings.rationale: Confirmed: in dry-run escalate sets success_count to escalated_count with nothing applied. Note: due to U166 (age always 0 for 3/4 providers) escalated_count is itself usually 0, limiting visible impact. Low — accounting cosmetic, and partly masked by the upstream no-op.

findings.title: audit default path built from args.provider unsanitized; provider is choice-restricted so low risk, but audit_file custom path has no path-traversal guard
findings.evidence: cli.py:370 audit_path = getattr(args,'audit_file',None) or default_path; :371 redact=getattr(args,'redact_audit',False) [defaults False]; :382-388 the non-audit/ branch only logger.warning()s, no guard/abort. core/audit.py _append (215-238) writes the path verbatim: os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(self.path))) + open(self.path,'a'). When not redacted, entry['sender']=sender (audit.py:209-210) so real addresses are written by default.
findings.rationale: Code does exactly what the finding says: --audit-file is an unvalidated operator path and the default writes un-redacted PII; but provider is argparse-restricted and the 'attacker' is the operator writing to their own disk, so blast radius stays low.

findings.title: remove_label only removed when computed label differs from remove_label; equality check is exact-string and case-sensitive
findings.evidence: cli.py:274 `if remove_label and label != remove_label:` then :275 action.remove_labels.append(remove_label). The comparison is a plain Python `!=` (case-sensitive, no .lower()/normalization). The documented re-label use-case in main() epilog (cli.py:958) `--remove-label "Misc/Other"` relies on exact-case equality with the rule-produced label to be skipped.
findings.rationale: The exact-case comparison is real as described; impact is minor churn only if an operator deliberately mismatches case on a self-supplied flag, so low severity.

findings.title: Edits SyncedSmartMailboxes.plist in place while Mail may be running, with a single .backup that prior runs overwrite
findings.evidence: Restates the fixed `.backup` clobber (lines 18,103) and in-place rewrite (123-124), plus a 'Mail.app may be running' race and 'no round-trip validation'. The race/round-trip points are plausible but the core defect is identical to U336.
findings.rationale: Same read-modify-write/backup cluster as U336; the added Mail-running-race angle is the same shared fragility, not a distinct verifiable defect.

findings.title: Audit JSONL append is not flushed/fsynced; tail of receipt can be lost on crash
findings.evidence: Lines 224-225 open in 'a' mode and write one line per record inside a `with`, so each line flushes to OS on context-manager close, but there is no os.fsync/flush-to-disk. On power loss/kernel crash buffered lines (incl. a violation line) can be lost. Reopening the file every record() is also O(n) open/close syscalls.
findings.rationale: Technically accurate durability gap for a 'trustworthy receipt' module. Low/minor: the OS page cache survives process crash (only kernel/power loss loses data), and the safety invariant is asserted from the in-memory violations list (not the file), so the breach detection itself is not dependent on fsync.

findings.title: Fallback domain parser strips '>' but not '<' / whitespace inside, and differs from canonical normalize_sender
findings.evidence: Line 92: `s.rpartition('@')[2].strip().strip('>').lower()`. For 'a@firm.com>, b@bulk.com' rpartition on the LAST '@' returns 'bulk.com', i.e. the last-address domain — exactly the last-'@' escape rules._iter_sender_domains (765-774) was hardened against. This only stamps the entry's 'domain' field; the protection decision uses _independently_protected (is_protected_sender), not this path.
findings.rationale: Real divergence between the fallback parser and canonical normalization, but it affects only the displayed domain on the rare fallback path (normalize_sender import/parse failure). Low severity; protection logic unaffected.

findings.location: record() entry timestamp line 198 vs receipt; concurrency on shared path
findings.title: Concurrent AuditLog writers to same path can interleave/lose lines (no locking)
findings.evidence: Each record() reopens path in 'a' mode (line 224) with no flock/locking. POSIX O_APPEND writes are atomic only up to PIPE_BUF (~4096 bytes); a JSONL line with a full sender address + labels could exceed that, risking interleaved lines if two processes share the same audit path. CLAUDE.md confirms a launchd daily job exists, so overlapping/concurrent runs are conceivable.
findings.rationale: Plausible but only manifests under a specific runtime config (two writers, same path, line > PIPE_BUF) that cannot be confirmed statically; default paths are per-provider/kind (cli.py:369 audit/<provider>-<kind>.jsonl) which mostly avoids collisions. Low severity, needs_runtime.

findings.location: record() lines 152/184/200 (message_id typed str but accepted/stored unvalidated)
findings.title: message_id accepted and stored without type/empty validation; empty or non-str ids silently pollute the violations trail
findings.evidence: record() declares message_id: str (line 150) and never validates it; appended raw to violations (184) and stored as entry['message_id'] (200). LabelAction.message_id is typed str (core/models.py:84) but has no non-empty constraint, so '' is constructible. An empty id in violations yields ['']  which weakens AuditInvariantError (256-259) / receipt_line (272) forensics. Unlike sender (guarded by isinstance in _domain_of:91), message_id has no defensive normalization.
findings.rationale: Real but speculative/overstated: no caller in the codebase passes an empty message_id (cli.py:79, base.py:384, gmail.py:489 all pass action.message_id from real provider results), and the type contract is str. A defensive validation would be nice-to-have; it is not a demonstrated defect with current callers.

findings.title: Path.expanduser() evaluated at import time freezes HOME
findings.evidence: Lines 17-21: DEFAULT_CONFIG_PATHS built at import with Path('~/...').expanduser(); ~ resolved once at import. find_config_file() (139-141) iterates these frozen paths. The MAIL_AUTOMATION_CONFIG env path (132-134) IS expanded reactively, but the defaults are not.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: ~ expansion is frozen at import for the default paths, affecting test harnesses/HOME changes. Info severity — almost never matters in practice (HOME rarely changes mid-process).

findings.title: DRY_RUN env var cannot be turned OFF and silently ignores invalid values
findings.evidence: Lines 270-271: `if os.getenv(f"{prefix}DRY_RUN"): config.dry_run = os.getenv(...).lower() in ("1","true","yes")`. A value like 'off'/'no' enters the branch and resolves to False (silently, no warning); empty-string is skipped so a YAML True stands. The finding's own text concedes the =0/=false cases work correctly.
findings.rationale: Partial — the only real sub-claim is the lack of feedback on unrecognized truthiness tokens (silent False) and the empty-string skip; the title 'cannot be turned OFF' is contradicted by its own body. Compounded by config.dry_run never being read outside tests. Info severity.

findings.title: merge() reorders labels via set(), losing deterministic label order
findings.evidence: Identical core claim to U575: list(set(...)) on add_labels/remove_labels (core/models.py:100-101) destroys order. The add+remove-contradiction sub-claim (same label in both add_labels and remove_labels after merge) is real as written but also requires a non-existent caller; it is the same set-merge code region.
findings.rationale: Same set()-ordering defect on the same lines as U575; merged as duplicate.

findings.title: combined_text emits a leading/trailing space for empty fields, polluting the match string (e.g. ' ' for empty sender+subject)
findings.evidence: core/models.py:58 f"{self.sender} {self.subject}".lower() yields ' ' for empty sender+subject — directly asserted by test_combined_text_empty (tests/test_models.py:69-71: assert msg.combined_text == ' '). Empty sender → leading space; empty subject → trailing space. Real for any ^-anchored pattern. Note: like U127, the property itself is unused in production (rules.py inlines the same f-string at 1007), but the inline copy has the identical behavior, so the latent hazard exists in the real match path.
findings.rationale: Behavior is exactly as described and test-confirmed; low severity is correct since production patterns use unanchored re.search.

findings.title: Tier 2 emails in the 24-72h window never escalate, contradicting the documented 'Tier 2-4 -> Tier 1' intent and time-sensitivity
findings.evidence: Reproduced: escalate_by_age(2, 50.0, True) -> should_escalate=False, escalated_tier=2, reason='Not time-sensitive or already Important tier'. Line 1132 gates the 24-72h escalation on current_tier>=3, so a time-sensitive tier-2 email is not escalated until >72h (line 1147). The reason string conflates 'already Important' with 'not time-sensitive'.
findings.rationale: Real behavior gap vs the docstring's '24-72h: Tier 3-4 -> Tier 2' (a tier-2 has nowhere to go in that window, so arguably by-design) plus a genuinely misleading reason string. Low / borderline-design; worth confirming intent as the finding itself flags (confidence low).

findings.title: Negative / NaN email_age_hours mis-handled and final return is dead code
findings.evidence: Lines 1122 `if email_age_hours < 24`, 1131 `if 24 <= email_age_hours < 72`, 1147 `if email_age_hours >= 72` are exhaustive over all real numbers. A negative age (clock skew/future-dated) falls into `<24` and is treated as fresh (benign). The final return 1155-1160 is reachable only by NaN (all comparisons false). No input validation.
findings.rationale: Real but trivial: the only path to the 'dead' final return is NaN, and email_age_hours is produced by calculate_email_age_hours which returns total_seconds()/3600 (never NaN); negative ages just read as fresh. Severity stays low; the NaN/dead-code concern is theoretical.

findings.title: Relay senders resolve to email='' so the Gmail self-match can never fire for relayed self-mail
findings.evidence: For an UNprotected relay whose local-part lacks `_at_`, line 732-734 returns ('', real_domain) with email_=''. _self_match (783) computes `addr.rpartition('@')[0]` on addr='' yielding '' which is never in SELF_LOCALPARTS. Also _self_match requires `domain in GMAIL_DOMAINS` (781), and the recovered real domain is rarely gmail.com — so the self-match cannot fire for relayed self-mail regardless.
findings.rationale: Accurate: relayed self-mail won't match _self_match. Impact is low — is_protected_sender still fails closed for undecodable relays, and self via gmail (non-relay) is unaffected; the weakening is real but narrow.

findings.location: categorize_with_tier VIP-no-override branch lines 991-995 vs normal branch 1006-1008
findings.title: VIP-without-override path re-categorizes on combined_text but defaults time_sensitive=True, diverging from non-VIP default of the rule's own value
findings.evidence: Line 993 `label = _find_best_label(combined_text)` then 994 `rule = LABEL_RULES.get(label, {})`, 995 `time_sensitive = rule.get('time_sensitive', True)`. _find_best_label (1038) returns `best_match or 'Misc/Other'` — always a LABEL_RULES key — so `.get(label,{})` always hits a real rule and reads its own time_sensitive (Marketing=False, line 451). The `True` default at 995 only applies if label absent from LABEL_RULES, which never happens.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: the 'VIP is time-sensitive' default is effectively unreachable; a VIP matching Marketing gets time_sensitive=False. Low severity — tier still comes from vip.tier, so VIP priority is preserved; only the time_sensitive flag diverges from the comment's intent.

findings.title: Personal rule's generic keywords (family/mom/dad) at tier 1 risk elevating marketing mail to Critical when it outranks competing rules
findings.evidence: Line 488 `"patterns": [r"family", r"mom", r"dad"]` at priority 18, tier 1 (490), and Personal is in KEEP_IN_INBOX (519). These are unanchored substrings matched via re.search against combined sender+subject. A subject like 'family plan' or 'Mom-approved deal' from a sender matching no higher-priority rule would be labeled Personal/Critical/kept-in-inbox.
findings.rationale: Real precision defect: bare 'family'/'mom'/'dad' substrings at tier 1. But priority 18 is near-lowest (only Awaiting Reply 19 and Misc 999 are lower) so almost any commercial keyword (Marketing 17, etc.) outranks it; over-classification fails toward keeping mail in inbox, not archiving. Low severity.

findings.location: _find_best_label() lines 1025-1038; LABEL_RULES catch-all pattern r'.*' line 502
findings.title: Catch-all regex r'.*' and broad substring patterns cause mojibake/over-matching; categorization is advisory but feeds archive decisions in tier_routing
findings.evidence: Line 502 Misc/Other pattern r'.*' at priority 999. Several unanchored substrings exist (Shopping r'uber' line 244 matches 'cyber'/'tuber'; r'target' line 248 matches 'targeting'; Marketing r'discount' line 436). _find_best_label feeds categorize_with_tier; a tier 3/4 non-keep label leads to archive. The note's own caveat is correct: the sender-based protected gate (is_protected_sender) is independent of categorization, so protected senders are shielded regardless.
findings.rationale: Over-matching substrings are real, but the security framing is overstated — protection is sender-based and decoupled from categorization. The defect is mislabeling/possible archive of non-protected normal mail; genuinely low. Note 'discount' matching 'discountinued' in the description is incorrect reasoning (no such word) but the broader substring concern holds.

findings.title: Duplicate/overlapping flash-sale regexes — one alternation is dead, and broad keywords mislabel transactional mail
findings.evidence: Line 435 `(% ?off|limited[- ]time|exclusive offer|flash sale|use code|coupon)` and line 437 `(flash sale|\d+% ?off|clearance sale)` both contain `flash sale` (redundant). However line 437 also adds `\d+% ?off` and `clearance sale` not in 435, so the alternation is NOT wholly dead. r'discount' (436), and Finance/Payments r'statement' (188) and r'invoice' (189) are unanchored substrings.
findings.rationale: Partial: 'flash sale' is genuinely duplicated but the claim 'one alternation is dead' overstates — line 437 carries unique tokens. Marketing is priority 17; 'statement'/'invoice' sit in Finance/Payments (priority 8, tier 2) — a higher-priority bucket, contradicting the 'dragging to lower-priority archive' framing for those. Low severity precision nit.

findings.title: appleid.com endswith check treats any '*.appleid.com' as a relay, mis-routing legitimate appleid subdomains
findings.evidence: Line 720 `if domain in RELAY_DOMAINS or domain.endswith('.appleid.com')` enters relay decoding for any *.appleid.com subdomain. appleid.com is in PROTECTED_SENDERS (563). For a real (non-encoded) subdomain like e.appleid.com, _relay_domain_candidates(local) yields junk and _best_relay_domain may return '' (704), so line 736-737 falls through to `return (addr, domain)` keeping the carrier domain — which _is_protected_domain still protects via appleid.com. Protection is safe; only categorization routing is affected.
findings.rationale: The finding itself concedes protection is safe and only routing is muddied. The endswith('.appleid.com') boundary is correct (would not match 'evil.appleid.com.attacker'). A minor categorization-quality nit, info/low.

findings.title: Protected-sender local config loaded once at import; file is read with no size bound and merged unvalidated, and a domain line that is actually a comment-only or malformed entry is added verbatim
findings.evidence: Lines 609-610: any non-self, non-comment line is appended as `line.lower()` with no domain validation (no '@'/dot/space check). Line 600 `open(path)` then iterates the whole file with no size bound. A line 'gmail.com' would be merged into PROTECTED_SENDERS (619) and _domain_matches would protect ALL gmail senders (never-archive). No warning emitted.
findings.rationale: Code-accurate: unvalidated merge of a user-controlled local file, no size cap. But this is a LOCAL, gitignored, user-authored config the user themselves writes — over-protection fails safe (never deletes mail) and a self-inflicted 'gmail.com' line is implausible-by-accident. Real but low; not attacker-reachable.

findings.title: last_run timestamp uses naive local datetime.now() (no tz) — inconsistent with audit's UTC
findings.evidence: Same naive-local datetime.now() vs audit.py UTC inconsistency as U195. Line 87.
findings.rationale: Same naive-timestamp root cause as U195.

findings.title: save() assumes history is mapping-like; a non-dict argument raises inside save
findings.evidence: core/state.py:86 `dict(history)` is outside the try/except (91-95), so a non-mapping arg raises out of save(). But the type hint is Dict[str,int] and all real callers pass self.stats/stats which are dict/defaultdict (gmail_labeler.py:73 get_history -> defaultdict; cli.py builds a dict).
findings.rationale: Technically true that line 86 is unguarded, but no current caller passes a non-mapping; purely defensive. Info-level / theoretical.

findings.location: is_resumable, lines 131-133 vs get_token usage in cli.py:172
findings.title: is_resumable only null-checks the token; an empty-string or non-string token is reported resumable
findings.evidence: core/state.py:133 `return self.state.get("next_page_token") is not None`. An empty-string token would report resumable. But save() stores whatever page_token the provider returns; providers return None at end-of-pages (gmail_labeler.py:308 passes None explicitly), not ''. No evidence any provider emits '' as a cursor.
findings.rationale: The '' edge case is speculative — no code path is shown to store an empty-string token. A minor correctness smell at most; info.

findings.title: Date condition 'is less than value:30' is likely wrong units for a date-received filter
findings.evidence: Line 19 verbatim: `{condition type:date received, qualifier:is less than value:30}`. Whether Mail.app interprets the bare integer 30 as a relative day-count, an absolute date coercion, or an error can only be confirmed by executing the script against Mail.app; the finding itself is confidence:low and states 'this is untested'.
findings.rationale: AppleScript Mail smart-mailbox date semantics are not statically determinable from this source; plausible but requires Mail.app at runtime to confirm wrong/empty behavior.

findings.title: 'message is in mailbox does contain Finance/Dev' depends on Gmail labels existing as Mail.app mailboxes
findings.evidence: Lines 18 and 24 match `message is in mailbox, qualifier:does contain value:"Finance"` and `"Dev"`. These depend on Mail.app mailboxes named with those substrings existing (e.g. synced Gmail labels Finance/Banking, Work/Dev/*). The assumption is unstated and the views match nothing if those mailboxes are absent.
findings.rationale: The cross-provider mailbox-existence dependency is a real, statically-visible assumption; low severity is right and the condition is config-dependent.

findings.title: Query 'label:Uncategorized' likely targets a non-existent label (taxonomy uses 'Misc/Other')
findings.evidence: "Uncategorized" is an actively-referenced real label in this codebase, not a phantom: gmail_labeler.py:120-122 caches it, :187 looks it up (uncategorized_id), :224-225 removes it on reassignment; gmail_labeler_legacy.py:282/364/386/430 uses 'Uncategorized' as its catch-all and creates/applies it; AGENTS.md:14 documents the second-pass sweep as targeting label:Uncategorized. The legacy labeler is what historically applied this label, so a real account that ran legacy will have it. Whether a given user's Gmail currently has the label (and thus whether the sweep is a no-op) is account-state dependent and not statically determinable.
findings.rationale: The catch-all taxonomy IS divergent (core uses 'Misc/Other', legacy uses 'Uncategorized'), but 'Uncategorized' is a genuine label produced by the legacy path that the sweep is intended to drain — not an obviously non-existent label. Whether it matches anything depends on live account state; finding's own confidence is 'low'.

findings.title: is_important `contains s` iterates over a `text item` reference, not the string value (missing `contents of`)
findings.evidence: Factual part confirmed: `repeat with s in senderList` (line 4) binds `s` as a list-item reference and line 5 uses `s` directly, whereas route_bulk_senders.applescript line 8 does `set dom to contents of d` first. But AppleScript's `contains` operator reliably coerces references to their values; the finding self-hedges ('low confidence', 'under some versions ... can behave unexpectedly') and provides no concrete failure path.
findings.rationale: Real consistency divergence from the sibling script, but the asserted correctness bug is speculative and not statically confirmable; it's a style smell, not a demonstrable defect — severity downgraded to info.

findings.title: Scope upgrade silently ignored when a cached token has narrower scopes
findings.evidence: Same defect as U603: from_authorized_user_info(token_info, scopes=scopes) at line 162 with no scope comparison; a cached token with narrower scopes passes creds.valid (line 164) and is accepted. Identical root cause to U603.
findings.rationale: Restates U603's scope-upgrade-silently-ignored defect; deduplicated.

findings.location: process_batch retry block lines 168-182, lambda closure line 173
findings.title: Late-binding closure over loop variable in retry lambda (msg_id) — works here but fragile
findings.evidence: Lambda at 173-176 captures msg_id by reference but is invoked synchronously inside the same loop iteration via _execute_with_backoff(...) at 172, so it reads the correct value. The finding itself states it 'works here' and is a latent footgun only under deferred-execution refactors.
findings.rationale: Not a present-tense bug; the finding self-identifies as low/no-risk-as-written, so downgrade to info.

findings.location: process_batch lines 247-261, batchModify lambda body closure line 258-260
findings.title: batchModify lambda closes over loop variable 'body' (rebuilt each iteration) — relies on synchronous invocation
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U792: both describe the batchModify lambda (lines 258-260 / 259) closing over the per-iteration `body`, both note it is synchronous-safe today and only fragile under refactor. Same root observation, same location.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U792; collapse to avoid double-counting. Not a present bug regardless.

findings.title: Batch HTTP retry re-executes an already-consumed BatchHttpRequest on rate limit, likely raising instead of retrying
findings.evidence: Line 164 passes the bound batch_get.execute into _execute_with_backoff. googleapiclient BatchHttpRequest is documented single-use; re-executing it after a rate-limit HttpError would generally raise rather than retry the constituent gets. But whether the batch-level execute() raises a 403/429 classified as rate-limited (vs surfacing per-request errors only through the callback->failed_ids path at 168-182) depends on googleapiclient version behavior at runtime; cannot be confirmed purely statically.
findings.rationale: Plausible API-misuse making the top-level batch retry ineffective, but its actual trigger/behavior is library-version- and runtime-dependent; per-request retry path still covers the common case.

findings.location: run() exception handler lines 333-335 saving page_token
findings.title: On fatal mid-batch error, state is saved with the NEXT page token though the current batch was only partially modified
findings.evidence: page_token is advanced only at line 319 AFTER process_batch returns (line 311), so a mid-batch raise saves the token that produced the CURRENT page (still pointing at the page being processed). On resume that page is reprocessed; for the default draining query already-labeled messages drop out and are skipped (safe), and total_processed (312) was not incremented so counts under-report. The non-transactional state claim is true, but the practical blast radius is mostly a count discrepancy, not data loss.
findings.rationale: Real non-atomic state-on-crash, but consequences are largely benign (safe reprocess + minor count under-report); severity-appropriate at low, hence partial vs the inflated framing.

findings.location: process_batch stats line 204 vs continue at line 213
findings.title: Stats counter increments for messages that are then skipped (no label cached), over-reporting processed distribution
findings.evidence: Line 204 `self.stats[label_name] += 1` executes before the `if not target_label_id: continue` guard at 212-213. A categorized label with no cached id increments stats without any modification being applied, so _print_final_stats (347-350) and persisted history over-report. Since _init_labels creates every LABEL_RULES label, this only occurs on a label outside LABEL_RULES (e.g. VIP label_override not in LABEL_RULES) or a creation failure.
findings.rationale: Reporting-accuracy bug confirmed; narrow trigger and cosmetic impact keep it at low.

findings.location: process_batch() lines 257-262; batchModify lambda closure over `body`
findings.title: Lambda in batch-modify retry closes over loop variable `body`; safe today but a latent closure-capture bug
findings.evidence: Lambda at 258-260 references `body` rebuilt each id_chunk iteration (252) and is invoked synchronously by _execute_with_backoff at 258, so it reads the correct body today. Finding explicitly says 'safe today', a latent closure-capture footgun only under refactor. (Note: U788 is the duplicate of this same observation.)
findings.rationale: Accurate description of a latent (non-active) footgun; not a present-tense defect, so info.

findings.title: execute_with_retry can return None implicitly (no exception, no value) and retries non-retryable errors blindly
findings.evidence: Identical root cause and location as U610: redundant bare `except Exception` retrying non-retryable errors + implicit None on loop fall-through. U793 itself notes 'With the default retries=4 the loop always either returns or raises, so it is currently safe'.
findings.rationale: Same defect in the same function as U610; merged.

findings.title: Legacy categorize_email accesses email_data['payload']['headers'] without guards (KeyError on missing payload)
findings.evidence: Confirmed: line 342 headers = email_data['payload']['headers'] (hard index), lines 346/348 header['name']/header['value'] (hard index); core/rules.categorize_message uses .get() defensively. KeyError is swallowed by `except Exception` at line 441 (print + continue). But the metadata get at 417-424 always requests From/Subject, so this only fires on a malformed/partial API response.
findings.rationale: Robustness divergence is real but minor and conditional (needs malformed response, and it's caught). Largely overlaps U357's header-indexing half. Downgraded low->info.

findings.title: Same draining-query-with-page-token hazard as gmail_labeler.py; plus no state persistence so a crash loses all progress
findings.evidence: Confirmed structurally: pages via nextPageToken (lines 405,449) while modify() at 432 adds a user label, removing each message from the 'has:nouserlabels' result set, so the result set shifts under pagination (potential skip). No StateManager here (grep: only gmail_labeler.py imports core.state.StateManager). verify_labeling_complete line 485 uses resultSizeEstimate. The shift-skip is a known Gmail-pagination hazard but is self-healing on rerun because the query drains; severity is bounded by that.
findings.rationale: The pagination-shift and no-state observations are real, but the practical risk is low: the query is self-draining so a rerun catches residue, and the finding itself concedes this. Low.

findings.title: resultSizeEstimate used as exact count; it is an ESTIMATE and unreliable for completion checks
findings.evidence: Line 485 reads results.get('resultSizeEstimate', 0) and line 486 declares SUCCESS when ==0. Gmail API documents resultSizeEstimate as an approximation. Used only in a print/return-bool for an end-of-run banner (verify_labeling_complete), no mutation or control flow depends on it beyond a console message.
findings.rationale: Accurate API-semantics observation but purely cosmetic (drives only a printed SUCCESS/WARNING line). The finding itself calls it cosmetic. Info.

findings.title: `--limit` slice keeps most-recent UIDs but archives them — opposite of typical 'archive old noise' intent, and limit semantics may surprise
findings.evidence: Line 112 `uids = uids[max(0, len(uids) - args.limit):]` keeps the tail (most-recent N). The --limit help text DOES document this: line 85 'max most-recent messages to scan'. So the 'undocumented' sub-claim is false; the design-intent concern remains.
findings.rationale: Not a bug — documented design choice; only a debatable-default observation. Downgraded to info.

findings.title: IMAP fetch failure for a single message returns ('','') which then bypasses protected check via empty sender = protected (OK) but mislabels via empty domain
findings.evidence: Lines 57-58 return ('','') on fetch failure. Verified is_protected_sender('') returns True (core/rules.py:797-798, fail closed) -> message marked protected (safe). No logging/counter for the failure, so a server hiccup silently inflates the 'protected' bucket the operator reads in dry-run.
findings.rationale: Accurate: fail-closed is safe, but the silent skew of disposition counts is a real low-severity observability gap.

findings.title: Mailbox name is wrapped in literal quotes but not escaped, breaking on names containing quotes/special chars
findings.evidence: Lines 67 and 72 both wrap the mailbox as f'"{archive_mailbox}"' with no IMAP quoting/escaping and no modified-UTF-7 encoding. A --archive-mailbox containing a quote/backslash or non-ASCII would malform the command or target the wrong folder. (The connect line 49-52 part of the location is irrelevant — connect has no mailbox quoting.)
findings.rationale: Real robustness/quoting issue, operator-supplied input, default 'Archive' is safe; low. Same root cause as U800.

findings.title: IMAP archive-mailbox name injected unescaped into UID MOVE/COPY
findings.evidence: Same unescaped f'"{archive_mailbox}"' interpolation at lines 67 and 72; not a remote-exploit (argparse-supplied), a quoting/robustness bug.
findings.rationale: Same mailbox-quoting defect as U799.

findings.title: Narrow except on MOVE only catches imaplib.IMAP4.error; MOVE returning non-OK without raising falls through to COPY duplicating the message
findings.evidence: Lines 67-71: if UID MOVE returns res != 'OK' without raising, control falls through to the COPY path (72+), and the except only catches imaplib.IMAP4.error so a socket.error would propagate. The duplication speculation ('MOVE partially succeeded then COPY also succeeds') is unverified hypothetical server behavior.
findings.rationale: The fall-through and narrow-except facts are real; the duplication scenario is speculative. Edge-case, low.

findings.title: data[0].split() on a failed/None search and most-recent-N slice assume sequential UID ordering
findings.evidence: Line 107 `uids = data[0].split()` runs only after res=='OK' (105-106); if data[0] were None it would AttributeError (minor edge). Line 112 tail-slice assumes ascending UID order for 'most recent', which IMAP SEARCH does not guarantee. Messages outside the slice are simply never evaluated -> left in inbox (safe), but dry-run counts can mislead.
findings.rationale: Real but minor; outcome is safe (unscanned mail stays in inbox), only count accuracy affected. Low.

findings.location: main() line 130; normalize_sender vs is_protected_sender domain basis
findings.title: Categorization uses normalize_sender's FIRST address only while protection uses the union; a multi-address From categorizes off one address
findings.evidence: Verified core/rules.py: normalize_sender returns the FIRST address only (docstring 740-743, returns single tuple) while is_protected_sender unions every address via _iter_sender_domains (800-805). Line 130-131 categorizes off normalize_sender's real_domain, falling back to `categorize_from_strings(real_domain or frm, subj)` when empty. Protection is sound; only the archive-bucket label for non-protected multi-address senders can be off.
findings.rationale: Premise accurate and protection-safe as the finding itself concedes; label-only mislabel, low.

findings.title: ensure_label caches label as created on IMAP error responses other than OK/NO, and treats NO as success blindly
findings.evidence: Lines 96-100: only caches when res in ('OK','NO'); a 'BAD'/error response is never cached (so create() retried every message for that label) and never logged. 'NO' is treated as 'already exists' per the line-99 comment but can also mean a genuine create failure, after which apply_label's COPY (line 109) to the missing folder fails silently (see U361/U362).
findings.rationale: Accurate read of current code: no logging, retry-on-non-OK, and NO conflated with success. Low severity (efficiency + invisibility of a systematic failure).

findings.title: Legacy IMAP label injection (unescaped quotes in STORE/COPY)
findings.evidence: Same unescaped-quote interpolation as U360: f'"{label}"' in STORE (line 106) and COPY (line 109). Correctly notes practical risk is low because labels come from the fixed categorize_from_strings taxonomy (core/rules.py:950).
findings.rationale: Same IMAP quoting/injection root cause as U360 in this file; U360 is the fuller statement (also catches the unquoted create() space bug).

findings.title: load_password has unreachable final return and broad except hiding 1Password failures; can silently proceed with empty password
findings.evidence: Same defects as U614: unreachable final 'return ""' (line 77), broad except (line 71) masking op failures and falling through to getpass which can hang/abort a daemon. Adds that subprocess omits --vault and trusts OP_ITEM/OP_FIELD verbatim (correctly noting no shell injection since shell=True is not used).
findings.rationale: Same load_password error-handling root cause as U614; the extra --vault/verbatim-env note is non-defect (no injection), so no upgrade.

findings.title: Route list omits POST /v1/triage and may drift from actual api/app.py routes
findings.evidence: POST /v1/triage does exist (api/app.py:91 and worker.mjs:260) and llms.txt omits it, listing only /v1/triage/preview (line 14). The advertised set is otherwise consistent with api/app.py routes — /mcp (app.py:143), /acp/checkout_sessions + /acp/feed.json (acp routers, app.py:136-137), /v1/audit/{run_id}, /v1/billing/plans, /v1/senders/check, /v1/triage/preview all map to real routes. Endpoints are listed without HTTP methods, which is a minor doc nit. Omitting the live-mutating /v1/triage from agent-facing discovery (advertising only the safe dry-run preview) reads as deliberate conservatism, not drift.
findings.rationale: True that /v1/triage is omitted and methods are absent, but this is largely intentional (safe-by-default discovery) and a cosmetic doc gap — low impact, not a correctness defect.

findings.title: Label names with spaces/slashes are interpolated into Gmail query without quoting
findings.evidence: Line 41 f-string interpolates category directly into the Gmail query with no quoting. All seven entries in TARGET_CATEGORIES (lines 23-31) are space-free, so no current entry malforms the query; the finding itself concedes 'none here'.
findings.rationale: Latent fragility, not an active defect — no current category contains a space; downgraded to info as a hardening note only.

findings.location: _clamp_limit() line 83-84; triage limit param typed int
findings.title: _clamp_limit assumes coercible limit; int(limit) can raise on bad MCP input
findings.evidence: _clamp_limit does int(limit) (line 84) and is called inside _triage's try block at line 133; the except clauses (137,140) catch only ProviderUnavailable and AuditInvariantError, so a ValueError/TypeError from int() on a non-coercible limit propagates uncaught.
findings.rationale: Technically correct, but FastMCP validates/coerces args against the int type annotation before the tool body runs, so a non-coercible value rarely reaches int(limit). Edge robustness gap — low/info.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' fully disables DNS-rebinding protection with no origin checks
findings.evidence: Lines 49-50: if raw=='*' returns TransportSecuritySettings(enable_dns_rebinding_protection=False), which per SDK validate_request (transport_security.py:113-115) skips Host AND Origin validation entirely. The docstring (43-46) assumes a fronting proxy validates Host but nothing enforces it.
findings.rationale: Confirmed opt-in foot-gun; '*' fully disables protection. Documented escape hatch, so low.

findings.title: DNS-rebinding protection fully disabled when MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*'
findings.evidence: Identical claim to U810: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS='*' -> enable_dns_rebinding_protection=False disables Host/Origin validation. Same lines 49-50.
findings.rationale: Verbatim duplicate of U810.

findings.location: module import time line 151 (http_app = mcp.streamable_http_app()) and lines 38-45 in api/app.py
findings.title: _transport_security() reads MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS once at import time, freezing the host allowlist for the process
findings.evidence: Same root cause as U621: _transport_security() runs once during FastMCP construction at import (line 79), snapshotting MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS; http_app built at line 151; api/app.py:39 pulls it in at module load. A post-import env change is ignored.
findings.rationale: Same import-time env-freeze defect as U621, framed as compounding the 421; merged.

findings.title: MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS hardcodes :8000 dev hosts into the production allowlist and builds origins by string concat, admitting any scheme-host on port 8000
findings.evidence: Line 52 unconditionally appends ['localhost','127.0.0.1','localhost:8000','127.0.0.1:8000'] in all modes; line 53 builds origins via http://+host and https://+host. So production always trusts loopback:8000 and there is no hardened mode that drops the dev hosts.
findings.rationale: Confirmed: dev :8000 loopback entries are permanently in the allowlist. Minor hardening gap (loopback-only, not externally reachable on a hosted box) — low.

findings.title: DNS-rebinding allowlist hardcodes only :8000 loopback variants; a hosted MCP on a non-8000 port loses loopback dev access and may push operators to set MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS=* (protection off)
findings.evidence: Line 52 hardcodes only :8000 loopback variants (the scheme-in-host sub-claim duplicates U366). The 'loses loopback dev access on a non-8000 port' framing is weak: the hosted app binds $PORT externally but loopback dev still targets localhost:8000 only if you run it there; the 'nudges operators to set MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS=*' is speculative. Loopback hosts are appended regardless of MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS, so they do not gate the production host.
findings.rationale: Real underlying facts (hardcoded :8000, scheme-mis-entry breaks matching) but the causal 'pushes operators to '*'' narrative is speculative and the scheme half overlaps U366; partial.

findings.title: Rename conflict handling updates local index incorrectly, and rename can collide with existing Gmail label causing API error mid-pass
findings.evidence: Line 63 `name_to_meta[new] = meta` does leave the old key present and stores the old meta object (true). But the finding's central claim — a later rename hitting an unexpected 'label name exists' HttpError that gets 'silently skipped' — cannot occur with the static RENAMES (lines 15-25): all 9 targets are distinct, freshly-prefixed paths (Work/, Tech/, Marketing/, Finance/) and none collides with another pending rename. The HttpError IS caught and logged (lines 64-65), which the finding itself concedes. No argparse/no other caller (grep confirms standalone script).
findings.rationale: Bookkeeping nugget is real but the HttpError-silent-skip narrative is speculative and the failure path is already caught; overlaps the latent defect described in U817.

findings.title: Hardcoded label-rename map with no dry-run mutates live Gmail label taxonomy immediately
findings.evidence: main() (line 32) calls svc.users().labels().update(...).execute() (line 61) directly against userId='me' with no --dry-run, no argparse, and no confirmation prompt (grep for argparse/dry-run/sys.argv returns nothing). The factual claim is accurate.
findings.rationale: Accurate observation, but renames are non-destructive (relabel, not delete) and this is a missing-preview/blast-radius note rather than a code defect — info severity.

findings.location: main() lines 49-63; rename conflict / mapping update
findings.title: Label rename updates name_to_meta[new] to the SAME meta object, so chained renames and conflict detection can misbehave
findings.evidence: Line 63 `name_to_meta[new] = meta` does store the OLD meta dict (whose 'name'/'id' are the source label's) under the new key, and the old key is never removed. The chain scenario (A->B then B->C) would misbehave — but RENAMES (lines 15-25) contains no chains: every value is a distinct prefixed path, none reappears as a key. The finding explicitly states this is 'latent' with the current static map.
findings.rationale: Real bookkeeping imprecision but entirely latent under the current static, chain-free RENAMES; harmless today, would only bite if the map grew chains.

findings.title: health_check swallows ALL exceptions into a generic unhealthy status, masking auth vs network vs quota distinctions
findings.evidence: base.py:409-413: `try: self.list_messages(limit=1) ... except Exception as e: return False, str(e)`. Bare Exception is caught and collapsed to (False, str(e)), so callers cannot programmatically distinguish auth failure vs network vs throttle — only a free-text string is returned. This is an accurate read of the code.
findings.rationale: Real observability/coarse-error-handling weakness in a diagnostic helper; low blast radius (health_check is informational), so info-to-low severity is appropriate.

findings.title: Gate neutralizes archive + INBOX-removal but does NOT clear action.star / action.category / move-on-label for non-LABEL_IS_MOVE folder providers when archive flag is the only departure
findings.evidence: Accurate that the in-place gate only clears archive + strips INBOX/\INBOX (base.py:301-304) and that suppression of label-as-move depends on the LABEL_IS_MOVE class attribute (base.py:334), not on a capability. The two shipping folder providers correctly declare LABEL_IS_MOVE=True (outlook.py:102, mailapp.py:55). The leak only manifests for a hypothetical future provider that sets LABEL_IS_MOVE=False while its apply_label physically moves — no such provider exists today (the finding itself says 'none ship today').
findings.rationale: Real fragility (invariant keyed on a per-provider boolean rather than capability) but only exploitable by a hypothetical misconfigured provider; correctly low severity.

findings.title: batchModify body captured by late-binding closure inside loop
findings.evidence: Lines 460-466: the lambda closes over `body`, but _execute_with_backoff invokes func() synchronously within the same loop iteration (line 107 `return func()`), before `body` is reassigned at line 454 on the next iteration. The finding itself states 'this is NOT a live bug today.' No defect exists in current code.
findings.rationale: Self-admitted non-bug; the synchronous execution makes the closure correct — false positive as a defect.

findings.title: batch.execute retried wholesale on rate limit can double-process already-succeeded messages and accumulate state in closure
findings.evidence: failed_ids (line 225) is reset per chunk but not between retry attempts of the same batch.execute (line 248 retried by backoff). Whether a partially-succeeded batch re-issues only unfinished requests on retry depends on google-api-python-client's BatchHttpRequest semantics, which cannot be determined statically. At worst it redundantly re-fetches at 252-255; the finding agrees it is 'not data-corrupting'.
findings.rationale: Behavior hinges on the client library's batch-retry semantics; confirmable only at runtime, low impact.

findings.title: left_inbox detection upper-cases label-cache INBOX id but real Gmail user-label ids are mixed-case opaque tokens
findings.evidence: Lines 432-438: inbox_id=str(_label_cache.get('INBOX','INBOX')).upper(); remove_ids is built from _label_cache.get(label) at 413 and the literal 'INBOX' appended at 417. For real Gmail the INBOX id IS 'INBOX', so the upper()-on-both-sides comparison matches. The theorized opaque-case-sensitive INBOX id does not occur for Gmail system labels.
findings.rationale: Best-effort audit flag that only over-reports (as the code comment states); the case-sensitivity concern is not reachable for Gmail INBOX — info-level at most.

findings.title: get_message_details bypasses 404-to-None handling when the 404 is wrapped by backoff RuntimeError, and only treats status==404 specially
findings.evidence: Line 182 `if e.resp.status == 404:` accesses e.resp.status directly without a getattr guard, unlike the backoff helper which uses getattr(e.resp,'status',None) at line 110. If e.resp is None (some HttpError construction / transport-layer paths), this raises AttributeError instead of returning None, crashing the caller.
