findings.rationale: Confirmed inconsistency: this site lacks the defensive getattr the backoff helper uses; narrow but real robustness gap, low severity.

findings.title: is_read derived as 'UNREAD not in label_ids' — relies on UNREAD always being present in metadata labelIds
findings.evidence: Lines 210/292: `is_read = "UNREAD" not in label_ids` is the correct Gmail convention. On a degraded/empty labelIds response it defaults to is_read=True. Gmail's metadata format reliably returns labelIds, so an empty list is an edge that does not occur normally.
findings.rationale: Correct-by-convention; the degraded-response failure mode is real but defaults toward the safe-ish 'read' and is not normally reachable — info-level.

findings.title: Late-binding closure over loop variable 'body' in batchModify lambda can apply the WRONG batch on retry
findings.evidence: Same late-binding-closure-over-loop-variable `body` at lines 460-466 as U820; the finding again concedes it is 'Lower confidence as a live bug' and 'currently fine because each is executed before the next reassignment.'
findings.rationale: Same non-defect as U820; synchronous execution makes the closure correct.

findings.title: Batch callback records failures but the surrounding batch.execute via _execute_with_backoff can lose individual successes on a whole-batch retry
findings.evidence: googleapiclient/http.py BatchHttpRequest.execute (lines 1528-1606) delivers per-request HttpErrors to the callback (lines 1596-1604), it does NOT raise them from execute(). _execute_with_backoff only catches HttpError raised by execute() itself (a transport-level/global failure). On re-execution, execute() re-iterates self._order and re-fires every callback; results[request_id]=msg (line 234) is an idempotent dict overwrite and failed_ids would only get duplicate appends — no double-processing of mutations (this path is read-only get), no dropped successes, no corruption.
findings.rationale: Read-only batch get; re-exec is idempotent overwrite and the feared whole-batch-429-mixed-with-success drop cannot corrupt results — speculative and self-rated low.

findings.title: Reverse label-id->name map silently drops labels created after connect() (cache populated only at connect), under-reporting message.labels
findings.evidence: The reverse map (lines 198-202, 280-284) does silently drop label ids not in _label_cache, and system/category ids (CATEGORY_*, IMPORTANT) are never user-cached, so EmailMessage.labels under-reports — that part is real. BUT the stated impact is false: grep shows NO code branches on EmailMessage.labels for skip/keep. cli.py's protected gate keys on action.sender / msg.sender (cli.py:71,213), and core/rules.py categorize_message/categorize_with_tier take only sender+subject (rules.py:928,965). msg.labels is effectively unconsumed for decisions.
findings.rationale: Reverse-map drop is real but the 'cascades into skip/keep/categorization' blast radius is fabricated — nothing reads msg.labels for branching.

findings.location: apply_actions lines 459-466 (lambda over loop variable body)
findings.title: batchModify lambda captures loop-mutated 'body' by closure — safe today but a latent footgun if execution is ever deferred
findings.evidence: Lines 459-466: lambda: self._service.users().messages().batchModify(userId='me', body=body).execute() closes over the loop-rebuilt 'body' (line 454) by reference. _execute_with_backoff (line 107) invokes func() synchronously before body is reassigned, so it is correct today. The finding's own framing is 'safe today but latent footgun' — accurate.
findings.rationale: Closure-over-loop-var is genuinely present and correctly self-scoped as a latent/style hazard, not a current bug; default-arg binding would harden it.

findings.title: INBOX 'left_inbox' detection upper-cases the cached INBOX id but compares against remove_ids that were built from raw label-cache ids, so a non-literal INBOX id mismatch can hide a real archive from the audit
findings.evidence: Lines 432-438 do compute left_inbox via heuristic uppercase set-membership against _inbox_forms={'INBOX','\\INBOX',inbox_id.upper()} rather than comparing resolved ids. The literal 'INBOX' append for action.archive (line 417) means the common path is reliably caught, and the code comment (424-431) explicitly states the broadening-only intent. The mismatch scenario requires Gmail to return a non-literal INBOX system id, which it does not.
findings.rationale: Heuristic string-match is real and only affects the audit receipt's archived flag (not the actual API mutation), under a scenario Gmail does not produce — overstated; info.

findings.title: Read/starred status via substring match '\\Seen'/'\\Flagged' in raw FLAGS string can false-positive on label/user-flag names
findings.evidence: Lines 257-259: is_read = '\\Seen' in raw_str and '\\Flagged' in raw_str do naive substring tests over the whole raw FLAGS response (which includes UID and command echo). A custom keyword/user-flag containing 'Seen'/'Flagged' would false-positive. Should tokenize the FLAGS list and match exactly. Real but low-impact (read-only enrichment, and \-prefixed system flags rarely collide).
findings.rationale: Genuine substring-vs-token correctness issue affecting only read/star detection; low severity is accurate.

findings.title: 1Password subprocess uses check_output with broad except; password failures swallowed, and op CLI arg injection via env-controlled item name
findings.evidence: Lines 114-121: `try: result = subprocess.check_output(["op","item","get",op_item,"--account",op_account,f"--field={op_field}"], text=True).strip(); return result except Exception as e: logger.warning(...)` then falls through to `raise ValueError(...)` at 123. Part (a) is real: broad `except Exception` swallows the true cause (e.g. op binary missing) and emits a generic ValueError. Part (b) `--field={op_field}` f-string interpolation into argv could allow op-flag injection IF op_field were attacker-controlled.
findings.rationale: Broad-except masking is real (low); the 'arg injection' half is overstated because op_account/op_item/op_field are entirely user-controlled env vars, not an attacker channel — no privilege boundary is crossed.

findings.title: Password potentially returned as None type leading to confusing login failure
findings.evidence: Lines 105-106: `if os.getenv("IMAP_PASS"): return os.getenv("IMAP_PASS")` — getenv called twice. The empty-string falls through to 1Password/ValueError.
findings.rationale: Double-getenv is a real redundancy smell, but treating an empty-string password as unset is correct intended behavior, not a bug; severity is info, not low.

findings.title: Methods dereference self._connection without verifying connect() ran -> AttributeError if used outside context manager
findings.evidence: _select_mailbox (158), get_message_details (216), apply_label (279/288), star (331) etc. all call `self._connection.uid(...)` with no None-guard. However every caller enters via `with provider:` (cli.py 439/475/542/637/743/853 -> base.__enter__ -> connect) or explicit `provider.connect()` (cli.py 505, api/service.py 100) before any method runs.
findings.rationale: The missing precondition check is real, but no current code path invokes these methods on an unconnected provider, so the AttributeError is unreachable; pure defensive-robustness, info.

findings.title: logout() may leave socket open on certain server errors; no close()/shutdown fallback
findings.evidence: Lines 145-153: `if self._connection: try: self._connection.logout() except Exception: logger.debug(...) finally: self._connection=None; self._current_mailbox=None`. No explicit shutdown()/close() fallback in the except path.
findings.rationale: Real but minor: imaplib.logout() itself calls self.shutdown() which closes the socket; a leak only on the narrow case where logout raises before shutdown. FD-leak-across-cycles framing is overstated for typical single-process daily runs.

findings.title: UID decoded to str for EmailMessage.id but downstream STORE/FETCH pass it back as str — fine, but no validation that data[0] split tokens are UIDs
findings.evidence: Line 188: `uids = data[0].split() if data[0] else []`. imaplib UID SEARCH returns all matching UIDs space-separated in a single `data[0]` bytes element; the empty-result case is guarded by `if data[0]`.
findings.rationale: The 'server chunks UIDs across data[1:]' concern does not match the IMAP SEARCH response contract — SEARCH returns one untagged response line; no real defect, and the candidate itself rates it low-confidence.

findings.title: Broad except Exception when loading IMAP password swallows real errors and falls through
findings.evidence: Lines 114-121 broad `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...)` then fall-through to generic ValueError at 123 — identical code region and identical root cause as U831 part (a).
findings.rationale: Same broad-except-swallows-real-error defect already captured by U831; deduplicated.

findings.title: IMAP write operations assume the correct mailbox is selected, but apply_actions issues UID STOREs without re-selecting INBOX
findings.evidence: list_messages accepts `mailbox="INBOX"` (168) and is the only caller of _select_mailbox (182). Grep of all callers (cli.py 186/481/543/646/744/855) shows none pass a `mailbox` arg; base.py's abstract signature has no mailbox param. So _current_mailbox is always INBOX during apply_actions.
findings.rationale: The wrong-mailbox UID scenario requires a caller to list a non-INBOX mailbox then mutate — no such call site exists; the latent risk is real only if a future caller uses the mailbox param, so not a current defect.

findings.title: is_read/is_starred parsed from raw AppleScript boolean string is fragile to localization/format
findings.evidence: Line 195 `is_read.lower() == "true"` / line 196 `is_flagged.lower()`. Whether `(msgRead as string)` (line 160) yields exactly 'true'/'false' across locales is AppleScript runtime behavior.
findings.rationale: Locale-sensitivity of AppleScript boolean coercion cannot be confirmed statically; finding itself rates low confidence. Plausible but needs runtime.

findings.title: Mail.app launch uses fixed `delay 2` race; subsequent operations may run before Mail is ready
findings.evidence: Lines 96-99: if Mail not running, `tell application "Mail" to activate` then `delay 2`, then return "ok" (line 100). No readiness poll for loaded accounts.
findings.rationale: Code clearly uses a fixed 2s delay with no readiness check; genuine but low-severity timing race on cold start.

findings.title: Pagination next_token off-by-one boundary when total is exactly a multiple of limit
findings.evidence: Line 201 `next_token = str(next_offset) if next_offset < total else None`. Finding's own worked example shows the exact-multiple boundary is CORRECT. The residual claim is offset drift under concurrent mailbox mutation between pages.
findings.rationale: The titled off-by-one is a false alarm (finding admits 'Correct here'); only the concurrent-mutation drift edge-case remains, which is a low-severity inherent limitation of offset pagination.

findings.title: AppleScript repeat upper bound uses string-interpolated arithmetic that breaks for non-int page_token
findings.evidence: Line 128 `start_offset = int(page_token) if page_token else 0` is OUTSIDE the try (try begins at line 171), so a non-numeric page_token raises ValueError uncaught, crashing rather than returning empty.
findings.rationale: Confirmed in code: bad page_token crashes before the try block. Low severity since this provider's own tokens are always numeric; only a cross-provider token misuse triggers it.

findings.title: Mail.app list pagination builds an O(N) AppleScript that re-indexes 'item i of allMsgs' and uses unescaped account name
findings.evidence: Combines the U090 account-interpolation injection, the U387 O(N) indexing, and the U217 timeout->empty result, plus a note that line 183 `int(line.replace('---TOTAL:', ''))` can raise ValueError on truncated output (uncaught — outside the try at 171).
findings.rationale: Bundles three already-tracked defects; injection is the dominant root cause -> duplicate of U090. The int() ValueError on malformed total is a real minor parse-fragility but secondary.

findings.location: lines 191-228 (list_messages/get_message_details build EmailMessage with no date) cross-ref line 293-294 star ignores due_date
findings.title: Mail.app provider returns dates in local zone semantics nowhere normalized; combined with calculate_email_age_hours assume-UTC, any future date population would be tz-wrong
findings.evidence: Confirmed no date is populated (lines 191-197, 240-247). calculate_email_age_hours (rules.py:1180-1182) assumes UTC for naive datetimes. The tz-mismatch is purely hypothetical — it only materializes 'if/when date is wired in', which it is not.
findings.rationale: The present-tense defect (no date) is U093. This is a forward-looking latent-seam note about a future code change; valid as a design caveat but not a current bug. Low.

findings.title: Archive uses literal destinationId 'archive'; if the well-known alias is not accepted the message stays in inbox and only logs an error
findings.evidence: Line 548 data={'destinationId':'archive'}; failure caught (553-554), logs error, returns False. base.py:353-355 then leaves did_leave_inbox False, so the audit records NOT-archived. Whether 'archive' resolves as a well-known folder for consumer/MSA accounts is the load-bearing fact and is account/locale-dependent.
findings.rationale: The failure-handling claim is code-confirmed, but whether the well-known 'archive' alias actually fails for MSA accounts requires a live account to verify — needs runtime; low if it resolves.

findings.title: Category read-modify-write PATCH is non-atomic and can lose concurrent category changes
findings.evidence: apply_category (347-359) and remove_category (380-386) both GET categories then PATCH the whole array with no If-Match/ETag. Two overlapping runs (e.g. launchd job + manual) on the same message produce a lost-update.
findings.rationale: Genuine non-atomic read-modify-write race on shared mailbox metadata; categories-only and overlapping-run-only — low severity confirmed.

findings.title: Direct dict indexing msg['id'] in list_messages will KeyError if Graph omits id; not defensively handled like other fields
findings.evidence: Line 461 id=msg['id'] (hard index) inside the parse loop at 443-467 while every sibling field uses msg.get(...). The try at 436-440 wraps only the _api_get, not the loop, so a missing 'id' raises KeyError that propagates out of list_messages uncaught. Same pattern at 255 (folder['displayName'], folder['id']) and 270-271 in _fetch_child_folders (though those are inside try/except).
findings.rationale: Real inconsistent defensive handling; $select includes id so Graph normally returns it, making the KeyError an edge case (malformed/partial item) — low.

findings.title: requests.Session never closed on the no-connect / health-check error paths; potential socket leak across repeated provider instantiation
findings.evidence: Session created lazily (206) and closed only in disconnect() (243-245). If the provider is used without the context manager, or connect() raises during _init_folder_cache after _get_session was first called, disconnect() may not run and the urllib3 pool is not closed.
findings.rationale: Genuine cleanup gap, but GC reclaims sockets and api/service.py:122 calls disconnect() in finally; practical leak only on misuse — low. Duplicate root-cause with U851.

findings.title: Interactive token result accessed without None guard; acquire_token_interactive returning None would raise TypeError instead of clean error
findings.evidence: Lines 184-189: result = acquire_token_interactive(...); if 'access_token' not in result. The silent path (178) guards `if result and 'access_token' in result`, but the interactive path has no None guard, so a None result would raise TypeError on `'access_token' not in None`, masking the auth-failure message.
findings.rationale: Real inconsistency vs the silent path; MSAL returns a dict in practice, so this only surfaces on an unusual MSAL/mocked path — low.

findings.title: Folder cache stores only displayName; well-known folders (Inbox, Archive, Sent) keyed by localized displayName can mismatch move targets
findings.evidence: Line 255 keys _folder_cache by displayName. apply_label fallback (426) and archive (548) use literal aliases 'inbox'/'archive'; a localized mailbox (e.g. 'Posteingang') would not match the cache key, and get_message_details (497-499) resolves parentFolderId to a localized displayName the callers don't expect.
findings.rationale: Real i18n correctness risk on non-English consumer mailboxes; edge case, read/label-state only — low.

findings.title: Category create error is logged at debug and masked as 'race condition'; a real failure raises a generic RuntimeError losing the original cause
findings.evidence: Same code (309-321): debug-level log of the real error (316), 'race condition' assumption, and RuntimeError without `from e` chaining (321). Identical root cause as U645.
findings.rationale: Restates U645's masked-error / no-cause-chaining finding on the same lines; no distinct defect.

findings.title: requests.Session leaked on all error paths (no context manager / try-finally)
findings.evidence: Same Session-only-closed-in-disconnect() defect as U847 (243-245), with the added (correct) note that the Session header holds the bearer token for the object lifetime (207-208).
findings.rationale: Same resource-leak root cause as U847; the token-in-header lifetime point overlaps U408. Duplicate.

findings.title: OData filter injection via folder name single-quote
findings.evidence: Same f-string OData $filter on lines 641-643 with an unescaped single quote in `part`. Identical root cause as U409 (which cites line 642).
findings.rationale: Same OData-injection/breakage finding on the same code; U852 adds the correct fix (double the quote) but is the same defect.

findings.location: list_messages lines 417-420 (page_token used as full URL)
findings.title: Pagination follows server-provided @odata.nextLink as the request URL (open-redirect / SSRF-shaped trust)
findings.evidence: Lines 417-419: when page_token is set, `url = page_token` is used verbatim as the request URL via _api_get, and _get_session attaches the Bearer token to all requests (207-208). There is no host validation that the URL is graph.microsoft.com.
findings.rationale: Real credentialed-GET-of-response-supplied-URL pattern, but page_token only originates from Graph's own @odata.nextLink (469) persisted in state — no current attacker-controlled path — so it's a latent/defense-in-depth concern, low.

findings.title: Unanchored .replace("Z", ...) on the ISO timestamp corrupts any string containing a stray 'Z'
findings.evidence: Lines 455 and 506 use unanchored .replace('Z','+00:00'), a global substring replace. Graph returns Z only as the trailing UTC designator, so a stray internal 'Z' would only appear in a malformed value, producing a ValueError that is swallowed to date=None.
findings.rationale: Technically true that the replace is unanchored, but Graph timestamps never contain a non-trailing 'Z', so the only effect is on already-malformed input which is already swallowed — cosmetic smell, info severity.

findings.location: ensure_label_exists, lines 641-646 ($filter displayName eq '{part}' lookup) and 658-664 in __init__ folder cache by displayName
findings.title: Folder lookup by displayName eq '{part}' returns the FIRST match across the whole mailbox, ignoring parent scope when parent_id is None
findings.evidence: Lines 638-645: when parent_id is None, child_url = GRAPH_API_FOLDERS (whole-mailbox top-level), then $filter=displayName eq '{part}' and result['value'][0]['id'] takes the first match. _init_folder_cache line 255 also keys self._folder_cache[folder['displayName']] = folder['id'], so duplicate top-level displayNames overwrite each other. Both branches bind to an arbitrary first match across the unscoped top-level set.
findings.rationale: Real but narrow: requires duplicate-named or localized-collision top-level folders, which Graph permits but is uncommon; mis-routing only on that edge. Severity stays low.

findings.title: Folder names containing a literal '/' collide with the hierarchical path separator, corrupting the folder cache and routing moves to the wrong folder
findings.evidence: Line 270 builds full_name = f"{parent_name}/{folder['displayName']}" and line 607 does parts = label.split('/'). A literal '/' inside an Outlook displayName is indistinguishable from the hierarchy separator: a top-level folder named 'Work/Dev' and a real Work>Dev hierarchy both key to 'Work/Dev', and split('/') will resolve/create against the wrong node.
findings.rationale: Genuine separator-collision; distinct from the round-1 recursion/duplicate note. Probability low (folder names rarely contain '/'), so low severity.

findings.location: apply_category, lines 346-356 (de-dupe is case- and whitespace-sensitive)
findings.title: Category de-dupe is exact-string; Graph treats category names case-insensitively, so repeated runs append duplicate-by-case categories
findings.evidence: Line 353 is `if category not in current_cats:` — exact case-sensitive Python membership. The defect (duplicate-by-case appended) only manifests if Graph stores/returns a category displayName with different casing than the requested string. Whether Graph normalizes/case-folds master category names on read is not determinable from this source.
findings.rationale: The static code does use exact-string dedupe, but the bug requires Graph's actual case-handling behavior, which is not knowable statically — hence needs_runtime, low.

findings.title: Archive move and folder move share no idempotency/verification — re-running archive on an already-archived message issues a redundant move and a failed move is only logged
findings.evidence: archive() (544-555) POSTs /move with destinationId='archive' with no prior check of current folder and no read-back; the sole success signal is _api_post not raising (line 551-552). apply_label (527-537) likewise moves without verification. There is no idempotency guard analogous to apply_category's membership check.
findings.rationale: Factually accurate. A redundant re-move on an already-archived message is harmless (Graph treats it as a no-op move), so impact is low; the 'silent stay in inbox on failure' relies on the separate round-1 #652 alias concern.

findings.title: Folder-name attribution is ambiguous: reverse lookup of parentFolderId returns the first cache entry, mis-naming same-named child folders
findings.evidence: Lines 497-500 scan _folder_cache for the entry whose id == parentFolderId and break on the first match. Graph folder ids are globally unique, and the cache maps distinct full-path keys to distinct ids; thus at most one entry can match a given parentFolderId. The 'first of several names for the same id' scenario the finding describes cannot arise because two different folders never share an id. The finding's own text concedes 'collision is impossible'.
findings.rationale: The reverse id->name lookup is deterministic and correct since ids are unique; the claimed mis-attribution has no path to occur. False positive.

findings.title: Gmail batch label-get with no error surfacing and no batch-size chunking — silently drops failed/over-limit entries
findings.evidence: Lines 21-23 callback appends only on `if not exception:` with no else/log, so per-label errors vanish (confirmed, overlaps U899). The 'one batch over ALL labels, >100 silently dropped' claim is conditional/uncertain: googleapiclient's new_batch_http_request raises at add() when exceeding the 1000-request limit (not 100, and not a silent tail-drop) and the current taxonomy size is unverified; this part is speculative.
findings.rationale: The error-swallowing half is real, but the batch-size/silent-tail-drop half is overstated and condition-dependent.

findings.location: lines 1-15 (whole blueprint; envVars only sets PYTHONUNBUFFERED)
findings.title: render.yaml declares no Python/runtime version and no dependency install step, fully delegating reproducibility to the unpinned Dockerfile base `python:3.11-slim`
findings.evidence: render.yaml L6 'runtime: docker', L9 'autoDeploy: true', L13-14 envVars only sets PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 (no PYTHON_VERSION, no install step). Dockerfile L1 'FROM python:3.11-slim' is a moving tag, not digest-pinned; requirements installed at build via 'pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt -r requirements-api.txt -r requirements-mcp.txt' (Dockerfile L8-10) with no hash-lock. So every push rebuilds against whatever 3.11.x patch + dep versions resolve at that moment, and autoDeploy ships it with no human gate.
findings.rationale: Factual claims all check out; this is a genuine reproducibility/hardening weakness (no digest pin, no hash-lock, autoDeploy), but it is a config hygiene concern not a functional defect — low severity is correct.

findings.title: ACP charge path depends on a Stripe PREVIEW API surface (Shared Payment Tokens) only loosely tied to the `stripe>=15.2,<16` SDK pin
findings.evidence: Confirmed: acp/payment.py:28 `STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION = "2026-04-22.preview"`, passed via `options={"stripe_version": STRIPE_SPT_API_VERSION}` at L86 on the SPT PaymentIntent create; requirements pins `stripe>=15.2,<16`. But the per-call explicit `stripe_version` pin is the documented preview mechanism and is MORE robust than SDK defaults; the asserted breakage ('any 15.x minor that alters preview handling can break the charge') is unproven and the finder self-rated confidence 'low'.
findings.rationale: Real money-path-on-preview-API observation but the breakage scenario is speculative; overstated relative to the actual explicit-version pinning in code.

findings.title: stripe major pinned <16 — billing code uses StripeClient/v1 namespace; verify floor matches the API surface used
findings.evidence: Confirmed the API surface usage: api/billing.py:71 `stripe.StripeClient(key)`, :139 `client.v1.checkout.sessions.create`, :158 `client.v1.billing_portal.sessions.create`, :182-183 `stripe.Webhook.construct_event` / `stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError`. (Finding cited bare `billing.py`; actual path is api/billing.py.) Whether the literal floor `15.2` exposes the `.v1.*` service layout cannot be determined statically — it requires checking the Stripe SDK changelog or an installed pinned version. CI installs latest `<16`, so the floor is unexercised.
findings.rationale: Lower-bound-vs-API-surface risk is real but only confirmable by checking the actual stripe 15.2 release contents (runtime/dependency resolution).

findings.title: iCloud credentials passed as inline env assignments to python (visible in process list and unquoted expansion)
findings.evidence: Lines 47-50 do pass IMAP_PASS="$ICLOUD_IMAP_PASS" as an inline command-prefix env assignment, so the secret enters the python child's environment. But the cited exposure mechanism (/proc/<pid>/environ) does not exist on the target platform: `uname` = Darwin, `/proc/self/environ` absent. The value also originates from 1Password's standard env-injection pattern, which is already the project's secret-handling convention (CLAUDE.md `source ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh`).
findings.rationale: Real but minor secret-handling smell; the concrete /proc exposure vector is invalid on macOS, so severity is overstated.

findings.title: Invalid-item test relies on Pydantic default quantity but does not assert charge is blocked
findings.evidence: test_create_invalid_item_not_ready (65-71) posts {'id':'no_such_pack'} (quantity defaults to 1 via Item model, models.py:27) and asserts status not_ready_for_payment + code invalid_items, but never drives /complete on that not-ready session. router.py:252-255 raises invalid_state for non-ready /complete, and no test reaches that branch from an invalid-item session, so the completion-block half of build_line_items' valid=False protection is unexercised.
findings.rationale: Confirmed but narrow: the status-shaping is asserted; the downstream completion-block is a genuine but minor gap — low severity/confidence as filed.

findings.title: Unwritable-audit tests rely on directory-as-path raising, an OS/behavior-specific trigger
findings.evidence: test lines 449-451 / 460-462 mkdir() a path then pass it as AuditLog(path=...); core/audit.py:224-226 `with open(self.path, 'a' ...) ... except OSError`. On POSIX open(dir,'a') raises IsADirectoryError (OSError subclass) so the catch fires; the tests assert degradation indirectly via `log.write_error is not None` (lines 454/472), not the specific exception class. Brittleness claim is factually correct.
findings.rationale: Accurate description of an OS-behavior-dependent fault-injection trigger; works on this POSIX environment and is a deliberate test-design choice, not a defect — a test-quality nit, info severity.

findings.title: current_period_end set in the test event is never asserted on the resulting account
findings.evidence: Line 67 sets current_period_end=1893456000 in the event; billing.py:261 passes _period_end(obj) into store.set_subscription, and _period_end (billing.py:294-303) has top-level-vs-items[0] fallback + int() coercion. The test's assertions (lines 77-80) check only plan, status, stripe_customer_id — never the persisted period-end. So the period-end propagation and _period_end's coercion logic are unverified by any test in the file.
findings.rationale: Accurate as stated: data is set up then ignored, leaving _period_end output untested. Downgraded to info — a missing-assertion nit, not a defect in production code; finding itself flagged confidence low.

findings.title: Invalid-YAML test asserts silent {} fallback as correct, masking a swallow-all error path
findings.evidence: load_yaml_config (core/config.py:119-126) does catch `Exception` broadly and return `{}` on malformed YAML, and the test (test_config.py:78-82) asserts the `{}` fallback as correct without asserting any log/warning — so the test-coverage gap (no assertion that the failure is observable) is real. However the finding's framing that the failure is fully unobservable / silent is inaccurate: line 125 emits `logger.warning(f"Failed to load config from {path}: {e}")`, so the failure IS logged at WARNING; it is simply not asserted by the test. The 'partially-parsed dangerous config' scenario is not demonstrable — yaml.safe_load returns parsed data or raises, it does not partially parse into the except branch.
findings.rationale: Real test-quality gap (no assertion that the swallow is logged) but overstated: the source already logs a warning, so the error path is not as silent/unobservable as claimed.

findings.title: Mail.app star test mocks _run_applescript and so never exercises AppleScript message_id interpolation/injection surface
findings.evidence: Test at line 12 monkeypatches provider._run_applescript = lambda script: scripts.append(script) or "ok", and line 25 only asserts 'flagged status' in scripts[0] — confirming the f-string template, not execution or escaping. providers/mailapp.py:297 does interpolate message_id raw into AppleScript via f-string: 'set targetMsg to first message whose id is {message_id}'. Test uses numeric '123' (line 18), never a metacharacter-bearing id, and _run_applescript is fully mocked so no real interpolation/execution is probed.
findings.rationale: Coverage observation is accurate, but framing it as an 'injection-shaped' surface overstates risk: Mail.app message ids are internally-generated integers (not attacker-controlled), so this is a narrow test-quality gap, not a security finding — hence partial.

findings.title: Frozen-dataclass tests use try/except + assert False idiom that misclassifies non-AttributeError outcomes
findings.evidence: test_models.py:59-63 uses try: msg.id='changed'; assert False, '...'; except AttributeError: pass. FrozenInstanceError subclasses AttributeError so the happy path works. The 'assertion message is swallowed' claim is wrong: if the assignment succeeded, the AssertionError from `assert False` is NOT an AttributeError and would propagate, failing the test correctly. (The test_rules.py reference is out of scope for this slice file.)
findings.rationale: Real but overstated/partly inaccurate: only a stylistic preference for pytest.raises stands; the control-flow critique about swallowed messages is incorrect, and the author self-rated confidence low.

findings.title: Escalation boundary values (exactly 24h and exactly 72h) are untested
findings.evidence: core/rules.py:1122 `if email_age_hours < 24` and core/rules.py:1131 `if 24 <= email_age_hours < 72` confirm the two branch boundaries (24, 72). tests/test_rules.py TestEscalation cases pass ages 12, 48, 50, 80, 100 (lines 269,274,280,285,289,293,298,303) — none equal 24.0 or 72.0, so an off-by-one flip of < to <= at either boundary would not be caught. The claim accurately reads both the code and the test ages.
findings.rationale: Real coverage gap (exact-boundary cases 24.0/72.0 untested) but it is a test-suite enhancement, not a production defect; correctly low/low — overstated only insofar as it is filed as a bug finding.

findings.title: Plan run-cap renders 'NaN' if the API returns a non-numeric monthly_run_cap
findings.evidence: Lines 906 and 936 both compute `${Number(p.monthly_run_cap).toLocaleString()} runs / mo` after only `== null` guard. A non-numeric string would yield 'NaN runs / mo'. (The finding's own self-correction is right: `== null` does catch undefined, so the undefined sub-claim is moot.) In practice both backends emit integer or null caps (plans.py monthly_run_cap:int|None; worker.mjs numeric|null), so NaN requires a malformed backend.
findings.rationale: Real fragility — no NaN guard — but the value comes from typed backends, so it is a graceful-degradation nit; info severity.

findings.title: Lint and type-check steps are advisory-only and can never fail the build (assertion-that-cannot-fail smell)
findings.evidence: ci.yml:35/51 both steps `set +e`, capture ${PIPESTATUS[0]}, emit only `::warning::` + step-summary, never propagate nonzero. Comment at 46/62 'pytest remains the hard gate'. Coverage upload at ci.yml:74 `fail_ci_if_error: false`.
findings.rationale: Accurate and explicitly the same defect as U135 (ruff/mypy advisory-only), restated at info severity. Marking duplicate of U135 (same-file root cause: lint/type steps cannot fail CI).

findings.title: Committed discovery artifacts are byte-in-sync with their generators (verified, no drift)
findings.evidence: build_agent_manifest (well_known.py:17-51) emits field-for-field the committed .well-known/agent.json (spec_version:34, oauth_scopes:45, all URLs from base) and build_feed (feed.py:33-64) matches acp/product_feed.json. scripts/gen_commerce_artifacts.py exists. Endpoint mappings confirmed: /v1/billing/plans→billing.py:94, /v1/audit/{run_id}→receipts.py:114, senders/check→app.py:79, triage/preview→app.py:85, /acp/checkout_sessions→router.py:166, /acp/feed.json→feed.py:67, /mcp mount→app.py:143, /openapi.json auto. (Could not run byte-diff: fastapi not installed in verify env; confirmed by structural field-level read instead.)
findings.rationale: Positive verification that the discovery surface is in sync with its generators and advertises only real routes; all cited route line numbers check out, so the clean-surface record stands as info.

findings.title: Bare `except:` in extract_domain swallows all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt
findings.evidence: Lines 27-33: try: ... except: return 'unknown' (bare except). Grep confirms the same anti-pattern in auto_drain.py line 63, with its own def extract_domain at line 60. The try body (split/strip on a string) cannot raise, so the handler is effectively dead.
findings.rationale: Bare except confirmed and repeated across scripts; pure code smell over unreachable code, info severity is correct.

findings.title: Local protected-senders file with real PII exists on disk and is correctly gitignored / never committed (verified clean)
findings.evidence: git check-ignore -v returns '.gitignore:8:config/protected_senders.local.txt' (matched). git ls-files returns empty (untracked). git log --all -- config/protected_senders.local.txt returns empty (never in history). File contains real protected-sender PII across legal, financial, government, and self-mailbox entries; specific values redacted for public review artifact safety. Loader core/rules.py:583 def _load_local_protected() reads ./config/protected_senders.local.txt; invoked at line 616 (_local_domains, _local_selfs = _load_local_protected()). 35 domains + 1 self parse cleanly.
findings.rationale: Positive privacy verification: PII-bearing local file is correctly gitignored, untracked, and absent from all history; loader reads it as designed. No leak.

findings.title: Scope assumption wrong: create_smart_mailboxes.scpt is plain UTF-8 AppleScript source, NOT a compiled binary — it IS reviewable (and unreviewed)
findings.evidence: `file create_smart_mailboxes.scpt` -> 'Unicode text, UTF-8 text'; first bytes are literal source 'tell application "Mail"...'. grep for 'create_smart_mailboxes' across *.sh/*.py/*.plist returns no hits (only this review's own findings.json/REPORT.md). 27 lines of readable AppleScript, unreferenced by deploy.sh, run_automation.sh, or any launchd plist.
findings.rationale: Both claims verified: it is plaintext source (not a compiled binary, so no binary coverage gap) and it is unwired from the scheduling pipeline; info severity correct.

findings.location: imports line 23 (re) and line 22 (email) unused; stats dict not defaultdict
findings.title: Minor: unused imports and re-decode of UID per fetch
findings.evidence: import re (line 23) is unused — grep confirms 're.' appears nowhere in the file. But 'email' (line 21, not 22) IS used: line 84 email.message_from_bytes(...). The claimed double uid.decode() is real: line 150 and line 156 each call uid.decode() for the same uid. stats uses stats.get(target,0)+1 (line 152), not a defaultdict (cosmetic).
findings.rationale: Mixed: 're' unused and double-decode are correct, but the title's claim that 'email' is unused is wrong (used at line 84). Cosmetic/info only.

findings.location: lines 6-7 (docstring: 'The heavy mcp SDK is imported lazily by mcp_server.server')
findings.title: mcp_server package docstring misstates lazy-import behavior — server.py imports the mcp SDK eagerly at module top level
findings.evidence: mcp_server/__init__.py lines 6-7 state the mcp SDK 'is imported lazily by :mod:`mcp_server.server`'. But mcp_server/server.py imports it eagerly at module top level: line 31 `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` and line 32 `from mcp.server.streamable_http import TransportSecuritySettings` (also line 34 `from api import service`). No lazy/deferred import of mcp exists inside server.py functions.
findings.rationale: Documentation defect only: server.py imports the mcp SDK eagerly, so the docstring's 'imported lazily by server' wording is factually wrong; no runtime/behavioral impact, so info severity stands.

findings.title: Dead/confusing config: module-level DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID reads env but __init__ re-reads the same env, so DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID is never used
findings.evidence: Line 63 defines DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID', ''); __init__ line 120 does self.client_id = client_id or os.getenv('OUTLOOK_CLIENT_ID') — re-reading the env directly. Repo-wide grep shows DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID is referenced only at its definition (line 63) and in the review docs; never consumed by any code.
findings.rationale: Dead, mildly misleading constant. Pure cosmetic/maintainability — info severity confirmed (the candidate's info matches; REPORT.md mislabels it 'high').

findings.title: Unused module constant GRAPH_API_MESSAGES (inbox-only) — never referenced; list_messages builds its own URL
findings.evidence: Line 25 defines GRAPH_API_MESSAGES = f'{GRAPH_API_BASE}/me/mailFolders/inbox/messages'. Repo-wide grep shows it referenced only at its definition and in review docs; list_messages (422-426) builds its own URL from _folder_cache / mailFolders/{folder}/messages and never uses the constant.
findings.rationale: Genuinely unused module constant. Cosmetic — info severity confirmed.

findings.title: fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/stripe ARE properly declared (false-positive killer)
findings.evidence: Verified declarations: fastapi>=0.110 (L3), uvicorn[standard]>=0.29 (L4), httpx>=0.27 (L6), pydantic>=2.0 (L8), stripe>=15.2,<16 (L13). Dockerfile L8-9 and .github/workflows/ci.yml:30 both install requirements.txt + requirements-api.txt + requirements-mcp.txt. Matches the task's known-declared list (fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/stripe).
findings.rationale: Accurate reconciliation note; these deps are declared, so any 'undeclared dependency' claim for them is a false positive. Informational, no action.

findings.title: mcp SDK IS properly declared (false-positive killer)
findings.evidence: requirements-mcp.txt:6 declares `mcp>=1.2,<2`. mcp_server/server.py imports the mcp SDK; api/app.py:39 imports `from mcp_server.server import http_app, mcp` inside try/except and degrades gracefully (app.py:41-45). Dockerfile and ci.yml:30 both install requirements-mcp.txt. The dependency is genuinely declared and installed on the deploy target.
findings.rationale: Correct reconciliation — mcp is properly declared and installed; any 'undeclared mcp import' claim is indeed a false positive.

findings.title: Core deps (google-api-python-client, msal, requests, pyyaml) ARE declared
findings.evidence: requirements.txt declares google-api-python-client>=2.0.0 (l2), google-auth-httplib2>=0.1.0 (l3), google-auth-oauthlib>=0.5.0 (l4), msal>=1.25.0 (l7), requests>=2.28.0 (l8), pyyaml>=6.0 (l11). Code imports match (providers/outlook.py imports msal/requests; core/config.py imports yaml). No undeclared core runtime dep.
findings.rationale: Accurate reconciliation/anti-false-positive note: all core runtime deps are declared; confirms no 'undeclared dependency' finding applies here.

findings.location: lines 52-53 (a { color: var(--green); } a { color: var(--accent); })
findings.title: Duplicate conflicting anchor color rule (dead CSS)
findings.evidence: Lines 52-53 are two consecutive `a { color: var(--green); }` then `a { color: var(--accent); }`; the second wins by source order, making line 52 dead. Both --green and --accent resolve to #0f766e (lines 18,22), so no visible effect today.
findings.rationale: Confirmed dead/duplicate CSS rule and a real maintenance hazard if the tokens diverge; cosmetic, info severity as filed.

findings.title: Smoke test asserts '"status":"ok"' as an exact substring, which is brittle against worker JSON.stringify spacing
findings.evidence: ci.yml:113 greps the literal `'"status":"ok"'` (no spaces). worker.mjs:68-72 json() does `JSON.stringify(body)` (no spaces) and worker.mjs:247 returns json({ status: "ok", ... }), so it passes today. The grep is coupled to serializer spacing, not JSON parsing.
findings.rationale: Correct and verified against worker source: passes now, brittle if a differently-formatted serializer (e.g., FastAPI/Starlette) ever serves /health. Genuine info-grade brittleness; prefer jq over substring grep.

findings.title: DB path default read from environment at module import time
findings.evidence: store.py:39 does read os.environ.get('MAIL_DB_PATH', 'data/app.db') at module import. However get_store() at store.py:451 re-reads os.environ.get('MAIL_DB_PATH', DEFAULT_DB_PATH) at first-use, so the live override IS honored; only the literal 'data/app.db' fallback is captured at import, which never changes. The finding itself states 'the practical effect is benign here'.
findings.rationale: Factually true that there is an import-time env read, but it is redundant/harmless because the runtime read in get_store() honors the live env — a cosmetic nit, correctly rated info.

findings.location: drain_loop line 180 print(...end='\r') interleaved with logging
findings.title: print with carriage-return progress interleaves badly with logging to stdout/file
findings.evidence: Line 180 `print(f'   Moved {len(batch_ids)} items...', end='\r')` writes carriage returns to stdout while the logger (line 28-32) also has a StreamHandler on stdout plus FileHandler. The \r and logger lines interleave; the console handler garbles. Cosmetic.
findings.rationale: Accurate cosmetic/observability nit. Info severity as the finding itself assigns.

findings.title: Worker /health version is hardcoded '0.1.0' and can drift from the API's __version__
findings.evidence: Worker line 247: `version: "0.1.0"` string literal. api/app.py:73-76 returns `__version__`; api/__init__.py:8 currently `__version__ = "0.1.0"` — so they match TODAY, but the worker's literal is an independent constant no build step keeps in sync, so a future Python version bump silently diverges.
findings.rationale: Hardcoded duplicate constant confirmed; currently in sync, drift is latent — info severity as the finding itself rated it.

findings.location: calculate_email_age_hours line 1163 signature `Optional["datetime"]`; import inside body line 1176
findings.title: datetime type annotation is an unresolved forward-ref string and import is function-local
findings.evidence: grep confirms `datetime` appears only at line 1163 (the string annotation), 1176 (`from datetime import datetime, timezone` inside the body), 1178-1179. There is no module-scope import; line 12 imports only from typing. So `Optional["datetime"]` is an unresolvable forward-ref for typing.get_type_hints(), and the function relies on a per-call local import.
findings.rationale: Confirmed exactly as described. Purely cosmetic/introspection — runtime behavior is correct because the import is function-local. Info severity is right.

findings.title: Group-creation failure silently swallowed by bare try
findings.evidence: Lines 3-5: `try` / `make new mailbox with properties {name:"Ω Automation"}` / `end try` with no `on error` clause and no logging. Any failure other than already-exists is silently swallowed with no operator signal.
findings.rationale: Bare try with no handler is exactly as described; info severity correct since swallowing the already-exists case is partly intentional and this is a benign manual script.

findings.title: Redundant unpinned pip install of msal/requests duplicates pinned requirements.txt entries
findings.evidence: requirements.txt lines 7-8: `msal>=1.25.0` and `requests>=2.28.0`, installed at deploy.sh:31. deploy.sh:32 then runs `pip install msal requests -q` again, unpinned and redundant. Since both lines run in the same venv and pip honors already-satisfied constraints, the second install is effectively a no-op but still a redundant resolver pass.
findings.rationale: Redundant unpinned re-install of already-pinned deps; correctly rated info. (corroborated=false echoed from slice.)

findings.location: lines 19-26 packages_distributions shim; line 12 unused 'import re' is used; line 14 socket
findings.title: Dead/likely-unnecessary importlib_metadata shim with bare-except swallow
findings.evidence: Lines 19-26 monkeypatch importlib_metadata.packages_distributions inside a try/except that swallows all exceptions (line 25 `except Exception: pass`). grep confirms packages_distributions is only assigned, never read elsewhere in the module - dead shim. The finding's own parenthetical concedes 're' IS used (verified: re.search at line 358) and socket IS used (socket.error at line 304), so those are non-issues.
findings.rationale: Dead-code shim with bare-except swallow is real but cosmetic copy-paste cruft; the import-re/socket asides are explicitly non-defects. Info severity as filed.

findings.title: resultSizeEstimate used as a 'Total' count is unreliable/approximate
findings.evidence: Line 18: `print(f"Remaining Items Sample (Total: {results.get('resultSizeEstimate', 'Unknown')}):")`. Gmail API documents resultSizeEstimate as an approximate count; labeling it 'Total' overstates precision. The line even prints a 'Sample' (maxResults=10 on line 15), so the 'Total' label is doubly misleading.
findings.rationale: Accurate API-semantics observation; cosmetic/informational only in a diagnostic script, so info severity stands.

findings.title: Direct subscripting of results['labels'] and response['payload']['headers'] / h['name'] with no guards (read-only, low impact)
findings.evidence: Describes the identical defect as U615: direct subscripting of results['labels'] (line 8) and response['payload']['headers']/h['name']/h['value'] (lines 26-28). Same root cause, same lines, same read-only-impact framing.
findings.rationale: Same brittle direct-indexing defect already captured by U615; line-range overlaps entirely.

findings.title: providers package __all__ omits the public DTO ListMessagesResult
findings.evidence: providers/__init__.py:14 imports only `EmailProvider, ProviderCapabilities` and __all__ (lines 16-19) lists only those two. providers/base.py defines three public classes: ProviderCapabilities (line 20), ListMessagesResult (line 39), EmailProvider (line 46). ListMessagesResult is omitted from the package facade. tests/test_api.py:8 and tests/test_audit.py:29 import `ListMessagesResult` directly from providers.base. Repo-wide grep for `from providers import` returns zero hits — every consumer already reaches into providers.base.
findings.rationale: Accurate facade-incompleteness observation; ListMessagesResult is a public DTO not re-exported, but since no code uses `from providers import` anywhere, there is no functional breakage — purely cosmetic, info severity stands.

findings.location: line 12 (from googleapiclient.errors import HttpError, module top level)
findings.title: providers.gmail imports googleapiclient at module top level, so direct import crashes without the optional Google dependency
findings.evidence: Line 12 imports googleapiclient.errors at module top level; providers/__init__.py uses lazy getters and __all__ excludes gmail, so importing the package does not pull gmail. google-api-python-client>=2.0.0 IS declared in requirements.txt (core dep), so this is NOT an undeclared dependency — confirmed only as a descriptive note that a deep import raises raw ModuleNotFoundError when the optional google stack is absent. Finding self-rates info.
findings.rationale: Dependency is declared and the lazy boundary works as designed; finding is an accurate info-level boundary note, not a defect.

findings.location: apply_actions, lines 419-421 and audit pending build 434-439
findings.title: Audit labels_added omits the STARRED label even though apply_actions adds it to addLabelIds
findings.evidence: When action.star is true, STARRED is appended to add_ids (lines 419-421) and physically written via batchModify (addLabelIds, line 456). But the audit pending record sets add_labels=list(action.add_labels) (line 437) — which does NOT include STARRED — and audit.record is called with labels_added=info['add_labels'] (line 494). audit.record (core/audit.py:172,205) stores labels_added verbatim. So the trust receipt's labels_added under-reports the actual STARRED mutation.
findings.rationale: Genuine audit-fidelity gap on the trust-receipt path: STARRED is applied but never appears in labels_added; minor but real, so low rather than info.

findings.title: Batch callback silently drops failed label fetches; report omits them with no warning
findings.evidence: Lines 21-23: `def cb(...): if not exception: detailed_labels.append(response)` — any failed label-detail GET is silently omitted, never logged. Such labels never reach sorted_labels (line 30) and so are absent from both the printed rows and total_archived (lines 34-42).
findings.rationale: Accurate, narrowly-scoped statement of the silent-failure; info severity fits a report-only script with no log of dropped entries.

findings.title: render.yaml relies entirely on the Dockerfile CMD; no PORT export and starter plan caveats are implicit
findings.evidence: render.yaml sets no PORT (correct: Dockerfile L19 'CMD uvicorn api.app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT:-8000}' honors the platform-injected $PORT). healthCheckPath '/health' (L8) matches app.py health() at L70 returning {status: ok, ...}. No secrets committed — credentials deferred to dashboard (L11-12). The finding is explicitly self-labeled as 'informational confirmation that the Render path is internally consistent.'
findings.rationale: Not a defect — it confirms the Render config is correct; the only residual is a latent starter-plan/single-uvicorn-worker scaling note that is unconfirmable statically, so it stays info-level rather than an actionable bug.

findings.title: uvicorn started via shell-form CMD as root with no worker/uvicorn pinning
findings.evidence: No USER directive anywhere in the Dockerfile (FROM python:3.11-slim, never drops privileges) and line 19 binds --host 0.0.0.0, so uvicorn runs as root. Shell-form CMD is acknowledged as intentional for ${PORT:-8000} expansion. This is a standard container-hardening gap, not a functional bug.
findings.rationale: Accurately described and self-scoped as info-level hardening; container does run as root with no USER, confirmed.

findings.title: Provider capability matrix claims Gmail batch 'Yes (1000/batch)' but text elsewhere references batchModify 1000 — verify against gmail.py batch size
findings.evidence: providers/gmail.py:31 'BATCH_MODIFY_SIZE = 1000   # Max IDs per batchModify (API limit)', applied at :452 'for i in range(0, len(msg_ids), BATCH_MODIFY_SIZE)' / :453 chunk. README:488 'Batch operations: Yes (1000/batch)' and :493 'up to 1,000 messages in a single API call' match the code exactly.
findings.rationale: The numeric claim flagged as unverified is verified CORRECT against the provider constant; the documentation is accurate, so there is no defect.

findings.title: Idempotency-Key claimed (begin) before request body validation cost is bounded; large body is read via await request.body() with no explicit size limit at this layer
findings.evidence: Every POST does `raw = await request.body()` (router.py:169,204,239,345) and _request_hash sha256's the full bytes (router.py:96-97); FastAPI also fully parses/validates the pydantic body before the handler's _gate runs. No max-content-length guard exists in this layer; the finding correctly notes protection 'relies on the ASGI server / upstream proxy'.
findings.rationale: Accurate code observation; exploitability hinges on uvicorn/proxy body limits not visible statically, so info severity is correct.

findings.location: create_checkout exception handler line 140-142; create_portal line 164-166
findings.title: Stripe error logged with exc_info=True may capture sensitive request internals server-side (low risk, log-only)
findings.evidence: billing.py:141 and 165 log with exc_info=True; client responses are generic (502, billing.py:142/166). The Stripe secret key is never in params (params built at 125-137 contain price_id, urls, account_id, email). Exposure is log-side only.
findings.rationale: The traceback can carry params/internal ids into server logs, but no secret/credential is in scope (the title's 'secret leakage' framing is overstated) -> info, log-only.

findings.title: Per-run pricing is internally inconsistent across the three agent pay paths (metered $0.01, pack_100 $0.01, pack_1000 $0.009)
findings.evidence: Same per-run price-ladder observation as U290/U508 (metered $0.01, pack_100 $0.01, pack_1000 $0.009). Self-classified info severity; framed as a 'deliberate decision' prompt rather than a defect.
findings.rationale: Identical arithmetic/economics observation to U290; intentional volume discount, advisory only. Corroborated echoed.

findings.title: verify() returns True for an empty body+empty signature edge and accepts None signature as empty string
findings.evidence: verify() (74) does hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature or '') with no hex-shape/length pre-validation. None coerces to '' and compares False against a real digest (harmless). The 'empty body + empty signature returns True' edge is incorrect: sign({}) yields a non-empty 64-char hex digest, so compare_digest(digest, '') is always False — verify never returns True for an empty signature.
findings.rationale: The robustness point (no input shape validation on the exported verifier) is valid and minor; the headline 'returns True for empty body+empty signature' is wrong (sign() never produces an empty string). Info.

findings.title: Header name match for 'From' is case-insensitive but value extraction takes first match only; OK, but fail-closed empty-string relies on is_protected_sender semantics
findings.evidence: Lines 61-68: cb sets out[rid]='' on exc/no-resp, and the `next(..., '')` default also yields '' when no From header exists. is_protected_sender('') returns True (core/rules.py line 797-798), so a message with a missing From header is treated as protected and never archived. This is benign over-protection (the documented fail-closed intent) but leaves such items stuck in inbox with no diagnostic distinguishing 'no From' from 'protected sender'.
findings.rationale: Self-described minor/defensive note; the behavior is exactly as stated and is intentional fail-closed conservatism, so info severity is appropriate.

findings.title: store_json_secret ignores account override and is dead/divergent relative to gmail_auth
findings.evidence: Confirms the same dead-code/divergent-duplication observation as U446: store_json_secret (no callers) vs gmail_auth.store_token_info (line 144, the live path). The 'ignores account override' sub-claim is weak — op_item_edit resolves OP_ACCOUNT internally via os.getenv (line 112), so account IS honored on writes too.
findings.rationale: Same dead-code/duplication root cause as U446; the account-override sub-claim is partly inaccurate since op_item_edit reads OP_ACCOUNT itself.

findings.title: Direct dict indexing on profile fields can KeyError; assumes keys always present
findings.evidence: Line 13: `total_msgs = profile['messagesTotal']` and line 22: `print(f"📨 Email Address: {profile['emailAddress']}")` both use direct dict indexing on the getProfile() response. No .get() guard, no try/except. A missing key (or an error-shaped response) raises KeyError and crashes check_health().
findings.rationale: Claim accurately describes the code; both fields are normally present in a Gmail getProfile response, so the failure mode is narrow, and this is a read-only diagnostic tool — info severity is appropriate.

findings.title: Health-check batch callback uses parameter name 'id' shadowing builtin and drops errors silently
findings.evidence: Lines 32-34: `def cb(id, resp, exc):` / `if not exc:` / `label_stats[resp['name']] = resp`. The param `id` shadows the builtin (cosmetic). When `exc` is truthy the label is silently skipped — no log, no print — so the unread report can be incomplete with no indication. `resp['name']` is also unguarded direct indexing.
findings.rationale: All three sub-claims are literally present in the code; impact is low because this is a read-only health report where a dropped label only yields an incomplete listing, not data corruption.

findings.title: in_audit_dir check uses normpath string-prefix and misses './audit' equivalence edge but flags absolute audit/ paths as outside
findings.evidence: cli.py:374-375 norm=os.path.normpath(audit_path); in_audit_dir = norm=='audit' or norm.startswith('audit'+os.sep). An absolute path inside the repo's audit dir (e.g. /repo/audit/x.jsonl) normpath-keeps it absolute, fails the relative-prefix test, and falls to the :382-388 'OUTSIDE gitignored audit/' branch -> false-alarm warning. The classification is purely lexical.
findings.rationale: Confirmed lexical misclassification for absolute/symlinked audit paths, but it only mis-words a warning (no behavioral effect), so info severity as the finding rates it.

findings.location: cmd_summary line 596/611 and cmd_pending — percentage/division and bar math
findings.title: Summary table bar uses int(pct/5) which can exceed the 20-cell width only if pct>100, but '░'*(20-int(pct/5)) goes negative-safe; verify pct cap
findings.evidence: cli.py:596/611 pct=(count/total*100) if total>0 else 0; :612 bar='█'*int(pct/5)+'░'*(20-int(pct/5)). total increments once per message (:562) and each message adds to exactly one tier (:564), so per-tier count<=total => 0<=pct<=100 => int(pct/5) in 0..20 => (20-int(pct/5)) in 0..20. No negative repetition, no division-by-zero (guarded). The finding itself concludes 'fine' and 'mostly defensive observation.'
findings.rationale: No defect exists in current code; the finding is an explicitly hypothetical/defensive note, and Python str*negative yields '' rather than erroring anyway.

findings.location: cmd_label exit code lines 454-457 vs cmd_escalate 945-947
findings.title: apply error_count>1 returns generic exit 1 but partial-success runs with some errors are indistinguishable from total failure
findings.evidence: cli.py:455-457 cmd_label returns 2 on violation else `0 if result.error_count==0 else 1`; cli.py:945-947 cmd_escalate identical pattern. A run with 1 error out of 1000 and a run where all failed both return exit 1.
findings.rationale: Accurate observability gap: exit status conflates partial and total failure; purely a refinement for automation gating, info severity.

findings.title: subparsers not marked required; no-command path returns 1 but args.func may be missing for unknown handling
findings.evidence: cli.py:1004 subparsers=parser.add_subparsers(dest='command', ...) with no required=True; :1197-1199 guards `if not args.command: print_help(); return 1`; every subparser calls set_defaults(func=...) (e.g. :1063 cmd_label, :1071 cmd_report, :1120 cmd_escalate, :1145/1165/1190), and :1201 calls args.func(args). All current subparsers set func, so no AttributeError today.
findings.rationale: Confirmed exactly as worded: safe now, but a future subparser added without set_defaults(func=...) would AttributeError at line 1201; an implicit-coupling/style note at info severity.

findings.title: CORS allow-origin is wildcard `*` on all API responses
findings.evidence: Line 63: `access-control-allow-origin: *` in COMMON_HEADERS applied to every json() response (line 71), including billing-plans and triage. As the finding states, this is likely intentional for a public share demo with no credentials. Same root cause as U555 (the more specific audit-endpoint variant of the wildcard-CORS observation).
findings.rationale: Same wildcard-CORS root cause as U555; the general policy note duplicates the audit-endpoint-specific finding. Intentional for a credential-free public demo, info severity.

findings.title: Subclass dataclasses redeclare 'name' with a default while base has it without — fragile field ordering
findings.evidence: ProviderConfig (25-31) declares `name: str` with no default as its first field; subclasses (37,46,58,68) redeclare `name: str = '...'`. Verified the file imports cleanly today (no TypeError), so it works currently. The hazard is prospective: adding a no-default field to ProviderConfig after a defaulted one would trigger 'non-default argument follows default argument'.
findings.rationale: Confirmed as a latent maintainability footgun, not a present defect — current code is valid. Info severity, matching the slice's own rating.

findings.title: DRY_RUN env var only togglable to True, never explicit False over a config-file True
findings.evidence: Same DRY_RUN empty-string-skip / truthy-present behavior described in U767 (lines 270-271). Both note =0/=false work and only the empty-string case leaves a config True in force.
findings.rationale: Identical root cause and behavior analysis as U767; de-duplicated.

findings.title: label_counts annotated as bare 'dict' not Dict[str,int]
findings.evidence: core/models.py:121 'label_counts: dict = field(default_factory=dict)' is a bare dict annotation; add_label_stat (line 126) does self.label_counts.get(label, 0) + 1 assuming int values. Accurate type-hygiene observation; the TypeError scenario requires a non-int value to be injected (no such path observed in-repo).
findings.rationale: True style/type-hygiene point exactly as stated; info severity appropriate — no demonstrated runtime failure path.

findings.title: add_label_stat is not thread-safe (read-modify-write on shared dict) — lost counts if apply_actions is ever parallelized
findings.evidence: core/models.py:124-126 is a non-atomic read-modify-write on a shared dict. Confirmed shared usage: base.py:343-345 and gmail.py:444-446 call result.add_label_stat per label on a single ProcessingResult, and base.py docstring (315-316) explicitly invites 'Providers with batch APIs should override for better performance'. All current paths are sequential, so today it is safe.
findings.rationale: Latent concurrency hazard correctly described and currently dormant (all loops sequential); info severity is right.

findings.title: Inner `break` after first matching pattern is correct but masks intent; combined with strict < it cannot pick the highest-priority within scanning
findings.evidence: Lines 1031-1036: the `break` (1036) is nested inside `if rule_config['priority'] < best_priority` (1033), itself inside `if re.search(...)` (1032). So a pattern that matches but does not improve best_priority does NOT break and the inner loop continues scanning remaining patterns of the same rule (harmless wasted work since all patterns in a rule share one priority).
findings.rationale: Accurate control-flow reading; no behavior bug, only a readability/efficiency smell and a fragility note for future edits. Info.

findings.title: TLD heuristic for relay domain recovery uses 2<=len<=18 all-alpha terminal label, can pick a wrong real domain for categorization
findings.evidence: Lines 702-704: `real = [c for c in cands if c.split('.')[-1].isalpha() and 2 <= len(c.split('.')[-1]) <= 18]; return min(real, key=lambda c: c.count('.')) if real else ''`. This is a TLD heuristic (alpha terminal label, fewest dots) that can pick a wrong real domain for categorization. It is only invoked at line 732 AFTER the protection loop (723-728), so it never affects the fail-closed protection decision.
findings.rationale: Heuristic is brittle as described but provably limited to categorization (protection iterates all candidates first). Info severity correct.

findings.title: Catch-all pattern r".*" combined with re.search makes the LABEL_RULES priority scan order-fragile; only priority 999 saves it
findings.evidence: Line 502 Misc/Other r'.*' at priority 999 (503), inserted last in LABEL_RULES. _find_best_label (1033) keeps the strictly-lowest priority, so r'.*' only wins as fallback. Correctness depends on every other rule having priority < 999. (The cited line numbers mix in _load_local_protected, which is unrelated to this claim — the real subject is Misc/Other + _find_best_label.)
findings.rationale: The maintainability trap is real (magic 999 + unanchored r'.*'), but no current defect — all rules are well below 999. Info. Location citation is partly mislabeled but the substantive claim is verifiable.

findings.title: Relay local-part decoding can fabricate non-protected real domains for categorization, but never lowers protection — verify over-recovery cannot mis-skip
findings.evidence: _resolve_addr checks protection FIRST: lines 723-728 iterate all cands and return on any _is_protected_domain match BEFORE _best_relay_domain is called at 732. is_protected_sender (800-805) iterates _iter_sender_domains, which yields the resolved domain from _resolve_addr (772-774). So protection wins; the finding is a verification request, not a defect, and the code is currently sound.
findings.rationale: Confirmed as a no-current-bug verification note: the fail-closed ordering is correct today; the finding correctly flags it as a single-reorder-away fragility. Info; not an actionable defect.

findings.title: deploy.sh writes a LaunchAgent plist and bootstraps it (matches the user's hard-blocked plist/LaunchAgent rule)
findings.evidence: deploy.sh:47-48 mkdir + cp into $PLIST_DEST=$HOME/Library/LaunchAgents/com.user.mail_automation.plist (line 10), then :54/:57 bootout and :60 bootstrap. The cp is unconditional and overwrites in place (non-atomic). The deployed job runs run_automation.sh, which per repo docs sources ~/.config/op/mail_automation.env.op.sh.
findings.rationale: Factually accurate description of a runtime LaunchAgent write; governance/config observation, not a code defect; info severity is correct.

findings.title: No Content-Security-Policy meta tag; inline script/style only (defense-in-depth gap, currently low risk)
findings.evidence: File has inline <style> (line 9) and inline <script> (line 247) with no Content-Security-Policy meta tag and no security headers. No fetch(), no innerHTML, no user input — all DOM writes (textContent at line 263) use a hardcoded section id. The file is static and not served by the ./web worker.
findings.rationale: Factually accurate defense-in-depth-only observation on a static, no-input, undeployed page; correctly self-rated info.

findings.title: Nav-dot links get their accessible name from section id, then are visually hidden via text-indent:-9999px
findings.evidence: Line 263: a.textContent = s.id (raw ids like 'hero','cta','market'); line 45: #nav-dots a {... text-indent:-9999px} hides that text off-screen. No aria-current on the dots — active section is conveyed only by the visual .active class (line 272).
findings.rationale: Accurate minor a11y hygiene note (raw-id labels, dated off-screen technique, missing aria-current); not a functional bug — info is correct.

findings.title: ecosystem.yaml delivery/revenue status disagrees with seed.yaml metadata
findings.evidence: Same defect as U598: ecosystem.yaml lines 5-11 status (web_app not_started / subscription planned) contradict reality. Verified seed.yaml declares `implementation_status: ACTIVE` and `promotion_status: PUBLIC_PROCESS`, and the billing surface (api/billing.py + stripe in requirements-api.txt) is live — exactly the contradiction this finding describes. Both findings point at the identical stale status fields in the same file.
findings.rationale: Same root-cause stale-status defect in ecosystem.yaml lines 5-11 as U598 (U927 frames it as ecosystem-vs-seed, U598 as ecosystem-vs-implemented-reality); collapsed as duplicate of the more complete R2 finding.

findings.title: Token re-serialized via creds.to_json then re-dumped — redundant parse/dump, and full token (incl refresh_token) persisted
findings.evidence: Lines 151-152: `token_json = json.loads(creds.to_json()); compact = json.dumps(token_json, separators=(',',':'), sort_keys=True)`. The parse-then-redump is real and purely cosmetic (canonicalize/compact). The finding itself states 'No functional bug'.
findings.rationale: Accurate observation; benign style/redundancy with no defect — info severity, as the finder self-rated.

findings.location: process_batch lines 149-154 callback closure over failed_ids per-chunk
findings.title: Batch callback appends to a per-chunk failed_ids but batch_results is shared across chunks (correct) — note time.sleep(2.0) fixed delay regardless of size
findings.evidence: Fixed sleeps confirmed: time.sleep(2.0) per batch-get chunk (165), 0.5s per modify (265), 2.0s per list page (325). With BATCH_GET_SIZE=20 and up to LIST_PAGE_SIZE=500 messages, that is 25 chunks x 2s = 50s of fixed sleep per page on gets alone. The per-chunk callback closing over a fresh failed_ids (149-154) is indeed correct as the finding states.
findings.rationale: Throughput observation is factually accurate and self-classified as a non-correctness perf note; info is correct.

findings.title: Broad `except Exception` swallows logout errors (acceptable) but no close of select state / no NOOP error surfacing
findings.evidence: Lines 168-171 wrap imap.logout() in bare `except Exception: pass`. The finding itself calls this acceptable for cleanup; the only substantive note is reduced observability if the connection died mid-apply.
findings.rationale: Accurate but acknowledged-acceptable cleanup pattern; informational only.

findings.title: apply_category default returns False even when CATEGORIES capability is set, with comment 'Subclasses override' — silent no-op if a subclass forgets
findings.evidence: base.py:268-270: after the CATEGORIES capability check, `return False  # Subclasses override`. A subclass advertising CATEGORIES but not overriding apply_category silently drops categories with no error/log. The one real CATEGORIES provider (Outlook) DOES override it (outlook.py:323), so no current provider hits the trap.
findings.rationale: Accurate description of a silent-no-op design trap; no live provider triggers it, so info severity is correct.

findings.title: Base batch_get_details default has no throttling and could hammer the API; not an abstractmethod so a provider can silently inherit a slow/unsafe sequential path
findings.evidence: base.py:167-172 loops get_message_details with no rate limiting; not an abstractmethod. A provider declaring BATCH_OPERATIONS but not overriding inherits the sequential path with no consistency check. Gmail (the batch-capable provider) does override (gmail.py:213). So capability/impl divergence is possible but not present in shipped code.
findings.rationale: Correct maintainability observation about capability-vs-implementation divergence; no current provider is affected, so info severity holds.

findings.title: star() silently ignores due_date and does not check STAR capability before applying
findings.evidence: base.py star() (lines 219-232) guards with 'if not (self.capabilities & ProviderCapabilities.STAR): return False'. The Gmail override (gmail.py:336-338) unconditionally calls apply_label(message_id,'STARRED') with no capability guard. Gmail declares STAR (lines 55-61), so it is harmless today; the override merely diverges from the base contract.
findings.rationale: Accurately confirmed contract-divergence smell, harmless given Gmail always has STAR — info as self-rated.

findings.title: _init_label_cache does not paginate labels().list (assumes all labels fit in one response)
findings.evidence: Lines 132-135 read results.get('labels',[]) with no nextPageToken loop. The Gmail labels().list endpoint is not paginated (returns all labels in one response), so this is correct in practice; finding correctly self-rates info as a defensive note only.
findings.rationale: True observation that pagination is absent, but Gmail labels.list is non-paginated, so no real defect — info.

findings.location: 417, 441 (archive via INBOX in remove_ids + batch key grouping)
findings.title: Star id resolution uses cache 'STARRED' but ensure_label_exists never adds STARRED to cache, so star always appends literal 'STARRED' (benign) while add label dedup may be defeated
findings.evidence: STARRED is resolved at line 420 via _label_cache.get('STARRED','STARRED') without routing through ensure_label_exists, so STARRED is never cached; ensure_label_exists caches system labels name->name only when called (lines 350-353). batchModify accepts mixed opaque ids and literal system names in the sorted-tuple key (line 441), so functionally fine. Pure benign-duplication note.
findings.rationale: Accurate dead-code/harmless-duplication observation with no behavioral impact — info.

findings.location: 248 (batch.execute not wrapped to reset between backoff retries)
findings.title: On batch.execute rate-limit retry, results dict may receive duplicate parses but request_id keys dedupe — no corruption, but failed_ids not cleared
findings.evidence: failed_ids is per-chunk (line 225) and never reset across _execute_with_backoff retries of batch.execute (line 248); results[request_id]=msg is idempotent (line 234). A message failing attempt 1 then succeeding on a re-exec stays in failed_ids and is re-fetched individually (lines 252-255), overwriting an identical good result — wasted quota only. Confirmed in source; only matters if execute() raises a global HttpError (rare, since per-request errors go to callback).
findings.rationale: Real but strictly wasted-quota under a rare retry path; no data corruption — info as self-rated.

findings.location: _execute_with_backoff lines 97-127 (lambda closure over loop var in apply_actions line 460)
findings.title: Late-binding closure: batch-modify lambda captures loop variable `body` by reference
findings.evidence: Identical root cause to U828: the batchModify lambda at line 460-464 captures loop variable 'body' (line 454) by reference, invoked synchronously by _execute_with_backoff (line 107) so correct today. Same code site, same latent-closure hazard.
findings.rationale: Same defect as U828 (late-binding closure over body in apply_actions) — duplicate.

findings.title: Star-only actions with no add/remove labels still create an empty batchModify body, and protected senders retaining star can be grouped with non-protected stars
findings.evidence: Same STARRED-omission root cause as U898, specialized to protected senders: a protected star-only action yields add_ids=[STARRED], remove_ids=[] (after _drop_if_protected clears archive/INBOX), and the receipt records labels_added=[] (line 437/494, add_labels empty) while left_inbox=False — so STARRED applied but unrecorded. The empty-batch claim is benign: add_ids=[STARRED] is a valid batchModify body, not empty.
findings.rationale: Compounds the U898 audit gap for protected senders (same defect); empty-body sub-claim is incorrect — partial/duplicate.

findings.title: IMAP star() ignores capability gating and always attempts \\Flagged even when STAR capability absent (non-gmail mode)
findings.evidence: __init__ adds STAR capability only `if use_gmail_extensions` (95-98). star() (328-335) and unstar() (337-344) override the base and unconditionally issue `uid('STORE', id, '+FLAGS', r'(\Flagged)')` regardless of capability, bypassing base.star's `if not (self.capabilities & STAR): return False` guard (base.py 230-232).
findings.rationale: Genuine declared-capability-vs-behavior inconsistency: a caller told STAR is unsupported (non-gmail mode) can still successfully star; harmless in practice (generic IMAP supports \Flagged), hence info.

findings.title: IMAP password resolved from env returns os.getenv twice (TOCTOU/None edge) — minor
findings.evidence: Lines 105-106 double `os.getenv('IMAP_PASS')` — identical observation to U832. Also notes the 1Password except logs only `e` (not the secret value), which is accurate.
findings.rationale: Same double-getenv smell as U832; the secret-not-logged observation is correct and benign. Deduplicated.

findings.title: os.getenv('IMAP_PASS') checked then re-read, and an empty-string IMAP_PASS bypasses the 1Password fallback returning ''
findings.evidence: Lines 105-106 double getenv; notes whitespace-only IMAP_PASS ('  ') is truthy and returned verbatim, causing an opaque login() failure rather than the clear ValueError.
findings.rationale: Same root cause as U832 (double getenv / falsy-empty handling). The whitespace-password nuance is a real but trivial edge; deduplicated to U832.

findings.title: Mail.app auto-launch + fixed 2s delay race
findings.evidence: Identical fixed-`delay 2` (line 98) readiness-race claim as U839.
findings.rationale: Exact duplicate of U839 (same lines, same defect).
